documents.t: This appendix lists the normative references on which the Interslavic specification rests, followed by a classified bibliography of descriptive sources for the individual Slavic languages and for comparative and historical Slavic linguistics. Entries are grouped thematically and ordered alphabetically by author within each group. Citation style follows a modified Chicago author–date convention.
About This Specification
documents.s: A.10. Changelog and About This Specification

documents.t: A false friend (lažny prijatelj) is a word that looks or sounds similar to a word in another Slavic language but differs substantially in meaning. Because Interslavic draws its vocabulary from the common Slavic stock, a learner who already knows one or more Slavic languages may occasionally misread an Interslavic word through the lens of their own language. This register documents the most consequential such pairs.

The glossary provides definitions for technical terms used throughout the specification. Users unfamiliar with specific linguistic terminology are encouraged to refer to this section for clarification.
All paradigms shown in both scripts. Hard stems listed first; soft-stem variants in parentheses where they differ.
Prefixes attach primarily to verbs but may also modify nouns and adjectives. The base form of any prefixed verb is perfective; secondary imperfectives are derived from it.
Prepositions grouped by governed case. Where a preposition governs multiple cases the alternation rule is noted.

This appendix presents annotated sample texts drawn from authentic Interslavic sources. Each entry provides the text in standard Latin orthography and an English translation. Brief grammar notes highlight features that recur across the Interslavic system.
Both scripts. Conj. I = consonant-stem / -aj- stems; Conj. II = -i- stems.
Interslavic is designed to occupy the centre of the Slavic linguistic space, offering solutions that serve the broadest possible range of Slavic speakers.
Lexical flavorization selects among documented Interslavic synonyms according to their regional distribution.
Morphological flavorization selects among documented inflectional and derivational variants of Interslavic to increase familiarity for speakers of a particular dialect area.
Orthographic flavorization adapts the written form of an Interslavic text to resemble the conventions of a particular national orthography.

Phonological flavorization makes use of the etymological alphabet  — the set of optional letters whose standard pronunciation is fixed but whose realization varies historically across the Slavic dialect continuum.

Sections through  (Normative status, Scope, and What this specification does not cover) are new specification framework content with no basis in the source material. The normative keyword definitions, module breakdown, and scope exclusions are not derived from any source.

This file has no direct source material in the original Interslavic documentation corpus. All content in this section requires expert review and committee validation before it can be considered authoritative.

This section specifies the normative principles governing word selection, the treatment of international vocabulary, the management of false cognates, the policy on calques and borrowings, and the criteria for neologism approval in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.
Interslavic morphology is founded on the common grammatical categories shared by the Slavic language family: grammatical gender, number, case, verbal aspect, tense, mood, and person.

This section specifies the inflection, short forms, possessive formation, and degrees of comparison of adjectives in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.
This section specifies the formation, comparison, and use of adverbs in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

This section specifies the productive morphological processes by which new words are formed in Interslavic: suffixation, prefixation, zero derivation, and compounding. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

The source material does not use RFC 2119 terminology (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY). The normative framing throughout this document is an editorial addition not present in the original grammar description.

The RFC 2119 normative framing (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY) is not present in the source material. The source uses informal language throughout. The RECOMMENDED and MUST claims in this introduction go beyond what the source states; the source simply says "it deserves recommendation to write digits instead of number words."
The RFC 2119 normative framing (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY) is an editorial addition. The source material does not use normative language or RFC 2119 terminology.

The source material does not use RFC 2119 normative language. The original text uses informal phrasing like "should only be used" rather than formal MUST/SHALL/SHOULD/MAY distinctions. The RFC 2119 framing below is an addition not present in the source.
This section specifies the forms, inflection, and usage rules for all pronominal categories in Interslavic. All normative statements use MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

This section specifies the grammatical categories, conjugation classes, tense and mood systems, non-finite forms, and irregular verb paradigms for verbs in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.
Historical Context

The Interslavic etymological alphabet — historically known as Naučny Medžuslovjansky ("Scientific Interslavic"), also informally called MS Plus or etimologičny pravopis ("etymological orthography") — is an OPTIONAL extension of the standard orthography.

Interslavic has two fully equivalent standard alphabets — one Latin-based and one Cyrillic-based — reflecting the historical division of Slavic writing traditions. Neither alphabet is based on any single national orthography; both were designed as compromises that are intuitively readable across the Slavic world and that permit straightforward, unambiguous transliteration between the two scripts. Published texts are encouraged to provide both Latin and Cyrillic versions so that readers on either side of the Latin–Cyrillic boundary can engage with the text without difficulty.

This section specifies the normative orthographic conventions for Standard Interslavic beyond the letter-by-letter correspondences already established in the relevant section The rules below apply equally to both the Latin and Cyrillic scripts unless a script is explicitly named.

Transliteration is the systematic, rule-governed conversion of text from the Latin script to the Cyrillic script, or vice versa, without reference to pronunciation rules beyond what the orthography itself encodes.
Sound system: phonemic inventory, phonotactics, stress, and phonological processes.
Inflection in Interslavic is kept as regular as possible.
Interslavic is neither a national language, nor does it attempt to emulate one.

Interslavic orthography is based on etymology rather than pronunciation, so consonant clusters arise that may appear unpronounceable to speakers of non-Slavic languages — for example, :isv[vozvršenje].
Stress in Interslavic is free: no single fixed-position rule is imposed for all words.
Core Design Principles

This section specifies the semantic and pragmatic rules of Interslavic: how grammatical and lexical choices map onto meaning, how context shapes interpretation, and how speakers deploy language to perform social actions. The rules defined here apply to all standard written and spoken Interslavic regardless of flavorizational register.
This file has no direct source material for verification. All content in this section requires expert review to confirm accuracy and consistency with established Interslavic standards.

This file has no direct source material for fact-checking. The pragmatic rules, ISV example forms, and phrasebook entries below have not been verified against an authoritative reference and require expert review.

This section specifies the semantic interpretation of tense and aspect combinations, the interaction of aspect with negation and phase verbs, the system of modal expressions, and the treatment of evidentiality in Interslavic.

This section specifies the syntactic rules of Interslavic: the principles that govern how words combine into phrases, clauses, and sentences. The rules defined here apply to all standard written and spoken Interslavic regardless of flavorizational register.

This section specifies the structural principles governing simple clauses in Interslavic: canonical constituent order, pragmatic deviations from that order, subject-verb agreement, pro-drop, and the syntax of existential sentences.

This section specifies the syntactic rules governing coordination in Interslavic: the behavior of coordinating and correlative conjunctions, agreement in coordinated noun phrases, and the conditions under which material may be elided or gapped in the second conjunct.

This entire file lacks direct source material. The source file (grammar-syntax.md by Jan van Steenbergen) does not cover negation syntax. All rules, examples, and paradigms in this section are AI-generated extrapolations from general Slavic patterns and cannot be verified against the original Interslavic documentation.

The primary source material for syntax (Jan van Steenbergen's grammar-syntax) contains only a brief note that "modifiers usually precede the noun" and that this is "the most neutral and the clearest way." The vast majority of this document — including canonical modifier ordering, numeral case government, apposition rules, possessive adjective distribution, agreeing/non-agreeing modifier theory, sentence-to-NP transformations, and capitalization rules — has no basis in the provided source material and appears to be editorial elaboration.

This file has no dedicated source document for question syntax. The core examples in the relevant section and  can be partially verified against Jan van Steenbergen's syntax page (grammar-syntax.md), but the detailed rules and additional sections ( li-placement,  multiple wh-questions,  embedded questions) are elaborations without direct source backing.

This file has no direct source material in the original Interslavic grammar by Jan van Steenbergen. The source material covers relative pronoun forms (ktory, koj, iže) and their inflection (in the pronouns section), but does not address relative clause syntax, gap vs. resumptive pronoun strategies, long-distance extraction, or free relative clause structure. Most of the syntactic analysis in this file is likely AI-generated and should be reviewed by the committee.

This section specifies the syntax of special clause types in Interslavic that have distinctive structural properties: imperatives (direct commands, hortatives, and optatives); impersonal constructions (meteorological predicates, dative-experiencer constructions, modal impersonals); reflexive constructions and their three functions; and complement control (subject-control and object-control verbs).

The majority of this document cannot be verified against the provided source material. The primary source (grammar-conjunctions.md) covers prepositions, not subordinating conjunctions or clause types. The secondary source (grammar-syntax.md) covers basic word order, questions, passive voice, and purpose clauses only. Sections on complement clauses, infinitival complements, tense correlation, conditionals, temporal clauses, concessive/adversative clauses, compound sentences, subordination connectors, and punctuation rules have no corresponding source material and may reflect the document author's synthesis rather than established Interslavic norms.

Consonant mutations before selected suffixes: k/c→č, g→ž, h→š (before -ji, -ka, -ko, -nik, -ny, -ok, -sky, -stvo). Soft nj hardens before -nik and -ny. Suffixes starting with -o- change to -e- after soft consonants.
documents.s: Appendix 5: Derivational Suffix and Affix Inventory

documents.t: Suffix Semantic class Output gender Example -ok diminutive (m., hard stem) m. pes → pesok "doggy" -ik / -ek diminutive (m., soft stem) m. muž → mužik "little man" -ka diminutive (f.) f. kniga → knižka "booklet" -ko diminutive (n.) n. polje → poljko "small field" -stvo abstract quality from person/thing n. prijatelj → prijateljstvo "friendship"; muž → mužstvo "manhood" -išče place associated with noun n. voz → vozišče "parking lot"; ogonj → ognišče "hearth" -je (+ prě-/pri-/pod- etc.) place in relation to another place n. morje → primorje "land by the sea"; brjuh → podbrjušje "underbelly" -nik person in charge of / handler of; container m. glava → glavnik "boss"; kniga → knižnik "bookcase" -nica female equivalent of -nik f. rada → radnica "female counsellor" -ec person representing quality; inhabitant m. Albanija → Albanec "Albanian"; glupy → glupec "fool" -ica female equivalent of -ec f. Albanec → Albanica "Albanian woman" -ka (person) female equivalent (general) f. prijatelj → prijateljka "girlfriend"; gradžanin → gradžanka "female citizen" -an(in) inhabitant (alternative to -ec) m. (cf. Slovjanin, Moskvjanin) -e (-et- stem) young animal n. kot → kote "kitten"; jelenj → jelene "fawn"

Suffix Semantic class Output gender Example -ost abstract quality from adjective f. dobry → dobrost "goodness" -ec person embodying quality m. izgnany → izgnanec "exile" -išče place with quality n. mokry → mokrišče "wetland"

Suffix Semantic class Output gender Example -telj agent/professional (one who does) m. učiti → učitelj "teacher" -ač professional/trade m. kovati → kovač "blacksmith"; krojiti → krojač "tailor" -ar professional/trade m. pekti → pekar "baker" -išče place of activity (often from L-participle) n. igrati → igrališče "playground"; žiti → žilišče "dwelling" -nje verbal noun (gerund), from past pass. part. n. dělati → dělanje "doing"; žiti → žitje "life" -tje verbal noun (alternative after -t stem) n. piti → pitje "drinking"

Suffix Semantic class Example -sky relating to geography, entity, profession žena → žensky "female"; morje → morsky "marine" -ji relating to animals, divine entities, persons člověk → člověčji "human"; konj → konji "horse's" -ny general relational adjective noč → nočny "nocturnal"; pismo → pisemny "written" -ěny made of (material) drěvo → drěvěny "wooden"; steklo → steklěny "of glass" -ovy / -evy having characteristics of; colour/type pomaranča → pomarančevy "orange (colour)" -ovity / -evity resembling, -like malpa → malpovity "ape-like"

Suffix/Prefix Semantic class Example ne- (prefix) negation dobry → nedobry "not good" prě- (prefix) intensification ("very/too") umorjeny → prěumorjeny "exhausted" -ovity weakening/approximation ("-ish") zeleny → zelenovity "greenish" bez- … -ny (circumfix) absence of quality dom → bezdomny "homeless"; naděja → beznadějny "hopeless" -šnji from adverb: adjectivising temporal/local adverbs včera → včerašnji "yesterday's"; dnes → dnešnji "today's"

Form Formation Example Analytic comparative vyše / bolje + positive vyše prosty "simpler" Synthetic comparative (adj.) -ějši / -ejši (soft) replacing -y/-i bogaty → bogatějši Synthetic comparative (adj. in -ky) -ši kratky → kratši; vysoky → vysši Comparative adverb -ěje / -eje novo → nověje Comparative adverb (from -ky) -je (+ iotation) daleko → dalje; široko → širje Superlative naj- + comparative najnověje, najvysši Superlative (simplified) naj- + positive najnovy Irregular comparatives: Positive Comparative (adj.) Comparative (adv.) dobry "good" lěpši / lučši lěpje / lučše zly "bad" gorši gorje veliky "big" večši / bolši veče / bolje maly "small" menši menje blagy "pleasant" unši unje legky "easy" legši legše mekky "soft" mekši mekše

Suffix Semantic class Example -livy tendency/habit govoriti → govorlivy "talkative" -omy / -emy / -imy possibility ("-able/-ible") nesti → nesomy "portable"; razuměti → razumlivy "understandable" Participles four adjectival participle forms dělajuči, dělajemy, sdělavši, sdělany

Suffix/Pattern Semantic class Example -ovati general denominal verb ("act like; do with") malpa → malpovati "act like a monkey"; noč → nočevati "spend the night" o- + -iti give/acquire quality (noun/adj. → verb) kamenj → okameniti "stone"; světlo → osvětliti "illuminate"; svoboda → osvoboditi "free" obez- + -iti deprive of quality (noun → verb) glava → obezglaviti "decapitate"; dom → obezdomiti "make homeless" -ěti become (adj. → verb of becoming) běly → bělěti "turn white"; bogaty → bogatěti "grow rich" o- + -iti (from adj.) make (someone) have the characteristic črny → očrniti "blacken" u- + -iti (from comparative) make more/better lěpši → ulěpšiti "improve"; menši → umenšiti "reduce" od- + -iti (from adj.) remove the characteristic črny → odčrniti "remove black colour"
Suffix Example -šnji nyně → nyněšnji "current, present"
Pattern Example Replace adj. ending with -o dobry → dobro "well"; svěži → svěže "freshly" Replace comparative -(ěj)ši with -(ě)je slabějši → slaběje "more weakly"

Two roots joined by connecting vowel -o- (hard) / -e- (soft): Example Components Meaning vodopad voda + padati waterfall samokritika sam + kritika self-criticism myšelovka myš + loviti mousetrap zemjetresenje zemja + tresenje earthquake Borrowings from English may use hyphen: rok-muzika "rock music", veb-stranica "web page".

Prefix Function Example ne- negation (adj., nouns) neprijetny "unpleasant", nedobry "not good" prě- intensification, excess prěumorjeny "exhausted", prěsladky "too sweet" naj- superlative najdobry "best", najnověje "most recently" bez- absence (with -ny or -nik) bezdomny "homeless", bezbožnik "godless person" malo- little, small (compounding) maloznačny "insignificant" mnogo- much, many (compounding) mnogokratno "many times" samo- self- (compounding) samokritika "self-criticism" su- co-, together (compounding) surabotati "co-work"

info This appendix lists the normative references on which the Interslavic specification rests, followed by a classified bibliography of descriptive sources for the individual Slavic languages and for comparative and historical Slavic linguistics. Entries are grouped thematically and ordered alphabetically by author within each group. Citation style follows a modified Chicago author–date convention.

The following sources have normative status for this specification. Any conflict between this document and a normative reference is resolved in favour of this specification as the primary standard; normative references are authoritative for factual claims about individual Slavic languages and for the reconstructed Proto-Slavic forms cited in morphological tables.

Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Grammar (online reference grammar). Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/grammar.html. Continuously updated; the version current at the date of publication of this specification is considered normative for all grammatical paradigms. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Orthography. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/orthography.html. Normative for the standard Latin and Cyrillic alphabets and the etymological alphabet extension. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Phonology and Pronunciation. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/phonology.html. Normative for the phoneme inventory and pronunciation guidelines. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Vocabulary / Derivation. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/derivation.html. Normative for the word-formation system and morphophonological alternation rules. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Vocabulary / Word Formation. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/word_formation.html. Normative for derivational affixes and compounding rules. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Flavourisation. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/flavorizacija.html. Normative for the description of dialectal and register variation (flavorization) within Interslavic. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Design Criteria. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/design-criteria.html. Normative for the principles governing lexical selection and grammatical compromise. Van Steenbergen, Jan, and Vojtěch Merunka. Medžuslovjansky jezyk (unified post-2017 standard). Committee document produced following the First Conference on the Interslavic Language (CISLa 2017), Staré Město, Czech Republic, June 2017. Normative for all post-merger standardisation decisions.

Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Dictionaries (multilingual). Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/slovniky.html. Normative word-list for lexical entries cited in this specification. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Interslavic Voting Machine (computational tool). Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/voting_machine.html. Implements the majority-voting algorithm described in the relevant section of this specification; results produced by this tool are considered authoritative for lexical derivation questions not otherwise resolved by the grammar.

Bambas, Vaceslav. Vsjeslovianьskyь jazyk. Prague, 1861. Early pan-Slavic language project based on Church Slavonic; cited in the historical survey. Herkeľ, Ján. Elementa universalis linguae Slavicae e vivis dialectis eruta et sanis logicae principiis suffulta. Budapest: Landerer, 1826. First systematic West-Slavic-based pan-Slavic grammar; basis for the entry Universalis Lingua Slavica in the historical overview. Križanić, Juraj. Gramatično izkazanje ob russkom jeziku [grammatical exposition of the Russian language]. Written c. 1666; first published in part in: Bezsonov, Pyotr, ed. Русское государство в половине XVII века [The Russian State in the mid-seventeenth century]. Moscow, 1859–1860. The earliest documented constructed Interslavic language. Majar-Ziljski, Matija. Uzajemni pravopis slavjanski [Mutual Slavic Orthography]. Zagreb, 1865. Introduced the comparative-grammar approach to pan-Slavic language design; directly anticipates the methodology of modern Interslavic. Merunka, Vojtěch. Novoslověnsky jazyk [Neoslavonic]. Prague, 2010. Published grammar of the Novoslověnsky/Neoslavonic project; merged with Interslavic after CISLa 2017. Merunka, Vojtěch, and Jan van Steenbergen. "Medžuslovjansky: A Proposal for a Modern Pan-Slavic Language." Slovjani.info, 2017. Available at: http://slovjani.info/. Summarises the post-merger unified standard. Van Steenbergen, Jan. A Short History of Interslavic. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/history.html. Primary narrative source for (historical context) of this specification. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Language Comparison: Interslavic and Other Languages. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/faq.html. Source for the comparative discussion of Interslavic versus Russian, Esperanto, Slovio, and Old Church Slavonic. Van Steenbergen, Jan. Introduction to Constructed Slavic Languages. Available at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/conlangs.html. Surveys the full tradition of pan-Slavic constructed languages from the 17th century to the present. Zamenhof, Ludwik Łazarz. Fundamento de Esperanto. Paris: Hachette, 1905. Included for comparative reference; Esperanto's design principles are contrasted with those of Interslavic in the relevant section
documents.s: Section A. Interslavic and Pan-Slavic Constructed Languages

documents.t: Diels, Paul. Altkirchenslavische Grammatik. 2 vols. Heidelberg: Winter, 1932–1934. Standard reference grammar of Old Church Slavonic used for canonical paradigm reconstruction. Lunt, Horace Gray. Old Church Slavonic Grammar. 7th revised ed. Berlin: Mouton de Gruyter, 2001. The standard English-language reference grammar of OCS; used for paradigms cited in the historical morphology sections of this specification. Mayer, Gerald L. "The Proto-Slavic Vowel System." In: Papers in Slavic Phonology, edited by H. Baran. Ann Arbor: Michigan Slavic Publications, 1976. Background source for vowel reconstruction principles. Meillet, Antoine. Le slave commun. 2nd ed. Paris: Champion, 1934. Foundational comparative grammar of Common Slavic (Proto-Slavic); primary reference for reconstructed proto-forms cited in this specification. Shevelov, George Y. A Prehistory of Slavic: The Historical Phonology of Common Slavic. Heidelberg: Winter, 1964. Detailed treatment of Proto-Slavic phonological development; consulted for etymological alphabet correspondences. Vaillant, André. Grammaire comparée des langues slaves. 5 vols. Paris: Klincksieck / Lyon: IAC, 1950–1977. Most comprehensive comparative grammar of Slavic; normative reference for reconstructed morphological paradigms.
documents.s: Section B. Old Church Slavonic and Proto-Slavic

documents.t: Carlton, Terence R. Introduction to the Phonological History of the Slavic Languages. Columbus, OH: Slavica, 1991. Accessible overview of Common Slavic phonological developments; used for vowel shift summaries. Comrie, Bernard, and Greville Corbett, eds. The Slavonic Languages. London: Routledge, 1993. Comprehensive typological survey of all Slavic languages; primary reference for individual-language data used in the voting system. Sussex, Roland, and Paul Cubberley. The Slavic Languages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2006. Up-to-date reference for synchronic and diachronic properties of all Slavic languages; used throughout for language-specific grammatical and lexical data.

In: Comrie and Corbett 1993, 388–451. Ukrainian​ Shevelov, George Y. A Historical Phonology of the Ukrainian Language. Heidelberg: Winter, 1979. Pugh, Stefan M., and Ian Press. Ukrainian: A Comprehensive Grammar. London: Routledge, 1999.

Wiktionary contributors. False Friends of the Slavist. English Wikibooks. Available at: http://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/False_Friends_of_the_Slavist. Ongoing collaborative list of inter-Slavic false cognates; cited in the design criteria documentation and used as a starting point for Appendix 10.7. Zaliznyak, Andrey A. Grammatičeskij slovar' russkogo jazyka [Grammatical Dictionary of the Russian Language]. 6th ed. Moscow: AST-Press, 2008. Used for inflectional class data in Russian.
documents.s: Section E. Lexicographic and False-Friend Resources

documents.t: Auger, Julie, and Jeffrey Steele. "Structure of a Constructed Language." In: Oxford Handbook of the History of Linguistics, edited by Keith Allan. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2013. Background on the typology of constructed auxiliary languages. Blanke, Detlev. Internationale Plansprachen: Eine Einführung. Berlin: Akademie-Verlag, 1985. Standard theoretical survey of planned/constructed languages; provides the analytic framework for comparing Interslavic with Esperanto and other auxlangs. Gröschel, Bernhard. Das Serbokroatische zwischen Linguistik und Politik. Munich: Lincom, 2009. Referenced for the discussion of Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian standardisation. Matthews, Peter H. Oxford Concise Dictionary of Linguistics. 3rd ed. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2014. Terminological reference for all linguistic technical terms defined in Appendix 10.6.

documents.t: Interslavic Language Portal. https://interslavic.online/. The primary public-facing home of the Interslavic standard, housing the grammar, dictionary, and learning materials. Interslavic Wikipedia. https://isv.wikipedia.org/. Collaborative encyclopaedia written in Interslavic; an ongoing corpus of authentic Interslavic text used for style reference. Interslavic Facebook Group. https://www.facebook.com/groups/interslavic. Principal community forum; referenced for information about community size and language testing events. CISLa 2017 — First Conference on the Interslavic Language. Proceedings and programme archived at: http://cisla.slavic-union.org/. Historical record of the conference at which the modern unified Interslavic standard was agreed. Slovjani.info. http://slovjani.info/. Scientific journal publishing articles in or with abstracts in Interslavic; established 2017. The Painted Bird (film). Dir. Václav Marhoul. Czech Republic, 2019. Feature film whose dialogue was written in Interslavic; the first large-scale public deployment of Interslavic in a mass-media context. Information at: http://steen.free.fr/interslavic/the_painted_bird.html.
