documents.t: The Interslavic Language Standard Specification is the normative reference document for Medžuslovjansky jezyk / Меджусловјанскы језык — a zonal auxiliary language constructed to be broadly intelligible to speakers of the Slavic language family without prior study. This specification consolidates and supersedes the body of prior descriptive work on Interslavic. It is based primarily on Jan van Steenbergen's materials at steen.free.fr/interslavic, which represent the accumulated result of the Slovianski project (2006–2017) and its subsequent merger with Novoslověnsky (Neoslavonic) at CISLa 2017. The specification covers: Standard Interslavic — the full grammatical system including seven cases, three genders, verbal aspect, and full conjugation. Basic Interslavic (Slovianto) — a simplified subset structured in progressive levels, intended for beginners and non-Slavic learners.

Changes to normative content are managed by the Interslavic Language Committee, the successor body to the five-member unification committee formed in June 2017 following CISLa 2017. Proposed normative changes SHALL undergo: Review against the twelve source languages ; Assessment of impact on mutual intelligibility; A public comment period of not less than thirty (30) days. The version identifier follows the form MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH as specified in the relevant section

This specification draws on the work of the following principal contributors: Jan van Steenbergen — primary architect of modern Interslavic; author of the foundational grammar, orthography, and vocabulary at steen.free.fr/interslavic. Vojtěch Merunka — author of Novoslověnsky (Neoslavonic); co-chair of the 2017 unification committee. The CISLa 2017 unification committee: Jan van Steenbergen, Vojtěch Merunka, Roberto Lombino, Michał Swat, Pavel Skrylev. The broader Interslavic community whose contributions, corrections, and discussions have shaped the language over more than a decade. Historical contributions are acknowledged in the relevant section of the specification.
For feedback, suggestions, or inquiries regarding this specification, contact the Interslavic Language Committee via the official project channels at interslavic.online.

Status: Draft — under committee review Scope of changes: Complete restructuring of the specification into ten normative sections plus appendices, superseding the earlier informal descriptive structure. Adoption of RFC 2119 normative keyword conventions (SHALL, MUST, SHOULD, MAY) throughout. Formal definition of the weighted six-subgroup voting mechanism previously described only informally. Explicit codification of the Basic Interslavic / Standard Interslavic register distinction . Expanded phonological section with IPA transcriptions and explicit phonotactic rules. Reorganized morphology chapter with machine-readable paradigm tables. New sections on syntax , semantics and pragmatics , and flavorization . Version identifier scheme introduced . Known open items: Sections marked :::info[TODO: Committee Input Needed] require formal committee decisions before this version is designated as a stable normative release. Sections marked :::caution[Committee Review Needed] contain unresolved source conflicts.

Status: Superseded Date: 2017 — following CISLa 2017 (Staré Město, Czech Republic, 1–2 June 2017) Scope of changes: Unification of Slovianski and Novoslověnsky into a single standard named Medžuslovjansky. Adoption of a unified orthography for both Latin and Cyrillic scripts. Harmonization of the morphological system drawing on both precursor projects. The name Medžuslovjansky formally adopted as the standard moniker, replacing Slovianski, Medžuslovjanski, and Novoslověnsky. Flavorization system formalized as the mechanism for regional adaptation, replacing ad-hoc variation. Principal authors: Jan van Steenbergen, Vojtěch Merunka, and the CISLa 2017 unification committee.

Status: Superseded Date: 2011–2017 Scope of changes: The name Medžuslovjansky (Interslavic) adopted in 2011 as the common moniker for converging projects, replacing Slovianski. Slovianto (Basic Interslavic) introduced and documented as a simplified entry-level subset, structured in three progressive levels. Ongoing collaborative development on vocabulary, orthography, and grammar, coordinated via the Interslavic Language Portal and associated forums. Etymological (scientific) alphabet (Naučny Medžuslovjansky) described as an optional extension. Principal author: Jan van Steenbergen, with contributions from the Slovianski community.

Status: Historical Date: 2006–2011 Summary: The Slovianski project was launched in 2006 as the first fully collaborative, internet-era inter-Slavic language effort. Its methodology — basing the language on systematic weighted comparison of twelve living Slavic languages rather than on any single model — established the core design principles that persist in the current specification. In 2009, Slovioski (Radzikowski, Moraczewski, Borovička) was launched as a hybrid project; it merged into Interslavic in 2010. In 2010, Novoslověnsky (Neoslavonic, Merunka, Molhanec) launched as a parallel naturalistic project based on Old Church Slavonic modernization; the two projects collaborated increasingly closely until the 2017 unification.

This specification acknowledges the following historical projects as direct methodological precursors: Project Author Year Significance Ruski (manuscript) Juraj Križanić 1666 First systematic inter-Slavic grammar Universalis Lingua Slavica Ján Herkeľ 1826 West Slavic comparative basis Uzajemni Pravopis Slavjanski Matija Majar-Ziljski 1865 Introduced systematic comparison methodology Mežduslavjanski jezik Ladislav Podmele 1950s 20th-century naturalistic attempt These projects are incorporated into the historical record of the language; they are not normatively superseded by this specification, as they were never normative documents. Full historical context is provided in the relevant section

info A false friend (lažny prijatelj) is a word that looks or sounds similar to a word in another Slavic language but differs substantially in meaning. Because Interslavic draws its vocabulary from the common Slavic stock, a learner who already knows one or more Slavic languages may occasionally misread an Interslavic word through the lens of their own language. This register documents the most consequential such pairs. Entries are sorted alphabetically by Interslavic form. The columns record: the Interslavic word and its meaning in Interslavic; the confusable form in a specific Slavic language; the language in question; and the meaning that form carries in that language. A usage note explains the scope of the conflict and advises caution where it matters most.

The register does not claim that an Interslavic word is wrong or ambiguous in isolation. Interslavic meaning is determined by the specification; the foreign-language meanings listed here are purely those that a native speaker of the language in question might inadvertently project onto an Interslavic text. The practical risk of a misunderstanding depends on context: some pairs produce only a slight semantic shift, while others could reverse the entire sense of a sentence. Abbreviations used in the Language column: BG = Bulgarian; BS/HR/SR = Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian; CS = Czech; MK = Macedonian; PL = Polish; RU = Russian; SK = Slovak; SL = Slovene; UK = Ukrainian.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note aktivnost activity (abstract) aktywność PL activity (same) Near-identical; risk is low, but note that PL stresses this word on the antepenult, which may cause phonological confusion in spoken contexts. armija army армія UK army (same) Minimal divergence; both derive from the same borrowing, but armija competes with the native Slavic synonym vojsko. Writers addressing Ukrainian audiences may prefer vojsko to avoid the perception of a loanword-heavy register.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note blag good, kind, mild благ BG good, gracious (same) Largely consistent across South Slavic. The divergence appears with West Slavic speakers, for whom blago primarily evokes "treasure, wealth" (CS blaho = wellbeing, happiness; PL błogo = blissfully). An ISV sentence using blag in a moral sense may be decoded by a Czech speaker as something vaguely material. brat brother brat BS/HR/SR brother (same) No meaning conflict; included here because PL brat (brother) and CS brat (brother) are identical. The risk is zero. However, note that the derived verb bratati se (to fraternise) can mislead: RU братать has the same meaning, but PL bratać się is more narrowly associated with drunken camaraderie. bůg / bog God bog BS/HR/SR, SL God (same); also "rich" in archaic SL Fully consistent as a noun referring to the deity. In older Slovene, the adjective bog had a sense of "rich", which is obsolete in modern SL but surfaces occasionally in literary texts. No practical risk in standard use.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note cas time, hour (in compounds) czas PL time PL czas = time in general. ISV prefers čas for "moment/time" and časina for a unit of one hour. The graph divergence (ISV č vs PL cz) is purely orthographic; semantically the words align. The entry exists because čas can suggest "hour" to South Slavic readers, while to Polish readers it simply means "time". crěvo intestine, gut crevo BS/HR/SR intestine (same) Consistent in South Slavic. A Czech or Slovak reader may associate the root with a word for "leather" (archaic CS střevo = gut, but kůže = skin). No major conflict. cukr sugar cukr CS sugar (same) Identical in Czech. PL cukier, RU сахар. The ISV form is taken from the common West/Central Slavic stock. No meaning conflict, but speakers of purely East Slavic background may not recognise it immediately.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note dar gift, donation dar PL, CS, BS/HR/SR gift (same) Consistent. However, RU дар can carry a sense of "talent, natural gift" more strongly than a physical present. In ISV, both senses are available. divan sofa, couch divan BS/HR/SR sofa (from Turkish) The word entered South Slavic from Turkish and is also the word for an Ottoman council chamber. ISV uses it as a common word for sofa; the political/historical sense is not active in Interslavic usage. duma thought, idea думa RU thought (same) Critical false friend. PL duma = pride. An Interslavic sentence like jego duma bě velika could be parsed by a Pole as "his pride was great" and by a Russian as "his thought was great." ISV prefers mysl for "thought" and reserves duma as an acknowledged Russian-flavoured word; in neutral ISV texts duma should be avoided. dvojnik double, lookalike dvojník CS, SK double, understudy Consistent in meaning.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note glas voice; also vote glas BS/HR/SR, SL voice, sound (same) No conflict for South Slavic speakers. RU голос (golos) covers both meanings too but has a different surface form. The "vote" sense (glasovati = to vote) can confuse speakers accustomed to hearing glas only as "voice". glava head (body part); chapter glava RU, BS/HR/SR head (same) Consistent. The secondary ISV sense of "chapter" (as in a book) mirrors the South Slavic usage and is transparent to most Slavs. gora mountain; forest (secondary) gora BS/HR/SR, SL mountain (same); also "forest" in older usage No conflict with major languages. PL góra = mountain. The forest sense is archaic. grad city, town grad BS/HR/SR, MK city (same); also fortress/castle RU город (gorod) = city; PL gród = old fortified town, now largely historical. The ISV word is standard and carries no ambiguity in modern use, but historically grad meant "enclosed settlement" or "fortress" across Slavic. grozny threatening, terrible гро́зный RU terrible, formidable Culturally loaded. Russian speakers primarily associate grozny with Ivan Grozny (Ivan the Terrible). In ISV the word is purely adjectival meaning "menacing". No meaning conflict, but the cultural resonance is strong for Russian-speaking readers.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note jabloko apple jablko CS, SK, PL apple (same) Consistent. The ISV form with the inserted -o- follows the East Slavic pattern (RU яблоко). West Slavic readers recognise the root immediately. jak as, like, how jak PL how, as (same); also "like" PL jak = how/as/like. CS jak = how. BS/HR/SR jak = strong. Moderate risk. In ISV, jak functions as a comparative particle. A Croatian reader seeing on je jak may read it as "he is strong" (because HR jak = strong), while the ISV sentence means "he is like [something]" or contains jak as a conjunction. Writers should prefer kak (which unambiguously corresponds to RU как, PL jak, CS jak) or rephrase for clarity when addressing Croatian/Serbian audiences. jedino only, solely jedino BS/HR/SR only (same) Consistent.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note kamen stone, rock kamen BS/HR/SR, CS, SK stone (same) Consistent. karakter character (personal) karakter BS/HR/SR character (same) International borrowing; consistent across languages. konec end, finish konec CS end (same) PL koniec = end. RU конец = end. No conflict. However, the ISV word konjec (archaic, poetic) should not be confused with konj (horse) + diminutive suffix. kopati to dig; to kick kopati BS/HR/SR to dig; to kick The "kick" sense is present in South Slavic but less expected by East Slavic readers. In ISV, both senses are available; context normally disambiguates. kosa scythe; hair kosa BS/HR/SR, SL, CS scythe; hair (both senses present) The dual meaning mirrors South and West Slavic usage. RU коса also covers both (scythe and braid/plait). No conflict; the polysemy is pan-Slavic. kost bone kost Most Slavic languages bone (same) Consistent. krěpost fortress, stronghold крепость RU fortress; also strength, firmness The RU word also means "strength" or "firmness" in an abstract sense (e.g. krěpost dukha = strength of spirit). ISV krěpost retains primarily the concrete architectural meaning. The abstract use is not wrong in ISV but less standard.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note leto year (literary/archaic); summer leto BS/HR/SR, MK, BG summer RU лето = summer. CS, SK, PL léto/lato = summer. High-frequency conflict. ISV leto primarily means "year" (following OCS and East Slavic literary usage) but can mean "summer" in a more colloquial register. In South Slavic, leto is exclusively "summer". Writers should use god or godina for "year" when addressing mixed Slavic audiences, and reserve leto only for unambiguous contexts. list leaf (of a plant or tree); sheet of paper list Most Slavic languages leaf; sheet; letter (in some) The "letter (correspondence)" sense is present in some dialects and archaic registers (CS list = letter, document). ISV list covers leaf and sheet of paper; ISV pismo is preferred for a letter/correspondence. listopad leaf-fall; the month name (varies) listopad PL November Critical false friend. HR listopad = October. PL listopad = November. The same word refers to two different months in two Slavic languages. ISV does not use listopad as a month name precisely for this reason; it prefers the Latin-based month names or unambiguous Slavic periphrases. ljubiti to love lubić PL to like, to be fond of Moderate risk. ISV ljubiti = to love (strongly). PL lubić = to like (casually). A Polish speaker may underread the emotional weight of an ISV declaration such as ljubim te. luk onion; bow (archery) luk BS/HR/SR, SL onion; bow PL łuk = arch, bow. RU лук = onion; also bow. The polysemy is old and pan-Slavic; no false friend issue between ISV and individual languages, but learners should note both senses.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note malo little, few (quantity) malo BS/HR/SR, SL little, a bit (same) Consistent in South Slavic. PL mało = little (same). No conflict. mast ointment, grease, fat mast CS, SK fat, lard (same); grease RU масть = coat colour (of an animal); suit in cards. Moderate risk for Russian speakers. An ISV text about cooking using mast will be understood correctly by Czechs and Slovaks but may puzzle Russian readers who associate масть with card games or horse coloration. mati / mater mother mati SL, BS/HR/SR mother (same) Consistent. měsec month; moon měsíc CS month; moon (same) Consistent. The polysemy is pan-Slavic and derives from the same Proto-Slavic root. mir world; peace мир RU world; peace (same dual meaning) The dual meaning of RU мир (resolved after the 1918 spelling reform: мир = peace, мiр = world in pre-reform orthography) has a direct parallel in ISV. Context disambiguates. No practical risk but worth noting for learners. mraz frost mraz BS/HR/SR, SL frost, freeze (same) Consistent. RU мороз = frost (different form). CS mráz = frost. No conflict.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note narod people, nation narod Most Slavic languages people, nation (same) Consistent. nedelja week; Sunday nedeľa SK Sunday BS/HR/SR nedjelja / nedelja = Sunday. RU неделя = week. High-frequency conflict. ISV nedelja can mean both, following the pan-Slavic ambiguity. In practice, ISV prefers nedelja = week (following the more widespread modern usage) and nedela or an explicit gloss for "Sunday". Writers should verify from context which meaning is intended. nos nose nos Most Slavic languages nose (same) Consistent.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note obraz image, face, icon obraz CS, SK image, picture PL obraz = picture, painting. RU образ = image, icon (religious). BS/HR/SR obraz = cheek. Significant divergence for South Slavic. A Croatian or Serbian reader of an ISV phrase involving obraz will likely parse it as "cheek", not "image". ISV texts addressed to South Slavic audiences should prefer lik or slika for "image/face". oko eye oko Most Slavic languages eye (same) Consistent. ostrov island ostrov CS, RU island (same) BS/HR/SR ostrvo = island. SL otok = island (different root). No conflict for Czech or Russian readers; Slovene readers must simply learn the ISV word.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note palec thumb; big toe palec CS thumb (same) PL palec = finger (any finger). RU палец = finger (any finger). Moderate risk. ISV palec specifically means thumb/big toe. Writers addressing a general Slavic audience who want to say "finger" should use prst. pamjat memory, remembrance pamět CS memory (same) PL pamięć = memory. RU память = memory. Consistent in meaning; orthographic variation only. pas belt, girdle; also dog (colloquial South Slavic) pas BS/HR/SR dog RU пас = passed (cards); PL pas = waist, waistband. Moderate risk for South Slavic. In Croatian/Serbian, pas = dog (the primary everyday word). ISV pas = belt/girdle; ISV for dog is pěs or sobaka. A South Slavic reader encountering ISV pas in a non-clothing context may automatically read "dog". pěsok / pesok sand pesok MK sand (same) RU песок = sand. PL piasek = sand. No major conflict. pismo letter (correspondence); writing pismo BS/HR/SR, SL letter; writing (same) Consistent. pit to drink pit BS/HR/SR to drink (same) CS pít = to drink. PL pić = to drink. Consistent. plod fruit (of a plant); offspring plod CS, SK, BS/HR/SR fruit; offspring (same) PL płód = fetus, embryo. Moderate risk. In Polish, płód primarily means fetus, not generic fruit. ISV plod = fruit (edible or figurative). Writers should use frukta or specify context. počet beginning, start počet CS, SK number, count Critical false friend. CS/SK počet = number, count. ISV počet = beginning. RU начало = beginning; PL początek = beginning. Czech and Slovak readers will parse ISV na početu ("at the beginning") as "at the count" or "at the number". ISV should use načalo or začatok for unambiguous usage with Czech/Slovak audiences. posol ambassador, envoy посол RU ambassador (same) Consistent. CS posel = messenger (slightly weaker sense). pravda truth prawda PL truth (same) Consistent. The cultural connotation of the Soviet newspaper Pravda (RU) does not affect ISV meaning. proba test, trial, attempt próba PL test, rehearsal CS próba = rehearsal. RU проба = test, sample, hallmark. Consistent across most Slavic languages; the "rehearsal" sense in Polish and Czech is an extension of the general meaning.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note rad glad, happy; work (in compounds) rad BS/HR/SR, SL glad (same) RU рад = glad (same). PL rad = glad (archaic). The work-related sense (radio, rabota root) is a separate etymon; no conflict. reč speech, word, sentence reč BS/HR/SR word, speech (same) RU речь = speech, discourse. CS řeč = speech. Consistent in the broad sense, though the precise scope (word vs. sentence vs. speech) varies. reka river rijeka BS/HR/SR river (same) PL rzeka = river. RU река = river. Consistent. rod clan, kin, birth; gender (grammatical) rod Most Slavic languages clan, family, gender (same) Consistent. The grammatical use (masculine/feminine/neuter rod) mirrors all Slavic grammatical traditions.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note sad garden; orchard sad BS/HR/SR, SL orchard, garden (same) RU сад = garden/orchard (same). PL sad = orchard. CS sad = orchard. Consistent. sestra sister sestra Most Slavic languages sister (same) Consistent. sila force, strength, power sila Most Slavic languages force, strength (same) Consistent. sin son sin BS/HR/SR, SL, BG, MK son (same) RU сын = son (same root, different vowel). PL syn = son. Consistent in meaning; orthographic variation only. skora soon; quickly skoro BS/HR/SR soon; nearly CS skoro = almost. Slight risk. In Czech, skoro primarily means "almost/nearly" rather than "soon". ISV skoro shares both senses; a Czech reader may default to the "almost" reading. slovo word slovo Most Slavic languages word (same) Consistent. The proper name Slovo (as in the Old Slavonic text Slovo o polku Igoreve) is a culturally weighted homonym but presents no meaning conflict. smert death смерть RU death (same) Consistent. sobaka dog собака RU dog (same) A Turkic borrowing into Russian that has become standard; less familiar to West and South Slavic speakers, who use pes/pies type words. ISV offers both pěs and sobaka as synonyms. strana side; country, land страна RU country (same) BS/HR/SR strana = side, page; also country in a formal register. CS strana = page; political party. Moderate risk. A Czech reader may parse ISV strana as "page" or "political party" rather than "country". ISV prefers zemlja or država for "country" in texts where Czech readership is primary. stvar thing, matter stvar BS/HR/SR thing, matter (same) RU вещь = thing (different word). PL rzecz = thing. ISV stvar is familiar to South Slavic speakers; others may need a moment to recognise it. svetlo light (illumination) světlo CS light (same) RU свет = light (same root). SL svetloba = light. Consistent in meaning; form variation only. svjat holy, sacred svjat (archaic/liturgical) holy RU святой = holy. PL święty = holy. The ISV short-form adjective is consistent in meaning.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note telo body тело RU, BG body (same) Consistent. trava grass, herb trava Most Slavic languages grass (same); also "herb" (BS/HR/SR) Consistent; the "herb" sense is a natural extension. trud labour, effort; difficulty труд RU labour, work (same) BS/HR/SR trud = effort, difficulty. PL trud = effort, toil. The ISV word covers both "labour" and "effort/difficulty"; the scope is broader than in any single language but consistent with all of them.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note um mind, intellect um BS/HR/SR, SL mind, reason (same) RU ум = mind (same). CS um = mind (same). Consistent. urok lesson; also fate, doom (archaic) урок RU lesson (same) BS/HR/SR urok = spell, hex, evil eye. Moderate risk. South Slavic readers may associate ISV urok with a magic curse rather than a school lesson. ISV prefers lekcija in educational contexts.

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note večer evening večer CS, BS/HR/SR, SL evening (same) RU вечер = evening (same). PL wieczór = evening. Consistent. vera faith, belief вера RU faith (same) Consistent. Also a common female proper name in Slavic; no meaning conflict in common-noun use. voda water voda Most Slavic languages water (same) Consistent. vojna war vojna BS/HR/SR, SL war (same) RU война = war (same). PL wojna = war. Consistent. volja will, freedom, volition volja BS/HR/SR, SL will (same) RU воля = will; also freedom, open space. CS vůle = will. Consistent in meaning; the "open space/liberty" shade in Russian is a nuance, not a conflict. vrag enemy, foe vrag BS/HR/SR, SL enemy; also devil RU враг = enemy (same). CS vrah = murderer, killer. Moderate risk for Czech speakers. ISV vrag means enemy in general; Czech readers may interpret it as specifically "murderer".

ISV word ISV meaning Confusable form Language Meaning in that language Usage note zelen green zelen BS/HR/SR, SL green (same) RU зелёный = green. PL zielony = green. Consistent. zemlja earth, ground; land, country zemlja BS/HR/SR, SL, MK earth; land; country (same) RU земля = earth, land. PL ziemia = earth, land. Consistent across all Slavic languages; the polysemy (soil vs. country) is pan-Slavic. zima winter; cold zima Most Slavic languages winter (same) Consistent. zlo evil zlo BS/HR/SR, SL, CS evil (same) RU зло = evil (same). PL zło = evil. Consistent. zlato gold zlato BS/HR/SR, SL, CS, SK gold (same) RU золото = gold. PL złoto = gold. Consistent; orthographic variation only. znati to know znati BS/HR/SR, SL to know (same) RU знать = to know. PL znać = to know. Consistent.

The entries in this register are drawn from three main sources: (1) documented false-friend pairs discussed in Jan van Steenbergen's Interslavic design criteria documentation (particularly the False friends subsection); (2) the English Wikibooks list False Friends of the Slavist; and (3) the general comparative Slavic linguistics literature. Priority was given to pairs where the meaning divergence is sufficient to cause genuine miscommunication in context, rather than minor semantic shading. Not all potential false friends are listed. Pairs where the meanings are closely related and only differ in degree or register are generally omitted. Pairs where the Interslavic word is so rare in practice that the risk of confusion is negligible are also excluded. This register should be updated as the Interslavic lexical corpus grows and as community experience identifies new problem pairs.

info The glossary provides definitions for technical terms used throughout the specification. Users unfamiliar with specific linguistic terminology are encouraged to refer to this section for clarification. Ablaut: A system of vowel changes that indicates grammatical contrasts, such as tense or number. Abessive Case: A grammatical case indicating absence or lack of something. Absolute Construction: A phrase with a noun and a participle that functions independently within a sentence. Abstract Noun: A noun that represents an idea, quality, or state, rather than a concrete object. Accentuation: The application of stress or emphasis on certain syllables in words. Accusative Case: A grammatical case used to mark the direct object of a verb. Active Voice: A sentence structure where the subject performs the action of the verb. Adjective Agreement: The grammatical rule requiring adjectives to match the gender, number, and case of the nouns they modify. Adjective Comparison: The formation of comparative and superlative forms of adjectives. Adjectival Declension: The inflection of adjectives based on gender, number, and case. Adjunct: An optional element in a sentence that adds information but is not essential to its structure. Adposition: A preposition or postposition used to express relationships between elements in a sentence. Adverbial Modifier: A word or phrase that modifies a verb, adjective, or other adverb, often providing information about time, manner, place, or degree. Affixation: The process of adding prefixes, suffixes, infixes, or circumfixes to a root to form a new word. Affricate: A consonant sound that begins as a stop and transitions into a fricative. Agglutinative Language: A type of language in which words are formed by stringing together morphemes, each with a distinct meaning or grammatical function. Agreement Rules: Grammatical rules requiring parts of a sentence to agree in features like gender, number, or case. Aorist Tense: A past tense used to describe simple or completed actions. Apocope: The loss of a sound or syllable at the end of a word. Apposition: A grammatical construction in which two elements, usually noun phrases, are placed side by side, with one identifying or explaining the other. Archaism: A word or expression that is outdated but used for stylistic or historical effect. Article: A word used to define a noun as specific (definite) or unspecific (indefinite). Aspectual Pair: A pair of verbs that express the same action but differ in aspect (e.g., imperfective vs. perfective). Assimilation: The process by which a sound becomes more like a neighboring sound in articulation. Attributive Adjective: An adjective that directly modifies a noun (e.g., "red apple"). Augmentative: A form of a word that indicates a larger or more intense version of something. Auxiliary Verb: A verb used to form tenses, moods, or voices of other verbs (e.g., "to be," "to have"). Back Vowel: A vowel sound produced with the tongue positioned towards the back of the mouth. Balto-Slavic Languages: A branch of the Indo-European language family including Baltic and Slavic languages. Bilabial Consonant: A consonant sound made with both lips (e.g., "p," "b"). Borrowing: The adoption of words or phrases from another language. Cardinal Numeral: A numeral that expresses quantity (e.g., "one," "two"). Case Endings: The inflectional suffixes that indicate grammatical case. Causative Verb: A verb that expresses the action of causing something to happen. Cerebral Consonant: A retroflex consonant produced with the tongue curled back towards the palate. Circumflex Accent: A diacritical mark used to indicate a specific type of stress or tone. Clitic: A word with syntactic independence but phonological dependence on another word. Codification: The process of standardizing and developing rules for a language. Collective Numeral: A numeral used to denote a group or set (e.g., "a pair," "a dozen"). Common Slavic: The hypothetical ancestor language of all Slavic languages. Comparative Degree: The form of an adjective or adverb used to compare two entities. Complementizer: A word used to introduce a subordinate clause (e.g., "that," "if"). Compound Word: A word formed by combining two or more words (e.g., "sunflower"). Conditional Mood: A verb form expressing hypothetical or contingent actions. Conjugation Class: A category of verbs with the same pattern of inflection. Conjunction: A word used to connect clauses, sentences, or words (e.g., "and," "but"). Consonant Cluster: A sequence of two or more consonant sounds within a syllable. Consonant Gradation: A systematic alternation of consonants in different morphological contexts. Consonant Mutation: A change in consonant sound due to grammatical or phonological factors. Continuant: A consonant sound produced with continuous airflow (e.g., "s," "m"). Copula: A verb that links the subject to a complement (e.g., "is" in "He is a teacher"). Coronal Consonant: A consonant articulated with the front part of the tongue. Corpus Planning: Efforts to develop, expand, and standardize the vocabulary and structure of a language. Cursive Script: A style of writing where letters are joined together for faster handwriting. Dative Case: A grammatical case used to indicate the indirect object of a verb. Declarative Sentence: A sentence that makes a statement or provides information. Definite Article: A word that specifies a noun as something particular (e.g., "the" in English). Definiteness: A grammatical category indicating whether a noun is specific or known. Demonstrative Pronoun: A pronoun used to point to specific things (e.g., "this," "that"). Denominal Verb: A verb derived from a noun (e.g., "to fish" from "fish"). Dental Consonant: A consonant articulated with the tongue against the upper teeth (e.g., "t," "d"). Dependent Clause: A clause that cannot stand alone and depends on a main clause. Derivation: The process of creating new words by adding affixes or altering roots. Descriptive Grammar: The study of language as it is actually used, without prescribing rules. Determiner: A word that modifies a noun to indicate reference (e.g., "a," "the," "some"). Diachronic Linguistics: The study of the historical development and evolution of languages. Dialectology: The study of regional language variations and dialects. Diglossia: A sociolinguistic situation where two varieties of a language are used in different contexts. Diminutive: A word form that conveys smallness or endearment (e.g., "duckling" from "duck"). Diphthongization: The process of a single vowel sound becoming a diphthong. Discourse Analysis: The study of how language is used in texts and contexts. Disjunctive Pronoun: A pronoun used independently or for emphasis (e.g., "me" in "It’s me"). Dissimilation: A phonological process where two similar sounds become less similar. Dual Number: A grammatical number category used for exactly two entities. Dynamic Verb: A verb that indicates an action or process rather than a state. Elision: The omission of a sound or syllable in speech or writing (e.g., "I’m" for "I am"). Ellipsis: The omission of elements that are understood from context in a sentence. Emphatic Particle: A word used to add emphasis (e.g., "just" in "I just wanted to say"). Endocentric Compound: A compound word where one part defines the overall meaning (e.g., "toothbrush"). Epenthesis: The insertion of an extra sound into a word (e.g., "athlete" pronounced as "ath-a-lete"). Ergative Case: A case marking the subject of a transitive verb in ergative-absolutive languages. Etymology: The study of the origin and history of words. Euphony: The quality of being pleasing to the ear, often achieved through harmonious sounds. Exocentric Compound: A compound word whose meaning cannot be deduced from its parts (e.g., "pickpocket"). Experimental Phonetics: The study of speech sounds using experimental methods. Extensive Reading: A language learning approach focusing on reading large amounts of text for general understanding. Facultative Form: A grammatical form that is optional or context-dependent. Feminine Gender: A grammatical category used to classify nouns, pronouns, and adjectives associated with female entities. Finite Verb: A verb that is marked for tense, person, or number and can serve as the main verb of a clause. First Conjugation: A category of verbs that share the same inflectional patterns, often based on their endings. First Declension: A category of nouns that share common inflectional patterns, typically feminine in gender. Flapped Consonant: A consonant sound produced with a single rapid movement of the tongue against the alveolar ridge (e.g., "r" in some dialects). Focus Particle: A word that emphasizes or highlights a part of a sentence (e.g., "only," "even"). Foreigner Talk: Simplified speech patterns used by native speakers to communicate with language learners. Formant: A concentration of acoustic energy in speech sounds, important in vowel identification. Fortis Consonant: A consonant produced with greater force or intensity compared to lenis consonants. Fricative: A consonant sound made by forcing air through a narrow constriction (e.g., "f," "s"). Front Vowel: A vowel produced with the tongue positioned towards the front of the mouth. Future Perfect: A verb tense indicating an action that will have been completed by a certain point in the future. Future Tense: A verb tense used to describe actions that will occur in the future. Gender Agreement: The grammatical requirement that adjectives, pronouns, and verbs match the gender of the noun they refer to. Genitive Case: A grammatical case used to indicate possession, origin, or relationship. Gerundive: A verbal adjective expressing necessity or obligation (e.g., "to be read"). Glide: A sound produced with little obstruction of the airstream, often transitioning to or from a vowel (e.g., "w" in "we"). Glottal Stop: A consonant sound produced by briefly obstructing airflow at the glottis (e.g., the sound between the syllables of "uh-oh"). Grapheme: The smallest unit of a writing system, such as a letter or character. Grimm's Law: A historical linguistics rule describing sound changes in the Germanic branch of Indo-European languages. Habitual Aspect: A verb aspect indicating actions that occur regularly or habitually. Hard Sign: A Cyrillic letter (ъ) used to indicate a slight separation between syllables. Head-final Language: A language in which the head of a phrase (e.g., the verb in a clause) typically comes last. Head-initial Language: A language in which the head of a phrase typically comes first. High Vowel: A vowel sound produced with the tongue positioned high in the mouth (e.g., "i" in "machine"). Homograph: Words that are spelled the same but may differ in pronunciation or meaning (e.g., "lead" [to guide] vs. "lead" [a metal]). Homonym: Words that sound alike or are spelled alike but have different meanings (e.g., "bat" [animal] vs. "bat" [sports equipment]). Homophone: Words that sound the same but have different meanings or spellings (e.g., "flower" and "flour"). Hypercorrection: A nonstandard usage resulting from the incorrect application of a perceived rule. Hypocorism: A pet name or diminutive form of a name (e.g., "Maggie" for "Margaret"). Imperative Mood: A verb form used to express commands, requests, or instructions. Impersonal Verb: A verb that does not have a specific subject, often referring to weather or general conditions (e.g., "It rains"). Indefinite Pronoun: A pronoun that refers to nonspecific persons or things (e.g., "someone," "anything"). Independent Clause: A clause that can stand alone as a complete sentence. Indicative Mood: A verb form used for statements of fact or reality. Indirect Object: The recipient of the action of a verb (e.g., "She gave him a book"). Infinitive: The base form of a verb, often preceded by "to" in English (e.g., "to run"). Inflectional Paradigm: The complete set of inflected forms of a word. Instrumental Case: A grammatical case used to indicate the means by which an action is performed. Intensive Pronoun: A pronoun used to emphasize its antecedent (e.g., "I myself did it"). Interfix: A linking element used between parts of a compound word. Interrogative Particle: A word or particle used to form questions (e.g., "li" in Slavic languages). Intervocalic: A sound occurring between two vowels. Intransitive Verb: A verb that does not require a direct object (e.g., "She sleeps"). Irregular Verb: A verb that does not follow standard inflectional patterns. Isogloss: A geographical boundary marking the distribution of linguistic features. Iterative Aspect: A verb aspect indicating repeated or habitual actions. Labialization: The rounding of the lips during the articulation of a sound. Labiodental Consonant: A consonant produced with the lower lip against the upper teeth (e.g., "f," "v"). Language Acquisition: The process of learning a language, either as a first or additional language. Language Family: A group of related languages descended from a common ancestral language. Language Planning: Efforts to develop, promote, or modify the structure and use of a language. Language Policy: Rules or guidelines governing the use and status of languages within a community or country. Lateral Consonant: A consonant produced by airflow around the sides of the tongue (e.g., "l"). Lexeme: The base form or dictionary entry of a word, including all its inflected forms. Lexical Borrowing: The incorporation of words from one language into another. Lexical Gap: A concept or idea for which a language lacks a word. Lexicography: The practice of compiling dictionaries. Linguistic Typology: The study of similarities and differences among languages to classify them into types. Loan Translation: A type of borrowing where a phrase or word is translated literally (e.g., "skyscraper" as "wolkenkratzer" in German). Locative Case: A grammatical case indicating location. Low Vowel: A vowel sound produced with the tongue positioned low in the mouth (e.g., "a" in "father"). L-vocalization: The replacement of the sound "l" with a vowel or semivowel (common in some dialects). Metathesis: A linguistic phenomenon where sounds or letters in a word switch places (e.g., "aks" for "ask"). Middle Voice: A grammatical voice indicating the subject performs an action that affects itself or is inherently reflexive. Minimal Pair: Two words that differ by only one sound and have different meanings (e.g., "bat" and "pat"). Modal Verb: A type of auxiliary verb that expresses possibility, necessity, or permission (e.g., "can," "must"). Modality: The linguistic expression of attitudes or possibilities, such as necessity, ability, or likelihood. Monophthong: A single, pure vowel sound (e.g., "a" in "father"). Morphological Case: A grammatical category that indicates the function of a noun or pronoun in a sentence. Morphophonemics: The study of how morphemes are affected by phonological rules in speech. Nasal Assimilation: A process where a nasal sound changes to match the place of articulation of a neighboring sound. Nasal Consonant: A consonant produced by allowing airflow through the nose (e.g., "m," "n"). Nasalization: The process of making a vowel or consonant nasal by allowing air to pass through the nose. Natural Gender: The grammatical gender corresponding to the biological sex of the noun's referent. Negation: The grammatical process of denying or contradicting a statement (e.g., "not" in "I do not know"). Neologism: A newly coined word or expression. Neuter Gender: A grammatical category often used for inanimate objects or abstract concepts. Nominalization: The process of converting a verb or adjective into a noun (e.g., "to decide" → "decision"). Nominative Case: The grammatical case used for the subject of a sentence. Non-finite Verb: A verb form that does not indicate tense, person, or number (e.g., infinitives, participles). Number Agreement: A grammatical rule requiring agreement in number between parts of a sentence, such as subjects and verbs. Numeral Classifier: A word or morpheme used in some languages to categorize nouns when counting. Oblique Case: A general term for any grammatical case other than the nominative or vocative. Old Church Slavonic: The oldest documented Slavic language, used in religious texts and liturgy. Onomatopoeia: A word that imitates the sound associated with its meaning (e.g., "buzz," "clang"). Open Syllable: A syllable that ends in a vowel rather than a consonant. Optative Mood: A grammatical mood used to express wishes, hopes, or desires. Orthographic Reform: Changes made to standardize or simplify a writing system. Orthographic Standardization: The process of establishing consistent spelling and writing conventions. Palatal Approximant: A consonant sound where the tongue approaches but does not touch the hard palate (e.g., "j" in "yes"). Palatalization: A phonetic process where a consonant becomes closer to a palatal sound, often influenced by neighboring vowels. Paradigm: A set of related forms of a word showing its inflectional variations (e.g., verb conjugations). Paragogic Vowel: An extra vowel added at the end of a word, often for phonological reasons. Participle: A non-finite verb form used as an adjective or for tense formation (e.g., "running" in "running water"). Particle Verb: A verb combined with a particle to create a specific meaning (e.g., "give up"). Passive Voice: A sentence structure where the subject receives the action of the verb (e.g., "The book was read by her"). Past Perfect: A verb tense expressing an action completed before another past action (e.g., "She had left"). Past Tense: A verb tense used to describe actions that happened in the past. Perfect Aspect: A verb aspect indicating completed actions with relevance to the present. Periphrastic Construction: A grammatical construction using multiple words to express what could be conveyed by a single word or inflection (e.g., "will be running"). Pharyngealization: A secondary articulation involving retraction of the root of the tongue toward the pharynx. Phoneme: The smallest unit of sound in a language that can distinguish meaning. Phonemic Orthography: A writing system where each grapheme corresponds to a single phoneme. Phonetics: The study of speech sounds and their physical properties. Phonological Process: A pattern of sound change or alteration in speech. Phonotactics: The rules governing the permissible arrangements of sounds in a language. Pitch Accent: A system where variations in pitch differentiate words or syllables. Plosive: A consonant sound produced by stopping airflow completely and then releasing it (e.g., "p," "t"). Politeness Form: A grammatical or lexical form used to express politeness or formality. Polysynthetic Language: A language type where words often consist of multiple morphemes representing complex ideas. Portmanteau Morpheme: A single morpheme that expresses multiple grammatical meanings (e.g., French "au" for "à + le"). Possessive Adjective: An adjective indicating ownership (e.g., "my," "his"). Possessive Pronoun: A pronoun that shows ownership (e.g., "mine," "yours"). Postposition: A type of adposition that follows the noun or pronoun it governs (e.g., "ago" in "two years ago"). Prepositional Phrase: A phrase consisting of a preposition and its object (e.g., "in the house"). Present Continuous: A verb tense used to describe actions currently happening (e.g., "I am reading"). Present Perfect: A verb tense describing actions completed at an unspecified time in the past with relevance to the present (e.g., "I have finished"). Present Tense: A verb tense describing actions happening now or general truths. Preterite: A verb tense used to describe actions completed in the past, common in certain languages. Productive Suffix: A suffix actively used to form new words in a language. Progressive Aspect: A verb aspect indicating ongoing or continuous actions (e.g., "She is working"). Proto-Indo-European: The reconstructed common ancestor of the Indo-European languages. Proto-Slavic: The reconstructed common ancestor of the Slavic languages. Pulmonic Egressive: A speech sound produced by pushing air out of the lungs, the most common airflow mechanism in languages. Quantifier: A word or phrase expressing quantity (e.g., "all," "some," "many"). Recessive Accent: An accent pattern where stress tends to move toward the beginning of a word. Reciprocal Pronoun: A pronoun expressing mutual actions or relationships (e.g., "each other"). Reduplication: A morphological process where a word or part of a word is repeated to express a grammatical or semantic change. Reflexive Pronoun: A pronoun referring back to the subject of the sentence (e.g., "myself"). Reflexive Verb: A verb where the subject and object are the same, often marked by a reflexive pronoun (e.g., "She washed herself"). Register: A variety of language used in specific social contexts, ranging from formal to informal. Relative Clause: A subordinate clause that modifies a noun, often introduced by a relative pronoun (e.g., "who," "which"). Relative Pronoun: A pronoun used to introduce a relative clause (e.g., "who," "which," "that"). Retroflex Consonant: A consonant produced by curling the tongue back toward the palate (e.g., certain "r" sounds). Rhotic Consonant: A consonant associated with "r"-like sounds. Root Morpheme: The base or core part of a word that carries its primary meaning. Rounded Vowel: A vowel produced with rounded lips (e.g., "o" in "go"). Sandhi: A set of phonological changes occurring at word or morpheme boundaries (e.g., assimilation). Schwa: The mid-central vowel sound represented by [ə], often found in unstressed syllables (e.g., the "a" in "sofa"). Second Conjugation: A category of verbs sharing the same inflectional patterns, often identified by their endings. Second Declension: A category of nouns sharing similar inflectional endings, typically masculine or neuter. Semantic Drift: The gradual change in the meaning of a word over time. Semivowel: A sound that is phonetically similar to a vowel but functions as a consonant (e.g., "w," "y"). Short Form Adjective: A form of an adjective used in certain contexts, often contrasting with a longer or more inflected form. Soft Sign: A Cyrillic letter (ь) that indicates palatalization of the preceding consonant. Sonorant: A speech sound produced with continuous airflow and minimal constriction, such as vowels, nasals, and liquids. Source Language: The language from which a word, phrase, or grammatical structure is borrowed or translated. Standard Language: A standardized form of a language used in formal settings and official communication. Stem-changing Verb: A verb that undergoes changes in its root or stem when conjugated (e.g., "write" → "wrote"). Stress Accent: A feature of prosody where certain syllables are emphasized through greater loudness or pitch. Subjunctive Mood: A verb mood used to express doubts, wishes, or hypothetical situations. Sublation: A linguistic process where a feature or form is replaced by another over time. Subordinate Clause: A clause that cannot stand alone and depends on a main clause to form a complete sentence. Substantive: A term for a noun or a word functioning as a noun. Suffixation: The addition of a suffix to a root or stem to form a new word or change its grammatical function. Superlative Degree: The form of an adjective or adverb used to express the highest degree of a quality (e.g., "best," "most quickly"). Syllabary: A writing system where each symbol represents a syllable. Syllable Nucleus: The central part of a syllable, typically a vowel or syllabic consonant. Syncope: The omission of sounds or syllables within a word (e.g., "family" pronounced as "famly"). Syncretism: The merging of distinct grammatical forms into a single form (e.g., the same ending for different cases). Syntactic Ambiguity: A situation where a sentence can be interpreted in more than one way due to its structure. Syntactic Parsing: The process of analyzing a sentence to identify its grammatical components and structure. Synthetic Language: A type of language where grammatical relationships are expressed through inflection or word modifications, rather than word order. Telic Verb: A verb that implies a clear endpoint or goal for the action (e.g., "build a house"). Temporal Adverb: An adverb that indicates time (e.g., "yesterday," "soon"). Third Conjugation: A category of verbs sharing common inflectional patterns, typically based on their endings. Third Declension: A group of nouns with similar inflectional endings, often feminine or irregular in nature. Transcription: The written representation of spoken language, often using phonetic symbols. Transitive Verb: A verb that requires a direct object to complete its meaning (e.g., "She reads a book"). Triphthong: A sequence of three vowel sounds pronounced within a single syllable. Umlaut: A phonological process where a vowel is altered to become more similar to a vowel in a nearby syllable. Unaccusative Verb: An intransitive verb where the subject experiences or undergoes the action (e.g., "The vase broke"). Unaspirated Consonant: A consonant produced without a burst of air (e.g., the "p" in "spin"). Uncountable Noun: A noun that cannot be pluralized and is measured in terms of quantity rather than number (e.g., "water," "information"). Uvular Consonant: A consonant produced with the back of the tongue against the uvula (e.g., the French "r"). Velar Consonant: A consonant produced with the back of the tongue against the soft palate (e.g., "k," "g"). Velarization: A secondary articulation where the back of the tongue approaches the velum during the production of a consonant. Verbal Aspect: A grammatical category expressing how an action extends over time (e.g., imperfective vs. perfective). Verbal Noun: A noun derived from a verb that retains some verbal characteristics (e.g., "running" in "Running is fun"). Verbal Prefix: A prefix added to a verb to modify its meaning, often related to aspect or direction. Verb-final Sentence: A sentence structure where the verb comes at the end (common in languages like Japanese). Verb-initial Sentence: A sentence structure where the verb appears first (e.g., in Celtic languages). Verb-second Rule: A syntactic rule where the verb always appears as the second element in a sentence (e.g., German main clauses). Vocative Case: A grammatical case used to address or call someone directly (e.g., "John!" in English). Voiced Consonant: A consonant sound produced with vibration of the vocal cords (e.g., "b," "d"). Voiceless Consonant: A consonant sound produced without vocal cord vibration (e.g., "p," "t"). Vowel Harmony: A phonological process where vowels within a word harmonize to share certain features (e.g., frontness or backness). Vowel Length: The duration of a vowel sound, which can distinguish meaning in some languages. Word Order: The arrangement of words in a sentence, which can vary by language and affect meaning. Zero Copula: The omission of the verb "to be" in sentences where it would normally occur (e.g., "She happy" instead of "She is happy"). Zero Derivation: The creation of a new word without any change to the base form (e.g., using "run" as a noun or a verb). Zero Ending: The absence of an explicit ending on a word, typically in inflected forms (e.g., nominative singular in some nouns). Zero Morpheme: An implied grammatical morpheme that has no phonological realization. Zero-marking Language: A language that uses word order or context rather than explicit markers to indicate grammatical relationships. Zirconization: A playful or hypothetical linguistic term to denote the refinement or modernization of a language element.
All paradigms shown in both scripts. Hard stems listed first; soft-stem variants in parentheses where they differ.
