Prefixes attach primarily to verbs but may also modify nouns and adjectives. The base form of any prefixed verb is perfective; secondary imperfectives are derived from it.

Prefix Complementary Primary spatial/directional sense Aspectual/modifying function Representative examples do- od- to, up to (the goal/end) completion up to a limit; doing additionally dojdti "arrive", doletěti "arrive by flight", dozvati "call sb. to come"; dopisati "add in writing", dopolniti "fill by adding" iz- v- / vy- out, off, away from inside ex-, out of izgnati "exile", izbrati "pick out, select", izdati "give out" na- — up, onto, over perfective marker; doing a lot; (+ se) doing enough navrgnuti "throw onto", naložiti "lay on", najdti "find"; napisati "write down"; nagovoriti "speak a lot", najesti se "eat one's fill" nad- pod- over, above (motion) surpassing, outdoing nadsiliti "overbear", nadigrati "defeat, outplay", nadpisati "write over", nadkryti "cover over" nedo- do- incompleteness doing partially / not to the end nedopisati "write without finishing", nedopolniti "fill only partially" o-/ob- obez- around, all sides acquiring a characteristic (from adj./n.); encircling okrugliti "make round", okameniti "turn to stone", okružiti "encircle" ob- — around going/wrapping around obidti "go around, avoid", obmotati "coil around" obez- ob- deprivation, removal dis-, de- (remove what noun names) obezsiliti "disempower", obezhrabriti "discourage" od- do- / pri- away, off, back completing/finishing (to be rid of); undoing odidti "go away", oddati "give away", odvinuti "unscrew", odgovoriti "answer"; odrabotati "work off" po- — beginning of motion starting an action; brief/partial action; action affecting many; completion početi "start", pojdti "set off"; pospati "sleep a little", posmatrjati "watch here and there"; poskryvati se "hide (many)"; pojesti "eat everything", pokryti "cover entirely" pod- nad- under, below (motion) acting from below; acting secretly/surreptitiously podmetnuti "put under", podbiti "knock from below"; podkazati "tell secretly", podgledati "watch secretly" prě- — across, over (motion) transformation; through from one side to other; excess prějdti "cross over", prěložiti "translate", prěvesti "transfer"; prěbudovati "rebuild"; prěsěkti "cut through", prěpisati "copy"; prěsoliti "over-salt", prěvariti "over-boil" prěd- za- before, in front doing in advance, beforehand prědviděti "foresee", prědstaviti "introduce, imagine", prědplatiti "pay in advance" pri- od- close, near; onto approaching; attaching; adding; doing slightly prijdti "approach", privinuti "screw on"; pripisati "write in addition", prigovoriti "object"; priodkryti "open a bit", prikryti "close partially" pro- — through, past thoroughness throughout; missing/going past; losing all probiti "break through", protěkti "flow through", progrějati "heat through"; projehati mimo "drive past"; protratiti "lose all (money)" raz- s(o)- apart, in different directions dispersal, separation; de-, un-; being in middle of action razidti "go apart", razkydnuti "break apart"; razminovati "de-mine"; razcvětati "be in flower", razgovarjati "converse" s-/so- raz- together; downward uniting; bringing down; completing entirely svezati "bind together", sozvati "call together", sobrati "gather"; sojdti "come down", snesti "bring down"; srězati "cut down", sdělati "get done", sbudovati "build completely" su- — beside, parallel working side by side; moving face to face surabotati "work together", suhoditi "come together, meet" u- — away flying/running away; total (often negative) completion; making more (from adj.) uletěti "fly away", uběgti "run away"; ubiti "kill", uginuti "die off", usohnuti "dry up"; ulěpšiti "improve", umenšiti "reduce" v-/vo- vy- / iz- in, into (motion) entering; (+ se) doing carefully/attentively vojdti "enter", vnesti "bring in"; včitati se "start reading closely", vslušati se "start listening attentively" voz- s(o)- up, upward; back going up/aloft; sudden intensive start; doing again vozletěti "fly up", vozidti "go uphill"; vozvyti "start crying suddenly", vozbuditi "stir up"; vozvratiti se "come back", vozživiti "reanimate" vy- v- out, outward (N/W Slavic; S Slavic uses iz- instead) going out; completing entirely; doing well/thoroughly vyjdti "go out", vynesti "carry out"; vyvariti "boil completely", vysušiti "dry out completely"; vyležati se "lie around well" za- prěd- behind, under; closing putting behind; closing/sealing; beginning an action; beginning intensive (+ se) zajdti "go behind", zametnuti "put behind, forget"; zatvoriti "shut", zavinuti "screw tightly"; zapěvati "start singing", zaplakati "start crying"; zagledati se "look intently"

| Perfective ending | Imperfective suffix | Example | |------------------|--------------------|---------|| | -ati | -yvati (hard) / -ivati (soft) | sodržati → sodrživati | | -iti | -jati (vowel of stem lengthened) | obnoviti → obnavjati | | -nuti | -ati | oddohnuti → oddyhati | | monosyllabic + vowel | -vati | prodati → prodavati |
documents.s: Secondary Imperfective Formation from Prefixed Verbs

documents.t: A single verb may carry two prefixes (e.g., prě- + u-, nad- + do-), though this is uncommon. obez- is a fused prefix (ob- + bez-) and always derives verbs meaning deprivation. nedo- is a fused prefix (ne- + do-) expressing incomplete or insufficient action. Prefix ne- on adjectives/nouns is strictly negative (not a verbal prefix): nedobry "not good". Prefix naj- on adjectives forms superlatives: najdobry "best" (not a verbal prefix). South Slavic varieties use iz- where North/West Slavic uses vy-: both are valid in Interslavic.
documents.s: Notes on Prefix Stacking and Interaction

documents.t: Prepositions grouped by governed case. Where a preposition governs multiple cases the alternation rule is noted.

Form Meaning bez without do to, until, up to, as far as iz out of, from (inside) od from, away from; of (possession) okolo around, approximately posle / poslě after prěd in front of (with motion → acc.) prěz / prěs through, across, over; during raz (used in expressions of difference) sred / srěd among, amidst u at, by, near; (possession) vyše above, more than za behind, beyond (static → gen.) že (archaic/literary: same as u/od)
Form Meaning k / ko to, toward, for naprotiv opposite, against po along, through, about, after (motion); according to prěki across (from), on the other side voprekj in spite of, despite

Form Meaning na onto, on (motion); for (time span) o / ob about, concerning; against pro for, about, through (colloquial) s / so with (size/measure); to (along with → ins.) skvoz through, straight through za behind, beyond (motion)
Form Meaning meže / medžu between, among nad above, over (static) pod under, below (static) prěd in front of (static) s / so with, together with za behind (static)
Form Meaning na on, at (static) o / ob about, at (time) po after, following; throughout (static) pri at, by, near; during; in the time of v / vo in, inside (static)

Several prepositions govern different cases depending on motion (direction) vs. static location: Preposition Static (location) Motion (direction) na locative: na stolu "on the table" accusative: na stol "onto the table" v/vo locative: v domu "in the house" accusative: v dom "into the house" pod instrumental: pod stolom "under the table" accusative: pod stol "under the table (motion)" nad instrumental: nad stolom "above the table" accusative: nad stol "up above the table (motion)" prěd instrumental: prěd domom "in front of the house" genitive/accusative: prěd dom "(motion) in front of" za instrumental: za domom "behind the house" accusative: za dom "behind the house (motion)" meže/medžu instrumental: meže dvoma domami "between two houses" —

Preposition + Gen + Dat + Acc + Ins + Loc na — — motion — static o/ob — — against — about po — according to, along — — after, throughout prěd motion — — static — s/so — — with (size) together with — v/vo — — motion — static za static (possession) — motion/purpose static (position) —

bez "without" always governs genitive: bez vody "without water" do "to, until" always governs genitive: do Moskvy "to Moscow", do večera "until evening" iz "out of" always governs genitive: iz doma "out of the house" k/ko "to, toward" always governs dative: k mně "to me", ko dveri "to the door" od "from" always governs genitive: od brata "from (a) brother" pri "at, near, during" always governs locative: pri rěcě "at the river" s/so + instrumental = "together with": s bratom "with (a) brother" u "at, by" always governs genitive: u mene "at my place / I have"
Form Meaning a and; but ale but i and ili or abo or jednako however
Form Meaning aby / da by in order to, so that ače / hoti although ako (li) if jer / jerbo because jako / kako as, like kada / kogda when poka while poněže whereas, inasmuch as že that

This appendix presents annotated sample texts drawn from authentic Interslavic sources. Each entry provides the text in standard Latin orthography and an English translation. Brief grammar notes highlight features that recur across the Interslavic system. Sources: dialogues adapted from the Ranmaru educational series (files 56–74); prose relay texts compiled by Jan van Steenbergen.
Topic: Describing a friend; personal appearance and character

Line Interslavic Moj najlěpši prijatelj A Imaš li najlěpšego prijatelja? B Råzuměje sę! A Kako je jego imę? B Imę mu je Miša. A Kako on izględa? B On je vysoky. Ima světle vlåsy i modre oči. Mysljų, že on je veljmi lěpy. A Začto on ti sę podoba? B On je istinny optimist. Ako li imam problem, on vsegda vě, čto kazati. On je veljmi spokojny. Takože ima odličny smysl za humor. A Koliko dòlgo vy sę poznate? B Poznamo sę uže mnogo lět. Studovali jesmo zajedno.

Line English My best friend A Do you have a best friend? B Of course! A What is his name? B His name is Misha. A What does he look like? B He is tall. He has fair hair and blue eyes. I think he is very good-looking. A Why do you like him? B He is a true optimist. If I have a problem, he always knows what to say. He is very calm. He also has an excellent sense of humor. A How long have you known each other? B We have known each other for many years already. We studied together. Grammar notes. The superlative najlěpši (best) uses the prefix naj- added to the comparative lěpši. The reflexive particle sę in poznate sę and Råzuměje sę signals a mutual or impersonal action. Imę mu je Miša uses the dative of interest (mu) to express possession: "his name is Misha" (literally, "the name to-him is Misha"). Mysljų is first-person singular present of mysljati (to think).
Topic: Daily routine; present tense narration

Line Interslavic Obyčny denj A Čto ty dělaš každy denj utrom? B Ja ljubjų vstavati rano. Ja vsegda vstajų v 7 časov i běgam v parku. Podčas běga, obyčno ja slušam muzykų na mp3. Potom sę myjų pod dušem i zautrakajų. A Čto ty obyčno imaš za zautrak? B Obyčno ja pijų čaj i jěm sendvič. Poněkda, kògda imam malo vrěmena, ja samo pijų kavų s mlěkom. A Jězdiš li na råbotų avtom? B Ne, prědvyšam pųtovati metrom. A Kdě råbotaš i čto dělaš? B Ja råbotam v kompaniji. Každy denj råzgovarjam s klientami i odpovědam na e-maily. Moja råbota ne je veljmi zanimliva. A Čto dělaš poslě råboty? B Poslě råboty idų do doma. Doma kuham večerjų, a potom ględam noviny ili čitam knigų.

Line English An ordinary day A What do you do every morning? B I love to get up early. I always get up at 7 o'clock and jog in the park. While jogging I usually listen to music on my mp3 player. Then I shower and have breakfast. A What do you usually have for breakfast? B I usually drink tea and eat a sandwich. Sometimes, when I have little time, I only drink coffee with milk. A Do you go to work by car? B No, I prefer to travel by metro. A Where do you work and what do you do? B I work in a company. Every day I talk with clients and answer e-mails. My work is not very interesting. A What do you do after work? B After work I go home. At home I cook dinner, and then I watch the news or read a book. Grammar notes. Poněkda (sometimes) and podčas (during, while) are common adverbial time expressions. The verb prědvyšati (to prefer) takes an infinitive complement. Doma (at home) functions as a locative adverb without a preposition. Accusatives with motion prepositions: na råbotų, do doma.
Topic: Leisure activities; weekends

Line Interslavic Svobodno vrěmę A Čto ty dělaš v svobodno vrěmę? B V svobodno vrěmę ja ljubjų hoditi v basen. Mi sę podoba plavati. Ovo je dobro za zdråvje. A Hodiš li sama ili s prijateljami? B Moji prijatelji ne ljubęt plavati. Zato obyčno hođų sama. A A čto ty ljubiš dělati vikendom? B V vikend ljubjų dòlgo spati, ibo v tečeńju tydnja vsegda vstajų veljmi rano. Takože mi sę podoba hoditi v fitness klub. V sobotų strěćam sę s prijateljami i idemo na diskotekų. A A čto ty ljubiš dělati v nedělji? B V nedělji ja ljubjų odpočivati. Mi sę podoba ględati televizijų ili čitati knigų. Veljmi ljubjų takože teatr i kino. Nedělja je idealny denj, da sę odpravim v kino.

Line English Free time A What do you do in your free time? B In my free time I love going to the swimming pool. I enjoy swimming. It is good for one's health. A Do you go alone or with friends? B My friends don't like to swim. So I usually go alone. A And what do you like to do at weekends? B At the weekend I like to sleep in late, because during the week I always get up very early. I also enjoy going to the fitness club. On Saturday I meet up with friends and we go to a disco. A And what do you like to do on Sunday? B On Sunday I like to relax. I enjoy watching TV or reading a book. I also really love the theatre and cinema. Sunday is the ideal day to go to the cinema. Grammar notes. Mi sę podoba (I enjoy / it pleases me) uses the dative subject mi — the verb agrees with the infinitive or noun, not with the experiencer. Zato (therefore, that is why) is a causal connective. Da sę odpravim introduces a purpose clause using da + present subjunctive.
Topic: Meeting someone new; origins and language learning

Line Interslavic Odkųdy jesi? A Ahoj! Jesi li nova? B Ahoj! Da, dneś je moj pŕvy denj v školě. Kako sę zòveš? A Zòvų sę Stefani. A kako ty sę zòveš? B Zòvų sę Marta. Prijatno mi je tę poznati. Odkųdy jesi? A Jesm iz SŠA. A ty? B Jesm iz Išpanije. Žijų ovdě uže 2 měsęca. A Veljmi dobro govoriš russky. B Dękujų. Moja najlěpša prijateljka je rusinka i ona mę uči. A Udačnica. Može byti, tòž trěbam russkogo prijatelja, da byh lučše govorila.

Line English Where are you from? A Hi! Are you new here? B Hi! Yes, today is my first day at school. What is your name? A My name is Stefani. And what is your name? B My name is Marta. Nice to meet you. Where are you from? A I am from the USA. And you? B I am from Spain. I have been living here for 2 months already. A You speak Russian very well. B Thank you. My best friend is Russian and she teaches me. A You are lucky. Maybe I also need a Russian friend to speak better. Grammar notes. Zòvų sę (my name is; I am called) is the reflexive construction using first-person singular of zòvati (to call). Trěbati (to need) here governs a genitive object (russkogo prijatelja). The conditional da byh govorila uses the auxiliary byh (conditional first-person singular) plus the l-participle.
Topic: Ordering in a bar; asking for help

Line Interslavic Pivo, prošų (V baru) A Dajte mi dva piva, prošų. B Kakòvo pivo? Temno ili světlo? A Čto? Žalj mi je, ja ne govorjų russky. Samo pivo. B Pa, sdaje sę mi, že vy govorite russky. A Govorjų trohų i ne råzumějų, čto vy pytate. B Kakòvo pivo? Ukažite pŕstom. A Ovo za 50 rubljev. B Dobro. Dva piva. Bųde 100 rubljev.

Line English A beer, please (In a bar) A Give me two beers, please. B What kind of beer? Dark or light? A What? I'm sorry, I don't speak Russian. Just beer. B Well, it seems to me that you do speak Russian. A I speak a little and I don't understand what you are asking. B What kind of beer? Point with your finger. A This one for 50 roubles. B Fine. Two beers. That will be 100 roubles. Grammar notes. Sdaje sę mi (it seems to me) is an impersonal expression with dative mi. Pytati (to ask, to question) is distinct from prositi (to request). Trohų (a little) is used adverbially; the nasal vowel reflects the Proto-Slavic ǫ preserved in Interslavic orthography. The imperative dajte and ukažite use the standard second-person plural polite form.
Topic: At the hairdresser; appearance and colour

Line Interslavic Ostrižeńje A Zdråvo! Hòtěla byh sę ostrići. B Sadite sę, prošų. Kakòvų pričeskų vy želate? A Ja želala byh pričeskų kako děvčina na tom foto. Ględajte, ima grivkų i kråtke vlåsy. Čto myslite, bųde li ta pričeska izględati na mně dobro? B Da, mysljų tako. Imate ovalno lice i grivka bųde izględati u vas dobro. Ale jeste blondinka, a děvčina na foto je brunetka. Želate li farbovati vlåsy? A Želala byh farbovati v rudy. Čto myslite? B Mysljų, že rudy vam ne bųde dobro. Može byti, temno rudy. Kako o ovom časopisu. Podoba li sę vam tòj kolor? A Veljmi originalny kolor! Sųglåšų sę. Večeraś mę moj mųž ne pozna.

Line English A haircut A Hello! I would like to have my hair cut. B Please sit down. What kind of hairstyle would you like? A I would like the hairstyle like the girl in that photo. Look, she has a fringe and short hair. What do you think — will that hairstyle look good on me? B Yes, I think so. You have an oval face and the fringe will look good on you. But you are a blonde, and the girl in the photo is a brunette. Would you like to colour your hair? A I would like to dye it red. What do you think? B I think red won't suit you. Perhaps dark red. Like in this magazine. Do you like that colour? A A very original colour! I agree. This evening my husband won't recognise me. Grammar notes. The conditional hòtěla byh (I would like) is feminine form, with the l-participle hòtěla agreeing with the female speaker. Večeraś (this evening, tonight) uses the suffix -aś indicating "today" (cf. dneś — today). Sųglåšati sę (to agree) is a reflexive verb.
Topic: Family; physical resemblance; future plans

Line Interslavic Moj rodinny album A Želaš viděti moj rodinny album? B S radosťjų. A Ovo sųt moji roditelji. Tu sųt veljmi mlådi. Ovo je jih svaťba. B Izględaš velje kako tvoja mati. Imate jednakòve oči i usměh. A Mysliš tako? Vsi srodniki mojego otca kažųt, že izględam velje kako on. Imamo jednakòvy nos. Ględaj, ovo je moja babica. Ovdě ima 25 lět. B Kakòva krasavica! Tęžko je věriti, že ty i ja bųdemo imati někògda tòž 70 lět. A Raděje ne råzmysljam o tom. B A kto je tòj privlěkateljny hlåpec? A Ovo je moj brat. On je morjak. Uniforma izględa dobro na njem, ale ne? B Zělo. I kdě je sejčas? A On plava po Srědizemskomu morju. Už skoro prijěde v odpusk. Imati bųdemo zabavų za pråzdnovańje jego prijězda. Prijdeš? B Bezsųmno!

Line English My family album A Would you like to see my family album? B With pleasure. A These are my parents. They are very young here. This is their wedding. B You look a lot like your mother. You have the same eyes and smile. A You think so? All my father's relatives say that I look a lot like him. We have the same nose. Look, this is my grandmother. She is 25 years old here. B What a beauty! It is hard to believe that you and I will also be 70 years old someday. A I would rather not think about that. B And who is that attractive young man? A This is my brother. He is a sailor. The uniform looks good on him, doesn't it? B Very much so. And where is he now? A He is sailing on the Mediterranean Sea. He will be coming home on leave soon. We will have a party to celebrate his arrival. Will you come? B Certainly! Grammar notes. Velje (a lot, much) is an adverb used in comparisons. Raděje (rather, preferably) is a comparative adverb from råd (glad, willing). Bezsųmno (certainly, without doubt) uses the prefix bez- (without) plus sųmněńje (doubt). The future compound imati bųdemo places the infinitive before the auxiliary.
Topic: Unusual hobbies; comparison and surprise

Line Interslavic Kakòvo strånno hobbi! A Imaš li hobbi? B Imam. Ljubim pleteńje i sambo. A Sambo? Ne je li slučajno něko bojevo uměńje. B Da, da, to je russko bojevo uměńje. A Ale pleteńje i sambo sųt popòlno ine věći! Pleteńje je råzumlivo. Mnogo děvčęt ljubęt pleteńje. No sambo! Udiviteljno! I koliko dòlgo praktikuješ sambo? B Uže desęť lět. Počęla jesm praktikovati sambo, kògda jesm studovala v školě. Zato sųt sę hlåpci iz našego klassa vsegda bojali mę. A Dobro. A tvoje drugo hobbi? B Pleteńje je veljmi uspokojęća dějateljnosť. Moja babica mę je naučila. Kromě togo, to je sposob ščęditi pěnęzi. Ja pletų dlja sebe modne šali, šapky i svetry. Ględaj, jesm splela sama tòj sveter. Podoba li ti sę to? A Zělo je... originalny. B Dobro, i jestli ti sę to ne podoba, možeš povědati pravdų, ja tę ne bųdų biti!

Line English What a strange hobby! A Do you have a hobby? B I do. I love knitting and sambo. A Sambo? Isn't that some kind of martial art? B Yes, yes, it is a Russian martial art. A But knitting and sambo are completely different things! Knitting is understandable. Many girls love knitting. But sambo! Amazing! And how long have you been practising sambo? B Ten years already. I started practising sambo when I was studying at school. That is why the boys in our class were always afraid of me. A I see. And your other hobby? B Knitting is a very calming activity. My grandmother taught me. Besides, it is a way to save money. I knit fashionable scarves, hats and sweaters for myself. Look, I knitted this sweater myself. Do you like it? A It is very... original. B Fine, and if you don't like it, you can tell the truth — I won't hit you! Grammar notes. Bojevo uměńje (martial art; literally "combat skill") is a common compound. Děvčę (girl) belongs to the neuter -ę stem declension; its genitive plural děvčęt shows the characteristic zero ending with vowel lengthening. Ščęditi pěnęzi (to save money) uses the accusative plural pěnęzi from the i-stem plural pěnęzi (money). Uspokojęća is a present active participle (calming).
Topic: Asking a favour; describing people's appearance

Line Interslavic Velika prośba A Leno, imam k tobě velikų prośbų! Možeš li večeraś strětnųti mojų prijateljkų v aeroportu? Nemam vrěmena. B Råzuměje sę. Ne je problem. Kako ona izględa? A Ona je privlěkateljna blondinka s zelenymi očami. Ona ne je vysoka. Ljubi nositi sportivnų odeđų. Bųde imati jarko oranževy kufer. B Imam nadejų, že ne bųde nekoliko blondinok s oranževymi kuframi. A Ne trevoži sę, ja opišų jej tvoj izględ. B Povědaj jej, že ja bųdų v koričnevoj kurtkě i černyh džinsah. A Jej bųde dostatòčno znati, že jesi skoro dva metra vysoka. B Imaš pravdų, tęžko je mę ne primětiti.

Line English A big favour A Lena, I have a big favour to ask you! Can you meet my friend at the airport this evening? I don't have the time. B Of course. No problem. What does she look like? A She is an attractive blonde with green eyes. She is not tall. She likes wearing sportswear. She will have a bright orange suitcase. B I hope there won't be several blondes with orange suitcases. A Don't worry, I'll describe your appearance to her. B Tell her that I'll be wearing a brown jacket and black jeans. A It will be enough for her to know that you are nearly two metres tall. B You are right, it is hard not to notice me. Grammar notes. Imam k tobě prośbų (I have a request of you) uses the prepositional phrase k + dative for the person addressed in a request. Skoro (almost, nearly) in this context is an approximative adverb. Tęžko je (it is difficult) is an impersonal construction with infinitive complement. Instrumental plural in clothing: v koričnevoj kurtkě i černyh džinsah uses locative for "in/wearing".
Topic: Planning a holiday; disagreement and compromise

Line Interslavic Kamo pojědem v odpusk? A Ja ne hòćų jěhati v Egipt. Jězdili jesmo minųlym godom i mi ne je spodobalo sę. B A mi je spodobalo sę. Hòćų snova onamo pojěhati. A Teplo morje, odlično nyrjańje. B Ale tam je prěmnogo goręće v sej sezon. Može, pojědemo v bolje hlådno město. A Na Sěverny polus? B Ne rěči bezsmyslice! Pojědimo v Evropų, na priměr, v Rim. Možemo posětiti toliko kulturnyh měst. Poznamo toliko zanimlivogo. A Ja ne govorjų italijansky. B Arabsky ty takože ne govoriš, a hòćeš pojěhati v Egipt! A To ne je tòjže stvår... B Jědų v Rim i točka. A Nu, dobro... Ja takože.

Line English Where shall we go on holiday? A I don't want to go to Egypt. We went last year and I didn't enjoy it. B I enjoyed it. I want to go there again. A Warm sea, excellent diving. B But it is far too hot there at this time of year. Perhaps we should go somewhere cooler. A To the North Pole? B Don't talk nonsense! Let's go to Europe, for example to Rome. We could visit so many cultural sites. We would learn so much interesting stuff. A I don't speak Italian. B You don't speak Arabic either, yet you want to go to Egypt! A That is not the same thing... B I am going to Rome and that's that. A Well, fine... Me too. Grammar notes. The directional kamo (where to, whither) contrasts with kdě (where, at what place). Prěmnogo (far too much, excessively) uses the prefix prě- (over-, too). Tòjže stvår (the same thing) employs the identity particle -že attached to the demonstrative. I točka (and that is that; and full stop) is a colloquial emphatic ending.
Topic: Household chores; narrating past events; in-laws

Line Interslavic Nenaviđų domovų råbotų! A Mašo, pozdråv! Kako provela jesi svoj vikend? B Raděje ne pytaj. A Začto? Čto sę je slučilo? B Vse je započęla v sobotų. Izpŕva jesm počistila v dnevnom pokoju i izmyla vśų posųdų. Potom jesm oprala vśų odeđų i očistila koverec. I potom jesm izmyla okna. A Oh! Tako trudoljubiva jesi! B Počkaj, ješče jesm ne zakončila! Potom takože jesm izmyla podlogų. Poslě sego jesm šla na zakupy. Konečno, jesm ogladila odeđų i začęla gotoviti večerjų. A A mųž? B A mųž je pomogal děťam s domašnjim zadańjem. Do nedělje jesmo musěli zavŕšiti vse zadaće i izglęđati kako idealno semejstvo. A Ale začto? B V neděljų mę je svekròv posětila. A Dobro. Pa, i kako je bylo vse? B Ona je primětila pråh v garderobě i kazala, že povinna jesm čistiti čęšće.

Line English I hate housework! A Masha, hi! How did you spend your weekend? B Better not ask. A Why? What happened? B It all started on Saturday. First I cleaned the living room and washed all the dishes. Then I washed all the laundry and vacuumed the carpet. And then I washed the windows. A Oh! You are so hardworking! B Wait, I haven't finished yet! Then I also washed the floor. After that I went shopping. Finally, I ironed the laundry and started cooking dinner. A And your husband? B My husband helped the children with their homework. By Sunday we had to finish all the chores and appear to be an ideal family. A But why? B On Sunday my mother-in-law came to visit. A I see. Well, and how did it all go? B She noticed dust in the wardrobe and said that I should clean more often. Grammar notes. Izpŕva (first of all, to begin with) is an adverb built from iz- + pŕv- (first). Musěti (must, to be obliged) is a modal verb. Svekròv (mother-in-law, husband's mother) is a consonant-stem noun. The dialogue also shows an alternative version in the source using simple past (aorist/imperfect) forms (počistih, izmyh), illustrating register variation between the compound past and the simple past.
Topic: Gossip about relationships; character judgements

Line Interslavic Anja ima novogo prijatelja A Znaš li, že Anja ima novogo prijatelja? B Oh! Je li lěpy? A Ne je moj vkųs, no on je veljmi umny i zanimlivy. On zna mnogo směšnyh pritč i veljmi prijazny. B To znači, že on je lěpši, než jej poslědnji prijatelj. Byl on je někako čudny. Nikògda sę ne je usměhal i ne jesmo imali obćih tem za råzgovor. A Jesi prava, mi je sę takože ne podobal. Ne ljubjų pessimistov. Kako Anja je mogla hoditi s njim...? B Kto vě, može byti, je imal někы skryty talent... A Pravdopodobno. Ili prosto ona ljubila, čto on je tako bogaty i vsegda daril jej dråge podarky. B A kto ne ljubi dråge podarky? A Točno. A novy prijatelj ješče studuje. Bųde učiteljem biologije. B Užas, učitelji segda zaråbotyvajųt duže malo... A Oh, ględaj! Anja ide! B Čao, Anjo! Kakòv surpriz! Govorimo o tom i onom, o politikě, o modě... a kamo ideš?

Line English Anya has a new boyfriend A Do you know that Anya has a new boyfriend? B Oh! Is he good-looking? A Not my type, but he is very intelligent and interesting. He knows many funny stories and is very friendly. B That means he is better than her last boyfriend. He was somehow strange. He never smiled and we had no common topics to talk about. A You are right, I didn't like him either. I don't like pessimists. How could Anya go out with him...? B Who knows, maybe he had some hidden talent... A Probably. Or she simply liked that he was so rich and always gave her expensive presents. B And who doesn't like expensive presents? A Exactly. And the new boyfriend is still studying. He will be a biology teacher. B How awful, teachers always earn so little... A Oh, look! Anya is coming! B Hi, Anya! What a surprise! We're talking about this and that, about politics, about fashion... and where are you going? Grammar notes. Hoditi s njim (to go out with him, to date him) is an idiomatic use of hoditi (to go, to walk). Pritča (story, tale; parable) is a common Slavic literary word. Pravdopodobno (probably) is an adverb from pravdopodobny (probable, literally "resembling the truth"). The vocative Anjo! shows the standard Interslavic vocative ending -o for feminine names ending in -a.
Topic: Ideals and reality; appearance; gentle humour

Line Interslavic Moj idealny mųž A Čao! Začto jesi tako zamysljena? B Nu, dneś utrom čitala jesm moj obljubjeny časopis o holivudskih zvězdah i sråzuměla, že moj sųprųg sòvsěm ne izględa kako idealny mųž! A A kako izględa tvoj idealny mųž? B On je vysoky, muskulisty brunet s velikymi gnědymi očami. Kako Javier Bardem. A Nu, prinajmenje, tvoj sųprųg je takože brunet... B Da, toliko je maly, tòlsty i v okuljarah. Očevidno, kako možeš imati muskuly, pokųd jedino, čto dělaš je ležańje na divaně! A Ględaj plusy. Prinajmenje, ostatne ženy na njego ne ględajųt. Nemaš povoda revnovati. B Ne věm... Mušų něčto měnjati v svojem životu. Može byti, někdě mę čeka moj Javier Bardem! A Ako želaš něčto směniti, izpŕva poprobuj izglęđati kako Penelope Cruz.

Line English My ideal husband A Hi! Why are you so lost in thought? B Well, this morning I was reading my favourite magazine about Hollywood stars and I realised that my husband doesn't look at all like my ideal man! A And what does your ideal man look like? B He is tall, muscular, a dark-haired man with large brown eyes. Like Javier Bardem. A Well, at least your husband is also dark-haired... B Yes, except that he is small, fat and wears glasses. Obviously, how can you have muscles if the only thing you do is lie on the sofa! A Look on the bright side. At least other women don't look at him. You have no reason to be jealous. B I don't know... I need to change something in my life. Maybe somewhere my Javier Bardem is waiting for me! A If you want to change something, first try looking like Penelope Cruz. Grammar notes. Sråzuměti (to realise, to understand suddenly) uses the prefix s- for completion. Gnědy (brown, chestnut — of eyes) is a traditional Slavic colour term. Pokųd (as long as, while) introduces a temporal/conditional clause. Okuljary (glasses, spectacles) is a masculine plural noun. Prinajmenje (at least) is an adverbial phrase.
Topic: Taking up a new sport; persuasion; humour

Line Interslavic Figurna jězda na ledu A Ty znaš, nyně ja hođų na lekcije figurnoj jězdy na ledu. B Napravdų? I je kako ti? A Super! Ja jesm popòlno vòoduševjena! Na počętku my sę učimo tancevati v salě, a potom idemo praktikovati na ledu. B Kako zanimlivo... A Ovo je veljmi krasivy sport. Začto ne načneš studovati so mnojų? B Ja? Bez šansov! Pŕvo, ja ne ljubjų, kògda hlådno. Vtoro, to je krajnje opasno. Mogų upasti i slomiti něčto. A Ne bųdi nudna. Každa disciplina sporta je nemnogo opasna. Kromě togo, poslě ovyh lekcij vsi prijatelji bųdųt nam zaviděti. B To je opasno i točka. A Ne, nikako! Na kolěnah je specialna zaščita. Ovo je absolutno bezopasno. B Nu, dobro, može byti, ja prijdų na jednų lekcijų. A Hura! Jesm tako rada! Ja snova imam partnerkų! B A čto sę slučilo s poprědnjejų partnerkojų? A Ničego strašnogo... na prošloj lekciji ja jesm upala na njų i slomila jej rųkų.

Line English Figure skating A You know, I am now taking figure skating lessons. B Really? And how do you find it? A Great! I am completely thrilled! At the beginning we learn to dance in the hall, and then we go to practise on the ice. B How interesting... A It is a very beautiful sport. Why don't you start learning with me? B Me? No way! Firstly, I don't like being cold. Secondly, it is extremely dangerous. I could fall and break something. A Don't be boring. Every sporting discipline is a little dangerous. Besides, after these lessons all our friends will envy us. B It is dangerous and that is that. A No, not at all! There is special protection on the knees. This is absolutely safe. B Well, fine, maybe I will come to one lesson. A Hurray! I am so glad! I have a partner again! B And what happened to your previous partner? A Nothing serious... at the last lesson I fell on her and broke her arm. Grammar notes. Pŕvo... vtoro... (firstly... secondly...) are ordinal adverbs used to enumerate arguments. Zaviděti (to envy) takes a dative object (nam). Poprědnji (previous, former) uses the prefix po- + prěd- (before, in front). The punchline demonstrates the perfect jesm upala (I fell) and slomila (broke), showing the narrative use of the compound past in dialogue.
Topic: Travel planning; fears; negotiation

Line Interslavic Samoletom ili vlåkom? A Uže junij, a my ješče ne jesmo splanovali naše pųtovańje v Soči. Tutčas musimo kupiti bilety, inače ostavjene bųdųt samo najdråžejši! B Jesi prava. Pojdemo na stancijų, da bysmo kupili bilety na vlåk. A Na vlåk? Čemu by nam ne letěti samoletom? Cěna je jednakòva. B Ty znaš, že sę bojų letati samoletom. Kromě togo, samoletom mi vsegda staje zlo. A Od Moskvy do Soči vlåkom je okolo dvoh dnjev. Napravdų želaš provesti dva dnji v neudobnom vlåku? B A mi sę to podoba. Ja mogų piti čaj i ględěti v okno. A Imam karmų na detij, koji kričęt, ili na mųžev, koji hrapųt. B No vlåkom skoro nikògda ne byvaje avarij... A Samolety takože padajųt veljmi rědko. Kromě togo, ty ljubiš pokupati podarky v duty free.

Line English By plane or by train? A It is already June, and we still haven't planned our trip to Sochi. We must buy tickets right now, otherwise only the most expensive ones will be left! B You are right. Let's go to the station to buy train tickets. A By train? Why don't we fly by plane? The price is the same. B You know I am afraid of flying. Besides, I always feel sick on a plane. A From Moscow to Sochi by train is about two days. Do you really want to spend two days in an uncomfortable train? B I actually enjoy it. I can drink tea and look out the window. A I always end up sitting next to children who cry or men who snore. B But by train there are almost never any accidents... A Planes also crash very rarely. Besides, you love buying gifts in duty-free. Grammar notes. Tutčas (right now, immediately) is a temporal adverb. Da bysmo kupili uses the conditional auxiliary bysmo (first-person plural conditional) with the past participle to express purpose ("so that we would buy"). Staje zlo (feels sick; literally "it becomes ill") is an impersonal construction with dative experiencer. Inače (otherwise, or else) is a conditional connective.

Topic: Facts about Russia; myth vs. reality; prose monologue with joke Some foreigners think that bears walk along the streets in Russia. Of course, that is not true! Bears live in forests and do not like people. An encounter with a bear can be very dangerous. Russian people love going into the forest to pick mushrooms and berries. They do it carefully, because bears also love berries and mushrooms and may attack a person. A bear eats everything: berries, fish, meat, and even insects. He especially loves honey. In winter bears usually sleep, because the weather is too cold and there is no food. While the bear sleeps, it can lose 80 kilograms in weight! Russian people look very much like bears. In winter they also become slow and sleep a lot, because outside it is far too cold and dark. That is why Russians love bears and call them "mishki". Misha was the mascot of the Summer Olympics of 1980 in Moscow. Bear joke: Cold winter. A bear wanders through the forest, stops from time to time, bangs his head against a tree and cries: "Why, oh why did I drink so much coffee?!"

Někòtori ćuđezemci mnęt, že v Rusiji medvědi hodęt po ulicah. Råzuměje sę, to ne je pravda! Medvědi žijųt v lěsah i ne ljubęt ljudij. Strěća s medvědem može byti duže opasna. Russki ljudi ljubęt hoditi v lěs i sòbirati griby i jagody. Oni dělajųt to s vnimańjem, ibo medvědi takože ljubęt jagody i griby i mogųt napasti na člověka. Medvěď jěst vse: jagody, rybų, męso i daže insektov. Osoblivo on ljubi med. V zimų medvědi obyčno spęt, ibo vrěmę je prěmnogo hlådno i nema jědy. Dokolě medvěď spi, on može shudnųti na 80 kilogrammov! Russki ljudi duže izględajųt kako medvědi. V zimų oni takože stajųt medli i mnogo spęt, ibo vo vně je prěmnogo hlådno i temno. Zato že rusi ljubęt medvědev i zòvųt jih «miški». Miša je talisman lětnjej olimpiady 1980 goda v Moskvě. Směška o medvědi: Hlådna zima. Medvěď blųka po lěsu, poněkda sę zaustavja, udarja glåvojų po drěvesu i kriči: «Nu, začto, začto jesm pil tako mnogo kavy?!» Grammar notes. This text demonstrates continuous prose style. Dokolě (while, as long as) is a temporal conjunction. Shudnųti (to lose weight) uses the prefix s- for completion. Zòvųt jih «miški» (they call them "mishki") uses the accusative jih (them) as the direct object with the instrumental or nominative predicate in a naming construction. The joke uses the perfect jesm pil (I have drunk / I drank) with an exclamatory question.
Topic: Extreme geography; community life; descriptive prose

Najhlådnějše město v světu je russko selo Ojmjakon. Ovo selo sę nahodi na Iztoku. Tam žijųt okolo 500 ljudij. V zimų obyčna temperatura je minus 50 gradusov. Raz v ovom selu temperatura je byla minus 71 gradus! No žitelji sela sųt privykli k hlådų. Sdě vsi jedin drugogo znajųt. Vsi jedin drugomu pomogajųt. Obljųbjeno město strěći je magazin, kdě prodajųt svěži hlěb. Selo nema bara, ibo poslě 18 V zimų denj traje samo 3 časa. A v lěto je světlo daže noćjų. V martě v selu byvaje festival. Na ovy festival prijězđaje mnogo ćuđezemcev. Oni želajųt odčuvati, čto −50 gradusov je tako.

The coldest place in the world is the Russian village of Oymyakon. This village is located in the East. About 500 people live there. In winter the normal temperature is minus 50 degrees. Once in this village the temperature was minus 71 degrees! But the villagers are used to the cold. Here everyone knows everyone. Everyone helps one another. The favourite meeting place is the shop where they sell fresh bread. The village has no bar, because after 18 In winter the day lasts only 3 hours. And in summer it is light even at night. In March there is a festival in the village. Many foreigners come to this festival. They want to experience what minus 50 degrees feels like. Grammar notes. Jedin drugogo / jedin drugomu (each other, one another) is the reciprocal construction using the indefinite numeral jedin (one) with matching case forms. Ne je dopušćeno (it is not permitted) is an impersonal passive construction. Ni... ni... (neither... nor...) is the negative correlative.
Topic: History and culture of a city; descriptive prose

Sankt-Peterburg je jedin iz najkrasivših grådov Rusije. On byl je osnovany imperatorom Petrom I (pŕvym). Imperator je rěšil izbudovati gråd sdě, da by odkryti dlja Rusije «okno v Evropų». Prěđe na ovom městě bylo je blåto, zato budovati gråd bylo je veljmi tęžko. Mnogo ljudij je umŕlo, kògda budovali tòj gråd. Zato i rěkųt, že to je «gråd na kosťah». V Sankt-Peterburgě je mnogo krasivyh palacev, muzejev i cŕkvij. Sdě sę nahodi znamenita Jantarova komnata. V lěto v Sankt-Peterburgě byvajųt «běle noći». To je dnji, kògda noćjų vně je světlo skoro råvno, kako dnjem. To je najbolje popularno vrěmę dlja turistov. Noćjų na ulicah je mnogo ljudij i sdaje sę, že veś gråd ne spi.

Saint Petersburg is one of the most beautiful cities in Russia. It was founded by Emperor Peter I (the First). The Emperor decided to build a city here in order to open a "window onto Europe" for Russia. Previously this place was a swamp, so building the city was very difficult. Many people died when they were building that city. That is why they say it is "a city built on bones". In Saint Petersburg there are many beautiful palaces, museums and churches. The famous Amber Room is located here. In summer Saint Petersburg has "white nights". These are days when at night outside it is almost as light as during the day. This is the most popular time for tourists. At night there are many people on the streets and it seems as if the whole city is not sleeping. Grammar notes. Da by odkryti uses the purpose construction da + conditional. Blåto (swamp, marsh) is an archaic/poetic word, preserved in Interslavic. Cŕkvij is the genitive plural of cŕkva (church), showing the consonant-cluster form cŕk- with vocalic r. Sdaje sę (it seems) is an impersonal reflexive.
Topic: Ghost story; historical legend; Saint Petersburg

Jedno iz najznamenitějših prividěnij Sankt-Peterburga je prividěńje Pavla Pŕvogo. Ovo prividěńje žije v Mihajlovskom zamku, kdě žil i byl je ubijen imperator. Raz podčas bala imperator naglo omdlěl. Kògda on je sę sbudil, to ględnųl s bezumnymi očami na vsih gostev i kazal: «Napravdų udušęt mę?» Kako rezultat, vse je sę i slučilo točno tako. Kògda imperator imal je 47 lět, on byl je udušeny v svojem zamku. Několiko dnjev prěd smŕťjų, on je rěkl, že vidi kròv na bělyh stěnah svojej spaľnji. Poslě smŕti imperatora v zamku dòlgo vrěmę nikto ne je žil. Gråd je byl pòlny strašnyh povědòk o prividěńji. Nyně v Mihajlovskom zamku sę nahodi Russky muzej. Råbotniky muzeja ne ljubęt ostavati tam na večer. Oni govoręt, že v pustom dvorcu sę odkryvajųt i zakryvajųt dveri i skripi podloga. Stråži poněkda vidęt figurų člověka so svěćejų v rukě. Kògda tako byvaje oni tiho rěkųt jej: «Dobra noć, Vaše Veličenstvo».

One of the most famous ghosts of Saint Petersburg is the ghost of Paul the First. This ghost lives in the Mikhailovsky Castle, where the Emperor lived and was murdered. Once during a ball, the Emperor suddenly fainted. When he came round, he looked at all the guests with wild eyes and said: "Will they really strangle me?" As a result, that is exactly what happened. When the Emperor was 47 years old, he was strangled in his own castle. Several days before his death he said that he could see blood on the white walls of his bedroom. After the Emperor's death, no one lived in the castle for a long time. The city was full of terrifying stories about the ghost. Now the Russian Museum is located in the Mikhailovsky Castle. The museum staff do not like staying there in the evening. They say that in the empty palace doors open and close by themselves and the floor creaks. The guards sometimes see the figure of a man holding a candle. When this happens, they say quietly to him: "Good night, Your Majesty." Grammar notes. Omdlěti (to faint) and sbuditi sę (to come round, to wake up) are prefixed verbs of the body. Naglo (suddenly, unexpectedly) is the adverb from nagly. Udušati (to strangle, to smother) uses the prefix u- for completion. So svěćejų v rukě (with a candle in hand) uses the instrumental svěćejų and the locative v rukě. Vaše Veličenstvo (Your Majesty) is a fixed honorific form.

Topic: Village life; nostalgia; literary prose This text originates from the Pan-Slavic Relay of September–October 2009, an event in which participants in various Slavic constructed languages translated a passage in relay fashion. The source passage was taken from a nineteenth-century English essay by Mary Russell Mitford. The Interslavic version below is by Jan van Steenbergen, representing the Slovianski stage of the relay; it is included here as a literary register sample distinct from the conversational dialogues above.

Iz vsih možlivih ideal'nih mest, v ktorih živut ljudi, ja najviše-mnogo ljubim male selo, daleko od šumnogo groda, s jego maloju společnostju. Ono ne ima prepolnenih blokov, tol'ko male drevjane budinki – proste i malovatel'ne mesto dlja žitja, s žiteljami, ktorih obliča sut rovno znajome kak cveti v našem sade. To je zatvoreni svet s nemnogo ljudami, blizko sojedinenimi kak mrovki v mrovkovoj kope, pčeli v pčel'nike, ovci v ovečkom gumne, muhi v mušskom gnezde ili morniki na korable, gde vsekto znaje vsekogo i vsekto je znani od vsekogo, gde každi interesuje sja v každom i každi može imat' nadeju, čo nekto interesuje sja v njego. Kak milo bilo bi zanurit' sja v to serdečne čuvstvo ljubenija i neznanih običajov, sobrat' sja i bit' prijateli s timi vsimi jedinstvenimi ljudjami okolo nas! Tak, čo mi budeme znat' i ljubit' vse kuti i obroti tenjovih ulic i solnečne pokrivne kanali, prez ktore mi prehodime celi den'. Libokak, mala social'na grupa, ktora je sel'ska společnost', je to, čemu poezija i proza djakujut najviše-mnogo. Dolga, roztjagnuta droga, bludžuča sja v milem, teplom dne i prehodžuča veliku, široku drogu polnu avt i tirov. Vi ne hočete putovat' s mnoju, droge čitateli? Put' ne bude dolga. Mi počeme na konce dolini i odtud poideme v goru.

Of all possible ideal places for people to live, the one I love the most is a small village, far from the noise of the city, with its small community. It does not have crowded apartment blocks, only small wooden cottages — a simple and picturesque place to live, with inhabitants whose faces are as familiar as the flowers in our garden. It is an enclosed world with few people, closely united like ants in an ant-hill, bees in a hive, sheep in a sheep-fold, flies in a swarm, or sailors on a ship — where everyone knows everyone and everyone is known by everyone, where each person takes an interest in every other and each person may hope that someone takes an interest in him. How pleasant it would be to immerse oneself in that warm feeling of love and unfamiliar customs, to gather together and be friends with all those unique people around us! So that we would come to know and love all the corners and turns of the shaded streets and sun-drenched meadows that we pass through every day. In any case, a small social group — which is a village community — is what poetry and prose value most of all. A long, stretched-out road, winding its way through a mild, warm day and crossing a large wide road full of cars and lorries. Do you not want to travel with me, dear reader? The journey will not be long. We start at the end of the valley and from there we go uphill. Grammar notes. This relay text represents an early stage of what became modern Interslavic (then called Slovianski). It uses -t' for the soft sign in infinitives and sja as the reflexive particle, reflecting the Slavic-normalised orthography of the period. Several lexical choices (libokak for "in any case", djakujut for "are grateful for", drogu polnu avt for "road full of cars") illustrate how literary and colloquial registers handled internationalisms. The passage also demonstrates complex relative clause nesting and the deliberate pastoral register that distinguishes literary Interslavic from the conversational dialogues in sections 1–19.

This section reproduces the Russian–Interslavic phrasebook from Vojtěch Merunka's Novoslovienskij jazyk (2012), pages 87–92. The phrasebook is presented in eight thematic categories. Interslavic is given in Latin script as in the original. Parenthesised letters such as (A), (G), (L), (D) indicate the grammatical case required for the blank slot.

Directional impf. Perfective hoditi "walk (around)" idti "go (toward)" pojdti "set off" jezditi "ride (around)" jehati "ride (toward)" pojehati "set off by vehicle" letati "fly (around)" letěti "fly (toward)" poletěti "fly off" nositi "carry (around)" nesti "carry (toward)" ponesti "carry off" voziti "drive/transport" vezti "convey (toward)" povezti "convey off" běgati "run (around)" běgti "run (toward)" poběgti "run off" plovati "swim (around)" pluti "swim (toward)" popluti "swim off" In compounds, non-directional = imperfective, directional = perfective: odhoditi (impf.) / oditi (pf.) "go away"

Perfective ending Imperfective derived by Example -ati → -yvati (hard) / -ivati (soft) dokazati → dokazyvati -iti → -jati (vowel lengthened) napraviti → napravjati -iti (with consonant mutation) sj/zj/tj/dj/stj/zdj → š/ž/č/dž/šč/ždž oprostiti → opraščati monosyllabic stem → -vati priznati → priznavati

Interslavic is designed to occupy the centre of the Slavic linguistic space, offering solutions that serve the broadest possible range of Slavic speakers. Those solutions are not always equally optimal for every subgroup. What is maximally intelligible in Bulgaria or Croatia is not necessarily maximally intelligible in Poland or Russia. Flavorization is the systematic mechanism by which Interslavic is adapted — on one or more linguistic dimensions — to increase intelligibility or familiarity for a specific audience, without abandoning the language's identity as Interslavic. The term flavorization (flavorizacija) denotes the deliberate addition of regional colour to a text or utterance. A flavorized text remains Interslavic: it is not a translation into a national language, and it MUST remain fully intelligible to any speaker of standard Interslavic.

Flavorization is the systematic adaptation of Interslavic on phonological, morphological, lexical, or orthographic levels to increase intelligibility or familiarity for a specific subgroup of Slavic speakers. The following normative constraints apply: Flavorization is optional in all registers; standard Interslavic is always acceptable. Flavorization MAY be applied on any single dimension or on multiple dimensions simultaneously. A flavorized text MUST remain mutually intelligible with standard Interslavic (see the relevant section). Flavorization is not the creation of a separate language or dialect in the sociolinguistic sense; it is a stylistic and communicative register within Interslavic. The process is bidirectional in practice: a speaker's native language inevitably influences the Interslavic they produce, even without deliberate flavorization. The normative framework in this chapter concerns deliberate, rule-governed adaptation; incidental native-language influence is not regulated.

Flavorization MAY be applied independently on any of the following four dimensions: Dimension Description Example Phonological Substitution of phonemes or sound patterns according to regional reflexes y → i for South Slavic audiences Morphological Selection of inflectional or derivational variants that favor particular dialect areas infinitive -ť (Northern) vs. -ti (standard) Lexical Choice among regional synonyms documented in the Interslavic dictionary ktory (Northern) vs. koj (Southern) Orthographic Adaptation of spelling conventions to resemble a familiar national orthography sz, cz conventions for Polish readers The four dimensions are fully independent: a text MAY apply phonological and lexical flavorization without any orthographic or morphological adjustment, and vice versa. However, canonical flavor models specify coordinated settings across multiple dimensions. The following chapters address each dimension in turn: — Phonological Flavorization — Morphological Flavorization — Lexical Flavorization — Orthographic Flavorization

The normative constraint on flavorization is intelligibility. A flavorized Interslavic text MUST remain mutually intelligible with standard Interslavic. The operational threshold is: a speaker of standard Interslavic SHOULD be able to understand a fully flavorized text with no more difficulty than the difficulty of understanding a regional variety of their own language. This constraint implies: Phonological substitutions MUST be systematic and reversible; no substitution MAY introduce phonemic ambiguity that destroys lexical contrast. Morphological variants MUST remain within the paradigm space documented in the relevant section; ad-hoc morphological inventions are not flavorization. Lexical choices MUST draw from the documented Interslavic dictionary; they MUST NOT introduce words that are borrowed exclusively from a single national language without Slavic etymological grounding. Orthographic variants MUST map to standard Interslavic in a one-to-one or rule-governed fashion; the mapping MUST be recoverable by any reader of the standard. Flavorized texts that violate these constraints are not flavorized Interslavic; they are national-language texts or code-switching.

For practical use, Interslavic defines two canonical flavor models. These models represent coordinated settings across all four dimensions and are intended to cover the most common communicative scenarios. Property Northern Model Southern Model Primary target audience Russians, Belarusians, Poles, Sorbs Serbs, Croats, Slovenes, Bulgarians, Macedonians Secondary target audience Czechs, Slovaks, Ukrainians Czechs, Slovaks, Ukrainians Phonological center of gravity East and West Slavic reflexes South Slavic reflexes Morphological features Infinitive -ť; neuter adj. -e; relative pronoun ktory Infinitive -ti; neuter adj. -o; relative pronoun koj Key lexical alternations Prefix vy-; preposition dlja Prefix iz-; preposition za Orthographic conventions East Slavic Cyrillic or Polish-based Latin Croatian/Slovene Latin or South Slavic Cyrillic These two models are not exhaustive. The full combinatorial space of phonological, morphological, lexical, and orthographic variants is much larger, and a text oriented toward Czech readers, for instance, may apply a distinct combination that does not map cleanly to either canonical model. The canonical models serve as reference points and as a practical shorthand in this specification. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should determine whether any additional canonical models (e.g., a West Slavic model oriented toward Czech and Slovak, or an East Slavic model distinguishing Russian from Ukrainian) should be formally specified alongside the Northern and Southern models. Criteria for canonicalization and the relationship between the canonical models and the dictionary's regional synonym markings also require committee decision.
documents.s: Two Canonical Flavor Models: Northern and Southern

documents.t: Lexical flavorization selects among documented Interslavic synonyms according to their regional distribution. Unlike phonological or morphological flavorization, which alters the form of words, lexical flavorization chooses between distinct lexical items — each independently attested in the Slavic languages — whose geographic distribution differs across the Slavic dialect continuum. All lexical choices specified in this chapter draw exclusively from the documented Interslavic dictionary. Introducing words borrowed solely from a single national language without broader Slavic etymological grounding does not constitute flavorization; it constitutes code-switching .

Unverified Content The formal labeling system (North/South/East/West) with the specific definitions in the table below is not described in the source material. The source mentions that the dictionary "sometimes offers synonyms, with one word covering one part of Slavic territory and a second word the other," but does not define a formal label taxonomy. The Interslavic dictionary marks many synonyms with a regional preference label indicating which part of the Slavic territory the word is primarily associated with. The labels used are: Label Meaning North Primarily attested in North Slavic (Russian, Belarusian, Polish, Sorbian) South Primarily attested in South Slavic (Serbian, Croatian, Bosnian, Slovene, Bulgarian, Macedonian) East Primarily attested in East Slavic (Russian, Ukrainian, Belarusian) West Primarily attested in West Slavic (Polish, Czech, Slovak, Sorbian) A synonym marked North is not incorrect in Southern contexts; it is simply less likely to be recognized immediately by a South Slavic audience. The principle governing lexical flavorization is therefore: for a given target audience, prefer the synonym whose regional distribution best overlaps with that audience. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should specify the formal criteria by which a synonym pair receives a North/South/East/West label in the dictionary, and whether any words can bear multiple labels simultaneously (e.g., East+South). The relationship between lexical flavorization labels and the phonological voting weight system also requires clarification.
The following table documents the principal lexical alternations between the Northern and Southern canonical models. Items are listed by semantic category.

Meaning Northern Southern Notes which, who (relative) ktory koj See also Unverified Content The tot/taj and onen/onaj demonstrative pronoun alternations are not discussed in the source material. Only the ktory/koj relative pronoun distinction is sourced. | this | tot / ta / to | taj / ta / to | | | that | onen / ona / ono | onaj / ona / ono | | Regional reference: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian który котрий який který који

Meaning Northern Southern for (purposive) dlja za Unverified Content The potomu čto/jer, čtoby/da bi, and hotja/mada/premda alternations are not discussed in the source material. Only the dlja/za alternation is sourced. | because | potomu čto / ibo | zato što / jer | | in order to | čtoby | da bi | | although | hotja | mada / premda | Regional reference for 'for (purposive)': Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian dla для за pro за

The prefix alternation vy- / iz- (documented morphologically in the relevant section) also has a lexical dimension: many prefixed verbs are lexicalized with one prefix but not the other, creating Northern and Southern vocabulary items. Meaning Northern Southern to study, learn vyučiti izučiti to speak out, pronounce vygovoriti izgovoriti to carry out vynesti iznesti to produce vyrobiti izrobiti

Unverified Content This entire vocabulary alternation table is not present in the source material. The source mentions that the dictionary offers synonyms with regional distribution but does not list specific word pairs such as gorod/grad, glaz/oko, vsegda/uvěk, uže/već, toliko/samo, očenь/veoma, teper/sada, etc. These pairs may be plausible but cannot be verified against the source. The following list covers frequently occurring synonyms where Northern and Southern lexical choices diverge perceptibly. Meaning Northern Southern city gorod grad time (duration) vremja vrěme eye glaz oko snow sneg sněg hand ruka ruka word slovo reč / rěč always vsegda uvěk / vazda already uže već only toliko samo very očenь veoma / jako because potomu čto jer now teper sada Regional reference for 'city': Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian gród / miasto місто град město град TODO: Committee Input Needed The vocabulary alternation table above is illustrative and not exhaustive. The committee should review and expand this table to cover all major synonym pairs in the Interslavic dictionary that carry a regional preference marking. In particular, the committee should confirm whether gorod is the recommended Northern form or whether město is preferred for a West-Slavic-leaning Northern flavor.

Unverified Content This entire section on conjunction and discourse marker alternations (no/ali, esli/ako, kogda/kad, poka/dok, posle/nakon) is not present in the source material. These pairs may be plausible but cannot be verified against the source. Meaning Northern Southern but no / jednak ali / nego or ili ili / al' if esli / jesli ako when kogda kad / kada until poka dok after posle posle / nakon Regional reference for 'if': Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian jeśli якщо / якби ако jestliže / pokud ако

The following parallel texts illustrate lexical flavorization. The same propositional content is expressed in standard Interslavic, then in the Northern canonical model, then in the Southern canonical model. Lexically flavorized items are marked with square brackets and annotated below. Standard Interslavic: Člověk, ktory žije v velikom gorode, ne vsegda imaje čas dlja čitanja knih. Northern lexical flavorization: Člověk, ktory žije v velikom gorode, ne vsegda imaje čas dlja čitanja knih. Lexical choices (Northern): ktory [Northern relative pronoun; Southern alternative: koj] gorod [Northern word for city; Southern alternative: grad] vsegda [Northern word for always; Southern alternative: uvěk] dlja [Northern purposive preposition; Southern alternative: za] Southern lexical flavorization: Člověk, koj žije v velikom gradu, ne uvěk imaje čas za čitanje knih. Lexical choices (Southern): koj [Southern relative pronoun; Northern alternative: ktory] grad [Southern word for city; Northern alternative: gorod] uvěk [Southern word for always; Northern alternative: vsegda] za [Southern purposive preposition; Northern alternative: dlja] Observation: The Northern and Southern versions are mutually intelligible: all four substituted words are attested in the standard Interslavic dictionary, and any speaker of standard Interslavic can recover the meaning without difficulty. This illustrates the intelligibility threshold requirement of the relevant section TODO: Committee Input Needed The worked example above should be expanded to include at least one example that exercises conjunctions, verbal prefix alternations, and pronoun/determiner choices simultaneously. The committee should also specify whether annotated lexical flavorization examples should mark the standard form, the Northern form, and the Southern form in a three-column parallel format, or whether the current inline annotation style is preferred.

Morphological flavorization selects among documented inflectional and derivational variants of Interslavic to increase familiarity for speakers of a particular dialect area. Unlike phonological flavorization, which operates on sounds, morphological flavorization operates on grammatical forms: endings, pronouns, prefixes, and prepositions. All morphological variants specified in this chapter are optional. Standard Interslavic forms are always acceptable regardless of target audience. Unverified Content The MUST requirement for intelligibility and the cross-reference to are not stated in the source material. The source discusses intelligibility as a general goal but does not formalize it as a normative constraint with a section reference. A morphologically flavorized text MUST remain fully intelligible to any speaker of standard Interslavic .

Standard Interslavic uses the infinitive ending -ti, which is the form found across South Slavic, Czech, Slovak, and Ukrainian. Rule for Northern flavor: The infinitive ending SHOULD be shortened to -ť. Unverified Content The characterization of Belarusian as -ць and the claim about Polish/Sorbian "tendency toward reduced endings" are not in the source material. The source simply states that Northern infinitives end in -ť. This ending reflects the East Slavic pattern (Russian -ть, Belarusian -ць) and aligns with the Polish/Sorbian tendency toward reduced endings. Standard Northern Gloss byti byť to be govoriti govoriť to speak Unverified Content Internal inconsistency: the Northern form dělaľ does not follow the stated rule of replacing -ti with -ť. The expected form would be dělať. | dělati | dělaľ | to do | | pisati | pisať | to write | | znajti | znajť | to find | The shortened ending applies uniformly to all infinitives regardless of conjugation class. The internal stem, including any final consonant alternation, is unchanged. Flavor Infinitive marker Example Standard (default) -ti pisati Northern -ť pisať Regional reference: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian -ć / -c -ти - (no infinitive) -t -ти Note: Bulgarian and Macedonian speakers use analytical constructions rather than an infinitive . The Southern model retains -ti.

Standard Interslavic uses the neuter singular adjective ending -o (e.g., dobro), which is the South Slavic dominant form. Rule for Northern flavor: The neuter singular nominative/accusative adjectival ending SHOULD be changed to -e (e.g., dobre). Standard Northern Gloss dobro děte dobre detja a good child novo slovo nove slovo a new word staro město stare město an old city The ending change applies to all adjectival forms in the neuter nominative/accusative singular, including possessive, demonstrative, and interrogative pronouns used adjectivally. Regional reference: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian -e -е -о -é -о Unresolved: The Neuter -e/-o Tension The neuter singular ending -o vs. -e is one of the most visible North–South splits in the Slavic world. The standard Interslavic choice of -o (following the South Slavic and East Slavic pattern for most adjective classes) means that Polish, Czech, and Slovak speakers encounter an unfamiliar ending in high-frequency words like dobro (where they expect dobre, dobré, dobré). The reverse choice would alienate South Slavic and some East Slavic speakers. This tension extends beyond adjective endings to neuter noun forms and affects the overall "feel" of the language for different speaker groups. Some community members have proposed encoding both values with a special letter (analogous to the å compromise for the a/o alternation), which would allow each reader to interpret the vowel according to their own language's pattern. However, no concrete notation has been adopted, and the standard currently resolves the tension by choosing one form (-o) and documenting -e as a Northern flavorization option.

Interslavic provides two relative pronouns: ktory — primarily North Slavic in orientation (cf. Polish który, Russian который). koj — primarily South Slavic in orientation (cf. Serbian/Croatian koji, Macedonian кој). Both forms are standard and fully grammatical. Their choice is the primary relative-pronoun marker of Northern vs. Southern flavor. Rule for Northern flavor: ktory SHOULD be preferred. Rule for Southern flavor: koj SHOULD be preferred. Context Northern Southern Masc. sg. nom. ktory koj Fem. sg. nom. ktora koja Neut. sg. nom. ktore / ktoro koje / kojo Regional reference: Unverified Content The Bulgarian entry який appears incorrect — який is Ukrainian, not Bulgarian. Bulgarian uses който for the relative pronoun. Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian który котрий який který који

Interslavic provides two competing prefixes expressing outward direction or origin: vy- — North Slavic orientation (cf. Polish wy-, Russian вы-, Czech vy-). iz- — South Slavic orientation (cf. Serbian/Croatian iz-, Slovene iz-). Rule for Northern flavor: The prefix vy- SHOULD be used. Rule for Southern flavor: The prefix iz- SHOULD be used. Meaning Northern Southern to go out vyjti izjti to speak out vygovoriti izgovoriti to write out vypisati izpisati to learn vyučiti izučiti Both prefixes may co-occur in the standard dictionary as synonyms with the same meaning; the regional marking governs which is preferred in a given flavor register. Regional reference: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian wy- ви- из- vy- из-

The preposition 'for' with purposive meaning is expressed by two competing forms: dlja — North Slavic orientation (cf. Russian для, Polish dla, Ukrainian для). za — South Slavic orientation (cf. Serbian/Croatian za, Slovene za, Bulgarian за). Rule for Northern flavor: dlja SHOULD be used for purposive 'for'. Rule for Southern flavor: za SHOULD be used. Context Northern Southern Gloss for the people dlja ljudej za ljudej — for this reason dlja togo za to — for a long time dlja dolgogo vremene za dolgo vrěme — Note: za has other meanings in standard Interslavic (behind, after, during) that are unaffected by this flavorization rule. Only the purposive 'for' sense is subject to Northern/Southern alternation. Regional reference: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian dla для за pro / pro за

Unverified Content This entire section () on case ending variants has no basis in the source material. The source does not provide detailed case ending tables differentiated by Northern/Southern flavor. The specific feminine genitive singular Southern form -e, dative -i, instrumental -om, and locative -i variants, as well as the adjectival ending variants, cannot be verified against the source. Several case endings have documented Northern and Southern variants. The following table summarizes the principal morphological alternations across the major paradigm classes.

The following table shows the main variant endings. An asterisk marks the form recommended in the respective canonical model. Masculine/neuter nouns (hard stem), singular: Case Standard Northern Southern Gen. sg. -a -a -a Dat. sg. -u -u -u Instr. sg. -om -om -om Loc. sg. -u -u -u Masculine/neuter nouns (hard stem), plural: Case Standard Northern Southern Nom. pl. -i (masc.) / -a (neut.) -i / -a -i / -a Gen. pl. -ov -ov -ov Dat. pl. -am -am -am Instr. pl. -ami -ami -ami Loc. pl. -ah -ah -ah Feminine nouns (hard stem, -a class), singular: Case Standard Northern Southern Nom. sg. -a -a -a Gen. sg. -y -y -e Dat. sg. -ě -ě -i Acc. sg. -u -u -u Instr. sg. -oju -oju -om Loc. sg. -ě -ě -i Regional reference for genitive singular feminine: Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian -y / -i -и -а (analytic) -y -е

Full adjectival declension variants are documented in the paradigm tables . The key flavor-sensitive endings are: Form Standard Northern Southern Masc. sg. nom. -y -y / -i -i Neut. sg. nom./acc. -o -e -o Fem. sg. gen. -oj -oj -e TODO: Committee Input Needed A comprehensive comparison table of all case endings across Northern and Southern paradigms has not yet been finalized. The committee should specify whether the standard paradigm tables should include a dedicated flavor column or whether a separate summary table should appear in the relevant section

Unverified Content The source material only briefly mentions "avoiding the use of infinitives and noun cases when addressing Bulgarians" as an example of grammatical-level flavourisation. The detailed rules in this section — the da + finite verb substitution patterns, the constraint on accusative retention, the prohibition on postposed articles, and the MUST/MUST NOT normative requirements — are elaborations not present in the source. Bulgarian and Macedonian have eliminated the inherited Slavic case system and the infinitive, replacing them with analytical constructions. Standard Interslavic uses full case morphology and the infinitive; these are obligatory in the standard, and MUST be used unless a Bulgarian/Macedonian-specific accommodation is explicitly applied. Accommodation rule (Bulgarian/Macedonian flavor): Infinitive: Replace infinitive constructions with a finite verb introduced by the particle da + present tense. Standard Bulgarian/Macedonian flavor Gloss Hoču pisati. Hoču da pišu. I want to write. Trěba govoriti. Trěba da govoriš. You need to speak. Mogu čitati. Mogu da čitam. I can read. Case morphology: Where context permits, analytical prepositional constructions MAY substitute for synthetic case endings. For the accusative (direct object), no preposition is available in Interslavic; the accusative form is retained even in Bulgarian/Macedonian flavor. Definiteness: Bulgarian and Macedonian mark definiteness by a postposed article. Interslavic has no postposed article; this feature MUST NOT be introduced into flavorized Interslavic. Definiteness, where necessary, MAY be indicated by a demonstrative pronoun in prenominal position. Critical constraint: This accommodation is intended strictly for texts directed at Bulgarian or Macedonian speakers who lack familiarity with case morphology. It MUST NOT be applied as a general simplification in other contexts; doing so would reduce the morphological integrity of the text without communicative justification. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should specify: (a) the complete inventory of constructions for which a da + finite verb substitute is permitted; (b) whether any Interslavic vocabulary items borrowed from Bulgarian/Macedonian analytical expressions should be documented; (c) how Bulgarian/Macedonian flavor interacts with the standard Northern/Southern canonical models.
documents.s: Avoiding Infinitives and Cases: Bulgarian/Macedonian Flavor

documents.t: Unverified Content These worked examples are not present in the source material. While the individual rules they demonstrate (infinitive shortening, prefix alternation, dlja/za substitution) are sourced, the specific sentences and especially the Bulgarian/Macedonian accommodation example (with da piše, može da) are original compositions that cannot be verified against the source. The following parallel texts illustrate morphological flavorization. The base sentence is given in standard Interslavic; Northern and Southern variants follow with individual changes annotated. Base sentence (standard Interslavic): Dobry člověk hoče pisati dlǎgu knigu, da by ju izučiti. Northern morphological flavorization: Dobry člověk hoče pisať dljagu knigu, da by ju vyučiť. Changes applied: Infinitive -ti → -ť: pisati → pisať; izučiti → vyučiť Prefix iz- → vy-: izučiti → vyučiť Preposition dlja retained (Northern) Southern morphological flavorization: Dobri člověk hoče pisati za dugu knigu, da by ju izučiti. Changes applied: Neuter/masc. adjectival ending: dobry → dobri (South Slavic preference) Preposition dlja → za (Southern) Prefix iz- retained (Southern) Infinitive -ti retained (Southern) Bulgarian/Macedonian accommodation: Dobri člověk hoče da piše za duga kniga, da može da ja izuči. Changes applied: Infinitive replaced by da + finite present: pisati → da piše Infinitive replaced by da + finite present: izučiti → da izuči da by modal replaced by može da (analytic modal) TODO: Committee Input Needed The worked examples above should be expanded with a longer text exercising all documented morphological rules simultaneously. The committee should confirm the canonical annotation format — inline glosses, footnote references, or diff tables — and validate each example against current paradigm specifications.

Orthographic flavorization adapts the written form of an Interslavic text to resemble the conventions of a particular national orthography. It operates independently of phonological, morphological, and lexical flavorization: a text may be orthographically flavorized without any phonological or morphological changes, and vice versa. Orthographic flavorization is governed by the following normative constraint: every orthographic variant MUST map onto standard Interslavic by a rule-governed, one-to-one or context-conditioned correspondence. The mapping MUST be recoverable by any reader of standard Interslavic . Orthographic conventions that introduce ambiguity — where the standard form cannot be reliably recovered — are not orthographic flavorization; they are non-standard spellings. The standard Interslavic Latin alphabet is based primarily on the Slovene orthography, with additions from West Slavic (y) and Czech/Sorbian (ě). The standard Interslavic Cyrillic alphabet is based on Serbian/Macedonian Cyrillic, with additions from Russian/Belarusian (ы) and Ukrainian (є). Both standard alphabets are specified in the relevant section (Latin) and (Cyrillic).

Polish speakers may find the standard Interslavic Latin alphabet unfamiliar because Polish does not use č, š, ž, ě, or v in the same way. The Polish-based orthographic flavorization replaces these characters with their Polish equivalents or nearest analogues. Systematic substitutions: Standard Latin Polish-based Latin Polish analogy Notes v w Polish w Polish has no v č cz Polish cz digraph for [tʃ] š sz Polish sz digraph for [ʃ] ž ż Polish ż or rz in some positions ě ie Polish ie reflex of Proto-Slavic ě j j unchanged Polish j is [j] lj l Polish l (soft) see the relevant section nj ń Polish ń č cluster cz — Etymological ę ię Polish ię from etymological alphabet Etymological ų ą Polish ą from etymological alphabet Complete character substitution table (Latin standard → Polish-based): Standard Polish-based Standard Polish-based a a p p b b r r c c s s č cz š sz d d t t dž dż u u e e v w ě ie y y f f z z g g ž ż h h lj l (soft) i i nj ń j j ę (etym.) ię k k ų (etym.) ą l ł å (etym.) o m m ȯ (etym.) o n n ć (etym.) ć o o đ (etym.) dź Example: Standard Latin Polish-based Latin Medžuslovjansky jest orudje dlja komunikacije s Slovjanami. Miedżusłowiański jest orudie dla komunikacije s Słowianami. Učenje ne jest težko i ne trovaje dolgo. Uczenie nie jest tiażko i nie trowaje dołgo. Regional reference (characters used in each language): Standard form Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian v w в в v v č cz ч ч č č š sz ш ш š š ž ż/rz ж ж ž ž ě ie i / є — e / ě e / ije

Czech and Slovak readers are generally comfortable with the standard Interslavic Latin alphabet, since both languages use diacritics in a similar fashion. Minimal adjustments suffice to increase familiarity. Systematic substitutions: Standard Latin Czech/Slovak-based Notes y y retained; Czech/Slovak distinguish y and i ě ě Czech uses ě; Slovak uses ie — author may choose lj l assimilated as in Czech standard nj ň Slovak/Czech ň for [nʲ] dž dž retained šč šč → šť West Slavic flavor; see also g h optional; reflects Czech/Slovak g→h shift Example: Standard Latin Czech/Slovak-based Latin Medžuslovjansky jest orudje dlja komunikacije s Slovjanami. Medžuslovjansky jest orudje dlja komunikacije s Slovjanami. For Czech audiences, the standard Latin alphabet requires minimal change; the primary orthographic adaptation is the optional g → h substitution and the treatment of nj as ň. Regional reference: Standard form Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian nj ni нь н ň nj lj li ль л l lj g g г [ɦ] г h [ɦ] g

The Croatian and Slovene orthographic systems are structurally close to the standard Interslavic Latin alphabet, which was partly modeled on them. Orthographic flavorization for Croatian/Slovene readers is therefore minimal. Systematic substitutions: Standard Latin Croatian/Slovene-based Notes y i South Slavic merger of y and i ě je / ije Croatian reflex ; Slovene uses e šč št Southern cluster reduction ć / đ ć / đ retained where etymologically justified lj lj retained; Croatian/Slovene standard nj nj retained; Croatian/Slovene standard Example: Standard Latin Croatian-based Latin Medžuslovjansky jest orudje dlja komunikacije s Slovjanami. Međuslovjanski jest orudje dlja komunikacije s Slovjanami. Učenje ne jest težko i ne trovaje dolgo. Učenje ne jest težko i ne trăvaje dălgo. Regional reference: Standard form Polish Ukrainian Bulgarian Czech Serbian / Croatian y y и и y i šč szcz щ щ (= št) šč šт ě ie і — e ije / e

The standard Interslavic Cyrillic is based on Serbian/Macedonian Cyrillic. It includes ы (from Russian/Belarusian) and є (from Ukrainian). The letters ј, љ, њ follow Serbian conventions. Two main Cyrillic orthographic flavor models are documented: East Slavic and South Slavic (Serbian/Macedonian).

East Slavic readers — particularly Russians — may not correctly recognize the Serbian Cyrillic letter ј, which they associate with the English sound [dʒ] rather than [j]. Similarly, љ and њ are unfamiliar to East Slavic readers.
