documents.t: The selection procedure in the relevant section applies across the entire lexicon, but priority is given to high-frequency concepts. The Interslavic lexicon is organized into three frequency tiers: Tier Description Target coverage Core Basic vocabulary needed for everyday communication ~2 000 entries Extended General vocabulary for written and formal registers ~10 000 entries Specialized Technical, scientific, and domain-specific vocabulary Unlimited Core-tier entries MUST be fully resolved using the voting procedure before any extended-tier entry for the same semantic field is accepted. Extended-tier entries SHOULD follow the same procedure. Specialized-tier entries MAY rely on international vocabulary or approved calques when no broadly understood Slavic root exists. TODO: Committee Input Needed The exact size thresholds for core and extended tiers are provisional. The committee should confirm or revise these figures based on the current state of the dictionary.

Two synonyms for the same concept MAY coexist in the Interslavic dictionary when they satisfy the following conditions: The two forms cover complementary portions of the Slavic territory (e.g., one is predominantly North Slavic, the other predominantly South Slavic). Both forms pass the voting threshold individually when assessed against their respective regional subgroups. Neither form is a high-risk false cognate of the other . When synonyms are listed, the dictionary entry MUST include a regional label (North, South, East, West) and, where relevant, a register label (formal, colloquial, archaic). Authors and translators SHOULD select the synonym that aligns best with their target audience; using either synonym is correct from the perspective of the standard. A third synonym MUST NOT be introduced unless the first two have been in active use and attested in independent texts.

International vocabulary — primarily words of Greek and Latin origin that have entered most European languages — constitutes an integral part of every Slavic language and provides a reliable channel for expressing concepts that lack a broadly recognized Slavic root. International vocabulary MUST be adopted into Interslavic only when the majority of Slavic languages (at least four of the six subgroups) already use a recognizably related form for that concept. If the international word is recognized by all six subgroups, adoption is REQUIRED. If recognized by fewer than four, a Slavic alternative MUST be sought first. Where an internationally recognized Slavic word exists — for example vampir "vampire" or vodka "vodka" — it is treated as international vocabulary already and requires no further justification for adoption.

When adapting an international word, the spelling MUST be brought as close as possible to the form of the original word while conforming to the following rules: Source feature Interslavic adaptation Example Geminated consonant Simplified to single consonant gramma → gram Greek θ (th) Written t theatro → teatr Greek φ (ph) Written f phenomenon → fenomen Greek υ (y) Written i systema → sistem /k/ sound Always written k, never c contact → kontakt /x/ sound (ch) Written h psychology → psiholog /y/ sound (ü, u) Written ju bureau → bjuro Intervocalic /z/ (-s-) Written z base → baza Intervocalic /s/ (-ss-) Written s mass → masa

The seven rules below specify how the most frequent Latin and Greek suffixes MUST be rendered in Interslavic. They supplement the general phonological table in the relevant section and apply in the order listed; the first matching rule wins. Rule 1 — Nominal endings -ια / -ia and -η / -ις Source Interslavic ending Examples Greek -ια or Latin -ia -ija filozofija, astronomija Greek -η or -ις -a baza Rule 2 — Adjectival ending -κος / -cus Greek adjectives ending in -κος and Latin adjectives ending in -cus MUST receive the palatalized Interslavic suffix -sky. When the stem-final consonant is k, it undergoes the regular Interslavic alternation k → č before this suffix. Source Interslavic ending Examples Greek -κος / Latin -cus -sky filosofijsky; ekonomičsky (k → č) Rule 3 — Verbal-noun endings -sio, -ssio, -tio / -tia Source Interslavic ending Examples Latin -sio -zija televizija Latin -ssio -sija diskusija Latin -tio / -tia -cija gravitacija Rule 4 — Intervocalic -s- and -ss-; suffix -σμος / -smus Source Interslavic rendering Examples Latin or Greek -s- between vowels -z- vaza, baza, fizika Latin or Greek -ss- -s- masiv Greek -σμος or Latin -smus -zm kapitalizm Rule 5 — Nominal endings -o(ν) / -um and -ος / -us Source Interslavic ending Examples Greek -ο(ν) or Latin -um -um forum Greek -ος or Latin -us -us or -os korpus; kosmos Note the following lexicalized exceptions: museum → muzej (m. inanim., Gen. muzeja); theatrum → theatro (n., Gen. theatra). Rule 6 — Adjectival ending -στικος / -sticus Greek adjectives ending in -στικος and Latin adjectives ending in -sticus MUST receive the Interslavic suffix -stičsky. Source Interslavic ending Example Greek -στικος / Latin -sticus -stičsky kapitalističsky Rule 7 — Agentive / participial ending -stus / -στος Latin or Greek -stus / -στος MUST be reduced to -st. Source Interslavic ending Example Latin -stus / Greek -στος -st kapitalist
documents.s: Latin and Greek Suffix Adaptation Rules (Merunka)

documents.t: In addition to the phonological rules in the relevant sectiona, international words MUST receive Interslavic inflectional endings in accordance with the following table. The table maps the most common source-language endings (English forms are given as the primary reference) to their Interslavic counterparts. Verbs: Source ending Interslavic ending Example -ate, -fy, -ise/-ize, Ger. -ieren -ovati organizovati Nouns: Source ending Interslavic ending Example -ia, -y (Lat. -ia) -ija ekonomija -sis (Gr.) -za kriza -ium -ij helij -um, -us retained forum; korpus -ty (Lat. -tas) -tet universitet -ics -ika ekonomika -ism -izm komunizm -ist -ist komunist -sion -zija televizija -ssion -sija diskusija -tion -cija akcija Adjectives: Source ending Interslavic ending Example Derived from -tion/-sion nouns -ijny televizijny -al (Lat. -alis) -alny neutralny -ic, -ical (Lat. -icus) -ičny specifičny -ive (Lat. -ivus) -ivny pozitivny -ous (Lat. -osus) -ozny seriozny

Words borrowed from languages that use the Latin alphabet (but are not of Latin or Greek origin) MUST retain their original Latin-script spelling and pronunciation. However, they MUST acquire the grammatical categories — gender, case declension paradigm, and number — appropriate to Interslavic. The following examples illustrate the principle (Nom. sg. → Gen. sg. → gender): Source form Interslavic Nom. / Gen. Gender Source language metro metro → metr-a n. French laser laser → laser-a m. English bypass bypass → bypass-a m. English Note: When producing text in Cyrillic, or in any mixed-script context, these loanwords SHOULD be rendered phonetically in Cyrillic in accordance with the general phonetic spelling policy in the relevant section

English loanwords present a special challenge because the English spelling system diverges significantly from the phonological values expected by Cyrillic or Interslavic Latin readers. The following rule applies: When writing in Cyrillic, or when producing a text that will be read in both scripts, English loanwords MUST be spelled phonetically in Interslavic rather than preserving the English orthographic form. English form Interslavic phonetic form bypass bajpas knockout nokaut jazz džaz teenager tinejdžer budget budžet business biznes musical mjuzikl football futbol cocktail koktejl When writing exclusively in the Latin script for an audience familiar with English, the original English spelling MAY be retained in parentheses as a gloss on first use.

Diminutives are common to all Slavic languages. They express that a person or thing denoted by a noun is smaller or younger than the base referent, or that the speaker feels a greater degree of closeness or affection toward it. Interslavic productive diminutive suffixes are drawn from the common Slavic stock and MUST follow the patterns below. Each gender has two grades: a simple diminutive (one suffix) and a double diminutive (intensified, indicating something very small/young or expressing strong affection). Masculine nouns: Grade Suffix Example Gloss Base — slon elephant Simple diminutive -ek slonek small/young elephant Double diminutive -iček sloničеk very small elephant; affectionate Feminine nouns: Grade Suffix Example Gloss Base — krava cow Simple diminutive -ka kravka small/young cow Double diminutive -ička kravička very small cow; affectionate Neuter nouns: Grade Suffix Example Gloss Base — děte child Simple diminutive -ko děko small/young child Double diminutive -ičko dětičko very small child; affectionate Note: for neuter stems belonging to the -T declension (e.g., tele "calf"), the oblique stem in -et- is used before the diminutive suffix: tele → teletko (Gen. teletka). See the relevant section (declension) for the -T paradigm. Note: some masculine nouns form their simple diminutive with the neuter-type suffix -ko, in which case they retain masculine gender but follow the sělo declension in the singular: e.g., brat "brother" → bratko (m., Gen. sg. bratka, declined as sělo). Cross-reference: the derivational affixes -ica (feminine agent/diminutive) and -ec (masculine agent/diminutive, with loss of thematic -e-) are covered in the morphology section and are distinct from the productive diminutive paradigm described here.

A false cognate (also: false friend; lažny drug) is a word whose phonological or orthographic form is similar — or identical — across two or more Slavic languages but whose meaning diverges between them, such that a reader familiar with form A in language X may incorrectly assume meaning B when encountering the same form in Interslavic or language Y. False cognates are classified by risk level: Risk level Criterion High The form is likely to be used in the same or a closely similar syntactic context in both languages; the meaning difference is substantial and pragmatically consequential. Medium The meaning difference is noticeable but the contexts in which the word is used differ enough that misunderstanding is less likely. Low The form differs in one language only marginally or belongs to a different word category; misunderstanding is unlikely in context. Only high-risk and medium-risk false cognates are subject to the mandatory dictionary warning in the relevant section

The following table documents the most consequential false cognate pairs across Slavic languages. This list is not exhaustive; it represents attested high-risk cases that have caused or are likely to cause pragmatic misunderstanding. Interslavic form Meaning assigned Divergent meaning(s) Language(s) duma "thought, reflection" "pride" Polish listopad "falling of leaves; October" "November" Polish, Czech uroda "harvest; produce" "beauty" Polish; "ugly person, freak" zbožje "grain, cereal" "goods, merchandise" some Slavic varieties pozor "attention, care" "disgrace, shame" Russian, Bulgarian zavisnost "dependence" "envy" some South Slavic varieties TODO: Committee Input Needed The false cognate register requires systematic expansion. The committee should commission a full cross-linguistic audit against the Interslavic core vocabulary to produce a complete high-risk and medium-risk list before this section is finalized.

For every high-risk false cognate that enters the Interslavic lexicon, the dictionary entry MUST include: A warning marker (e.g., ⚠ false friend) visible in both the printed and digital dictionary. A disambiguation note specifying in which source language(s) the form carries a different meaning, and what that meaning is. Where a broadly understood alternative exists, a recommended alternative clearly labeled as the preferred form for general use. Authors MUST NOT use a high-risk false cognate word in Interslavic texts without either using the recommended alternative or adding a contextual clarification. Editors reviewing Interslavic texts SHOULD flag high-risk false cognates for author attention. Slight divergences in meaning — where the Interslavic sense and the divergent sense belong to the same semantic area and misunderstanding is unlikely in context — do not require a warning. For example, the fact that ljubiti means "to love" in most Slavic languages but "to like" in Polish is a low-risk divergence that does not warrant a mandatory warning.

When a new concept must be expressed and no existing Interslavic word covers it adequately, the following preference order MUST be applied: Existing Slavic word or root compound: An attested Slavic word or a compound built from Slavic roots that transparently expresses the concept. This is always the first choice. Semantic extension of an existing word: Extending the meaning of an existing Interslavic word by regular metonymic or metaphorical shift, provided this does not create a false cognate. Calque (loan translation): A morpheme-by-morpheme translation of an international term into Interslavic roots, following the formation rules in the relevant section Adapted international borrowing: An international (Greco-Latin or widely recognized) term adapted according to – Phonetic borrowing: A word borrowed from a non-Slavic language (including English) adapted phonetically per , used only when no option above is feasible. Each step in this hierarchy MUST be explicitly considered and documented in the lexicon proposal before descending to the next level.

Calques MUST be formed in accordance with the following rules: Every morpheme in the calque MUST be either an attested Interslavic root or a productive Interslavic affix (see the relevant section). The compound MUST follow Interslavic compounding conventions: the first element is the modifying element, the second is the head. The resulting form MUST be phonotactically well-formed in Interslavic. The calque MUST be formally distinct from all existing Interslavic words. Examples of approved calques: International term Interslavic calque Gloss of components skyscraper nebodrap nebo "sky" + drapati "to scratch" earthquake zemjetrěs zemlja "earth" + trěsti "to shake" wheelchair kolokrěslo kolo "wheel" + krěslo "armchair" If two competing calques are both well-formed, the one whose components are more broadly attested across Slavic languages MUST be preferred.

A proposed neologism MUST satisfy all of the following formal conditions before it can be submitted for review: The proposed form MUST conform to Interslavic phonotactic rules as specified in the relevant section The proposed form MUST use only attested Slavic roots (traceable to Proto-Slavic), approved international vocabulary , or productive Interslavic affixes . The proposed form MUST be formally distinct from all existing Interslavic lexical entries: no homophony with an established word is permitted unless the difference in word class makes disambiguation reliable in context. If the proposed form contains a sequence not found in any living Slavic language, a phonological justification MUST be provided. Proposals that fail any of these conditions are inadmissible and MUST be returned to the proposer for revision before entering committee review.

A proposed neologism MUST fill a genuine lexical gap in Interslavic: the concept it expresses MUST NOT be adequately covered by any existing Interslavic word or phrase. Synonymy with an existing standard word disqualifies the proposal unless one of the following justifications is provided and accepted by the committee: Register differentiation: The proposed word occupies a register (e.g., highly formal, colloquial, poetic) clearly distinct from the existing word. Regional differentiation: The proposed word is more broadly understood in a geographic subgroup of the Slavic speech area than the existing word, and the existing word functions as the synonym for the complementary subgroup (see the relevant section). In the case of portmanteau words — forms that blend phonological material from multiple Slavic cognates to maximize pan-Slavic recognizability — the proposer MUST demonstrate that the blend is more readily understood by Slavic speakers than either of the contributing words alone. Examples include katka "duck" (from Russian utka, Polish kaczka, South Slavic patka).

The approval of a neologism follows a five-stage procedure: Stage Description Duration Proposal The proposer submits the form, its derivation, its intended meaning, and evidence of the lexical gap. — Discussion Open community discussion; counterproposals and objections are recorded. Minimum 30 days Voting Committee vote; simple majority required for provisional adoption. As scheduled Provisional adoption The word is marked as provisional (prov.) in the dictionary and may be used in Interslavic texts. Minimum 12 months Full adoption Confirmed after attestation in at least three independent published texts; the provisional marker is removed. After attestation A neologism that fails the vote MAY be resubmitted once if the proposer addresses the objections raised during the discussion period. A neologism that fails a second vote MUST NOT be resubmitted for a period of five years. The committee should confirm these thresholds or replace them with figures derived from the actual community review track record.

The following checklist provides a step-by-step procedure for adapting any international word to Interslavic orthography and morphology. Cross-references to –a are provided at each relevant step. Step Action Rule reference 1 Verify that the word is recognized in at least 4 of the 6 subgroups. 2 Apply general phonological adaptations: simplify geminates, adapt θ/φ/υ/k/ch/ü/intervocalic-s. 2a If the word is of Latin or Greek origin, apply the seven suffix-specific adaptation rules in the listed order (Rules 1–7). a 3 Assign the correct morphological ending based on the source-language suffix. 3a If the word is not of Latin or Greek origin but uses Latin script, retain the original spelling and assign Interslavic grammatical categories (gender, declension). a 4 If the word is of English origin and will appear in Cyrillic, apply phonetic spelling. 5 Check the result against the false cognate register. 6 Confirm that the adapted form is formally distinct from all existing Interslavic entries. 7 Record the etymology, source form, and adaptation decisions in the dictionary entry. — 8 If the word fails step 1, proceed to the calque/borrowing preference order. A word MUST NOT be entered into the dictionary without completing all applicable steps in this checklist.

Interslavic morphology is founded on the common grammatical categories shared by the Slavic language family: grammatical gender, number, case, verbal aspect, tense, mood, and person. The forms prescribed in this chapter are based on patterns attested in the majority of Slavic languages; where variation exists, the most broadly recognizable solution is selected. The result is a system that is both internally regular and immediately familiar to speakers of any Slavic language. This chapter covers all inflectional and derivational morphology. Sections through 5.9 treat inflectional categories for each part of speech in turn. Section covers derivational morphology, i.e., the formation of new lexemes by means of productive affixes. Unresolved: Register Stratification --- Basic, Standard, and Literary/Rich Interslavic The morphological system documented in this chapter represents Standard Interslavic --- the default register intended for broad pan-Slavic communication. However, an extended literary/rich register ("bogata forma") has been described by Roberto Lombino (2022), which proposes optional enrichments drawing on features attested in natural Slavic languages but excluded from the standard for reasons of simplicity. The core tension is between communicative simplicity (maximizing intelligibility across the Slavic world by minimizing the forms a speaker must know) and expressive completeness (providing the full range of morphological distinctions that the Common Slavic heritage makes available). The literary register adds: Phonology: Second palatalization in declension (k>c, g>dz before -i/-ě), distinction between inherently soft and secondarily softened consonants, optional nasal pronunciation of ę/:isv[ų] Nominal morphology: Additional case endings (-ove/-eve, -ovi/-evi, -om/-eh), definite/indefinite adjective declension with a postpositive article, supplementary cases (ablative, inessive, allative, partitive), fully productive dual number Verbal morphology: Distinct aorist and imperfect, subjunctive/conjunctive mood, supine, multiple future constructions (with htěti, imati, idti), gender-differentiated plural L-participles, full clitic system for byti Comparison: Alternative comparative formation through iotation or -ěj- suffix Pronouns: Relative pronoun iže (distinct from interrogative-based relatives), clitic vs. full forms of personal pronouns, inanimate masculine accusative jen Each of these features is documented in admonition blocks within the relevant subsections. They are marked as optional extensions; none is required for Standard Interslavic. The literary register is intended for literary prose, poetry (where additional endings provide rhyming options), and contexts where writers wish to showcase the breadth of the Interslavic system. No committee decision has been made on whether these features should be formally codified as a recognized "Literary Interslavic" register or remain informal proposals.

Interslavic words are analyzed in terms of five morpheme categories: Root — the irreducible semantic core of a word, shared by all members of a lexical family. For example, the root del- underlies dělati 'to do', dělanje 'doing', and dělnik 'worker'. Prefix — a bound morpheme attached before the root, typically modifying aspect or spatial meaning in verbs, and semantic scope in nouns and adjectives. Examples: na- in napisati 'to write (pf.)', bez- in bezradostny 'joyless'. Suffix — a bound morpheme attached after the root or stem, used both inflectionally and derivationally. Derivational suffixes form new lexemes (-nik in dělnik); inflectional suffixes express grammatical values (-ov- in del-ov-ogo). Interfix (linking element) — a connecting morpheme used in compound words. The vowel -o- is the default linking element after a hard consonant; -e- is used after a soft consonant. Examples: rukopis 'manuscript' (< ruka + -o- + pisati), zemjepis 'geography' (< zemja + -e- + pisati). Ending (inflectional suffix) — the final morpheme in an inflected word form, expressing grammatical categories such as case, number, gender, tense, and person. The ending is the primary domain of inflectional morphology; alternations in the stem (see the relevant section) may accompany changes of ending. Every inflected word form in Interslavic can be segmented as: [ prefix(es) ] + root + [ derivational suffix(es) ] + inflectional ending

Inflectional morphology produces different grammatical forms of the same lexeme without changing its core lexical identity or part of speech. Nominal inflection varies the form of nouns, adjectives, pronouns, and numerals across case, number, and (for adjectives) gender. Verbal inflection varies the form of verbs across tense, mood, aspect, person, number, and gender of the L-participle. Inflectional morphology is treated in Sections through 5.9. Derivational morphology forms a new lexeme — typically of a different part of speech or with a distinct lexical meaning — from an existing base. For example, the noun dělnik 'worker' is derived from the verb dělati 'to do' by means of the agentive suffix -nik. Derivational morphology is treated in Section The boundary between inflection and derivation is drawn at the lexeme: any process that produces a form that would receive a separate dictionary entry is classified as derivational. Aspect pairing of verbs occupies a special position: perfective/imperfective pairs are treated as distinct but related lexemes, and their relationship is documented under verbal morphology.

Morphological processes in Interslavic are classified along a productivity scale: Fully productive — the process applies freely to all bases of the relevant formal class, including newly coined and borrowed items. Fully productive affixes SHOULD be used when forming new words. Examples: the verbal noun suffix -nje/-tje ; the adverb-forming suffix -o/-e from adjectives ; the comparative prefix naj- . Semi-productive — the process applies to a definable subclass of bases but does not extend freely to all formal members of that class. Semi-productive affixes MAY be used when the base belongs to the specified subclass. Example: the agentive suffix -tel , which is semi-productive with verbal bases denoting controlled activity. Lexicalized (frozen) — the process is no longer synchronically active; the morphological structure is recoverable by analysis but the form is stored as a whole in the lexicon. Lexicalized patterns are described for transparency only and MUST NOT be treated as productive rules. Example: certain frozen prefix-root combinations in which the prefix no longer contributes its canonical spatial meaning. The productivity classification of each affix is stated explicitly in Section Where an affix is marked as lexicalized, its section serves an informative purpose only.

The following sections cover inflectional morphology for each part of speech: Section Part of speech Key categories 5.2 Nouns gender, number, case (7), declension class 5.3 Adjectives gender, number, case, degree of comparison 5.4 Adverbs degree of comparison 5.5 Pronouns person, gender, number, case, reflexivity 5.6 Numerals case, numeral-noun agreement 5.7 Verbs aspect, conjugation class, tense, mood, non-finite forms 5.8 Prepositions case government 5.9 Conjunctions and particles clitic placement Prepositions and conjunctions and particles are uninflected; their morphological treatment concerns case government and clitic behavior rather than paradigmatic variation.

Section specifies productive affixes organized by the part of speech of the derived output: **** — Noun-forming suffixes: agent nouns (-nik, -tel, -ec, -ač), abstract nouns (-ost, -stvo, -nje/-tje, -ina), collectives, diminutives, augmentatives, feminine person nouns, and inhabitant/nationality nouns. **** — Adjective-forming suffixes: relational (-ny, -sky), possessive (-ov, -in), and material (-any, -eny). **** — Verb-forming processes: denominative verbs (-iti, -ovati), deadjectival verbs, and prefix-based aspect formation. **** — Prefixes: the full inventory of native Slavic prefixes with their productive meanings and aspect effects.

This section specifies the inflection, short forms, possessive formation, and degrees of comparison of adjectives in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Adjectives in Interslavic MUST agree with the head noun they modify in gender, number, and case. This requirement applies both in attributive position (adjective preceding or following the noun) and in predicative position (adjective linked to the noun by a copula). Adjectival declension is entirely regular. Three systematic rules govern all paradigms: Hard vs. soft stem alternations. Adjective stems ending in š, ž, č, or j are soft stems. After soft stems, every -o- becomes -e- and every -y- becomes -i- in the endings (see the relevant section). Animate masculine accusative. The accusative of an adjective modifying an animate masculine noun MUST be identical to the genitive, both in the singular and in the plural. For inanimate masculines and for all other genders the accusative equals the nominative (singular and plural). Nominative–accusative syncretism in the plural. Except when modifying animate masculine nouns, the nominative plural and the accusative plural are identical across all genders.

The model adjective is dobry "good". Endings after hard stems are shown without parentheses; where a cell is shared across genders, a single form is given. Soft adjectives have stems ending in š, ž, č, or j. The alternations -o → -e and -y → -i apply systematically throughout. The model adjective is svěži "fresh".
