Unresolved: Definite vs. Indefinite Adjective Declension Lombino (2022) proposes a fully productive distinction between definite (determined) and indefinite (undetermined) adjective forms, modeled on Old Church Slavonic and modern Bulgarian. In this system: Indefinite forms (short forms) are fully declined as nouns: mlad, gen. mlada, dat. mladu, etc., following the corresponding noun declension pattern. They are used predicatively and with indefinite nouns. Definite forms (long forms) carry the standard pronominal endings: mladyj, mladaja, mladoje, etc. They are used when the noun is contextually definite. Additionally, this system includes a postpositive definite article suffixed with -t- to the noun itself (e.g., bratrŭt "the brother", zemjata "the earth", poljeto "the field"), following the Bulgarian pattern. The article is used only when the noun has no other determiner (adjective, possessive, or demonstrative). Standard Interslavic preserves only a vestige of this system: the short form in the masculine nominative singular. The question of whether to document the full definite/indefinite distinction as an optional literary feature remains open. The system would increase expressive precision but at a significant cost to learnability, and it is productive only in Bulgarian, Macedonian, and (in part) Old Church Slavonic among Slavic languages.

Beyond the definite/indefinite question discussed above, a separate tension exists regarding the productive grammatical function of long vs. short adjective forms in East Slavic languages. In Russian, the distinction between long forms (добрый, добрая, доброе) and short forms (добр, добра, добро) is not merely stylistic — it is syntactically productive: Short forms are obligatory in certain predicative constructions (e.g., Он болен "He is ill" vs. больной человек "a sick person"). Short forms can carry different semantic nuances from long forms (e.g., Он хороший "He is a good person [general quality]" vs. Он хорош "He looks good [at this moment]"). Ukrainian and Belarusian each preserve elements of this distinction, though with different distributions: Ukrainian tends to use short forms predicatively while Belarusian shows a mixed pattern. Standard Interslavic uses long adjective endings (-y, -a, -o in the nominative) as the default in all syntactic positions, with the short form available only as a stylistic option in the masculine nominative singular. The full paradigm of short-form endings across all genders and numbers — which would be needed for a productive short/long distinction — is not part of the standard grammar. Unresolved: Productive Short Adjective Forms for Eastern Flavorization East Slavic languages maintain a grammatically productive distinction between long and short adjective forms across all genders, with the short forms obligatory in certain predicative constructions. Interslavic currently treats the short form as vestigial (masculine nominative singular only). Formalizing a full set of short-form predicative adjective endings (-Ø masc., -a fem., -o neut., -y pl.) as an Eastern flavorization option would bring the language closer to East Slavic grammatical expectations, but would add a layer of complexity that most West and South Slavic speakers would find unfamiliar. The scientific notation has explored marking this distinction with special vowel diacritics on adjective endings (long -yj/-ij/-aja/-oje vs. short -y/-i/-a/-o), but no consensus has been reached on whether this should enter any practical register of the language.
documents.s: The East Slavic Long/Short Adjective Distinction

documents.t: Interslavic possesses a vestigial short form of adjectives, a relic of the indefinite (non-pronominal) declension inherited from Common Slavic. The short form differs from the regular (long) form only in the masculine nominative singular, where the ending -y / -i is omitted, leaving a bare stem. Short forms do not constitute a separate declension class; all oblique forms of short-form adjectives are identical to those of the corresponding long-form adjective. The short form MUST NOT be confused with adverbs, which are also derived from the bare stem (see the relevant section). The short form occurs productively in two classes of adjective: Possessive adjectives in -ov and -in : the masculine nominative singular is zero-ended — bratov, mamin — while all other forms add regular long endings. A small number of other adjectives where tradition preserves the short masculine nominative, e.g. velik alongside veliky.

The short form MAY be used when the adjective functions as a predicate nominal (i.e., after a copula such as jest "is"): Petr jest ščestliv. — "Peter is happy." Dom jest velik. — "The house is big." Use of the long form in predicative position is equally acceptable: Petr jest ščestlivy. — "Peter is happy." Dom jest veliky. — "The house is big." Neither form is obligatory in predicative position; the long form is more common in contemporary usage.

Possessive adjectives express that an object belongs to or is associated with a specific person or animal. They are formed from nouns as follows: Unverified Content The source material only mentions -ov and -in as possessive suffixes. The distinction of -ev for masculine soft stems and the separate feminine soft-stem row (knęgynja → knęgynjin) are not attested in the source. Also, the -ev suffix listed for krålj is inconsistent with the example form kråljov, which uses -ov. Possessor noun type Suffix Example Masculine (hard stem) -ov brat "brother" → bratov Masculine (soft stem) -ev krålj "king" → kråljov Feminine -in mama "mother" → mamin Feminine (soft stem) -in knęgynja "princess" → knęgynjin The masculine nominative singular takes the short form (zero ending): bratov, mamin. All other case forms add regular adjectival endings. The full paradigm (using mamin as the model) is: Unverified Content The source material does not provide a full declension table for possessive adjectives. It states only that "all other forms are regular" with two oblique examples (Petrova kniga, na materinom stolu). The full paradigm below is extrapolated. Case Masc. sg. (anim.) Masc. sg. (inan.) Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Masc. pl. (anim.) Masc. pl. (inan.) Neut. pl. Fem. pl. Nominative mamin mamin mamino mamina mamini mamine mamine mamine Accusative maminogo mamin mamino maminų maminyh mamine mamine mamine Genitive maminogo maminogo maminogo maminoj maminyh maminyh maminyh maminyh Dative maminomu maminomu maminomu maminoj maminym maminym maminym maminym Instrumental maminym maminym maminym maminojų maminymi maminymi maminymi maminymi Locative maminom maminom maminom maminoj maminyh maminyh maminyh maminyh

Possessive adjectives in -ov / -ev and -in MUST be used only when the possessor is expressed as a single uninflected word (a simple noun without modifiers or additional words): bratova kniga — "brother's book" (single-word possessor → possessive adjective) kniga mojego brata — "my brother's book" (modified possessor → genitive phrase) Puškinova poezija — "Pushkin's poetry" (single proper name → possessive adjective) poezija Aleksandra Puškina — "Aleksandr Pushkin's poetry" (full name → genitive phrase) When the possessor is a phrase, a multi-word name, or requires its own inflection, the genitive form of the possessor noun MUST be used instead. Unverified Content The entire section on demonstratives as quasi-articles has no basis in the source material on adjectives. The source does not discuss demonstrative pronouns, articles, or the declension of toj/ta/to in this context.

Interslavic has no grammaticalized definite or indefinite article. The demonstrative pronoun toj (masc.), ta (fem.), to (neut.) — "that; this" — serves as the closest functional equivalent to a definiteness marker. The full declension of toj / ta / to follows the standard adjectival paradigm with the following forms: Case Masc. sg. (anim.) Masc. sg. (inan.) Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all genders) Nominative toj toj to ta ti Accusative togo toj to tų tyh (anim.); te (inan.) Genitive togo togo togo toj tyh Dative tomu tomu tomu toj tym Instrumental tym tym tym tojų tymi Locative tom tom tom toj tyh
documents.s: Toj / ta / to (Proximal and Distal Demonstratives)

documents.t: Demonstratives in Interslavic serve two functions: Deictic use: pointing to a referent in the physical or discourse context — toj čelověk "that man (over there)". Anaphoric use: referring back to a previously mentioned or contextually established referent — Kupil jesm knigu. Ta kniga je interesna. "I bought a book. That/The book is interesting." Because Interslavic lacks a definite article, the demonstrative in its anaphoric use is the standard means of marking definiteness when contextual clarity requires it. Its use in this function is OPTIONAL; definiteness need not be marked when the context is unambiguous.
Adjectives (and the adverbs derived from them; see the relevant section) have three degrees of comparison: positive, comparative, and superlative.
The positive degree is the uninflected base form of the adjective, expressing a quality without comparison: dobry "good", prosty "simple", svěži "fresh".

Literary Register: Alternative Comparative Formation Lombino (2022) describes an alternative comparative formation used in the rich/literary register, based on iotation (consonant softening) or the suffix -ěj- rather than the standard -š- suffix: Monosyllabic adjectives form the comparative by iotating the final consonant (the same consonant alternation used in verbal derivation: t > ć, d > đ, s > š, etc.): mlad > mlađ/mlađa/mlađe, lěp > lěpj/lěpša. Polysyllabic adjectives add the suffix -ěj-: bogat > bogatěj/bogatěja/bogatěje, jasŭn > jasněj/jasněja/jasněje. Adjectives with -k- or -ok- drop the suffix before comparison: širok > širj, dalek > dalj. This system is closer to the inherited Common Slavic comparative (preserved in varying degrees across South and West Slavic languages) and avoids the -ějši suffix, which is a Interslavic innovation combining features from multiple language groups. The standard -ějši / -ši forms remain the default for Standard Interslavic. Lombino also documents an absolute superlative (elative), formed with the standard naj- prefix but used without comparison to express a high degree: Najuvažimji prijateli! ("Most esteemed friends!" --- not the most esteemed of all, but very esteemed). The primary synthetic comparative is formed by replacing the ending -y / -i with the suffix -ějši (after hard stems) or -ejši (after soft stems, i.e. after š, ž, č, j): Positive Comparative Gloss bogaty bogatějši "richer" slaby slabějši "weaker" zeleny zelenějši "greener" svěži svěžejši "fresher" The consonant alternations k → č, g → ž, h → š apply before the comparative suffix: Unverified Content The source uses blagy → blažejši to illustrate the g→ž alternation. The examples tihy → tišejši, tonky → tončejši, and drågy → dråžejši are not in the source. Additionally, tonky → tončejši is questionable since -ky adjectives normally use the shorter -ši suffix (as stated in the next subsection). Positive Comparative Gloss tihy tišejši "quieter" tonky tončejši "thinner" drågy dråžejši "dearer" Adjectives in -ky, -eky, -oky. Adjectives with these suffixes form the comparative with the shorter suffix -ši attached directly to the root (after the same consonant alternations): Positive Comparative Gloss kratky kratši "shorter" tenky tenši "thinner" daleky dalši "farther" vysoky vysši "higher" The comparative form is a fully inflected adjective and MUST agree with its head noun in gender, number, and case, following the soft adjective paradigm . Comparative adverbs are formed from the synthetic comparative by replacing -ši with -e (yielding the ending -ěje / -eje): Positive adverb Comparative adverb Gloss novo nověje "more newly" čisto čistěje "more cleanly" tiho tišeje "more quietly" svěže svěžeje "more freshly" For adjectives in -ky / -eky / -oky, the comparative adverb is formed by adding -je to the root, triggering iotation of the preceding consonant: Positive adverb Comparative adverb Gloss daleko dalje "farther" široko širje "more broadly" vysoky → vysoko vyše "higher" blizko bliže "closer" rědko rědže "more rarely"

The analytic comparative is formed by placing vyše or bolje ("more") before the positive form of the adjective or adverb: vyše prosty — "simpler, more simple" bolje sintetičny — "more synthetic" vyše smrdeči — "more stinking" Unverified Content The source says the analytic form "is most likely encountered in the case of very long words, foreign words, and participles." The doc upgrades this observation to a normative SHOULD recommendation and adds the criterion "difficult to pronounce or understand," which is not in the source. The analytic form MAY be used for any adjective or adverb. It SHOULD be preferred for: Long words and words of foreign origin; Participles used as adjectives; Contexts in which the synthetic form would be difficult to pronounce or understand. The analytic and synthetic forms are equivalent in meaning. In most everyday adjectives, either form is acceptable. Less-than comparative. The word menje ("less") functions as the analytic marker for the opposite direction: menje prosty — "less simple" menje prosto — "less simply" Unverified Content The rules distinguishing comparative adjective vs. adverb usage, the comparison examples with neželi, and the constructions with od and než/neželi are not discussed in the source material. Adjective vs. adverb in comparisons. When comparing a property of a noun, the comparative adjective (agreeing with the noun) MUST be used. When comparing a property of an action or verb, the comparative adverb is used: Aleksandr je bystrějši, neželi Dmitri. — "Alexander is faster than Dmitri." (adjective) Aleksandr bystrěje běgaje, neželi Dmitri. — "Alexander runs faster than Dmitri." (adverb) When introducing the standard of comparison, use the genitive with the preposition od "from" or the conjunction než / neželi "than": Ljubov je silnějša od smŕti. — "Love is stronger than death." Goręći čaj je lěpši, než hlådny. — "Hot tea is better than cold."

The superlative is formed by adding the prefix naj- to the comparative form: Comparative Superlative (adj.) Superlative (adv.) novějši najnovějši najnověje prostějši najprostějši najprostěje vysši najvysši najvyše The prefix naj- MAY also be applied to the analytic comparative: najvyše sintetičny — "most synthetic" najbolje prosty — "simplest, most simple" As a simplification, naj- MAY be prefixed directly to the positive form of the adjective or adverb (following the pattern of Bulgarian and Macedonian): najnovy — "newest" (adjective) najnovo — "most newly" (adverb) Least-superlative. The adverb najmenje expresses the opposite extreme ("least"): najmenje prosty — "least simple" najmenje prosto — "least simply" Excessive degree. The prefix prě- attached to the positive expresses an extreme or excessive degree ("very", "too", "overly"): prěprosty — "too simple, overly simple" prěneprijatny — "extremely unpleasant" Unverified Content The example prěneprijatny is not in the source. The source also spells the negative example as neprijetny (with -e-), not neprijatny (with -a-) as used below. Negative. The prefix ne- creates a negative adjective: neprijatny — "unpleasant, not pleasant"

Unverified Content The source says regular forms of these adjectives "will look childish or artificial," but does not use normative SHOULD language. Also, the source lists blagy → blažejši as a regular comparative example (with g→ž alternation) alongside its irregular form unši, but the doc omits the regular blažejši from the consonant-alternation examples in section The following seven adjectives have suppletive or irregular comparative forms. Use of the regular synthetic comparative (e.g., dobrějši) is not incorrect, but SHOULD be avoided in favor of the established forms given below: Positive Comparative (adj.) Comparative (adv.) Gloss dobry "good" lěpši or lučši lěpje or lučše "better" zly "bad" gorši gorje "worse" veliky "big, large" večši or bolši veče or bolje "bigger, larger" maly "small, little" menši menje "smaller, less" blagy "pleasant, joyful" unši unje "more pleasant" legky "easy, light" legši legše "easier, lighter" mekky "soft" mekši mekše "softer" Unverified Content The source lists najbolje prosty and najbolje prosto as additional superlative variants, but they are omitted from the summary table below. Summary example — prosty "simple": Form Latin Gloss Positive (adj.) prosty "simple" Positive (adv.) prosto "simply" Comparative (adj.) prostějši or vyše prosty or bolje prosty "simpler" Comparative (adv.) prostěje or vyše prosto or bolje prosto "more simply" Superlative (adj.) najprostějši or najprosty or najvyše prosty "simplest" Superlative (adv.) najprostěje or najprosto or najvyše prosto "most simply" Less-comparative (adj.) menje prosty "less simple" Less-comparative (adv.) menje prosto "less simply" Least-superlative (adj.) najmenje prosty "least simple" Least-superlative (adv.) najmenje prosto "least simply" Negative neprosty "not simple" Excessive prěprosty "too simple"
This section specifies the formation, comparison, and use of adverbs in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Adverbs in Interslavic are formed from adjectives by replacing the adjectival ending with the suffix -o (after hard stems) or -e (after soft stems — i.e., stems ending in š, ž, č, or j). The resulting form is identical to the neuter singular nominative/accusative of the corresponding adjective. Adjective Adverb Gloss dobry dobro "well" prosty prosto "simply" tihy tiho "quietly" svěži svěže "freshly" gorjači gorjače "hotly" The short form of adjectives MUST NOT be confused with the corresponding adverb. The adverb is always identical to the neuter singular form of the long adjective.

Comparative adverbs are derived from the synthetic adjective comparative by replacing the comparative suffix -ši with -e, yielding the ending -ěje (after hard stems) or -eje (after soft stems): Positive adverb Comparative adverb Gloss novo nověje "more newly" čisto čistěje "more cleanly" tiho tišeje "more quietly" svěže svěžeje "more freshly" For adjectives (and their derived adverbs) whose stem ends in -ky, -eky, or -oky, the comparative adverb is formed by adding -je directly to the root, triggering iotation of the preceding consonant (x): Positive adverb Comparative adverb Gloss daleko dalje "farther" široko širje "more broadly" vysoko vyše "higher" blizko bliže "closer" rědko rědže "more rarely"

The analytic comparative of adverbs is formed by placing vyše or bolje ("more") before the positive adverb. This construction is equivalent to the synthetic form in meaning: vyše prosto — "more simply" bolje tiho — "more quietly" Unverified Content The source describes the analytic form as "most likely encountered" with long/foreign words and participles — an observation of tendency, not a normative SHOULD recommendation. The MAY clause for common adverbs is also editorial. The analytic form SHOULD be preferred for long words, words of foreign origin, and adverbs derived from participles. Either form MAY be used for common adverbs.

The superlative of adverbs is formed by prefixing naj- to the comparative adverb: Positive Comparative Superlative Gloss prosto prostěje najprostěje "most simply" novo nověje najnověje "most newly" daleko dalje najdalje "farthest" The prefix naj- MAY also be applied to the analytic comparative: najvyše prosto — "most simply" najbolje tiho — "most quietly" As a simplification, naj- MAY be prefixed directly to the positive adverb: najnovo — "most newly" najprosto — "most simply"

The adverb menje ("less") functions as the analytic marker for the less-than comparative: menje prosto — "less simply" menje tiho — "less quietly" The adverb najmenje ("least") forms the least superlative: najmenje prosto — "least simply"

Unverified Content The claim that regular forms are "not incorrect" is not directly supported by the source, which instead says they "will look childish or artificial" — implying they are problematic rather than merely dispreferred. The following adverbs have suppletive or irregular comparative forms corresponding to the irregular adjective comparatives . Use of the regular synthetic form (e.g., dobrěje) is not incorrect, but SHOULD be avoided in favor of the established forms given below: Unverified Content In the table below, the positive adverb for "big/great" is listed as :isv[veliko] / :isv[bolje], but the source gives only :isv[veliky] as the base adjective (positive adverb would be :isv[veliko]). Listing :isv[bolje] as a positive form is inconsistent with the source, where :isv[bolje] is a comparative. Positive adverb Comparative adverb Gloss dobro lěpje / lučše "better" zlo gorje "worse" veliko / bolje veče / bolje "more, to a greater extent" malo menje "less" blago unje "more pleasantly" legko legše "more easily" mekko mekše "more softly"

The prefix prě- attached to the positive form of the adjective (from which the adverb is derived) expresses an extreme or excessive degree ("very", "too", "overly"). An adverb derived from such a prefixed adjective carries the same meaning: prěprosty → prěprosto — "too simply, overly simply"

Unverified Content The claim that :isv[ne-] is "most commonly encountered in fixed lexical items" has no basis in the source material, which simply states that :isv[ne-] creates a negative without restricting its usage scope. The prefix ne- creates a negative adverb. It is most commonly encountered in fixed lexical items: neprosty → neprosto — "not simply, in a non-simple manner"

Degree and type Latin script Gloss Positive (adv.) prosto "simply" Comparative (synthetic) prostěje "more simply" Comparative (analytic) vyše prosto / bolje prosto "more simply" Superlative (synthetic) najprostěje "most simply" Superlative (from positive) najprosto "most simply" Superlative (analytic) najvyše prosto / najbolje prosto "most simply" Less-than comparative menje prosto "less simply" Least superlative najmenje prosto "least simply" Negative neprosto "not simply" Excessive prěprosto "too simply"

This section specifies the productive morphological processes by which new words are formed in Interslavic: suffixation, prefixation, zero derivation, and compounding. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Interslavic possesses four productive agentive suffixes for deriving person nouns from verb and noun bases. Suffix -telj derives agent nouns from verb stems, denoting the person who habitually or professionally performs the action: Unverified Content The source only provides učitelj as an example of -telj. The examples pisatelj and vladatelj are not attested in the source material. Base Derived form Gloss učiti "to teach" učitelj "teacher" pisati "to write" pisatelj "writer" vladati "to rule" vladatelj "ruler" Unverified Content The source states that -nica is the feminine of -nik (not -telj). For -telj nouns, the source uses -ka as the feminine suffix (e.g. prijateljka). The feminine counterpart of -telj nouns is formed with -nica. Suffix -ač derives agent nouns from verb stems, with a focus on trades and crafts: Base Derived form Gloss kovati "to forge" kovač "blacksmith" krojiti "to cut (cloth)" krojač "tailor" Suffix -ar similarly derives trade nouns from verb stems: Base Derived form Gloss pekti "to bake" pekar "baker" Suffix -nik derives nouns denoting the person in charge of something, handler of something, or the object containing something. The base is a noun: Base Derived form Gloss glava "head" glavnik "boss, chief" kabel "cable" kabelnik "cable technician" kniga "book" knižnik "bookcase" Suffix -ec derives agent nouns and nouns denoting persons characterised by an adjective: Base Derived form Gloss glupy "stupid" glupec "fool" izgnany "banished" izgnanec "exile" Phonological conditioning: When the noun stem ends in k, g, or h, the following alternations apply before -nik, -ka, -ko, -ny, -ok, and -sky: Stem-final consonant Change Example k / c → č ruka → ručny g → ž kniga → knižka h → š muha → mušji The soft consonant nj hardens before -nik and -ny.

Suffix -ost is the primary suffix for deriving abstract quality nouns from adjectives. It is always feminine: Unverified Content The source only provides dobrost as an example of -ost. The examples slobodnost and starost are not attested in the source material. The claim that -ost is "always feminine" is not stated in the source. Base Derived form Gloss dobry "good" dobrost "goodness" slobodny "free" slobodnost "freedom" stary "old" starost "old age" Suffix -stvo derives abstract nouns from nouns denoting persons, expressing a quality, condition, or collective associated with that person type: Base Derived form Gloss prijatelj "friend" prijateljstvo "friendship" muž "man" mužstvo "manhood" brat "brother" bratstvo "brotherhood" Suffix -nje (verbal noun) is the productive suffix for action nominalisation (gerund). It is formed from the verb and is always neuter. When the past passive participle stem ends in -t-, the variant -tje applies: Base Derived form Gloss dělati "to do" dělanje "doing, making" žiti "to live" žitje "life" pisati "to write" pisanje "writing"

Unverified Content The source does not distinguish a separate "collective" use of -stvo. It lists bratstvo under abstract quality derivation (noun-to-noun), not as a collective suffix. Suffix -stvo (see also ) serves as a collective suffix when derived from animal or group nouns: Base Derived form Gloss brat "brother" bratstvo "brotherhood (collective)" Suffix -išče derives place nouns from verbs and nouns. It may also function as an augmentative (see the relevant section): Base Derived form Gloss igrati "to play" igrališče "playground" žiti "to live" žilišče "dwelling place" voz "wagon" vozišče "parking lot" ogonj "fire" ognišče "fireplace, hearth"

Diminutives are gender-sensitive and phonologically conditioned. The selection of suffix depends on both the grammatical gender of the base noun and the nature of the final stem consonant: Gender Condition Suffix Example Masculine Hard stem -ok (gen. -ka) pes "dog" → pesok "doggy" Masculine Soft stem -ik or -ek muž "man" → mužik "little man" Feminine Any -ka (appended to stem) kniga "book" → knižka "booklet" Neuter Any -ko (appended to stem) polje "field" → poljko "small field" Suffixes beginning with -o- change to -e- after a soft consonant: byk + -ok → byček

Unverified Content The source does not discuss augmentatives at all. The claim that -išče carries augmentative force and that -ina forms augmentatives is not attested in the source material. Suffix -išče may carry augmentative force alongside its place-noun function; context and base determine the primary reading. The suffix -ina is used to form augmentatives and large-instance nouns, as well as abstract nouns of quality or extent. Suffix -je derives place nouns from prepositional phrases or locative descriptions, often with a prefixal element: Base Derived form Gloss morje "sea" primorje "land by the sea" brjuh "belly" podbrjušje "underbelly"

The feminine equivalents of masculine agent nouns and national/professional designations are formed as follows: Masculine suffix Feminine suffix Example -nik -nica radnik → radnica "female counsellor" -ec -ica Albanec → Albanica "Albanian woman" Other -ka prijatelj → prijateljka "girlfriend" The suffix -ka is the default feminine suffix when no morphologically governed feminine counterpart exists: Base Derived form Gloss gradžanin "citizen (m.)" gradžanka "female citizen" Unverified Content The source does not provide učiteljka as an example. The source shows -ka used with prijatelj and gradžanin only. | učitelj "teacher (m.)" | učiteljka | "teacher (f.)" |

Unverified Content The source only mentions -ec (and "sometimes -an(in)") for inhabitant/nationality nouns, with Albanec as the sole example. The Moskvjanin/Moskvjane examples and the rule about -in- loss in the plural are not in the source. The suffix -(an)in derives nouns denoting inhabitants of a country, region, or city. This suffix loses -in- in the plural (yielding -ane): Base Singular Plural Gloss Moskva Moskvjanin Moskvjane "Muscovite(s)" grad "city" gradžanin gradžane "citizen(s)" The suffix -ec is an alternative for nationality nouns, particularly when the base already ends in a consonant cluster: Base Derived form Gloss Albanija Albanec "Albanian"
Baby animal nouns are derived by adding -e to the name of the animal, producing a neuter noun of the -et- stem class: Base Derived form Gloss kot "cat" kote "kitten" jelenj "deer" jelene "fawn"

Three primary suffixes derive relational adjectives from nouns. The choice among them is governed by the semantic class of the base noun: Suffix Condition Example -sky Geographical names, nationalities, professions žena "woman" → žensky "female, feminine"; morje "sea" → morsky "maritime" -ji Animals, persons, divine entities člověk "person" → člověčji "human"; konj "horse" → konji "horse's" -ny All other cases noč "night" → nočny "nocturnal"; pismo "letter" → pisemny "written" Suffixes beginning with -o- change to -e- after a soft consonant: konec + -ovy → koncevy Softness Preservation at the Root–Suffix Boundary​ When an adjective-forming suffix (especially -sky, -ny) is attached to a root whose final consonant is inherently soft, the softness of that root consonant should in principle be preserved through derivation. This follows the general morphophonological principle that root-internal softness is a property of the lexeme, not of the suffix. Examples where root softness is preserved: Root Derived adjective Rationale morje (root morj-, where rj = rʲ) morjsky The rj is an iotated consonant inherent to the root; it persists before -sky (cf. Czech mořský, BCMS morski with soft r) konj (from PS *konjь) konjsky The final nj is iotation-derived softness, preserved before -sky (cf. Czech koňský, BCMS konjski) In the etymological register (MS Plus), where yer-derived softness is explicitly marked, the soft mark persists before suffixes: Root (MS Plus) Derived adjective (MS Plus) Standard form svinь- (from svinja, PS *svinьja) svińsky svinsky Unresolved: Pre-Suffix Softening of S and Z In MS Plus, certain consonants undergo softening before derivational suffixes such as -sky, -ny, -no, -ba. This is already established for consonants like r (e.g., boŕba) and t (e.g., svat́ba). However, the extension of this pattern to s → ś and z → ź is not consistently applied. Cases where pre-suffix softening of s and z is expected by analogy with the broader pattern: Root Expected MS Plus form Standard form Comparative evidence Rus + -sky ruśsky russky Polish ruski (historically soft s), Russian русский bliz + -no pribliźno priblizno Czech přibližně, Russian приблизительно (with soft z) obraz + -ny obraźny obrazny Russian образный (with soft z before derivational boundary) groza + -ba groźba grozba Already attested in the dictionary with soft ź Note that t and d are partial exceptions: they generally do not soften before most suffixes in the living Slavic languages, except before -ba (e.g., svat́ba). The trade-offs are: Extending softening to ś/ź would make the MS Plus system more internally consistent and align with the comparative Slavic evidence. Not extending it keeps the rule set smaller and avoids introducing distinctions that may not be salient to all Slavic speakers. A systematic review of all derivational contexts involving s and z before suffixes is needed to determine the correct scope of this rule. Full suffix inventory for adjective derivation from nouns: Suffix Meaning Example -sky "pertaining to (geo/national/professional)" morsky "maritime" -ji "pertaining to (animal/person/divine)" konji "horse's" -ny "pertaining to (general)" nočny "nocturnal" -ěny "made of (material)" drěvěny "wooden"; steklěny "of glass" -ovy "having characteristics of" pomarančevy "orange (colour)" -ovity "resembling, -ish" malpovity "ape-like"; zelenovity "greenish" Unverified Content This entire subsection on possessive adjectives has no basis in the source material. The source does not discuss possessive adjective suffixes -ov, -in, or the gender-based selection rules described here.

Possessive adjectives are derived from noun bases. The suffix selection follows the gender of the base noun: Base gender Suffix Example Masculine/neuter -ov brat "brother" → bratov "brother's" Feminine -in mati "mother" → matin "mother's" Animate (archaic/etymological) -ji — (see also )

Unverified Content The section heading includes suffix -any but it is not discussed in the text or source. The example kameny is not in the source and does not use the -ěny suffix pattern described here. The suffix -ěny derives adjectives expressing the material from which something is made: Base Derived form Gloss drěvo "wood" drěvěny "wooden" steklo "glass" steklěny "made of glass" kamenj "stone" kameny "of stone"

Verbal conjugation produces four adjectival forms in the shape of participles (see for full paradigms). In addition: Suffix -livy derives adjectives expressing tendency or habitual behaviour from verbs: Base Derived form Gloss govoriti "to speak" govorlivy "talkative" Unverified Content The example žalostlivy is not in the source. Additionally, the derived form appears to come from žalost (a noun) rather than the verb žaliti as stated. | žaliti "to grieve" | žalostlivy | "mournful" | Suffix -omy/-emy/-imy derives passive possibility adjectives (equivalent to English -able/-ible), attached to the present tense stem: Base Derived form Gloss nesti "to carry" nesomy "portable" razuměti "to understand" razumlivy "understandable" Suffix -šnji adjectivises adverbs of time: Base Derived form Gloss včera "yesterday" včerašnji "yesterday's" dnes "today" dnešnji "today's" nyně "now" nyněšnji "current, present"

Suffix -ovati is the primary suffix for deriving verbs from nouns, expressing behaviour or activity associated with the noun: Base Derived form Gloss malpa "monkey" malpovati "to act like a monkey" noč "night" nočevati "to spend the night" Suffixes beginning with -o- change to -e- after a soft consonant: noč + -ovati → nočevati Suffix -iti (with prefix o-) is used to form verbs expressing the act of causing someone or something to undergo treatment with what the noun denotes: Base Derived form Gloss kamenj "stone" okameniti "to stone; to turn to stone" světlo "light" osvětliti "to illuminate" svoboda "freedom" osvoboditi "to set free" The prefix obez- combined with -iti forms verbs meaning "to deprive of": Base Derived form Gloss glava "head" obezglaviti "to decapitate" dom "house" obezdomiti "to render homeless"

The prefix o- combined with -iti forms causative verbs from adjectives, expressing "to make something have the quality of the adjective": Base Derived form Gloss črny "black" očrniti "to blacken" krugl "round" okrugliti "to make round" The prefix u- combined with -iti forms causative verbs from comparatives, expressing "to make more": Base Derived form Gloss lěpši "better" ulěpšiti "to improve" menši "less" umenšiti "to diminish, reduce" The prefix od- combined with -iti forms verbs expressing removal of a quality: Base Derived form Gloss črny "black" odčrniti "to remove the black colour"

Prefixed perfective verbs derive imperfective counterparts through systematic suffix replacement. The rule set is as follows: | Perfective ending | Imperfective suffix | Example | |-------------------|--------------------|---------|| | -ati | -yvati | sodržati → sodrživati "to contain (ipfv)" | | -iti | -jati | obnoviti → obnavjati "to renew (ipfv)" | | -nuti | -ati | oddohnuti → oddyhati "to breathe (ipfv)" | | Monosyllabic + vowel | -vati | prodati → prodavati "to sell (ipfv)" | Unverified Content The source states that "the base form of a prefixed verb is always perfective" but does not use normative MUST language. The MUST requirement is an editorial addition. Normative rule: Prefixation of a bare verb always creates a perfective verb. An imperfective counterpart MUST be derived by one of the suffix-replacement processes above.

The suffix -ěti forms inchoative verbs from adjectives, expressing the process of becoming: Base Derived form Gloss běly "white" bělěti "to turn white" bogaty "rich" bogatěti "to grow rich" hvory "sick" hvorěti "to become sick"

The following table specifies all productive verbal prefixes with their primary spatial meanings, perfectivizing function, and idiomatic extensions. Prefixation of a bare imperfective verb always creates a perfective; for aspectual pairs, see the relevant section Prefix Primary meaning Key examples do- to the end, reaching a goal dojdti "to arrive"; dopisati "to add by writing" iz- out, off, ex- (= vy- for South Slavic) izgnati "to exile"; izbrati "to select" na- up, on, over; achieving a quantity naložiti "to place on"; napisati "to write down" nad- over, above, surpassing nadsiliti "to overbear"; nadigrati "to outplay" nedo- incompletely, not fully nedopisati "to write without finishing" o-/ob- around; giving characteristics of okrugliti "to make round"; obidti "to go around" obez- depriving of (dis-, de-) obezsiliti "to disempower" od- away, off; completing to be rid of odidti "to go away"; odrabotati "to work off" po- beginning; briefly; completion početi "to start"; pojesti "to eat everything" pod- under, from below; secretly podmetnuti "to put under"; podkazati "to tell in secret" prě- across; transformation; too much prějdti "to cross"; prěsoliti "to oversalt" prěd- before, in advance prědviděti "to foresee"; prědstaviti "to introduce" pri- close, towards; attachment; slightly prijdti "to approach"; priodkryti "to open a bit" pro- through; missing; duration; losing all probiti "to penetrate"; proigrati "to lose the game" raz- apart, dis-, undoing razidti "to disperse"; razminovati "to de-mine" s-/so- together; down from; entirely done svezati "to bind together"; sojdti "to come down" su- alongside, co-; face to face surabotati "to work alongside"; suhoditi "to meet up" u- away; totally; making more (from adj.) uletěti "to fly away"; ulěpšiti "to improve" v-/vo- in, into; attentively (with se) vojdti "to enter"; včitati se "to read attentively" voz- up; suddenly beginning; back, re- vozletěti "to fly up"; vozvratiti se "to return" vy- out; completely; well (North Slavic; = iz- in South Slavic) vyjdti "to go out"; vyvariti "to boil completely" za- behind; closing; beginning zajdti "to go behind"; zapěvati "to start singing"

Unverified Content The source says po- is "often used as a perfective marker" but does not state a SHOULD preference for po- over other prefixes. The normative SHOULD language is an editorial addition. The prefix po- functions as a default perfectivizer for a wide range of verbs, especially when no lexical directional meaning is needed. In this use, po- SHOULD be preferred when no other prefix adequately expresses the aspectual relationship. The prefix na- (with reflexive se) signals satiation or sufficiency: naspati se "to sleep one's fill"; najesti se "to eat one's fill". The prefixes prě- and u- are used specifically with adjectives as bases to produce perfective verbs expressing intensification (see the relevant section): prě- + adjective: "very/too much" → prěumorjeny "exhausted" ne- (negative prefix) + adjective: negation → nedobry "not good"

Unverified Content This section largely duplicates content from section (verbal nouns in -nje/-tje) and (agent nouns). The subsections on result nouns (-ok, -ba) below introduce suffixes not attested in the source material.

The verbal noun (gerund) is the standard action nominalization. It is formed from the verb infinitive by replacing -ti with -nje, or where the past passive participle stem ends in -t-, with -tje: Infinitive Verbal noun Gloss dělati dělanje "doing, making" žiti žitje "life; living" pisati pisanje "writing" govoriti govorenje "speaking" Unverified Content The example govorenje is not in the source. The claim that verbal nouns "are always neuter" is not stated in the source material. Verbal nouns are always neuter and decline following the -nje/-tje neuter paradigm. Unverified Content This entire subsection on result nominalization is not supported by the source material. The suffixes -ok (as result noun), -ba, and -ina (as result noun) and the examples krojok, borba, and prosba do not appear in the source.

Result nouns denote the product or outcome of an action rather than the action itself. Suffix -ok (masculine) derives result nouns from verb stems: Base Derived form Gloss krojiti "to cut" krojok "cut, piece" Suffix -ba derives abstract deverbal nouns: Base Derived form Gloss boriti "to fight" borba "struggle, fight" prositi "to beg" prosba "request"

Agent nominalization from verb bases is treated fully in the relevant section These three suffixes are the primary deverbal agent noun suffixes, with -telj restricted to verb bases and -nik more commonly derived from noun bases. Unverified Content This entire section on zero derivation is not discussed in the source material. The source mentions that an adjective "can also function as a noun, remaining unchanged" (with běly as example) but does not describe a "zero derivation" process, deverbal noun conversion, or the SHOULD rule about lexicalized items. The vezti to voz example is not in the source.

Zero derivation (conversion) is a minor process in Interslavic by which a word changes word class without any overt affixal change. The primary attested case is the deverbal noun directly from a verb root, where the noun denotes a vehicle, instrument, or container associated with the verbal action: Verb Zero-derived noun Gloss vezti "to carry, to transport" voz "cart, vehicle" An adjective may also function as a substantivized noun without morphological change: běly "white (adj.)" → běly "a white one (noun)". Zero derivation is not a productive process in Interslavic and its outputs SHOULD be treated as lexicalized items.

Interslavic permits free compounding of two roots to express a new concept. The standard linking element is -o- after hard consonants and -e- after soft consonants: First element Second element Compound Gloss voda "water" padati "to fall" vodopad "waterfall" sam "self" kritika "criticism" samokritika "self-criticism" myš "mouse" loviti "to catch" myšelovka "mousetrap" zemja "earth" tresenje "trembling" zemjetresenje "earthquake" Unverified Content The examples rukopis and zemljepis are not in the source material. The source provides vodopad, samokritika, myšelovka, and zemjetresenje only. | ruka "hand" | pis- (< pisati) | rukopis | "handwriting" | | zemja "earth" | pis- (< pisati) | zemljepis | "geography" | Unverified Content The source describes compounding with -o- (and -e- after soft consonants) but does not use MUST language. The rule about no linking vowel when the first element already ends in a vowel is not stated in the source. The linking vowel MUST be -o- after hard consonants and -e- after soft consonants. No linking vowel is inserted when the first element already ends in a vowel.

When the first element of a compound is a borrowing from English or another non-Slavic language, the elements MAY be separated by a hyphen (Interslavic: defis): Latin script Gloss rok-muzika "rock music" veb-stranica "web page" Native Interslavic compounds are written as a single word without a hyphen. Unverified Content The cross-reference to cannot be verified against the source material. For the treatment of compound orthography in the context of the writing system, see the relevant section

The standard adverb is formed from an adjective by substituting the adjectival ending with -o: Adjective Adverb Gloss dobry "good" dobro "well" tihy "quiet" tiho "quietly" skory "fast" skoro "quickly, soon"

The comparative form of the adverb is derived by replacing the comparative adjective suffix -(ěj)ši with -(ě)je: Comparative adjective Comparative adverb Gloss slabějši "weaker" slaběje "more weakly" lěpši "better" lěpje "better (adv.)"

Unverified Content The source material does not use RFC 2119 terminology (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY). The normative framing throughout this document is an editorial addition not present in the original grammar description. This section specifies the grammatical categories, declension classes, and morphological paradigms for nouns in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Interslavic has three grammatical genders: masculine, feminine, and neuter. Gender is an inherent property of the noun lexeme and is listed in the dictionary entry for each noun. Gender is not determined by the referent's biological sex, although in the case of nouns denoting persons and higher animals there is typically a natural correspondence. Gender governs agreement in adjectives, pronouns, numerals, and past-tense verb forms.

Unverified Content The source does not present a formal gender-assignment table. The table below is an editorial synthesis. The -ę ending row is derived from the athematic declension discussion but is not stated as a gender-assignment rule in the source. The following default rules assign gender on the basis of the noun's nominative singular form: Ending Default gender Examples Consonant (zero ending) Masculine brat "brother", dom "house", kost "bone" (fem.) -a Feminine žena "woman", zemja "earth" -o Neuter slovo "word", okno "window" -e Neuter morje "sea", polje "field" -ę Neuter imę "name", telę "calf" The following exceptions and special cases apply: A small class of nouns denoting male persons ends in -a but is grammatically masculine: sluga "servant", sudja "judge", kolega "colleague". These nouns MUST be treated as masculine for purposes of adjective agreement and animacy. Unverified Content The example věšč "thing" does not appear in the source material. Its form as a Third Declension noun cannot be verified. A small number of nouns ending in a consonant are grammatically feminine, most notably those forming the Third Declension: kost "bone", noć "night", věšč "thing". Unverified Content Internal inconsistency: pani ends in -i, not -a. The source material describes pani as a word on -i that belongs to the second declension group (inflected as if the nominative were *panja). Some nouns ending in -a (e.g., pani "lady") follow a soft-stem pattern.

The category of animacy applies exclusively to masculine nouns in Interslavic. Feminine and neuter nouns are always treated as inanimate regardless of their referent. Animate masculine nouns comprise: Nouns denoting male persons (and some epicene person-nouns); Nouns denoting animals. Normative rule: For animate masculine nouns, the accusative case MUST be identical to the genitive case. For inanimate masculine nouns, the accusative MUST be identical to the nominative. Type Nominative sg. Accusative sg. Genitive sg. Animate brat brata brata Inanimate dom dom doma This distinction also applies in the plural accusative: animate masculine nouns take the genitive plural, inanimate masculine nouns take the nominative plural.

Interslavic has two primary grammatical numbers: singular and plural. All nouns have both forms unless lexically restricted (see for irregular plurals). The plural paradigms are specified within each declension class below. Unverified Content The concepts of pluralia tantum and singularia tantum, and the example dveri "door(s)", are not discussed in the source material. Certain nouns are used only in the plural (pluralia tantum), such as dveri "door(s)", děti "children" (as the suppletive plural of dětę). Certain nouns are used only in the singular (singularia tantum), such as many abstract and mass nouns.

Interslavic provides an optional dual number. Its use is not required in standard Interslavic but MAY be employed in literary or archaic registers and to explicitly denote exactly two entities, especially naturally paired body parts. The dual number is fully preserved as a productive category in Slovenian and the Sorbian languages (Upper and Lower Sorbian). It was a core feature of Old Church Slavonic and Proto-Slavic. All other modern Slavic languages have lost the dual as a productive category, but its traces survive universally in the special agreement patterns triggered by the numerals 2, 3, and 4 (where nouns take nominative plural rather than genitive plural), in the irregular plurals of paired body-part nouns (oči "eyes", uši "ears"), and in frozen lexical forms. Unresolved: Status of the Dual Number The dual number presents a genuine tension in Interslavic language design. On one hand, it is historically authentic (attested in Old Church Slavonic and Proto-Slavic), it is part of everyday grammar for approximately 2.5 million Slovenian speakers and 50,000 Sorbian speakers, and its traces permeate all Slavic languages. Including it preserves Common Slavic heritage and improves intelligibility for these communities. On the other hand, the dual is absent as a productive category from the vast majority of modern Slavic languages (Russian, Polish, Czech, Slovak, Serbo-Croatian, Bulgarian, Macedonian, Ukrainian, Belarusian), and requiring or even encouraging its use would add significant morphological complexity for the majority of Interslavic users who have no experience with dual forms. The current position treats the dual as entirely optional: it MAY be used but is never required. The dual paradigm documented here is based on Roberto Lombino's proposal (2021), which reconstructs a full Interslavic dual system from Old Church Slavonic and Slovenian models. No committee decision has been made on whether the dual should be more actively promoted for literary registers or explicitly discouraged in standard communication. The dual paradigm merges certain cases: nominative and accusative share one form (with an animacy distinction only in masculine, where accusative of animate nouns equals genitive), genitive merges with locative, and dative merges with instrumental. Dual paradigm for nouns: Case I masc. hard (brat) I masc. hard (grad) I masc. soft (muž) I masc. soft (kraj) II fem. hard (žena) II fem. soft (duša) III fem. (kost) I neut. hard (slovo) I neut. soft (polje) Athematic -ęt- (telę) Nom. brata grada muža kraja ženě duši kosti slově or slovesě polji telętě Acc. = Gen. (anim.) = Nom. (inan.) = Gen. (anim.) = Nom. (inan.) = Nom. = Nom. = Nom. = Nom. = Nom. = Nom. Gen./Loc. bratu gradu mužu kraju ženu dušu kosťu slovu or slovesu polju telętu Dat./Instr. bratoma gradoma mužema krajema ženama dušama kosťma slovoma or slovesoma poljema telętoma Notes on dual noun endings: The nominative of First Declension masculine nouns ends in -a regardless of animacy. The nominative of First Declension neuter and Second Declension feminine nouns ends in -ě after hard stems and -i after soft stems. The nominative of Third Declension nouns ends in -i. The genitive/locative takes -u across all declensions. The dative/instrumental takes -oma after hard stems, -ema after soft stems, -ama after vowel stems (Second Declension feminine), and -ma after a consonant (Third Declension feminine). For animate masculine nouns, the accusative dual equals the genitive dual: Vidžu dvu mužu "I see two men." For inanimate masculine, and all feminine and neuter nouns, the accusative dual equals the nominative dual: Vidžu dva grada, dvě ženě, dvě polji "I see two cities, two women, two fields." Special dual forms of oko and uho: The nouns oko "eye" and uho "ear", as well as nouns with stems extended by -en-, have a special nominative dual ending in -i, with stem softening for oko and uho: Case oko uho ramę (shoulder) Nom./Acc. oči uši rameni Gen./Loc. očju ušju ramenu Dat./Instr. očima ušima ramenoma Example sentences: Vidžu dvu mužu. — "I see two men." Vidžu dva grada, dvě ženě, dvě polji. — "I see two cities, two women, two fields."

Interslavic has seven grammatical cases: nominative, accusative, genitive, dative, instrumental, locative, and vocative. Unverified Content The detailed case function descriptions ( through ) with their MUST/SHOULD/MAY normative claims are not present in the source material, which only lists the seven case names without elaborating on their individual functions.
The nominative case marks the subject of a finite clause and the predicate nominal in equative constructions with byti "to be". It is the citation form of the noun.

The accusative case marks: The direct object of a transitive verb; The destination of motion with certain prepositions (e.g., v "into", na "onto", za "behind", pod "under", nad "above", prěd "before", meždu "between" — when motion is implied). For animate masculine nouns, the accusative is identical to the genitive .

The genitive case marks: Possessive and partitive relationships; Genitive of negation: when the verb is negated, the direct object MAY (and in some contexts SHOULD) appear in the genitive rather than the accusative; The object of numerous prepositions (e.g., do "to/until", iz "from/out of", od "from", bez "without", dlja "for", u "at/near"). Literary Register: Obligatory Genitive of Negation and Partitive Genitive In the rich/literary register (Lombino 2022), the genitive of negation is obligatory rather than optional: Vidžų drěvo. Ne vidžų drěva. ("I see a tree. I do not see a tree.") Additionally, the genitive may express partitivity (a portion of something), paralleling usage in Russian, Polish, and other Slavic languages: Naliju ti vody. ("I'll pour you some water" --- specifically a portion, not all water). In Standard Interslavic, the partitive genitive is permitted but not required; the literary register treats it as the expected construction when quantity is partial or unspecified. The literary register also permits a dative of possession: Mama jest mi prišla. ("My mother has come", lit. "Mother has-to-me come"), paralleling Czech and South Slavic patterns. This construction is not documented in Standard Interslavic but is widely attested across the Slavic family.

The dative case marks: The indirect object (recipient or beneficiary) of a ditransitive verb; Experiencer arguments with certain verbs (e.g., nraviti se "to please"); The object of certain prepositions (e.g., k "towards", protivu "against").

The instrumental case marks: The instrument or means by which an action is performed; Accompaniment, with the preposition s/so "with"; Predicative nominal after byti in some constructions, indicating a temporary or role-based state.

The locative case marks location in space or time. It MUST always be used with a preposition; it never appears without one. Common governing prepositions include v "in", na "on", pri "at/near", po "along/after", o "about".

The vocative is used for direct address. It differs structurally from the other cases: it exists only in the masculine and feminine singular, never affects adjectives or pronouns, and plays no role in the syntactic structure of the sentence. In neuter nouns and all plurals, the nominative form serves as the vocative. In informal registers, the nominative MAY substitute for the vocative throughout. Vocative forms are specified per declension class in the relevant section Unresolved: Literary Register Case Endings and Additional Cases Lombino (2022) documents a rich set of variant case endings and supplementary cases that exist in the broader Interslavic system but are not part of Standard Interslavic. These represent a tension between the standard language's goal of simplicity and the full expressive capacity of the Common Slavic heritage: Variant endings within the standard seven cases: Masculine animate nominative plural -ove/-eve (e.g., panove, muževe) alongside standard -i Masculine dative singular -ovi/-evi (e.g., bratrovi, muževi) alongside standard -u Masculine/neuter locative singular -ě (hard stems) / -i (soft stems) as the primary form rather than alternant Masculine/neuter dative plural -om/-em and locative plural -oh/-eh (Czech-Slovak pattern) alongside standard -am/-ah Masculine/neuter instrumental plural -y/-i alongside standard -ami Feminine genitive singular of adjectives in -ě (e.g., bez dobrojě ženy) Second palatalization in declension: Before the endings -i and -ě, the consonant alternations k > c, g > dz, h > s/š apply (e.g., vŭlk > vŭlci, rųka > rųcě, noga > nodzě). This pattern is attested in West and South Slavic languages and in Old Church Slavonic, but is excluded from Standard Interslavic to preserve stem transparency. Supplementary cases (extreme literary register): Lombino describes four additional cases that can replace prepositional constructions: Ablative (departing-case): replaces iz + genitive, with endings -u (masc.), -e (fem.) Inessive (inside-case): replaces v + locative, with endings -ě/-i Allative (approaching-case): replaces k + dative, with endings -ovi/-evi (masc./neut.), -i (fem.) Partitive: replaces genitive in partitive function, with ending -u (masc.), -e (fem.) These supplementary cases are marked with an asterisk in the source, indicating even the author considers them extreme extensions. No committee decision has been made on whether these should be formally documented as part of the Interslavic system.

Interslavic has three primary declension classes and one optional athematic (archaic) declension. Hard and soft stems. Every declension distinguishes between hard and soft stem variants. Soft stems are stems ending in š, ž, č, c, j, lj, or nj. The following systematic alternations apply throughout the declension system: After hard consonant After soft consonant -o -e -y -e -ě -i In the tables that follow, soft-stem endings are shown in parentheses where they differ from the hard-stem endings.

The First Declension covers: All masculine nouns ending in a consonant (zero ending); All neuter nouns ending in -o (hard) or -e (soft). Hard masculine nouns (brat, dom)​ brat "brother" (animate); dom "house" (inanimate) Case brat sg. dom sg. brati pl. domy pl. Nominative brat dom brati domy Accusative brata dom bratov domy Genitive brata doma bratov domov Dative bratu domu bratam domam Instrumental bratom domom bratami domami Locative bratu domu bratah domah Vocative brate dome — — Notes: The locative singular primary ending is -u, identical to the dative. Alternatively, -ě MAY be used after hard consonants: bratě, domě. The vocative singular of masculine hard-stem nouns ends in -e. Before this ending, the consonant alternations k > č, g > ž, h > š apply: člověk → člověče, Bog → Bože. Nouns ending in -ec take the vocative ending -če instead of the expected -cu: otec → otče, hlåpec → hlåpče. Unverified Content The vocative -u exceptions for dom, syn, děd are not mentioned in the source material. A small number of hard-stem nouns take vocative -u: dom → domu, syn → synu, děd → dědu. Soft masculine nouns (muž, kraj)​ muž "man" (animate); kraj "country/region" (inanimate) Case muž sg. kraj sg. muži pl. kraje pl. Nominative muž kraj muži kraje Accusative muža kraj mužev kraje Genitive muža kraja mužev krajev Dative mužu kraju mužam krajam Instrumental mužem krajem mužami krajami Locative mužu kraju mužah krajah Vocative mužu kraju — — Notes: For animate nouns (e.g., muž), the accusative plural equals the genitive plural: mužev. For inanimate nouns (e.g., kraj), the accusative plural equals the nominative plural: kraje. The locative singular MAY alternatively take -i after soft consonants: muži, kraji. Unverified Content The source material does not explicitly state that all soft-stem masculine vocatives end in -u. The source mentions -u as a soft-stem vocative only in passing for specific examples. The vocative singular of soft-stem masculine nouns ends in -u. The accusative is always identical to the nominative in this declension (no animacy distinction applies). The athematic declension derives from Old Church Slavonic and Proto-Slavic stem classes that have been lost or merged in most modern Slavic languages. In Interslavic, use of the athematic paradigm is optional (MAY be employed). Every athematic noun has a regular-declension equivalent that is equally correct. The athematic declension applies to nouns of all three genders organized by stem extension: Unverified Content The example denj "day" is not listed among the -en- stem nouns in the source material, which gives only kamen "stone" and koren "root". Also, the spelling doći "daughter" differs from the source material, which has doči. Subtype Gender Nominative sg. Stem extension Examples -en- stem Masculine -enj -enj- (obl. stem) kamenj "stone", korenj "root", denj "day" (see note below on softness) -en- stem Neuter -ę -en- imę "name", brěmę "burden", vrěmę "time" -et- stem Neuter -ę -et- telę "calf", kotę "kitten", prase "piglet" -es- stem Neuter -o -es- nebo "sky", drěvo "tree" -ov- stem Feminine -ov -v- crkov "church", mrkov "carrot", ljubov "love" -er- stem Feminine -i -er- mati "mother", doći "daughter" Unresolved: Softness in Athematic Masculine Nominatives The athematic masculine nouns listed above (kamenj, korenj, denj, and others such as jeleń, jęčmeń, kremeń, plåmeń, grebeń, remeń, stųpeń, šeršeń) present an unresolved orthographic problem. The final softness in these nouns is yer-derived (Proto-Slavic *kamy, oblique *kamene; *dьnь; etc.), not iotation-derived. Yer-derived softness is reflected in Northern Slavic languages (Russian камень, Polish kamień) but not in Southern Slavic (Croatian kamen, Serbian камен — both with hard N). Under the principle that standard Interslavic follows the South Slavic pattern for yer-derived softness (dropping it), the standard nominative should end in a hard consonant: kamen, koren, den. The MS Plus form would then use ń: kameń, koreń, dėń. The current notation with the iotation digraph -nj (kamenj, korenj) conflates yer-derived softness with iotation-derived softness. This affects how these nouns interact with the declension system: if the nominative ends in hard -n, the noun would pattern with hard-stem First Declension masculines; if it ends in soft -nj, it patterns with soft stems. The athematic paradigm itself (with the extended oblique stem -en-) sidesteps this problem, but the regular-declension alternative does not. No committee decision has been made on this question. Both kamenj and kamen are encountered in practice. Interslavic is designed to be as regular as possible, but the following nouns have suppletive or irregular plurals rooted in natural Slavic usage. The irregular plural forms are preferred in standard usage; regular alternatives exist but sound unnatural to Slavic speakers. člověk "human being, person" → plural: ljudi "people" The plural is formed from a completely different stem and declines as a Third Declension feminine noun: Case Singular Plural Nominative člověk ljudi Accusative člověka ljudij Genitive člověka ljudij Dative člověku ljuďam Instrumental člověkom ljuďami Locative člověku ljuďah The regular form člověki exists but SHOULD be avoided in favour of ljudi. dětę "child" → plural: děti "children" The singular declines as an athematic -et- stem noun (or alternatively as a regular neuter). The plural is irregular and declines as a Third Declension feminine: Unverified Content The genitive singular form dětęte does not match the source, which gives děteta or dětete. The form dětęta is also not in the source (source has děteta). The nouns oko "eye" and uho "ear" have irregular plural forms derived from the historical dual number, reflecting the fact that these body parts naturally come in pairs. The plural forms oči and uši are preferred; regular forms oka, uha (and athematic očesa, ušesa) are permitted alternatives. Case oko sg. uho sg. oči pl. uši pl. Nominative oko uho oči uši Accusative oko uho oči uši Genitive oka uha očij ušij Dative oku uhu očam ušam Instrumental okom uhom očami ušami Locative oku uhu očah ušah Unverified Content The full declension table for Slovjanin is not in the source material. The source only briefly mentions that words on -anin lose -in- in the plural and gives nom.sg., gen.sg., nom.pl., and gen.pl. forms.

Nouns ending in -anin (typically denoting members of an ethnic, national, or geographic group) lose the suffix -in- in all plural forms. This is a regular pattern for this lexical class, not a true irregularity, but it is documented here because it differs from the standard masculine First Declension. Example: Slovjanin "a Slav" Case Singular Plural Nominative Slovjanin Slovjani Accusative Slovjanina Slovjanov Genitive Slovjanina Slovjanov Dative Slovjaninu Slovjanam Instrumental Slovjaninom Slovjanami Locative Slovjaninu Slovjanah Unverified Content The claim that the zero-ending variant slovjan is "attested and acceptable in informal usage" is not in the source material. The genitive plural Slovjanov (with the full -ov ending) is normative; a zero-ending variant slovjan is attested and acceptable in informal usage.
The vocative is used for direct address and exists only in the masculine and feminine singular. The following rules govern vocative formation:

Stem type Ending Examples Hard stem -e pan → pane, profesor → profesore, Jan → Jane Hard stem (exceptions) -u dom → domu, syn → synu, děd → dědu Soft stem -u prijatelj → prijatelju, kraj → kraju, konj → konju Stem ending in -ec -če otec → otče, hlåpec → hlåpče, starec → starče Consonant alternations before the vocative ending -e: k → č: člověk → člověče g → ž: Bog → Bože h → š: (rare examples in this class)

Declension Ending Examples Second Declension (-a nouns) -o mama → mamo, Marija → Marijo, sudja → sudjo Second Declension (-i nouns) -i (no change) pani → pani Third Declension -i (no change) noć → noći, kost → kosti
documents.s: Feminine Second and Third Declension vocative

documents.t: Neuter nouns and all plural forms have no distinct vocative; the nominative serves in its place. In informal registers, the nominative MAY substitute for the vocative throughout.

Borrowed international vocabulary ending in -e, -i, or -u (e.g., alibi, hobi, intervju, kafe, kakao, kliše, menju, tabu, taksi) and abbreviations are indeclinable. These nouns do not take case endings and remain invariant across all grammatical contexts. If case endings are nonetheless added to such nouns (which is permissible), they SHOULD be separated from the noun by an apostrophe: togo alibi'a, te intervju'y, tyh taksi'ov. Unverified Content The declension table for video and the rules about borrowed nouns ending in -o, Slavic place names ending in -o, and personal names ending in -i (through the end of section) are not present in the source material. Nouns ending in -o borrowed from international vocabulary (e.g., video, metro, avto, džudo) MAY be declined according to the First Declension, treating the final -o as the neuter/masculine nominative ending: Case video sg. video pl. Nominative video videa Accusative video videa Genitive videa videj Dative videu videam Instrumental videom videami Locative videu videah Slavic place names ending in -o (e.g., Brno, Puškino) SHOULD be declined according to the regular neuter paradigm. Personal names ending in -i (e.g., Dmitri, Juri) SHOULD be declined as if they formerly ended in -ij: nom. Dmitri, gen. Dmitrija, dat. Dmitriju, instr. Dmitrijem, loc. Dmitriju; plural nom. Dmitriji, etc. Unverified Content This entire section () elaborates on genitive plural strategies for -j- stems well beyond what the source provides. The source only briefly mentions epenthetic vowel insertion in the genitive plural of neuter nouns. The three-strategy analysis and the specific recommendations are unsourced.

Nouns whose stem ends in -j- (primarily Second Declension feminine nouns and soft First Declension neuter nouns) may form the genitive plural by more than one strategy. The choice depends on the individual word and SHOULD favour the form closest to the majority of Slavic languages: Zero ending with consonant truncation (parallels Slovenian): banja → banj, nedělja → nedělj. This is not possible when the result would end in an impermissible cluster (e.g., burja → burj is avoided). Epenthetic vowel insertion (parallels Russian and Slovenian): morje → morij, večerja → večerij. This is the recommended form when truncation is phonologically impossible. Removal of -j- before zero ending (parallels Polish): zemja → zem, višnja → višen (with epenthesis between consonants). For First Declension masculine nouns with -j- stems, the genitive plural invariably takes -ev: konj → konjev. For Third Declension nouns with stem-final -j-, the genitive plural takes -ij: basnj → basnij. Committee Review Needed The exact recommended form for genitive plural of specific -j- stem nouns (e.g., burja, zemja, morje) is not uniformly specified across existing reference materials. A normative list or decision rule SHOULD be adopted by the committee.
documents.s: Genitive plural of nouns with -j- stems

documents.t: Unverified Content The RFC 2119 normative framing (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY) is not present in the source material. The source uses informal language throughout. The RECOMMENDED and MUST claims in this introduction go beyond what the source states; the source simply says "it deserves recommendation to write digits instead of number words." This section specifies the forms, inflection, agreement rules, and usage conventions for all numeral categories in Interslavic. All normative statements use MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119. Because numeral forms differ considerably across the individual Slavic languages — including lexically unique forms such as Russian sorok "forty" and devjanosto "ninety" — it is RECOMMENDED to write numerals as Arabic digits in written Interslavic wherever possible, so as to avoid misunderstanding. Nevertheless, the spoken and inflected forms remain necessary, and speakers MUST know how to combine numerals with the nouns they govern.

Unverified Content The structural classification into "simple," "compound," and "composite" numerals, and the further division by meaning into "definite cardinals," "fractional numerals," "collective numerals," and "indefinite cardinals," is not found in the source material. Cardinal numerals in Interslavic are divided by structure into simple (osm "eight", sto "hundred"), compound (petdeset "fifty", devetdeset "ninety"), and composite (devet sot trideset sedm "nine hundred thirty-seven"). By meaning they are further divided into definite cardinals (treated in the relevant section), fractional numerals , collective numerals , and indefinite cardinals .

The basic cardinal forms for zero through ten are: Digit Latin Notes 0 nula feminine noun, declines like žena 1 jedin / jedna / jedno agrees with noun in gender, number, case; see the relevant section 2 dva (m./n.) / dvě (f.) see the relevant section 3 tri see the relevant section 4 četyri see the relevant section 5 pet see the relevant section 6 šest see the relevant section 7 sedm see the relevant section 8 osm see the relevant section 9 devet see the relevant section 10 deset see the relevant section The neuter form of "2" is dva in Russian, Belarusian, Ukrainian, Polish, Kashubian, and Serbo-Croatian; it is dvě in Old Church Slavonic, Czech, Slovak, Upper and Lower Sorbian, Slovene, Macedonian, Bulgarian, and Rusyn. Both options are equally valid in Interslavic.

The teens are formed by suffixing -nadset (pronounced -nacet) to the units 1–9: Digit Latin 11 jedinnadset 12 dvanadset 13 trinadset 14 četyrinadset 15 petnadset 16 šestnadset 17 sedmnadset 18 osmnadset 19 devetnadset

The tens are formed by suffixing -deset to the units 2–9: Digit Latin 20 dvadeset 30 trideset 40 četyrideset 50 petdeset 60 šestdeset 70 sedmdeset 80 osmdeset 90 devetdeset Compound numbers from 21 to 99 are formed from high to low — tens first, then units — with the optional conjunction i "and" between them: dvadeset (i) jedin "21", petdeset (i) tri "53". This direct order is characteristic of most Slavic languages. Unverified Content The following claim about reversed order in Czech, Slovak, and Slovene under German influence, and its MAY/NOT RECOMMENDED status, is not found in the source material. The reversed order (units before tens), which exists in Czech, Slovak, and Slovene under German influence, MAY be used as a stylistic variant but is NOT RECOMMENDED in standard Interslavic.

Unverified Content The source material presents fused forms (e.g., dvasto, tristo) as the primary method for hundreds, with inflected forms (dvěstě, trista, petsot) as the alternative. The doc reverses this order, presenting inflected forms as primary. Additionally, the source's fused forms differ from those in the doc (e.g., source has dvasto, doc has dvěsto). Hundreds (100–900): The primary method for hundreds is to inflect the word sto "hundred" as a neuter noun (paradigm in the relevant section): Digit Latin Structure 100 sto simple 200 dvě stě 2 + dual of sto 300 tri sta 3 + nom.pl. of sto 400 četyri sta 4 + nom.pl. of sto 500 pet sot 5 + gen.pl. of sto 600 šest sot 6 + gen.pl. of sto 700 sedm sot 7 + gen.pl. of sto 800 osm sot 8 + gen.pl. of sto 900 devet sot 9 + gen.pl. of sto An alternative method — fusing the numeral and sto into a single uninflected word — is also acceptable and is characteristic of Slovak, Serbo-Croatian, and Slovene: Digit Fused form (Latin) 200 dvěsto 300 tristo 400 četyristo 500 petsto 600 šeststo 700 sedmsto 800 osmsto 900 devetsto When the two-part forms (e.g., dvě stě, tri sta, pet sot) are used, both components are declined (see the relevant section). Fused forms are not declined and are treated as indeclinable nouns. Unverified Content The counting nouns sotnja and sotka are not mentioned in the source material. Counting by hundreds may also be expressed with the counting nouns sotnja "a hundred (of)" and sotka, which decline as regular feminine nouns: tri sotnje "three hundreds". Thousands: Unverified Content The source mentions tyseč as masculine or feminine (gen. tyseča or tyseči) but does not describe a separate second-declension feminine paradigm tyseča (like žena), nor does it mention hiljada as an alternative form. The word for "thousand" is tyseč. It may be treated as a masculine noun of the first declension or as a feminine noun of the third declension (genitive tyseči). The feminine form tyseča, declined as a second-declension feminine noun (like žena), is also accepted and reflects East Slavic and Serbo-Croatian usage. The alternative South Slavic form hiljada is likewise acceptable. All paradigms for tyseč are given in the relevant section Qty. Latin 1 000 jedna tyseča 2 000 dvě tyseče 3 000 tri tyseče 4 000 četyri tyseče 5 000 pet tyseč 10 000 deset tyseč 11 000 jedinnadset tyseč 21 000 dvadeset jedna tyseča A hyphen MAY be inserted for clarity in cases like pet-tyseč "5 000", though this is not required. Millions and milliards: Milion "million" (10⁶) and miliard "milliard / billion" (10⁹) are masculine nouns declined like dom. Unverified Content The discussion of short scale vs. long scale, the SHOULD recommendations about preferring milion/miliard, and the entire scale names table (including bilion, trilion, kvadrilion) are not found in the source material. The larger scale terms (bilion, trilion, etc.) exist in Interslavic but their precise value depends on whether the short scale (used in Russian, Ukrainian, Belarusian, Bulgarian, Serbo-Croatian) or the long scale (used in Polish, Slovak, Czech, Slovene) is assumed. Up to 999 999 999 999, the terms milion and miliard are unambiguous across all Slavic languages and SHOULD be preferred; for larger numbers, numerals SHOULD be written as digits. The number scale names are: Name (Latin) Short scale Long scale tyseč / tyseča 10³ 10³ milion 10⁶ 10⁶ miliard 10⁹ 10⁹ bilion 10⁹ 10¹² trilion 10¹² 10¹⁸ kvadrilion 10¹⁵ 10²⁴

Composite numbers are always formed from high to low: thousands — hundreds — tens — units. The word i "and" MAY be inserted between tens and units: tri-tyseč četyristo petdeset (i) šest "3 456". For agreement of composite numbers with their dependent nouns, the rightmost component governs the agreement (see the relevant section).

Apart from the definite cardinals, Interslavic has a category of indefinite cardinal numerals: Latin Meaning mnogo much, many malo little, few velje a lot veče more menje less koliko how many nekoliko a few, some toliko this/that many para a few Unverified Content The claim that malo and pol are not declined, and that all other indefinite cardinals govern the genitive plural, is not stated in the source material. The source simply lists the indefinite cardinal forms without specifying their declension or agreement behavior. The words malo and pol "half" are not declined. All other indefinite cardinals are declined and govern the genitive plural of the noun they quantify, as the numerals 5 and above do. Unverified Content This entire section () significantly expands on the source material. The source provides only a brief recommendation: "nominative singular after 1, nominative plural after 2-4, genitive plural after 5 and more." The detailed agreement paradigm tables, Pattern A/B distinctions, and composite number agreement examples with full case declensions are not found in the source.
The case and number form of a noun following a cardinal numeral depends on the value of the numeral. Agreement patterns differ sharply between the numerals 1–4 and all higher numerals.

The numeral jedin "one" agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case, exactly as an adjective does. Examples: jedin dobry prijatelj "one good friend" (masc. sg.) jedna mlada žena "one young woman" (fem. sg.) jedno malo děte "one small child" (neut. sg.) jedne sinje džinsy "one pair of jeans" (plurale tantum) The numeral jedin is not restricted to its purely numerical sense; it is also used in the meanings "a certain", "lone", "individual", "single", making it even more adjective-like in behaviour.

The numerals dva, tri, četyri, oba, and obydva govern the nominative plural of the following noun (and its modifying adjective) in the nominative and accusative cases. In oblique cases, these numerals agree with the noun in case. Examples: dva věrni drugi "two faithful friends" (nom.) tri lětnje měsěce "three summer months" (nom.) četyri dolge dni "four long days" (nom.) In the genitive and other oblique cases: dvoh věrnyh drugov "of two faithful friends" trěh lětnjih měsěcev "of three summer months" Unverified Content The source mentions that "some languages, notably Serbo-Croat and Russian, prefer the genitive singular instead" after 2-4, but does not use MAY/NOT RECOMMENDED normative language. The source simply recommends the nominative plural "for reasons of clarity." The Russian and Serbo-Croatian pattern of using the genitive singular after 2–4 (cf. Russ. dva vernogo druga) MAY be used as a variant but is NOT RECOMMENDED in standard Interslavic.

The numerals pet and all higher numerals govern the genitive plural of the following noun and its modifiers in the nominative and accusative. In oblique cases, the numeral itself is declined and the dependent noun stands in the plural of the appropriate case. Two patterns exist: Pattern A (numerals 5–99 and compound numbers whose rightmost element is 5–99): In the nominative/accusative, the numeral governs genitive plural; in oblique cases, the numeral is in the singular (as a noun-like form) and the dependent noun is in the corresponding plural case.
