Case "5 important words" "7 famous people" "10 rubles" Nom./Acc. pet vaznyh slov sedm znamenityh ljudij deset russkyh rubljev Gen. peti vaznyh slov sedmi znamenityh ljudij deseti russkyh rubljev Loc. (o) peti vaznyh slovah (o) sedmi znamenityh ljudjah (o) deseti russkyh rubljah Dat. peti vaznym slovam sedmi znamenitym ljudjam deseti russkim rubljam Ins. petju vaznymi slovami sedmju znamenitymi ljudjami desetju russkimi rubljevami Pattern B (sto, tyseč, milion, miliard): These nouns govern genitive plural of their dependents in ALL cases (including oblique), because they function as true nouns. Case "100 pupils" "1 000 yen" Nom./Acc. sto usrdnyh učenikov tyseča japonskih jenov Gen. sta usrdnyh učenikov tyseče japonskih jenov Loc. (o) stu usrdnyh učenikov (o) tyseči japonskih jenov Dat. stu usrdnyh učenikov tyseči japonskim jenom Ins. stom usrdnyh učenikov tyseččeju japonskih jenov Composite numbers: For composite cardinals such as dvadeset jedin "21" or petdeset tri "53", the rightmost element determines the agreement pattern: Case "21 teachers" "53 people" "67 workers" Nom. dvadeset (i) jedin učitelj petdeset (i) tri ljudi šestdeset (i) sedm rabotnikov Acc. dvadeset (i) jednogo učitelja petdeset (i) trěh ljudij šestdeset (i) sedm rabotnikov Gen. dvadeseti (i) jednogo učitelja petdeseti (i) trěh ljudij šestdeseti (i) sedmi rabotnikov Dat. dvadeseti (i) jednomu učitelju petdeseti (i) trěm ljudjam šestdeseti (i) sedmi rabotnikam Ins. dvadesetju (i) jednym učiteljem petdesetju (i) trěmi ljudjami šestdesetju (i) sedmju rabotnikami Unverified Content This entire section () provides extensive declension paradigm tables that go far beyond the source material. The source gives only brief declension guidance: jedin is declined "like an adjective," numerals 5+ are "inflected like nouns of the pattern kost," and the remaining numbers "are declined as nouns." The full paradigm tables, dual-based declension options, compound hundreds declension, and three separate tyseč paradigms are elaborations not found in the source.
documents.s: 5 and above: genitive plural of noun

documents.t: The numeral jedin is declined like a hard adjective (dobry-type) for all forms except the masculine nominative singular animate, which ends in -in rather than -y. It has both singular and plural paradigms (plural for pluralia tantum nouns and distributive uses). Case Masc. sg. anim. Masc. sg. inan. Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all genders) Nom. jedin jedin jedno jedna jedni (m.), jedne (f./n.) Acc. jednogo jedin jedno jednu jednyh (m.anim.), jedne (others) Gen. jednogo jednogo jednogo jednoj jednyh Dat. jednomu jednomu jednomu jednoj jednym Ins. jednym jednym jednym jednoju jednymi Loc. (o) jednom (o) jednom (o) jednom (o) jednoj (o) jednyh

These three numerals are declined together because they share the same paradigm. Only dva and obydva have a gender distinction in the nominative/accusative (masculine/neuter vs. feminine). The endings preserve remnants of the ancient dual number. Standard plural-based declension (preferred): Case dva m./n. dva f. (dvě) oba m./n. oba f. (obě) obydva m./n. obydva f. (obydvě) Nom. dva dvě oba obě obydva obydvě Acc. dvoh (anim.) / dva (inan.) dvě oboh (anim.) / oba (inan.) obě obydvoh (anim.) / obydva (inan.) obydvě Gen. dvoh dvoh oboh oboh obydvoh obydvoh Dat. dvom dvom obom obom obydvom obydvom Ins. dvoma dvoma oboma oboma obydvoma obydvoma Loc. dvoh dvoh oboh oboh obydvoh obydvoh Dual-based declension (optional): Interslavic permits the use of dual forms for dva, oba, and obydva. In this paradigm, the neuter nominative/accusative coincides with the feminine form (i.e., only dvě, obě, obydvě are used for neuter, not dva, oba, obydva). The dual-based declension of dva documented by Lombino (2021) distinguishes gender in the dative/instrumental as well: Case Masculine Feminine/Neuter All genders Nom. dva dvě — Acc. inan. = Nom. = Nom. — Acc. anim. dvu — — Gen./Loc. — — dvu Dat./Instr. dvoma dvěma — This paradigm directly mirrors the dual case-merging pattern found in nouns: nominative = accusative (with animate exception), genitive = locative, dative = instrumental. See the nouns chapter for the full optional dual number paradigm. The endings of these numerals resemble adjectival plural endings, which is natural given that they originally were adjectival in function.

The numerals tri and četyri do not distinguish gender. They are declined similarly to the plural of adjectives. Case tri četyri Nom. tri četyri Acc. trěh (anim.) / tri (inan.) četyrěh (anim.) / četyri (inan.) Gen. trěh četyrěh Dat. trěm četyrěm Ins. trěmi četyrěmi Loc. (o) trěh (o) četyrěh

The numerals from pet upwards are inflected as nouns of the kost-type (third-declension feminine). Only one number (nominative = accusative) is used throughout; the form for all oblique cases other than instrumental is identical with the genitive. Paradigm for selected numerals: Case pet (5) šest (6) sedm (7) osm (8) devet (9) deset (10) Nom./Acc. pet šest sedm osm devet deset Gen./Dat./Loc. peti šesti sedmi osmi deveti deseti Ins. petju šestju sedmju osmju devetju desetju For compound tens (50–90), only the second element (-deset) changes in declension; the first element (the unit) remains in its base form: Case petdeset (50) sedmdeset (70) devetdeset (90) Nom./Acc. petdeset sedmdeset devetdeset Gen./Dat./Loc. petdeseti sedmdeseti devetdeseti Ins. petdesetju sedmdesetju devetdesetju For the teens (11–19), the entire compound word is declined as a kost-type noun: jedinnadset, gen. jedinnadseti.

The numeral sto declines as a neuter noun (like slovo) with singular, plural, and dual forms. The dual is used in the numeral dvě stě "200"; the plural nominative sta is used for 300–400; the genitive plural sot is used for 500–900. Case Singular Plural Dual Nom./Acc. sto sta stě Gen. sta sot stu Dat. stu stam stama Ins. stom stami stama Loc. (o) stu (o) stah (o) stu In compound hundreds (dvě stě, tri sta, pet sot, etc.) both components are declined: Case 200 dvě stě 300 tri sta 500 pet sot Nom./Acc. dvě stě tri sta pet sot Gen. dvoh sot trěh sot peti sot Dat. dvom stam trěm stam peti stam Ins. dvoma stami trěmi stami petju stami Loc. (o) dvoh stah (o) trěh stah (o) peti stah

Tyseč: The word tyseč MAY be declined as a masculine noun (type dom), as a third-declension feminine noun (type kost), or as tyseča — a second-declension feminine noun (type žena). All three paradigms are accepted. Case Masc. type (dom) Fem. III (kost) Fem. II (tyseča) Nom. sg. tyseč tyseč tyseča Acc. sg. tyseč tyseč tyseču Gen. sg. tyseča tyseči tyseče Dat. sg. tyseču tyseči tyseče Ins. sg. tysečem tyseččju tysečeju Loc. sg. (o) tyseču (o) tyseči (o) tyseče Nom./Acc. pl. tyseče tyseči tyseče Gen. pl. tysecev tysečij tyseč Milion and miliard: Both are masculine nouns declined like dom: Case milion (sg.) milion (pl.) miliard (sg.) miliard (pl.) Nom. milion miliony miliard miliardy Acc. milion miliony miliard miliardy Gen. miliona milionov miliarda miliardov Dat. milionu milionam milliardu miliardam Ins. milionom milionami miliardom miliardami Loc. (o) milionu (o) milionah (o) milliardu (o) miliardah

Ordinal numerals indicate the position of an item in a sequence. Grammatically they are adjectives and MUST agree with the noun they modify in gender, number, and case. All ordinals except trětji are declined as hard adjectives (dobry-type; see the relevant section). The ordinal trětji follows the soft adjectival declension (svěži-type; see the relevant section).
Cardinal Latin ordinal 1 prvy 2 vtory or drugy 3 trětji 4 četvrty

Ordinals from 5th upward are formed by adding the hard adjectival ending -y (with appropriate gender/number/case inflection) directly to the cardinal form: Cardinal Ordinal (Latin) pet pety šest šesty sedm sedmy osm osmy devet devety deset desety jedinnadset jedinnadsety dvadeset dvadesety petdeset petdesety For hundreds, two patterns are acceptable: Unverified Content The source only mentions sotny or stoty for "100th." The extended forms for 200th, 500th, etc. (dvasotny, petsotny, dvastoty, petstoty) are not in the source. Suffix -sotny: sotny "100th", dvasotny "200th", petsotny "500th" Suffix -stoty: stoty "100th", dvastoty "200th", petstoty "500th" For thousands: tysečny "1000th". Unverified Content The forms milionny and miliardny are not mentioned in the source material. For millions: milionny "millionth". For milliards: miliardny "milliardth".

In a composite ordinal numeral, only the last element is declined as an ordinal; all preceding elements remain as cardinals in their nominative form: v tyseč devetsot osmdeset četvrtom godu "in the year 1984". Unverified Content This composite ordinal example is not in the source material. jedna tyseča dvě stě petdeset prvy "one thousand two hundred fifty-first". Unverified Content The convention of indicating ordinals with a digit followed by a period, and the example sentence, are not found in the source material. When written with digits, ordinals are indicated by a digit followed by a period (even when not at the end of a sentence): Aleksandr ima den rodenija 1. januara "Aleksandr's birthday is January 1st."

Unverified Content This entire subsection with the full declension table of sto dvadeset pety across all genders, numbers, and cases is not found in the source material. The source only states that ordinals "are inflected like adjectives." Ordinals are fully declined as adjectives. In composite ordinals, only the final element inflects. The following table shows the full declension of sto dvadeset pety "125th" (only the last element changes): Case Masc. sg. anim. Masc. sg. inan. Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Masc. pl. anim. Masc. pl. inan. Neut./Fem. pl. Nom. sto dvadeset pety sto dvadeset pety sto dvadeset peto sto dvadeset peta sto dvadeset peti sto dvadeset pete sto dvadeset pete Acc. sto dvadeset petogo sto dvadeset pety sto dvadeset peto sto dvadeset petu sto dvadeset petyh sto dvadeset pete sto dvadeset pete Gen. sto dvadeset petogo sto dvadeset petogo sto dvadeset petogo sto dvadeset petoj sto dvadeset petyh sto dvadeset petyh sto dvadeset petyh Dat. sto dvadeset petomu sto dvadeset petomu sto dvadeset petomu sto dvadeset petoj sto dvadeset petym sto dvadeset petym sto dvadeset petym Ins. sto dvadeset petym sto dvadeset petym sto dvadeset petym sto dvadeset petoju sto dvadeset petymi sto dvadeset petymi sto dvadeset petymi Loc. sto dvadeset petom sto dvadeset petom sto dvadeset petom sto dvadeset petoj sto dvadeset petyh sto dvadeset petyh sto dvadeset petyh (Cyrillic: substitute сто двадесет for sto dvadeset throughout; the final ordinal element follows the same pattern.)

Collective numerals refer to members of a closed group and can be translated as "a group of" or "-some". They are: Latin Meaning dvoje twosome, pair troje threesome četvero group of four petero group of five šestero group of six sedmero group of seven osmero group of eight devetero group of nine desetero group of ten oboje both (mixed gender) Unverified Content The source says collective numerals "can be used" in these situations but does not use SHOULD normative language. Collective numerals SHOULD be used instead of cardinal numerals in the following situations: When the group consists of both men and women: dva studenti "two male students", dvě studentky "two female students", dvoje studentov "two students (mixed)". With gender-neutral words like ljudi and děti: osmero ljudij "eight people", četvero dětij "four children". With neuter words denoting young animals (singular -e, plural -eta): troje telet "three calves". For objects that naturally come in pairs: dvoje rukavic "two gloves". With pluralia tantum (words existing only in the plural): dvoje dverij "two doors". Unverified Content The source states the verb is "in the 3rd person singular (neuter)" but does not use MUST normative language. The noun following a collective numeral is always in the genitive plural. If this noun phrase is the subject of a clause, the verb MUST be in the 3rd person singular neuter. Collective numerals may also be used without a noun: my oboje "both of us (m. + f.)", zabava v troje "a party for three".

The stems of collective numerals serve as the base for multiplicative (aggregative) adjectives meaning "X-fold": Latin Meaning jediny only, single dvojny double, twofold trojny treble, threefold četverny quadruple, fourfold peterny quintuple šesterny sextuple With the prefix po-, these adjectives become verbs meaning "to multiply by X": podvojiti "to double", potrojiti "to triple", početveriti "to multiply by four". The prefix raz- produces the converse: razdvojiti "to split in two", raztrojiti "to split into three".

Multiplicative adverbs are formed by adding -kratno to the cardinal stem: Latin Meaning jednokratno once, one time dvakratno twice trikratno three times četyrikratno four times petkratno five times mnogokratno many times nekolicokratno a few times Unverified Content The source uses the form několikokratno for "a few times", not nekolicokratno as written here.

Differential numerals indicate qualitative differentiation — "of different types" or "of how many kinds". The older form uses the collective stem directly: dvoj, troj (declined like the possessive pronoun moj). The more common form uses the suffix -aky: Latin Meaning dvojaky of two kinds trojaky of three kinds četveraky of four kinds peteraky of five kinds kolikoraky of how many kinds When adverbialised, the meaning becomes "in X ways": trojako "in three ways".

For the word "half", Interslavic uses pol (indeclinable) or its full noun form polovina / polovica (declined as a feminine noun). Pol MAY also be used as a prefix: pol-mrtvy "half-dead". All other fractions are formed by replacing the ordinal ending -y with -ina: Fraction Latin 1/3 tretjina 1/4 četvrtina 1/5 petina 1/6 šestina 1/7 sedmina 1/8 osmina 1/9 devetina 1/10 desetina 1/16 šestnadsetina 1/100 sotina or stotina Unverified Content The source gives the form as sedm trideseti-osmin (with the genitive form trideseti-), not sedm trideset-osmin as written here. These fraction nouns are feminine and are declined as second-declension nouns. Thus "7/38" is sedm trideset-osmin (the hyphen enhances clarity). Ordinal-based fractions (e.g., dvě trětje "two thirds") are also used, reflecting the pattern common across most Slavic languages.

When a numeral is used as a noun (referring to a digit, a bus line, a school grade, a playing card, a banknote, etc.), the following forms apply: For 2–4, the collective numeral stem plus -ka is used: dvojka "a two", trojka "a three", četverka "a four". For all other numerals, -ka is appended to the cardinal form: jedinka "a one", petka "a five", šestka "a six", etc. Unverified Content The claim that "not all forms in -ica are equally established" and that "forms in -ka are preferred" is not stated in the source material. The source simply presents both suffixes as alternatives. Alternatively, the suffix -ica MAY be used: jedinica, petica, šestica, sedmica, etc. Not all forms in -ica are equally established; forms in -ka are preferred.

Interslavic accommodates several suffixal patterns for denoting one of a set of twins, triplets, etc., reflecting variation across the Slavic languages: Suffix Example Meaning Language models -ne (pl. -neta) dvojne, trojne, četverne twin, triplet, quadruplet East Slavic -če (pl. -četa) dvojče, trojče, četverče twin, triplet, quadruplet Czech, Slovak -ak dvojak, trojak, četverak twin, triplet, quadruplet Polish -ček dvojček, trojček, četverček twin, triplet, quadruplet Slovene -ka dvojka, trojka, četverka twin, triplet, quadruplet South Slavic Unverified Content The SHOULD recommendation about regional audience guidance is not found in the source material. All five patterns are acceptable in Interslavic. The choice SHOULD be guided by the regional audience.

Unverified Content The RFC 2119 normative framing (MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY) is an editorial addition. The source material does not use normative language or RFC 2119 terminology. This section specifies the inventory and usage of conjunctions, particles, clitics, and other uninflected words in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

A coordinating conjunction joins two or more words, phrases, or clauses of equal syntactic weight. Coordinating conjunctions are invariable and stand between the items they conjoin. Form Meaning a and; but (contrastive) a takože as well as abo or ale but i and ili or jednako however než, neželi than pa so poněže whereas ubo therefore Unverified Content The source material lists i, a, and ale but does not elaborate on their semantic differences. The nuance description below is not sourced. The conjunctions i and a differ in nuance: i introduces a simple additive relation, while a introduces a contrastive or adversative relation ("and yet", "but"). The conjunction ale is more exclusively adversative.

A subordinating conjunction links a dependent (subordinate) clause to an independent (main) clause. Subordinating conjunctions are invariable. Form Meaning aby in order to ače although, though ako (li) if ako ne unless či whether, if dokolě as long as, while dopoka as long as dopoka ne till, until hoti although ibo since, because jer, jerbo because jestli if kako by as if kogda / kogdy when li whether, if odkogda / odkogdy ever since, since odkųd since poka while, so far as pokoliko inasmuch as, insofar as poneže whereas tako že so that toliko kako as much as tomu že because zato že because že that že by in order to, so that

Final (purpose) clauses expressing purpose MAY be introduced by že or da followed by the conditional particle by: Turisti posětet Niderlandiju, že by fotografovali tulipany. — "Tourists visit the Netherlands to photograph the tulips." Ona prinosi jabloka, da by děti byli zdrave. — "She brings apples so that the children will be healthy."

Unverified Content The MUST constraint requiring both members of the pair to be present is an editorial addition. The source lists correlative conjunctions but does not state normative usage rules. Correlative conjunctions occur in fixed pairs and frame the items they correlate. Both members of the pair MUST be present in the clause. Form Meaning čim … tym … the … the … ili … ili … either … or … kako … tako i … both … and … koliko … toliko … the more … the more … ne jedino … ale takože … not only … but also … ni … ni … neither … nor …

Unverified Content The source material states that li is "placed right after the focus point of the question, usually the verb" but does not use normative MUST language. The MUST constraint is editorial. The particle li is a sentential clitic used to form yes/no questions. It MUST be placed immediately after the word that carries the focus of the question, typically the finite verb: Kupil li otec knigu? — "Has father bought a book?" The particle li is unstressed and functions as an enclitic (see the relevant section). When used as a subordinating conjunction introducing an indirect yes/no question, li means "whether, if": Ne věm, kupil li otec knigu. — "I don't know whether father bought a book."

Unverified Content The source describes či as preceding the sentence but does not specify normative rules about its position or its equivalence with other question constructions in the detail given below. The particle či introduces a yes/no question by fronting the entire clause. When used to introduce a question, či appears at the very beginning of the sentence: Či otec kupil knigu? — "Has father bought a book?" As a subordinating conjunction , či introduces indirect yes/no questions meaning "whether": Sprašal jesm go, či on prijde. — "I asked him whether he would come." A yes/no question MAY alternatively be formed by intonation alone, without any question particle: Otec kupil knigu? — "Father bought a book?" All three constructions (intonation, či, li) are grammatically equivalent for direct yes/no questions. Unverified Content This entire section on the negative particle ne, including double negation rules and the MUST/OBLIGATORY constraints, has no basis in the designated source material (grammar-conjunctions.md / grammar-prepositions.md). These rules may reflect general Slavic grammar conventions but are not sourced here.

The particle ne is the primary negation marker. It MUST immediately precede the verb it negates: On ne ide. — "He is not going." Ja ne věm. — "I don't know." When negative pronouns or adverbs are present in the clause, the negative particle ne before the verb is OBLIGATORY. Double (or multiple) negation is grammatically required in Interslavic: Nikto ne prijde. — "No one will come." (lit. "No one not-will-come") Ničto ne znaju. — "They know nothing." Omission of ne before the verb when a negative word is present constitutes a grammatical error in Interslavic. Unverified Content This entire section on the optative particle nehaj and its usage for third-person imperatives has no basis in the designated source material. The MUST constraint on placement is editorial.

The particle nehaj is used to form third-person imperatives (jussives). It MUST precede the third-person present-tense verb form: Nehaj on govori! — "Let him speak!" Nehaj ona pride! — "Let her come!" Nehaj živut narodi! — "Let the peoples live!" The particle nehaj is invariable and does not inflect for person, number, or gender. TODO: Committee Input Needed The inventory of additional particles (e.g., da, že used as optative/hortative markers, the affirmative particle da "yes", the discourse particle ot, nu, to etc.) requires committee decision on normative status and recommended usage. A supplementary list of common uninflected discourse markers should be specified here. Unverified Content This entire section on clitics and clitic placement (), including the Wackernagel position constraint, clitic inventory tables, clitic ordering rules, and reflexive sę placement rules, has no basis in the designated source material. The clitic pronoun forms, verbal clitic forms, ordering constraints, and all MUST requirements are editorial additions without source support.

Clitics are phonologically unstressed words that attach prosodically to a neighboring stressed host. In Interslavic, clitics cluster in second position in the clause (Wackernagel position): after the first stressed constituent of the clause.

The clitic inventory of Interslavic comprises the following items: Short (clitic) pronoun forms: Person / Case Dative clitic Accusative / reflexive clitic 1sg mi mę 2sg ti tę 3sg masc./neut. mu go 3sg fem. jej ju 1pl nam nas 2pl vam vas 3pl jim jih Reflexive — sę Verbal clitics (short forms of byti): Form Function jesm 1sg present copula jesi 2sg present copula je / jest 3sg present copula jesmo 1pl present copula jeste 2pl present copula Conditional clitic: Form Function by conditional particle (all persons)

Clitics in Interslavic MUST cluster in second position in the clause: immediately after the first stressed constituent (which may be a word, a phrase, or a fronted subordinate clause). Placement of clitics at any other position in the clause is non-standard. Example: Ja mu go dal jesm. — "I gave it to him." (clitics mu go jesm in second position, after the subject ja)

When multiple clitics co-occur, they MUST appear in the following fixed order within the clitic cluster: Conditional / verbal auxiliary clitic (by, short forms of byti) Dative pronoun clitic (mi, ti, mu, jej, nam, vam, jim) Accusative pronoun clitic or reflexive sę (mę, tę, go, ju, nas, vas, jih, sę) Example: On by mu sę znal. — illustrating conditional > dative > reflexive ordering. TODO: Committee Input Needed The interaction of clitic ordering with the question particle li (which itself occupies second position after the verb) needs formal specification. Edge cases involving fronted či, multiple auxiliary clitics in compound tenses, and split constructions in relative clauses require committee decision.

The reflexive clitic sę follows the conjugated verb form when the verb is finite. In compound tenses (auxiliary + past active participle), sę MUST follow the auxiliary, not the participle: On sę myje. — "He washes himself." (simple present) On je sę myl. — "He has washed himself." (compound past; sę follows auxiliary je) Placement of sę directly after the participle (e.g., *On je myl sę) is non-standard.

Unverified Content The source material does not use RFC 2119 normative language. The original text uses informal phrasing like "should only be used" rather than formal MUST/SHALL/SHOULD/MAY distinctions. The RFC 2119 framing below is an addition not present in the source. This section specifies the case government of prepositions and the inventory of conjunctions in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Unverified Content The source uses "should only be used" (informal recommendation), not "MUST" (normative requirement). The source also describes the place/time rules as "a guideline, although there are exceptions," which is weaker than the unconditional principle stated here. Prepositions in Interslavic MUST be used according to their logical meaning, not according to idioms of any particular national Slavic language. For prepositions referring to place and time, the following principle applies: The genitive is used when movement away from or out of a location is expressed. The accusative is used when movement toward or into a location is expressed. The instrumental or locative is used when a stable location is expressed, with no movement. Most prepositions govern the genitive. This is especially true of all prepositions derived from nouns, adjectives, or adverbs.

The following prepositions govern the genitive case: Preposition Meaning bez without blizko near, close to dlja for (purpose) do to, towards, till iz from, out of iz-medžu from between iz-nad from above iz-pod from under iz-prěd from before iz-srěd from among iz-za from behind kolo next to, around kromě except mimo past, by naprotiv opposite od of, from; since; by (agent) odnosno concerning, about okolo around; about, approximately podčas during, so long as podle near, beside pomimo in spite of poprěk across pored next to poslě after posrěd amidst, in the middle of, among posrědstvom by means of, using povrh to the upper side of, atop srěd among, amid suprotiv contrary to u at, at the place of vdolž along vměsto instead of vně outside of vnutri inside, within vslěd following, because of, as a result of za during, in times of

The following prepositions govern the dative case: Preposition Meaning blagodare, dekujuči, hvala thanks to k to, towards (direction) po according to, per, by, in the manner of; for … each protiv against soglasno, suglasno according to

The following prepositions govern the accusative case exclusively: Preposition Meaning črěz, prěz through, across, via po for (in pursuit of, to obtain); for (the Xth time); up to za for, because of, due to, in exchange for, in favour of, instead of
The following prepositions govern the instrumental case exclusively: Preposition Meaning ponad above, beyond s, so with, together with; by means of, using

The following prepositions govern the locative case exclusively: Preposition Meaning o, ob about; concerning, regarding po after, following; along(side); throughout, all over pri near; during (rule of a king)

The following five prepositions govern two cases. The instrumental is used when the location is stable; the accusative expresses directed motion toward the location. Example: Kot spi pod stolom — "The cat is sleeping under the table" (instrumental, static); Kot poběgl pod stol — "The cat fled under the table" (accusative, motion). Preposition Meaning medžu between (place and time) nad above, over, beyond pod under prěd before, in front of, ahead of (place and time); prior to, ago za behind (place); after (time) The preposition za also governs the genitive and the accusative in distinct meanings.

The prepositions v and na behave like those in the relevant section, except that the locative (not the instrumental) is used for a stable location. Preposition With locative (static) With accusative (motion) na on, at on(to), unto v, vo in, within into Unverified Content This entire subsection has no corresponding section in the source material. The source lists the forms s, so and v, vo but provides no explicit rule about when the extended forms should be used and does not contain a SHOULD requirement for this alternation.

The prepositions s and v take the extended forms so and vo before consonant clusters and before certain pronouns, in order to avoid difficult consonant sequences. Writers SHOULD apply this alternation wherever the resulting consonant cluster would otherwise be phonologically difficult. Examples: so mnoj "with me"; vo vsěh "in all."
documents.s: Fleeting Vowel in Prepositions s and v

documents.t: The following multi-word combinations function as prepositions and are often translated as single prepositions in other languages. This list is not exhaustive. Preposition Case governed Meaning bez obzira na acc. regardless of čto se tyče gen. regarding, with regard to na prěkor gen. in defiance of ne gledajuči na acc. disregarding, despite, notwithstanding od strany gen. by, on the part of po odnošenju k dat. regarding, with regard to pod vodstvom gen. under the leadership of s izključenjem gen. except, with the exception of s obzirom na acc. with regard to s pomočju gen. with the help of Unverified Content The source lists the meaning of sgodno s as only "conform." The additional gloss "in accordance with" is not present in the source. | sgodno s | instr. | conform, in accordance with | | susedno do | gen. | next to, neighbouring | | v času | gen. | during | | v imenu | gen. | in the name of | | v obličju | gen. | in the face of | | v odrazlikě od | gen. | unlike, in contrast to | | v odnošenju k | dat. | regarding, with regard to | | v ramah, v ramkah | gen. | within the framework of, as a part of | | v protivnosti k | dat. | unlike, in opposition to | | v smyslu | gen. | in the sense of | | v različju od | gen. | unlike | | v svezi s | instr. | in connection with | | v těčenju | gen. | during | | v vrěmenu | gen. | during | | v zaměn za | acc. | instead of, in exchange for | Many prepositions may also be used as verbal prefixes. See the section on word formation for details.

A conjunction is a part of speech that connects two words, sentences, phrases, or clauses. Conjunctions are invariable and stand between the items they conjoin. There are three types: coordinating conjunctions join items of equal syntactic weight; correlative conjunctions work in pairs; subordinating conjunctions link a dependent clause to an independent clause.
Conjunction Meaning a and, but a takože as well as abo or ale but i and ili or jednako however než, neželi than pa so poněže whereas ubo therefore

Correlative conjunctions occur in fixed pairs: Conjunction Meaning čim … tym … the … the … ili … ili … either … or … kako … tako i … both … and … koliko … toliko … the … the … ne jedino … ale takože not only … but also … ni … ni … neither … nor …

Conjunction Meaning aby in order to ače although, though ako (li) if ako ne unless či whether, if dokolě as long as, while dopoka as long as dopoka ne till, until hoti although ibo since, because jer, jerbo because jestli if kako by as if kogda / kogdy when li whether, if odkogda / odkogdy ever since, for as long as, since odkųd since poka while, so far as pokoliko inasmuch as, insofar as poneže whereas tako že so that toliko kako as much as tomu že because zato že because že that že by in order to, so that
This section specifies the forms, inflection, and usage rules for all pronominal categories in Interslavic. All normative statements use MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Personal pronouns in Interslavic inflect for six cases (nominative, accusative, genitive, dative, instrumental, locative). There is no vocative for pronouns. The reflexive pronoun sebe inflects identically to the second-person singular but has no nominative form. Third-person pronouns also distinguish gender (masculine, feminine, neuter in the singular) and animacy (masculine plural only: oni for groups containing male referents, one for all others). The third person is historically derived from demonstrative pronouns and therefore behaves differently from first- and second-person forms. Full paradigm — singular: Case 1sg ja 2sg ty 3sg m. on 3sg n. ono 3sg f. ona Nom. ja ty on ono ona Acc. mene, mę tebe, tę jego, go jego, go jų Gen. mene tebe jego jego jej Dat. mně, mi tobě, ti jemu, mu jemu, mu jej Ins. mnojų tobojų jim jim jejų Loc. (o) mně (o) tobě (o) njem (o) njem (o) njej Full paradigm — plural: Case 1pl my 2pl vy 3pl m. oni 3pl f./n. one Nom. my vy oni one Acc. nas vas jih jih Gen. nas vas jih jih Dat. nam vam jim jim Ins. nami vami jimi jimi Loc. (o) nas (o) vas (o) njih (o) njih Notes on the third-person plural: Unverified Content The source material does not describe this distinction as "obligatory" nor specify the exact conditions for using oni vs. one in this level of detail. In the plural, Interslavic maintains two nominative forms: oni is used for groups that include at least one male person or male animal; one is used for all other groups (exclusively female, mixed-gender inanimate, neuter). This distinction is obligatory in the nominative.

Several personal pronouns have two sets of forms: a long (full) form and a short (clitic) form. The clitic forms appear in parentheses in the paradigm above (e.g., mę, tę, go, mi, ti, mu). The following rules govern their distribution: The long form MUST be used after a preposition: k mně "to me", za tebe "for you", pri njim "with him". The long form MUST be used in emphatic or contrastive contexts: Podavali sųt ne mně, a jemu "They served not me but him." The long form SHOULD be used in sentence-initial position: Mně ješče ne podali sųt obědų "They have not yet served me lunch." The short clitic form SHOULD be used in all other positions (unstressed, non-initial, not after a preposition): Prinesi mi tų knigų "Bring me that book"; Vidiš li mę? "Do you see me?" Because not all verb endings are equally transparent to all speakers of Slavic languages, pro-drop (omitting personal pronouns when they are the subject of a clause) is NOT RECOMMENDED. Speakers SHOULD include the subject pronoun explicitly: ja čitaju is preferred over bare čitaju, though the latter is not ungrammatical. The pronoun on refers to any noun of masculine grammatical gender, not only to male beings. The pronoun ona refers to any noun of feminine grammatical gender.

The dual personal pronouns are entirely optional and correspond to the optional dual number in nouns and verbs (see the nouns chapter for the full discussion of dual number status). They are based on Roberto Lombino's reconstruction (2021) from Old Church Slavonic and Slovenian models. Dual nominative forms: Person Masculine Feminine 1du. ("we two") na ně 2du. ("you two") va vě 3du. ("they two") ona one Note on ně The feminine 1du. form ně is an innovation created by analogy with the dva / dvě gender distinction. It is not attested in Old Church Slavonic or any natural Slavic language, where only na or vě occur. It was proposed on the Interslavic server as a logical extension of the system. Full dual paradigm: Case 1du. na/ně 2du. va/vě 3du. ona/one Nom. na (m.) / ně (f.) va (m.) / vě (f.) ona (m.) / one (f.) Acc. naju vaju ju / jeju / neju Gen./Loc. naju vaju ju / jeju / neju Dat./Instr. nama vama jima / njima The third-person dual genitive/accusative forms ju / jeju / neju parallel the singular third-person alternation go / jego / njego (short / full / post-prepositional). Dual possessive pronouns: The dual possessive pronouns may be either indeclinable (genitive-based) or declinable (adjectival): Indeclinable Declinable Meaning naju najin "of us two, our (dual)" vaju vajin "of you two, your (dual)" jeju jejin (?) "of them two, their (dual)" The declinable forms are declined like moj. The 3du. declinable possessive jejin is tentative; in the singular, jejin means "her", so if that form is replaced by jejny (by analogy with jihny), then jejin becomes available for dual use. Example: naju knjiga = najina knjiga = "the book of us two" Dual adjective forms: Adjectives in the dual follow this pattern: Case Masculine Feminine/Neuter Nom. mlada / svěža mlade / svěže Gen./Loc. mladu / svěžu mladu / svěžu Dat./Instr. mladyma / svěžima mladyma / svěžima The possessive pronouns moj, tvoj, naš, vaš follow the same dual adjective pattern.

Unverified Content The source material consistently writes the reflexive clitic as se (e.g. Ja myju se), not sę. The use of sę throughout this section cannot be verified against the source. The reflexive pronoun sebe has no nominative form. It does not inflect for person, number, or gender. Its case endings are identical to those of the second-person singular pronoun ty. Case Long form Short (clitic) form Nom. — — Acc. sebe sę Gen. sebe — Dat. sobě si Ins. sobojų — Loc. (o) sobě — The short clitic sę is used with reflexive verbs: Ja myju sę "I am washing myself." When emphasis is required, the long form MUST be used: Ja myju jedino sebe "I wash nobody but myself." After a preposition, the long form MUST be used: on boji sę sebe. The absence of number and person inflection in the reflexive pronoun means it can indicate that the action of a transitive verb is directed at the subject regardless of whether that subject is singular or plural. The reflexive pronoun also serves as a reciprocal pronoun. Oni bijut sę may mean either "They are hitting themselves" or "They are hitting each other." To make the reciprocal reading explicit, the formula jedin drugogo SHOULD be added: Oni bijut sę jedin drugogo.

The reflexive possessive svoj "one's own" is used whenever the possessor is coreferential with the grammatical subject of the same clause, regardless of person or number. Ja myju svoje avto "I am washing my (own) car." My ljubimo svoju zemlju "We love our (own) land." Pjotr dal Ivanu svoju knigu "Pjotr gave Ivan his [= Pjotr's] book." Pjotr dal Ivanu jegovu knigu "Pjotr gave Ivan his [= Ivan's] book." The contrast above is normatively significant: when the subject in a third-person clause is also the possessor, svoj MUST be used to express that meaning; use of jego / jej / jih unambiguously refers to a possessor distinct from the subject.

Possessive pronouns agree with the noun they modify in gender, number, and case. They are inflected like adjectives, with the exception of the masculine nominative singular (and inanimate accusative singular), where the zero ending is used rather than the adjectival -y / -i. The core possessive pronouns are: Pronoun Meaning moj my, mine tvoj your, yours (singular familiar) naš our, ours vaš your, yours (plural or formal) svoj one's own (reflexive possessive; see the relevant section) Declension of moj (representative paradigm for moj, tvoj, naš, vaš, svoj): Unverified Content The source material states that these pronouns are declined like svěži (soft adjective pattern) but does not provide a fully spelled-out paradigm table for moj. The individual cell values cannot be verified against the source. Case Masc. sg. (anim.) Masc. sg. (inan.) Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Masc. pl. (anim.) Masc. pl. (inan.) Neut. pl. Fem. pl. Nom. moj moj moje moja moji moje moje moje Acc. mojego moj moje mojų mojih moje moje moje Gen. mojego mojego mojego mojej mojih mojih mojih mojih Dat. mojemu mojemu mojemu mojej mojim mojim mojim mojim Ins. mojim mojim mojim mojejų mojimi mojimi mojimi mojimi Loc. (o) mojem (o) mojem (o) mojem (o) mojej (o) mojih (o) mojih (o) mojih (o) mojih Unverified Content The claim that naš and vaš specifically follow the "soft adjectival pattern (svěži-type)" is not stated in these terms in the source material. The source says these pronouns decline "like svěži" but does not single out naš and vaš as having a distinct pattern from moj/tvoj. The pronouns naš and vaš follow the soft adjectival pattern (svěži-type) in their oblique forms. The pronouns moj, tvoj, naš, vaš, svoj, and čij decline like svěži; see the relevant section Third-person possessives: For third-person possession, Interslavic most commonly uses the genitive form of the corresponding personal pronoun, which is uninflected: Form Meaning jego his, its (masc./neut.) jej her, its (fem.) jih their These genitive-possessive forms are not declined and do not change to agree with the possessed noun. Alternatively, the following adjectival possessive forms MAY be used. These are declined like hard adjectives (dobry-type): Form Meaning jegov, jegova, jegovo his jejin, jejina, jejino her jihny, jihna, jihno their The genitive forms (jego, jej, jih) are preferred in standard Interslavic. The adjectival forms (jegov, jejin, jihny) are fully acceptable and reflect patterns found in several Slavic literary norms. Interrogative and indefinite possessives: The interrogative possessive pronoun čij "whose" and its derived forms ničij "nobody's", něčij "somebody's", etc. are inflected like moj. For the full system of derived possessives see the relevant section (indefinite pronouns) and (correlative table).

The primary demonstrative pronoun is toj (masculine), ta (feminine), to (neuter), meaning "this / that". This form SHOULD be used whenever there is no need to distinguish explicitly between proximal "this" and distal "that". The variant form ten for the masculine nominative singular is used in western Slavic contexts; in other contexts toj is the preferred form. Declension of toj: Unverified Content The accusative row appears to contain errors: neuter accusative is given as toj (should likely be to, matching nominative), and feminine accusative as to (should likely be tų). The values in the accusative row appear shifted by one column. The source material does not provide a full declension table for toj to verify against. Case Masc. sg. (anim./inan.) Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Masc. pl. (anim.) Masc. pl. (inan.) Fem./Neut. pl. Nom. toj / ten to ta ti te te Acc. togo toj to tų tyh / těh te Gen. togo togo toj tyh / těh tyh / těh tyh / těh Dat. tomu tomu toj tym / těm tym / těm tym / těm Ins. tym / těm tym / těm tojų tymi / těmi tymi / těmi tymi / těmi Loc. (o) tom (o) tom (o) toj (o) tyh / těh (o) tyh / těh (o) tyh / těh The plural oblique forms těh, těm, těmi are found particularly in Russian, Bulgarian, and Czech; the variants tyh, tym, tymi are found in other Slavic languages. Both are acceptable in Interslavic.

When a speaker needs to explicitly distinguish between "this one (here)" and "that one (over there)", Interslavic provides: tutoj "this (near the speaker)" tamtoj "that (over there, distant from both speaker and addressee)" Both tutoj and tamtoj are declined exactly like toj (see table above). These forms SHOULD be used when spatial deixis needs to be made explicit.
documents.s: Proximal vs. distal distinction: tutoj and tamtoj

documents.t: A more precise three-way deictic system exists and MAY be used, particularly in literary or more archaic registers: Pronoun Nominative Meaning sej sej / sa / se "this" (near the speaker) toj toj / ta / to "that" (near the addressee) onoj onoj / ona / ono "yonder" (distant from both) ovoj ovoj / ova / ovo "this" (near the speaker; variant of sej) The pronoun sej has largely disappeared from most modern Slavic languages except in fossilized forms. Its use may therefore not be immediately transparent to all readers. Speakers choosing this system SHOULD ensure context makes meaning clear. Unverified Content The source material mentions sej (f. sa, n. se) but does not provide a full declension paradigm. This table appears to be extrapolated. Additionally, the accusative row shows the same column-shift issue as the toj table: neuter accusative is sej instead of expected se, and feminine accusative is se instead of expected sų. Declension of sej: Case Masc. sg. Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all) Nom. sej se sa si / se Acc. sego sej se sų / sih Gen. sego sego sej sih Dat. semu semu sej sim Ins. sim sim sejų simi Loc. (o) sem (o) sem (o) sej (o) sih Unverified Content The source material states that onoj is "declined like toj" but does not provide a full declension paradigm. This table is extrapolated. The accusative row shows the same column-shift issue: neuter accusative is onoj instead of expected ono, and feminine accusative is ono instead of expected onų. Declension of onoj: Case Masc. sg. Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all) Nom. onoj ono ona oni / one Acc. onogo onoj ono onų / onyh Gen. onogo onogo onoj onyh Dat. onomu onomu onoj onym Ins. onym onym onojų onymi Loc. (o) onom (o) onom (o) onoj (o) onyh Unverified Content The source material mentions ov (not ovoj) and states it is "declined like toj" but does not provide a full declension paradigm. The expanded form ovoj and this table are extrapolated. The accusative row shows the same column-shift issue: neuter accusative is ovoj instead of expected ovo, and feminine accusative is ovo instead of expected ovų. Declension of ovoj: Case Masc. sg. Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all) Nom. ovoj ovo ova ovi / ove Acc. ovogo ovoj ovo ovų / ovyh Gen. ovogo ovogo ovoj ovyh Dat. ovomu ovomu ovoj ovym Ins. ovym ovym ovojų ovymi Loc. (o) ovom (o) ovom (o) ovoj (o) ovyh

The primary interrogative pronouns are kto "who?" and čto (also written što) "what?". These pronouns do not inflect for number or gender. Unverified Content The source material mentions the inflection of kto and čto but does not provide the actual case forms in the available text. The specific forms in this table (e.g. kym / kěm, čim) cannot be verified against the source. Case kto čto Nom. kto čto Acc. kogo čto Gen. kogo čego Dat. komu čemu Ins. kym / kěm čim Loc. (o) kom (o) čem Both kto and čto serve as interrogative pronouns in direct and indirect questions, and as relative pronouns in subordinate clauses. The same uninflected form functions in both roles: Oni sųt besědovali o tom, čto bylo je pisano v gazetah. "They were talking about what had been written in the newspapers." Ja zapytal sòm, u kogo oni sųt kupili ovų rybų. "I asked from whom they bought that fish."

The pronoun ktory "which, that" is the standard relative pronoun. It inflects like a hard adjective (dobry-type) and agrees with its antecedent in gender and number; it takes its case from its function in the relative clause. The South Slavic alternative koj is inflected like moj and is fully interchangeable with ktory in both relative and interrogative uses. A third, more archaic option is iže. In the nominative it is invariable across genders and numbers. In oblique cases it is formed from the corresponding form of the personal pronoun on / ona / ono with the suffix -že: genitive masculine singular jegože, dative masculine singular jemuže, etc. This form MAY be used in literary or archaic-register texts.

Additional interrogative determiners and pronouns include: Form Meaning Inflection koj which? like moj ktory which? hard adjective čij whose? like moj (zero ending in masc. nom. sg.) kaky what kind of? hard adjective koliko how many/much? uninflected kak how? uninflected (adverb) kdě where? uninflected (adverb) kamo whither? (goal) uninflected (adverb) kųdy whither? (path) uninflected (adverb) odkųdy whence? uninflected (adverb) kogda when? uninflected (adverb) čemu why? (cause) uninflected (adverb) začto why? (purpose) uninflected (adverb) The pronoun čij "whose?" is declined like moj with zero ending in the masculine nominative singular. Unverified Content The claim that koliko is uninflected in Interslavic is not discussed in the source material. The pronoun koliko "how many/much?" is uninflected in Interslavic, unlike its Russian cognate сколько. The pronoun kaky "what kind of?" and ktory "which?" are declined as hard adjectives. For speakers who wish to sound more natural to South Slavic audiences, koj may serve in place of both kaky and ktory in interrogative function. TODO: Committee Input Needed The adverb kakòv (also meaning "what kind of?") appears in South Slavic usage as a preferred alternative to kaky for interrogative predicative contexts. The committee should determine whether kakòv warrants its own entry and inflection paradigm in this section, or whether it is treated solely under adjectives. Unverified Content The source material lists both kamo and kųdy but does not state a preference for kųdy over kamo, nor characterize kamo as "more archaic." The adverbs kamo "whither (to a goal)" and kųdy "whither (along a path)" are both acceptable; kamo is more archaic and is mainly preserved in Serbo-Croatian cognate usage. Kųdy is the more widely understood form and SHOULD be used in standard Interslavic when the meaning is directional motion generally. Unverified Content The source material lists both "why (purpose)" and "why (reason)" as categories but does not elaborate on the semantic distinction or recommend one form over the other. The adverbs čemu "why (cause)" and začto "why (purpose / for what)" differ semantically: čemu is RECOMMENDED when asking for a reason or cause; začto when asking for a goal or purpose.
documents.s: Other interrogative pronouns: kaky, čij, koliko

documents.t: The prefix ně- attached to an interrogative pronoun or adverb forms an indefinite pronoun or adverb, equivalent to English "some-" or "-some": Form Meaning někto someone, somebody něčto something někoj some (which) někaky some kind of něčij someone's několiko some amount of, a few někdě somewhere někamo to somewhere někųdy by some path někogda sometime, once někak somehow The forms někto, něčto, etc. are inflected like kto and čto respectively. The forms někoj, něčij, etc. are inflected like moj.

The prefix vs- forms universal (collective) pronouns meaning "every-, all-": Form Meaning vsi or vsekto everyone, everybody vse or vsečto everything ves (f. vsa, n. vse) whole, entire; all vsaky each, every vsekaky all kinds of vsečij everybody's The pronoun ves "whole, all" follows a special declension: Unverified Content The source material states that ves has a special declension but the actual case forms in the available source text are not fully spelled out. The specific forms in this table cannot be verified cell-by-cell. Case Masc. sg. (anim.) Masc. sg. (inan.) Neut. sg. Fem. sg. Pl. (all) Nom. ves ves vse vsa vsi / vse Acc. vsego ves vse vsų vsih / vse Gen. vsego vsego vsego vsej vsih Dat. vsemu vsemu vsemu vsej vsim Ins. vsim vsim vsim vsejų vsimi Loc. (o) vsem (o) vsem (o) vsem (o) vsej (o) vsih The neuter singular vse is used in the collective sense "everything, all (of it)", equivalent to Russian всё. The plural vsi and vse are used in the collective sense "all (people/things)", with the same animacy distinction as the third-person plural pronoun: vsi ljudi "all people (men)", vsi mųži "all men", but vse ženy "all women".

The prefix ni- attached to an interrogative pronoun or adverb forms a negative pronoun or adverb: Form Meaning nikto no one, nobody ničto nothing nikoj not a single (which) nijedin not a single one nikaky no kind of ničij nobody's nikoliko none (in quantity) nikdě nowhere nikamo to nowhere nikųdy by no path nikogda never nikak in no way Unverified Content The forms nikoliko, nikamo, and nikųdy are not listed in the source material's negative pronoun inventory. They appear to be regular derivations but are unattested in the source. The forms nikto, ničto, etc. are inflected like kto and čto respectively. The forms nikoj, ničij, etc. are inflected like moj.

Unverified Content The double negation requirement and the constructions equivalent to Russian "некого/нечего" described below are not discussed in the source material. Negative pronouns MUST co-occur with the verbal negation particle ne. Double negation is grammatically obligatory in Interslavic and is not an error: Nikto ne zna. "Nobody knows." Ničto ne pomaga. "Nothing helps." Nikdě ne byh. "I was nowhere." Omitting ne when a negative pronoun is present is a grammatical error in Interslavic. This rule aligns with the universal Slavic pattern of negative concord. Note on constructions equivalent to Russian "некого/нечего": Interslavic does not have a separate series of pronouns corresponding to Russian некого, нечего, негде (meaning "there is no one to…, there is nothing to…, there is nowhere to…"). The equivalent construction in Interslavic uses a verbal copula in the present tense: Ne jest kogo spytati. "There is no one to ask." Ne jest o čem govoriti. "There is nothing to talk about." Ne jest kdě sę zajmati. "There is nowhere to study."

Interslavic has a systematic table of correlatives: a set of interrelated pronouns, determiners, and adverbs built on a regular grid of pronominal roots and semantic prefixes/suffixes. The system covers the semantic categories of person (kto / who), thing (čto / what), quality (kaky / what kind), quantity (koliko / how many), possession (čij / whose), selection (koj / which), manner (kak / how), location (kdě / where), direction-to (kamo / whither), path (kųdy / which way), origin (odkųdy / whence), and time (kogda / when). Semantic prefix keys: Prefix Meaning Example (none) interrogative / relative kto "who?" tu- here/proximal tukde "here" tam- there/distal tamtoj "that yonder" on- yonder ondě "over there" ně- some- (indefinite) někto "someone" poně- some (plural/vague amount) poněkdě "in some places" -koli / -либо / -небудь any- (free choice) ktokoli "anyone" ni- no- (negative) nikto "no one" vs- every- (universal) vsekto "everyone" in- other- (different) inokto "someone else" Correlative table (partial — person and thing columns): Semantic kto (who) čto (what) koj (which) kaky (what kind) čij (whose) koliko (how many) Interrogative kto čto koj kaky čij koliko Proximal — — tutoj — — — Distal — — tamtoj — — — Some (indef.) někto něčto někoj někaky něčij několiko Any (free choice) ktokoli čtokoli kojkoli kakykoli čijkoli kolikokoli No (negative) nikto ničto nikoj nikaky ničij nikoliko Every (universal) vsekto vsečto vsakov vsekaky vsečij — Other inokto inočto iny inaky inočij — Unverified Content The form vsakov in the "Every (universal)" row under the koj column is not attested in the source material, which uses vsaky for "each, every." Correlative adverb table: Semantic kak (how) kdě (where) kamo (whither) kųdy (which way) odkųdy (whence) kogda (when) Interrogative kak kdě kamo kųdy odkųdy kogda Proximal tak tu simo sųdy odsųdy sejčas Distal tak tam tamo tųdy odtųdy togda Some (indef.) někak někdě někamo někųdy odněkųdy někogda Any (free choice) kakkoli kděkoli kamokoli kųdykoli odkųdykoli kogdakoli No (negative) nikak nikdě nikamo nikųdy odnikųdy nikogda Every (universal) vsekak vsekdě vsekamo vsekųdy odvsekųdy vsekogda Other inako indě inamo inųdy odinųdy inogda Notes: In all cells where koj appears, ktory MAY be used interchangeably. In all cells where -gda appears, the variant -gdy MAY be used interchangeably. The prefix bylo MAY be combined with interrogative pronouns to indicate indifference: bylo kto "no matter who, just anybody", bylo čto "no matter what". Unverified Content The source material mentions poněkoj and poněkaky but does not list the adverbial forms poněkdě or poněkogda. The forms poněkoj "some, several" and poněkaky "several kinds of" exist for referring to several unspecified items, and poněkdě "in some places", poněkogda "sometimes" for vague plurality in adverbial use. TODO: Committee Input Needed The correlative table should be reviewed for completeness against all attested Interslavic corpus forms. In particular: (a) the in- ("other") series adverbs have limited attestation outside literary Old Church Slavonic-influenced texts and their status in standard Interslavic should be confirmed; (b) the distinction between kamo (goal) and kųdy (path) in the correlative table should be consistently applied.
documents.s: Pronominal Adverbs and the Correlative System

documents.t: This section specifies the grammatical categories, conjugation classes, tense and mood systems, non-finite forms, and irregular verb paradigms for verbs in Interslavic. All normative rules use the terms MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, and MAY as defined in RFC 2119.

Interslavic verbs possess verbal aspect, a grammatical category inherited from Proto-Slavic and present in all Slavic languages. Every verb is lexically marked as either perfective (pf.) or imperfective (impf.), and most verbs exist in aspect pairs that share a common meaning but differ in their temporal perspective. Imperfective aspect focuses on the action itself: its ongoing progress, habituality, iteration, or duration. It does not assert completion. Perfective aspect focuses on the result or completion of the action. It presents the action as a bounded, whole event. Examples: Imperfective Perfective Gloss dělati sdělati "to do/make" čistiti izčistiti "to clean" pisati napisati "to write" Both forms MUST be listed separately in the dictionary; they cannot be predicted from each other without reference to the lexical entry.

Aspect pairs are formed by one of three mechanisms: Prefixation of a bare imperfective creates a perfective with a narrowed or directional meaning. The resulting perfective then frequently acquires a secondary imperfective (see the relevant section): pisati (impf.) → napisati (pf.) "to write / to have written" čistiti (impf.) → izčistiti (pf.) "to clean / to have cleaned" Suppletive pairs use morphologically unrelated roots: nazyvati (impf.) / nazvati (pf.) "to call, to name" prihoditi (impf.) / prijdti (pf.) "to come" podimati (impf.) / podjeti (pf.) "to take up, to undertake" Unverified Content The "suffix alternation" category and its examples (vråćati/vråtiti, ględěti/ględnųti) are not present in the primary source material. The source describes only prefixation and irregular/suppletive pairs. These examples may come from other sources. Suffix alternation without a prefix change: vråćati (impf.) / vråtiti (pf.) "to return" ględěti (impf.) / ględnųti (pf.) "to look / to glance"

When a prefixed perfective verb requires an imperfective counterpart, a secondary imperfective is derived from the perfective stem according to the following rules: Perfective ending Secondary imperfective Mechanism Example -ati -yvati / -ivati -yvati after hard consonant, -ivati after soft dokazati → dokazyvati "to prove"; poddržati → poddrživati "to maintain" -iti -jati (with vowel lengthening) Stem-final consonant + iotation: s/z/t/d/st/zd → š/ž/č/dž/šč/ždž; preceding o → a napraviti → napravjati; pozvoliti → pozvaljati; oprostiti → opraščati Monosyllabic stem -vati Appended directly to the stem priznati → priznavati; ukryti → ukryvati Unverified Content The normative claim that rules "MUST be applied in the order given above" is not stated in the source material. The source lists the three formation patterns without specifying a priority order. These rules MUST be applied in the order given above. When none of the rules apply predictably, the secondary imperfective is listed separately in the dictionary.

Unverified Content The source material provides informal guidance on aspect usage but does not formulate these as MUST/SHOULD normative rules. In particular, the rules about phase verbs, verbs of perception, and negated contexts are not stated in the source. The following normative rules govern aspect selection: An imperfective verb MUST be used to express an ongoing or progressive action: ja jesm dělal "I was doing / I have been doing." A perfective verb MUST be used to express a completed action whose result is relevant: ja jesm sdělal "I have done (and finished)." An imperfective verb MUST be used for habitual or repeated actions: on jezdil do Moskvy "he used to go to Moscow." An imperfective verb MUST be used with phase verbs (begin, continue, stop) and with verbs of perception governing a subordinate action. An imperfective verb SHOULD be used in negated contexts where completion of the action is denied: ja ne pisal "I was not writing / I did not write."

Verbs of motion in Interslavic make a three-way distinction rather than the two-way distinction of most other verbs: Type Example Meaning Non-directional imperfective jezditi movement without a specific direction ("to drive around") Directional imperfective jehati movement toward a goal ("to drive / be driving to") Perfective pojehati completed departure ("to have gone, to set off") Examples in context: Igor jezdil po Moskvě — "Igor was driving around in Moscow." Igor jehal do Moskvy — "Igor was driving to Moscow." Igor pojehal do Moskvy — "Igor has gone to Moscow." When a non-directional verb of motion takes a goal complement, it expresses habitual movement: Igor jezdil do Moskvy "Igor [always/often] went to Moscow." In prefixed compounds, the non-directional form MUST be used for the imperfective member and the directional form for the perfective member: Imperfective Perfective Gloss odhoditi oditi "to go away" dolětati doletiti "to arrive by flight" prinositi prinesti "to bring"
documents.s: Verbs of Motion: Three-Way Aspect Distinction

documents.t: All inflected forms of a verb derive from one of two stems: The infinitive stem is obtained by removing the infinitive ending -ti. It serves as the base for the past tense, conditional mood, past participles, and verbal nouns. The present tense stem is used for the present tense, imperative mood, and present participles. The infinitive ending in Interslavic is always -ti. Verbs with stems ending in -k or -g retain those consonants in the infinitive (mogti, pekti) rather than using the contracted forms (moči, peči) in order to keep the stem transparent. Special case — verbs in -sti: The infinitive stem may end in d, t, or s. These verbs are listed in the dictionary with the 3rd person singular present tense shown in parentheses, e.g. klasti (klade), pasti (pade), gnesti (gnete), to distinguish them from verbs like nesti (stem: nes-). When the present tense stem cannot be derived by the regular rules below, it is given in the dictionary. Examples: pisati (piše), htěti (hoče), spati (spi), zvati (zove), brati (bere), vzeti (vozme).
documents.s: Two-Stem System: Infinitive Stem and Present Tense Stem

documents.t: The First Conjugation covers verbs whose present tense stem does not end in -i. The present tense stem is derived from the infinitive stem as follows: Infinitive type Present tense stem Example Stem ends in consonant Identical to infinitive stem nesti → nes-; vezti → vez-; klasti (< klad-ti) → klad- -ati verbs; monosyllabic vowel stems; some -ěti verbs Infinitive stem + -j- dělati → dělaj-; uměti → uměj-; biti → bij-; čuti → čuj- -ovati / -evati verbs Replace -ova- / -eva- with -uj- kovati → kuj-; organizovati → organizuj- -nuti verbs Drop -u- from the suffix tegnuti → tegn-; dvignuti → dvign- Present tense endings for the First Conjugation: Person/Number Ending Alternate 1sg. -u -em 2sg. -eš — 3sg. -e — 1pl. -emo — 2pl. -ete — 3pl. -ut — Morphophonological note: If the present stem ends in k or g, these consonants become č and ž respectively before the front vowel e: mog-eš → možeš; pek-eš → pečeš.

The Second Conjugation covers verbs in -iti and most verbs in -ěti. The present tense stem ends in -i-. Infinitive Present stem Example: hvaliti / viděti hvaliti "to praise" hval-i- — viděti "to see" vid-i- — Present tense endings for the Second Conjugation: Person/Number Ending Alternate 1sg. -ju -im 2sg. -iš — 3sg. -i — 1pl. -imo — 2pl. -ite — 3pl. -et — Iotation in 1sg.: When the stem-final consonant is s, z, t, d, st, or zd, it merges with the -j- of the ending to produce š, ž, č, dž, šč, ždž respectively: Infinitive 1sg. derivation 1sg. form prositi "to ask" pros- + -ju prošu viděti "to see" vid- + -ju vidžu / vidim nositi "to carry" nos- + -ju nošu

A Third (contracted) Conjugation is an optional variant used principally in West and South Slavic. It applies to -ati and -ěti verbs that otherwise belong to the First Conjugation with the -aj- stem. The contracted forms compress the -aje- sequence, producing endings: -m, -š, -Ø, -mo, -te, -jut. Person/Number Full form Contracted form 1sg. dumajų dumam 2sg. dumaješ dumaš 3sg. dumaje duma 1pl. dumajemo dumamo 2pl. dumajete dumate 3pl. dumajųt (dumajųt) Use of the contracted forms is permitted (MAY) and represents a natural stylistic variant. It MUST NOT be treated as an error in any register.

The present tense is formed by appending the personal endings of the appropriate conjugation to the present tense stem. Paradigm — First Conjugation, example nesti "to carry": Person/Number Form 1sg. nesų / nesem 2sg. neseš 3sg. nese 1pl. nesemo 2pl. nesete 3pl. nesųt Paradigm — First Conjugation, example dělati "to do/make": Person/Number Form 1sg. dělajų / dělam 2sg. dělaješ / dělaš 3sg. dělaje / děla 1pl. dělajemo / dělamo 2pl. dělajete / dělate 3pl. dělajųt Paradigm — Second Conjugation, example hvaliti "to praise": Person/Number Form 1sg. hvaljų / hvalim 2sg. hvališ 3sg. hvali 1pl. hvalimo 2pl. hvalite 3pl. hvalęt Unverified Content The 3pl. form hvalęt uses the nasal vowel ę, but the source gives the ending as plain -et. The secondary source (code observations) shows the 3pl. of govoriti as govorųt (with -ųt), which contradicts both. The use of ę here is unverified. Note on 1sg. alternates: The -em / -im variants in the 1st person singular (e.g. nesem, hvalim) are characteristic of Slovak and South Slavic usage; the -ų / -jų forms are characteristic of East Slavic and Polish usage. Both sets are correct in Interslavic. Unverified Content The rule that negation is "always written separately" is not discussed in the source material. The source does not address negation of verbs. Negation is formed by prefixing the particle ne to the conjugated form; it is always written separately: ne govorjų "I do not speak", ne kažų "I will not say."

The dual number in verbs is entirely optional and corresponds to the optional dual in nouns and pronouns (see the nouns chapter for the full discussion of dual number status). Dual verb forms distinguish masculine from feminine/neuter in the endings. The dual has distinct forms for 1st person (1du.) and 2nd/3rd person (2du./3du., which share one form). Source: Lombino (2021) The dual verb paradigm below is based on Roberto Lombino's reconstruction from Old Church Slavonic and Slovenian models. The ending -tě for 2du./3du. feminine/neuter was proposed by Konstantin as an innovation to avoid ambiguity with other forms, but is not attested in Old Church Slavonic. Present tense dual endings (example: dělati "to do"): Person Masculine Feminine/Neuter 1du. na dělajeva ně dělajeve 2du. va dělajeta vě dělajete (or -tě) 3du. ona dělajeta one dělajete (or -tě) The dual personal pronouns used with these forms are: na / ně (1du. masc. / fem.), va / vě (2du.), ona / one (3du.). Simple past (aorist) dual endings: Person Masculine Feminine/Neuter 1du. dělahova dělahově 2du./3du. dělasta dělaste (or -tě) For the imperfect, the same pattern applies with the imperfect stem: dělašta / dělašte. Compound past (perfect) dual: The L-participle takes dual gender agreement, and the auxiliary byti takes dual forms: Person Masculine Feminine/Neuter 1du. dělala (je)sva dělale (je)svě 2du./3du. dělala (je)sta dělale (je)ste (or -tě)
documents.s: Optional Dual Number in Verb Conjugation

documents.t: The primary past tense of Interslavic is the compound past, formed by combining the present tense of the auxiliary verb byti "to be" with the L-participle of the main verb. The L-participle is formed by appending the suffix -l (+ gender/number ending) to the infinitive stem: Gender/Number Suffix Example (pisati) Masculine singular -l pisal Feminine singular -la pisala Neuter singular -lo pisalo Plural (all genders) -li pisali Literary Register: Gender-Differentiated Plural L-Participle In Standard Interslavic, the L-participle plural ending is -li for all genders. Lombino (2022) proposes restoring the gender distinction in the plural for the literary register: -li for masculine animate plural: dělali (men did) -ly for masculine inanimate and feminine plural: dělaly (women did, things did) -la for neuter plural: dělala (neuter things did) Gender precedence in mixed groups follows: masculine animate > masculine inanimate/feminine > neuter. This system is preserved in Ukrainian (робили/робили/робила) and partially in Polish (robili/robiły), where the virile/non-virile distinction in past tense forms is a core grammatical feature. Standard Interslavic's unified -li follows the Russian and South Slavic pattern where the plural L-participle is invariant. Literary Register: Full vs. Clitic Forms of byti Lombino (2022) formalizes a distinction between full (stressed) and clitic (unstressed) forms of the present tense of byti: Person Full form Clitic form 1sg. jesmj sŭm 2sg. jesi si 3sg. jest je 1pl. jesjmo sjmo 2pl. jesjte sjte 3pl. jesųt sųt Full forms appear sentence-initially or after a personal pronoun; clitic forms appear in second position (Wackernagel position) after the first stressed phrase: Jesmj gladŭn. vs. Gladŭn sŭm. This distinction mirrors Serbian/Croatian, Slovenian, Bulgarian, and Slovak clitic systems. Standard Interslavic already notes that the short forms MAY be used but does not formalize their positional distribution. Similarly, the first person pronoun has full (az) and clitic (ja) forms, with az used when stressed and ja in second position: Az ne imějų vrěmene. ("I don't have time" --- emphasis on "I") vs. Ne imějų ja vrěmene. ("I don't have time" --- emphasis on "don't have"). Full compound past paradigm (pisati): Person/Number Form 1sg. (m./f.) jesm pisal / jesm pisala 2sg. (m./f.) jesi pisal / jesi pisala 3sg. (m./f./n.) jest pisal / jest pisala / jest pisalo 1pl. jesmo pisali 2pl. jeste pisali 3pl. sųt pisali In the 3rd person, the forms of byti (jest, sųt) are typically omitted in informal usage, though their use is not incorrect: on pisal "he was writing / he wrote."

The L-participle is always formed regularly from the infinitive stem, with one exception: idti "to go" has the suppletive L-participle šel (m.), šla (f.), šlo (n.), šli (pl.). The alternative forms išel, išla etc. are also acceptable. This suppletive form carries through all prefixed compounds: pojdti → pošel, vojdti → všel, prijdti → prišel, etc. Unverified Content The rule about consonant cluster preservation in the L-participle (no cluster simplification) is not discussed in the source material. The examples mogl, grebl, izrěkl are not attested in the source. When the infinitive stem ends in a consonant cluster (e.g. mogti → stem mog-), the L-participle does not undergo the cluster simplification that occurs in East Slavic: mogl, grebl, izrěkl (not mog, greb, izrěk).

The aspectual value of the compound past is determined by the lexical aspect of the main verb: Imperfective + compound past = ongoing or habitual past: ja jesm dělal "I was doing / I used to do." Perfective + compound past = completed past (perfect): ja jesm sdělal "I have done (and finished)."
documents.s: Interpretation: Aspect Determines Perfectivity of Past

documents.t: Committee Review Needed The simple past conflates the aorist and imperfect. The Steen grammar treats this as a single optional category; the Ranmaru sources note the distinction and propose treating them separately for audiences familiar with South Slavic. The present specification follows the Steen position: aorist and imperfect are merged into a single simple past paradigm for Standard Interslavic, with an informative note that speakers with South Slavic backgrounds MAY distinguish them. The detailed aorist/imperfect paradigms given in the relevant section are optional extensions. Literary Register: Distinct Aorist and Imperfect Lombino (2022) treats the aorist and imperfect as distinct tenses in the literary register, following the full Common Slavic and Old Church Slavonic system: The aorist is used primarily with perfective verbs and denotes a completed action in the past. The imperfect is used with imperfective verbs and denotes an ongoing or habitual action in the past. Its stem is formed from the infinitive stem with the suffix -ja- for consonant-stem verbs: nesti > nesjah, vezti > vezjah, idti > idjah. The literary register also includes a full tense system with: Pre-present (perfect): compound past with byti Pre-past (pluperfect): běh + L-participle Future: bųdų + infinitive Pre-future (future perfect): bųdų + L-participle Future-in-the-past: imperfect of byti (budjah) + infinitive This expanded tense system mirrors the full Old Church Slavonic temporal paradigm, which no single modern Slavic language preserves in its entirety but whose individual elements survive across the family. The simple past is an optional tense that fulfills the combined role of the aorist and imperfect. It denotes an action that took place in the past and is formed by adding personal endings directly to the infinitive stem. Its use is stylistically marked and SHOULD NOT be used in communication with speakers whose native language is East Slavic or Polish, as the forms are not broadly intelligible. Endings — vowel-final infinitive stem: Person/Number Ending 1sg. -h 2sg. -še 3sg. -še 1pl. -hmo 2pl. -ste 3pl. -hų Unverified Content The 3pl. ending is written as -hų (with nasal ų) but the source gives it as -hu (without nasal marker). The same applies to the consonant-stem variant -ehų vs. source -ehu. Endings — consonant-final infinitive stem (connecting vowel e is inserted): Person/Number Ending 1sg. -eh 2sg. -eše 3sg. -eše 1pl. -ehmo 2pl. -este 3pl. -ehų Unverified Content The First Palatalization rule for simple past consonant stems (k → č, g → ž, h → š) and the example paradigms for izrěkti and mogti are not in the source material. The source gives no examples of the simple past with velar-stem verbs. Before the connecting vowel e, the consonants k, g, h undergo the First Palatalization: k → č, g → ž, h → š. Simple past paradigm — selected examples: Person/Number kazati nesti izrěkti mogti 1sg. kazah neseh izrěčeh možeh 2sg. kazaše neseše izrěčeše možeše 3sg. kazaše neseše izrěčeše možeše 1pl. kazahmo nesehmo izrěčehmo možehmo 2pl. kazaste neseste izrěčeste možeste 3pl. kazahų nesehų izrěčehų možehų

In the extended tense system, the aorist and imperfect are distinct tenses rather than a single "simple past." The aorist denotes a past action that reached its endpoint; it is the narrative tense of foregrounded events. The imperfect denotes a past action that was ongoing, durative, or repeated; it provides background narration. As a general principle, perfective verbs typically take the aorist and imperfective verbs typically take the imperfect, since aspect and tense reinforce each other. However, cross-combinations are possible (see "Usage notes" below). Aorist Formation​ The aorist is formed from the infinitive stem (or equivalently, the L-participle stem without -l). Its endings are identical to the merged simple past given above, with one key difference: the 2sg. and 3sg. have a zero ending (no -še): Person/Number Vowel-stem example (sdělati) Consonant-stem example (odvesti) 1sg. sdělah odvedeh / odvedoh 2sg. sděla odvede 3sg. sděla odvede 1pl. sdělahmo odvedehmo 2pl. sdělaste odvedeste 3pl. sdělašę / sdělahų odvedešę / odvedehų The 3pl. ending -šę follows the Serbian/Croatian pattern. Other living languages with the aorist (Bulgarian, Macedonian) use only -hų in this position; both forms are acceptable in Interslavic. Imperfect Formation​ The imperfect is formed from the present tense stem (specifically the 3sg. present form). Three formation strategies exist, all attested in living Slavic languages. They may be combined. Strategy 1 — -jah endings (from the 3sg. present): The most regular approach. Take the 3sg. present form and append -jah, -jaše/-ješe, etc. If the 3sg. already ends in -j, the endings attach directly to it. Person/Number Ending 1sg. -jah 2sg. -jaše / -ješe 3sg. -jaše / -ješe 1pl. -jahmo 2pl. -jašte / -ješte 3pl. -jahų Examples: peče → pečejah; ǉubi → ǉubijah; pije → pijah; dělaje → dělajah. For verbs with 3sg. in -aje/-a (the -ati class), shortened forms without -ja- are also acceptable: dělah, dělaše (identical to the merged simple past). Strategy 2 — -ěh endings (with vowel ě): Close to Old Czech and often Bulgarian. Take the 3sg. present, drop the final vowel, and add -ěh, -ěše, etc. Examples: prosi → prosěh; vede → veděh; peče → pečěh/:isv[pečeh]. After č, j, š, ž, the vowel ě MAY be simplified to e. Strategy 3 — consonant alternation (with -ah endings): Close to Serbian/Croatian. Based on the past passive participle stem (the -eny/-eńje form). If a soft consonant precedes -eny, add -ah, -aše; otherwise add -jah, -jaše (which may trigger iotation). Examples: govorjeny → govorjah; prošeny → prošah; vedeny → vedjah/:isv[ved́ah]. Imperfect Paradigm Example​ Full paradigm of viděti (impf.) contrasted with uviděti (pf.) using Strategy 1: Person/Number Aorist (uviděti) Imperfect (viděti) 1sg. uviděh vidijah 2sg. uvidě vidijaše / vidiješe 3sg. uvidě vidijaše / vidiješe 1pl. uviděhmo vidijahmo 2pl. uviděste vidijašte / vidiješte 3pl. uviděšę / uviděhų vidijahų Distinctive Endings: Aorist vs. Imperfect​ The key formal differences between the two tenses are: Feature Aorist Imperfect 2sg./3sg. Zero ending (sděla, odvede) -jaše/-ješe/-ěše/-aše 2pl. -ste -šte (following Upper Sorbian and historical Proto-Slavic -šete) 3pl. -šę (optional) or -hų -hų only Stem base Infinitive stem Present tense stem (strategies 1–2) or passive participle stem (strategy 3) Irregular Imperfects​ byti: always běh, běše (alternative 2sg./3sg.: bě). iměti: imah/:isv[imaše] or imějěh/:isv[imějěše]. idti and compounds: id́ah/:isv[id́aše] or ideh/:isv[ideše]; compound prijdti → priideh/:isv[priide] (not *prišeh). dati: aorist dadeh/:isv[dadoh]/:isv[dah], with 2sg./3sg. dade/:isv[dado]/:isv[da]. Usage Notes​ Imperfect of perfective verbs is possible and expresses a repeated action that always reached completion: Vsegda, kogda pogledijah... "Every time I looked..." Aorist of imperfective verbs is possible, especially for verbs without a perfective counterpart, to emphasize that the action came to an end: Hodih v tu školu 9 let "I went to that school for 9 years (and then stopped)." Unresolved: Merging vs. separating aorist and imperfect The standard position (following Jan van Steenbergen's grammar) merges the aorist and imperfect into a single "simple past" with one set of endings. Roberto Lombino's proposal (2022) argues that this merger is unnatural for speakers of languages that preserve the distinction (Bulgarian, Macedonian, Serbian/Croatian) and provides detailed formation rules for keeping the tenses separate. The three imperfect formation strategies described above are from this proposal. Key tensions: Simplicity vs. naturalness: A merged paradigm is simpler for learners, but speakers of South Slavic languages expect the distinction. Stem choice for the imperfect: The three strategies (from 3sg. present with -jah, from 3sg. present with -ěh, from passive participle stem with consonant alternation) produce different forms; no single strategy has been adopted as normative. 2pl. ending: Should the imperfect use -šte (distinct from aorist -ste) or should both share -ste? The -šte form has historical and Upper Sorbian support but adds complexity. 3pl. aorist: Should the ending -šę (Serbian/Croatian pattern) be permitted alongside -hų?
