A committee decision is needed on whether the extended system formally recognizes two separate tenses or retains the merged simple past as the only option.
documents.s: Distinguishing the Aorist and Imperfect (Extended System)

documents.t: The pluperfect (past-before-past) is an optional tense. It is formed by combining the L-participle with the past tense of byti, either the simple past or compound past: ja běh dělal "I had been doing" (using simple past of byti) ja byl jesm dělal "I had been doing" (using compound past of byti) The pluperfect SHOULD be used to explicitly signal that one past action preceded another past action. Its use is optional and MAY be replaced by the compound past with appropriate temporal adverbs.

The primary future tense is formed by combining the future tense forms of byti with the infinitive of the main verb. The future forms of byti are conjugated as if from the stem bud- in the present tense: Person/Number byti future Full example (pisati) 1sg. bųdų / bųdem bųdų pisati 2sg. bųdeš bųdeš pisati 3sg. bųde bųde pisati 1pl. bųdemo bųdemo pisati 2pl. bųdete bųdete pisati 3pl. bųdųt bųdųt pisati Unverified Content The claim that the compound future is used for "both imperfective and perfective" verbs is not explicitly stated in the source. The source describes the compound future without specifying its applicability to perfective verbs. This construction is used for both imperfective and perfective verbs to express future meaning in Standard Interslavic.

In East and West Slavic languages, the present tense forms of a perfective verb regularly express future meaning (e.g. Russian напишу "I will write"). This usage is possible in Interslavic but SHOULD be avoided when addressing South Slavic speakers, for whom the present tense of a perfective verb does not carry automatic future meaning and may be misunderstood as present habitual. TODO: Committee Input Needed A normative ruling is needed on whether the use of the perfective present as future is (a) fully equivalent to the compound future, (b) a permitted stylistic variant, or (c) restricted to registers targeting non-South-Slavic audiences. Currently the Steen source recommends avoidance; the Ranmaru source explains the South Slavic motivation in detail. Unresolved: Multiple Future Tenses and Perfective Present Semantics Lombino (2022) describes a system of three semantically distinct future constructions in the literary register, each conveying a different degree of certainty or obligation: bųdų + infinitive = certain future at a concrete moment: Dnesj večer bųdų govoriti s prijateli. ("Tonight I will speak with friends." --- it is settled.) htěti/:isv[těti] + infinitive = planned or uncertain future: Za tydŭnj hoćų jěhati k roditeljem. ("In a week I intend to go to my parents.") imati + infinitive = obligatory future: Iz'jutra imajų cělyj dŭnj sěděti v školě. ("Tomorrow I have to sit in school all day." --- obligation, not desire.) Additionally, Lombino proposes that the perfective present may express general/gnomic truths where completion is inherent but time is unspecified: Bylŭn čaj pomože protiv mnogym bolestem. ("Herbal tea helps against many illnesses." --- a general fact about the effect, not a specific future event.) This semantic interpretation differs from both the East Slavic pattern (perfective present = future) and the South Slavic pattern (perfective present = subordinate clause only), and represents a third possibility attested in some West Slavic usage. All three future constructions can also be projected into the past (future-in-the-past) using the imperfect of the auxiliary: budjah čitati "I was going to read", hočah čitati "I intended to read", imah čitati "I was supposed to read". These distinctions are productive in individual Slavic languages (Czech, Slovak, and South Slavic for imati; Polish and Serbian/Croatian for htěti; English calques notwithstanding, the idti + infinitive pattern parallels French aller + infinitive and is attested colloquially in Czech and Polish). Whether and how to incorporate them into Interslavic norms remains undecided.

The conditional mood is formed by combining the conditional auxiliary forms of byti with the L-participle of the main verb. The conditional forms of byti derive historically from the aorist of byti: Person/Number Conditional auxiliary Example (dělati) 1sg. byh byh dělal(a) 2sg. bys bys dělal(a) 3sg. by by dělal(a/o) 1pl. byhmo byhmo dělali 2pl. byste byste dělali 3pl. by by dělali The L-participle MUST agree with the grammatical subject in gender and number (singular masculine, singular feminine, neuter, or plural). Examples: Ako byh ja věděl o tym, ne pošel byh tamo. — "If I had known about that, I would not have gone there." Dětę ne plakalo by, ako by go ničto ne bolělo. — "The child would not have cried if nothing hurt it." Unverified Content The distinction between South Slavic and West Slavic 3pl. conditional forms and the MAY permission for byhų are not discussed in the source material. Note on 3pl.: In the South Slavic tradition the 3pl. conditional auxiliary is by (same as 3sg.); in the West Slavic tradition a distinct 3pl. form byhų MAY be used.

The past conditional (expressing a counterfactual about the past) is formed by inserting the past form of byti into the standard conditional: ja byl byh dělal "I would have done" (masculine) ja byla byh dělala "I would have done" (feminine)

The imperative is formed from the present tense stem. Specifically, take the 3rd person singular present tense form and remove the personal ending to obtain the imperative base; then attach the imperative endings: Person/Number Ending after consonant Ending after -j- Ending after -j 2sg. -i -j -Ø (zero) 1pl. -imo -jmo -mo 2pl. -ite -jte -te Formation rule summary: Add -i to the present stem if the stem ends in a consonant; add -j if the stem ends in a vowel; add zero ending if the stem already ends in -j. Examples: Infinitive 3sg. present Imperative base 2sg. imp. 1pl. imp. 2pl. imp. nesti nese nes- nesi nesimo nesite dělati dělaje dělaj- dělaj dělajmo dělajte hvaliti hvali hvali- hvali hvalimo hvalite kovati kuje kuj- kuj kujmo kujte prositi proši proši- proši prošimo prošite Unverified Content The 3sg. present of prositi is shown as proši (with iotation), but the source states that iotation in 2nd conjugation only affects the 1sg. form (where -ju triggers the change). The 3sg. should be prosi (stem pros- + ending -i), and consequently the imperative forms should be prosi, prosimo, prosite. Morphophonological note: Before the connecting -i-, the consonants k, g, h undergo the First Palatalization: k → č, g → ž, h → š. This is triggered by the 3sg. form used as the base (pomože → pomoži, izrěče → izrěči). These forms are characteristic of West Slavic as well as Bulgarian and Macedonian. Unverified Content The special imperative rule for -avati verbs is not mentioned in the source material. The source describes imperative formation only from the present tense stem. Verbs in -avati (with suffix -va-): The imperative is formed from the infinitive stem via connecting -j-, not from the present stem: davati → davaj, sòzdavati → sòzdavaj. Accent note: It is recommended to shift stress to the ending in the imperative to distinguish it from the indicative, particularly for verbs with endings in -i/-imo/-ite: prósi (3sg. ind. "asks") vs. prosí (2sg. imp. "ask!"). Unverified Content The source says the accent shift "deserves recommendation" but does not formalize it as a normative rule. The indicative form cited here as "3sg." (prósi) appears to be the 2nd conjugation 3sg. present, which is correct.

Interslavic has no synthetic imperative for the 3rd person. Third-person commands and wishes are expressed with the optative particle nehaj followed by the indicative form of the verb: Nehaj umre! — "Let him die!" Nehaj žive dolgo! — "May he live long!"

The 1st person plural imperative functions as an inclusive hortative ("let us"): dělajmo — "let's do!" pojdimo --- "let's go!" Unresolved: Subjunctive Mood and Supine Lombino (2022) describes two additional verbal categories for the literary register that are absent from Standard Interslavic: Supine. The supine is a truncated form of the infinitive (infinitive minus the final -i, yielding forms ending in -t) used after verbs of motion: Hoćų spati. ("I want to sleep" --- infinitive) vs. Idų spat. ("I'm going to sleep" --- supine). The supine is preserved productively in Slovenian, Sorbian, and (vestigially) in Czech and Slovak. Its inclusion in Interslavic would provide a syntactic distinction between purpose-of-motion and general complement clauses that many Slavic speakers would recognize. Subjunctive (conjunctive) mood. Lombino proposes a full subjunctive mood distinct from the conditional, formed by adding shortened personal endings to the infinitive stem: dělam, dělas, děla(st), dělamo, dělate, děla. This mood would be used after conjunctions expressing uncertainty (da "that [subjunctive]", jestli "whether") and in subordinate clauses of purpose and doubt. The paradigm derives from the Old Church Slavonic subjunctive (itself historically the optative) and parallels the da-construction in South Slavic languages. The subjunctive would exist across multiple tenses (present, past, future, and their pre- variants), creating a substantial expansion of the verbal paradigm. The author acknowledges potential ambiguity between subjunctive and indicative forms for many verbs and proposes an epenthetic -d- to disambiguate: děladem, dělades (subjunctive) vs. dělajemo, dělajete (indicative). These categories reveal a fundamental tension: the conditional mood (byh dělal) already handles counterfactual and hypothetical meaning in Standard Interslavic, and adding a subjunctive would require speakers to master an additional paradigm whose functional territory overlaps significantly with the conditional. The supine, by contrast, is a small addition (a single form per verb) with clear distributional rules. No committee decision has been reached on either feature.

All infinitives end in -ti. This ending is invariant and serves as the dictionary citation form. The infinitive stem is the infinitive minus -ti. Unverified Content The source describes moči, peči, běči as "less transparent" but does not say a -ći variant is "fully acceptable" nor does it use the normative SHOULD NOT. The source also does not mention a -ći ending variant at all (it uses -či). Verbs whose stems end in -k or -g retain these consonants before -ti (mogti, pekti, běgti) rather than using the contracted South Slavic forms (moči, peči, běči). The -ći variant is fully acceptable as an alternative but SHOULD NOT be the primary form in Standard Interslavic. The form iti is an acceptable alternative to idti.

The present active participle is derived from the present tense stem by replacing the 3rd person plural ending -t with -či: First Conjugation: 3pl. ends in -ut → participle ends in -uči Second Conjugation: 3pl. ends in -et → participle ends in -eči Unverified Content The variant form dělajući (with -ući instead of -uči) is not attested in the source material. The source gives only -uči for the first conjugation. Infinitive 3pl. present Present active participle Gloss dělati dělajųt dělajuči / dělajući "doing" nesti nesųt nesuči "carrying" hvaliti hvalęt hvaleči "praising" viděti vidęt videči "seeing" When used as an attributive adjective, the present active participle inflects as a soft-type adjective: běgajuči muž "the running man", ljubeča mati "a loving mother." When used as an adverbial participle (transgressive), the form is uninflected for gender and is co-indexed with the grammatical subject: Iduči do raboty, ona vsegda dymi cigaretoju "While going to her work, she always smokes a cigarette."

The transgressive form (also called the adverbial present active participle) has the same endings as the attributive present active participle (-uči, -eči) but is not inflected for gender, number, or case. It MUST be co-indexed with the subject of the main clause. See the relevant section for formation.

The past active participle is formed from the infinitive stem: If the stem ends in a vowel: add -vši If the stem ends in a consonant: add -ši Infinitive Infinitive stem Past active participle Gloss sdělati sděla- sdělavši "having done" ukrasti (< ukrad-ti) ukrad- ukradši "having stolen" nesti nes- nesši "having carried" This participle can be used adjectivally or adverbially. Its adjectival use is very rare. The past active participle is a bookish form: it exists in most Slavic languages but is rarely used in spoken language. In Standard Interslavic it MAY be used in formal or literary registers.

The present passive participle is derived from the present tense stem: First Conjugation: present stem + -omy (or -emy after a soft consonant) Second Conjugation: present stem + -imy Infinitive Present stem Present passive participle Gloss dělati dělaj- dělajemy "being done" nesti nes- nesomy "being carried" hvaliti hvali- hvalimy "being praised" This participle is rarely used in most Slavic languages. Some forms have become lexicalized adjectives with shifted meanings: vidimy means "visible" rather than "being seen." Its use in Interslavic is permitted but not required; the reflexive passive construction is generally preferred.

The past passive participle is formed from the infinitive stem: Infinitive type Suffix Example Stem ends in vowel -ny dělati → dělany "done"; pisati → pisany "written" Stem ends in consonant -eny nesti → neseny "carried"; vezti → vezeny "carried (by vehicle)" -iti verbs -jeny (with iotation) govoriti → govorjeny "spoken"; ljubiti → ljubjeny "loved"; prositi → prošeny "required" -nuti verbs -neny tegnuti → tegneny "pulled"; dvignuti → dvigneny "moved" Monosyllabic stems (except -a), i.e. stems with -j- in present -ty biti → bity "beaten"; čuti → čuty "felt"; podjeti → podjety "undertaken" Iotation in -iti verbs: The stem-final consonant merges with -j- as follows: sj → š, zj → ž, tj → č, dj → dž, stj → šč, zdj → ždž. The past passive participle inflects as a regular hard-type adjective.

A verbal noun (gerund) is formed from the past passive participle by substituting: -ny → -nje -ty → -tje Verb Past passive participle Verbal noun Gloss dělati dělany dělanje "the act of doing" piti pity pitje "drinking" pisati pisany pisanje "writing" govoriti govorjeny govorjenije "speaking, speech" Unverified Content The verbal noun form govorjenije appears inconsistent with the stated rule (-ny → -nje), which would produce govorjenje, not govorjenije. The source gives no explicit example of verbal nouns from -iti verbs with -eny endings. Verbal nouns are grammatically neuter nouns and decline as regular Second Declension neuter nouns in -e (soft type). They express the action as an abstract concept.

Unverified Content The MUST requirement for participle agreement in "gender, number, and case" is not explicitly stated in the source. The source describes the passive construction but does not formalize agreement rules. The elaborated table below (with conditional and past conditional rows) goes beyond the source's examples. The analytical passive is formed by combining an appropriate form of byti with the past passive participle (or, for imperfective verbs in the present tense, the present passive participle). The participle MUST agree with the grammatical subject in gender, number, and case. Tense/Mood Construction Example Gloss Present (impf.) byti (pres.) + pres. pass. part. ja jesm nesomy "I am being carried" Present (pf.) byti (pres.) + past pass. part. ja jesm neseny "I am carried (resultative)" Past byl + past pass. part. ja byl neseny "I was carried / have been carried" Future bųdų + past pass. part. ja bųdų neseny "I will be carried" Conditional byh + past pass. part. ja byh neseny "I would be carried" Past conditional byl byh + past pass. part. ja byl byh neseny "I would have been carried" Unverified Content Sections and on the reflexive passive and the passive avoidance recommendation are not covered in the source material. The source does not discuss the reflexive-passive construction or register-based preferences for passive voice strategies.
documents.s: Passive with byti + Past Passive Participle

documents.t: The reflexive-passive construction, using the reflexive particle sę, is the preferred alternative to the analytical passive in most registers: pica dělaje sę — "pizza is being made" Kak sę to dělaje? — "How is that done?" The reflexive passive SHOULD be used in preference to the analytical passive in casual and colloquial speech.

Analytical passive constructions SHOULD be avoided in casual speech in favor of active or reflexive-passive constructions. In formal, administrative, and written registers the analytical passive is fully acceptable.

The verb byti "to be" is the only truly irregular verb in Interslavic. Its paradigm must be learned in full. Present tense: Person/Number Form 1sg. jesm 2sg. jesi 3sg. jest / je 1pl. jesmo 2pl. jeste 3pl. sųt Unverified Content The normative claims that the copula is "obligatory" (MUST NOT omit) and that short/enclitic forms are "not part of Standard Interslavic" are not stated in the source material. The source does not discuss copula omission rules or enclitic forms of byti. The use of the copula is obligatory in Interslavic in the present tense, following the West and South Slavic pattern. East Slavic speakers MUST NOT omit the present-tense copula in the way that Russian omits есть. The short (enclitic) forms of the copula (sòm, si, je, smo, ste, sųt) are present in many Slavic languages (Serbian/Croatian, Slovenian, Slovak, Bulgarian) but are not part of Standard Interslavic. Speakers who know these forms MAY use them; if doing so, the South Slavic convention that the short form cannot begin a sentence SHOULD be followed. Future tense of byti: Formed regularly from the stem bud-: Person/Number Form 1sg. bųdų / bųdem 2sg. bųdeš 3sg. bųde 1pl. bųdemo 2pl. bųdete 3pl. bųdųt Simple past (aorist) of byti: Two stems are available: Person/Number Stem by- Stem bě- 1sg. byh běh 2sg. by bě 3sg. by bě 1pl. byhmo běhmo 2pl. byste běste 3pl. byhų běhų Unverified Content The explanation that the bě- forms "arose under the influence of the imperfect" is not stated in the source material. The by- stem forms are used for the conditional auxiliary (see the relevant section). The bě- forms arose under the influence of the imperfect. L-participle of byti: byl (m.), byla (f.), bylo (n.), byli (pl.) Imperative of byti: bųď (2sg.), bųďmo (1pl.), bųďte (2pl.)

The verbs dati "to give", jesti "to eat", and věděti "to know" form their present tense according to the athematic conjugation, whose root ends in a vowel: Present tense: Person/Number dati jesti věděti 1sg. dam jem věm 2sg. daš ješ věš 3sg. da je vě 1pl. damo jemo věmo 2pl. date jeste věte 3pl. dadųt jedųt vědųt Unverified Content The 2pl. form of jesti is shown as jeste, but the secondary source (code observations) gives jete. The form jeste is identical to the 2pl. of byti ("you are"), which could cause ambiguity. All other forms of these verbs are formed regularly from their respective infinitive stems. Unverified Content The imperative forms for dati, jesti, and věděti are not given in the source material. The source states only that "all other forms of these three verbs are formed regularly" without providing imperative paradigms. Imperative forms: Verb 2sg. 1pl. 2pl. dati daj dajmo dajte jesti jěď jěďmo jěďte věděti věď věďmo věďte Note: The verb iměti "to have" is not athematic in Standard Interslavic. It follows the Third (contracted) Conjugation or the First Conjugation with the -aj- stem (see and ).

The verb idti "to go (on foot, directional)" is conjugated regularly in the present tense: Person/Number Form 1sg. idų / idem 2sg. ideš 3sg. ide 1pl. idemo 2pl. idete 3pl. idųt Its only irregularity is the suppletive L-participle: šel (m.), šla (f.), šlo (n.), šli (pl.), sometimes written with an initial vowel: išel, išla, išlo, išli. The suppletive form carries through all prefixed compounds: Compound Meaning L-participle (m.) pojdti "to go, to set off" pošel vojdti "to enter" všel prijdti "to come, to arrive" prišel odidti "to go away" otšel najdti "to find" našel Unverified Content The L-participle forms otšel and našel are not attested in the source material. The source only gives pošel and všel as examples of prefixed compounds of idti. The form otšel (with t instead of d) implies a devoicing rule not stated in the source. The alternative infinitive spelling iti (without the d) is fully acceptable. Unverified Content This entire subsection on iměti has no basis in the source material. The source does not mention iměti, its conjugation, the němati negative form, or the recommendation to use iměti instead of the Russian-style construction. The paradigm tables, the note on němati, and the impersonal usage of něma are all unsourced.

The verb iměti "to have" is the standard means of expressing possession in Interslavic. (Russian speakers should note that the u menja jest construction, while comprehensible, is not idiomatic Interslavic; the verb iměti SHOULD be used instead.) Iměti follows the Third (contracted) Conjugation or the First Conjugation with -aj- stem: Person/Number Full form (1st Conj.) Contracted form (3rd Conj.) 1sg. imajų imam 2sg. imaješ imaš 3sg. imaje ima 1pl. imajemo imamo 2pl. imajete imate 3pl. imajųt imajųt Negation: The negative "to not have" is expressed either with regular ne iměti or with the lexicalized form němati: Person/Number němati (contracted) 1sg. němam 2sg. němaš 3sg. něma 1pl. němamo 2pl. němate 3pl. němajųt The 3sg. form něma MAY be used impersonally with the meaning "there is no / there are no": Nema nas tu "We are not here / There's none of us here."

Verb stems that contain etymological yat (ě) SHOULD retain it consistently across all prefixed, suffixed, and aspect-derived forms. This follows from the general principle that Interslavic spelling preserves etymological vowel distinctions within a morpheme family (see the relevant section). The distinction between ě and e in verb stems is not merely orthographic. It encodes a systematic sound correspondence across Slavic languages: etymological ě corresponds to different reflexes in different language groups (e.g., Czech í, Polish a/ia, Russian e/ё, Bulgarian я/е). Inconsistent use of ě within a verb family disrupts these correspondences and impairs the flavourization potential of Interslavic.

Interslavic verb infinitives end in -ěti or -ęti when the preceding consonant is hard. The ending -eti occurs only after functionally soft consonants (j, č, š, ž, c), where ě is simplified to e by the standard orthographic convention. A verb infinitive ending in -eti after a non-soft consonant should be treated as a potential error and verified against its Proto-Slavic etymology.

Root Correct forms Incorrect forms Etymology plěn- "captive, plunder" plěniti, zaplěniti, zaplěnjati *pleniti alongside zaplěniti OCS плѣнъ trě- "to rub" trěti, utrěti *treti, *utreti PS *terti; cf. Ru. тереть, Cz. třít mrě- "to die" mrěti, zamrěti, umrěti *zamreti, *umreti PS *merti; cf. Ru. замереть, Cz. zemřít

The verb meaning "to tear" presents a distinct issue. While some dictionary entries list dreti, the majority of Slavic languages reflect a Proto-Slavic form *dьrati (with the same ablaut pattern as *bьrati → brati). The recommended form is therefore drati with the present tense dere, parallel to brati / bere. This is not a case of ě restoration but of correcting the stem vowel to match the predominant Slavic evidence. Unresolved: Verb stem yat consistency The current dictionary contains several verb families where etymological ě is applied inconsistently — present in some prefixed forms but absent in others, or absent from base verbs where Proto-Slavic and OCS evidence clearly indicates yat. The affected families include at minimum plěn-, trě- ("to rub"), and mrě- ("to die"). Additionally, the form dreti should likely be corrected to drati based on cross-Slavic evidence. A systematic audit of verb entries against Proto-Slavic and OCS etymologies is needed to identify and correct all such inconsistencies. The governing principle should be: if the Proto-Slavic or OCS form contains yat (ѣ), the Interslavic form retains ě, except after functionally soft consonants where ě is simplified to e (see the relevant section).

Over the course of its development, Interslavic and its predecessor projects have employed a wide variety of orthographic systems — including multiple Latin-based proposals, Cyrillic variants, and mixed schemes. A comprehensive archive of these historical orthographies is maintained at Jan van Steenbergen's reference page. The current standard recognises two official orthographies: a Latin alphabet and a Cyrillic alphabet. Both are fully specified, mutually transliterable, and enjoy equal normative status. Neither is derived from any single national Slavic orthography; both were designed as compromises that balance recognisability across the entire Slavic-speaking area.

The standard Latin alphabet comprises 27 letters — the 26 letters of the basic Latin alphabet (excluding q, w, and x, which have no native Slavic use) plus four letters with diacritics: č, ě, š, ž. Two digraphs — dž and lj/(nj) — are treated as single phonemic units. The full specification of the Latin orthography, including letter inventory, digraphs, capitalization rules, and fallback encodings, is given in Standard Latin and Cyrillic Orthography.

The standard Cyrillic alphabet comprises 29 letters, drawing on a pan-Slavic selection that avoids exclusive dependence on any single national Cyrillic tradition. It includes letters common to most Cyrillic-using Slavic languages, supplemented by є (from Ukrainian), ы (from Russian/Belarusian), and ј (from Serbian/Macedonian). The full specification of the Cyrillic orthography is given alongside the Latin in Standard Latin and Cyrillic Orthography.

The two standard orthographies are designed for bijective transliterability: every standard Latin text can be mechanically converted to Cyrillic and vice versa, with no loss of information. The transliteration rules are specified in Transliteration. All normative Interslavic texts — including this specification — SHOULD be available in both scripts. When only one script is used, neither is preferred over the other; the choice is left to the author or publisher.

In addition to the two standard orthographies, Interslavic defines an optional extended Latin alphabet known as the etymological or scientific alphabet (historically Naučny Medžuslovjansky). This alphabet adds 13 diacritical letters to the standard Latin inventory, encoding distinctions inherited from Proto-Slavic and Old Church Slavonic that have been neutralised in the standard spelling. The etymological alphabet is not a separate language or dialect. It is the same Interslavic, written with additional diacritics layered on top of the standard Latin orthography. Any etymological text becomes valid standard text when the diacritics are stripped. Its primary uses are: Dictionaries and grammars — conveying etymological information that helps learners connect Interslavic words to their cognates in individual Slavic languages; Flavorization — enabling systematic regional adaptation of Interslavic (see Flavorization); Linguistic analysis — providing a more precise notation of the ideal pronunciation and historical phonology. The etymological alphabet SHALL NOT be used as the default orthography in everyday communication. Its full specification is given in Etymological (Scientific) Alphabet.
Detailed rules for capitalization, punctuation, hyphenation, compound-word orthography, numeral formatting, and abbreviations are specified in Spelling Conventions.

The Interslavic etymological alphabet — historically known as Naučny Medžuslovjansky ("Scientific Interslavic"), also informally called MS Plus or etimologičny pravopis ("etymological orthography") — is an OPTIONAL extension of the standard orthography. It introduces additional diacritical letters that correspond to Proto-Slavic and Old Church Slavonic phonemes that have since diverged or disappeared in most modern Slavic languages. The etymological alphabet does NOT constitute a separate language or dialect. It is the same Interslavic written with additional diacritics layered on top of the standard spelling. A reader unfamiliar with these diacritics MAY ignore them entirely and still read the text correctly using the standard pronunciation.

The etymological alphabet is intended for use in scholarly, etymological, and high-precision contexts. It SHALL NOT be used as the default orthography in everyday communication. Specifically, the etymological alphabet serves the following purposes: conveying etymological information in dictionaries, grammars, and linguistic studies; supporting language learning by making Interslavic words easier to connect to their counterparts in other Slavic languages — the etymological diacritics encode systematic sound correspondences across Slavic languages, so that learning them effectively provides a key to recognising cognates in any natural Slavic language; providing a more precise notation of the ideal (most central) pronunciation; disambiguating homographs — the standard orthography inevitably produces pairs of words that are spelled identically but have different meanings and different etymologies; the etymological alphabet resolves these collisions (see below); enabling flavorization — the adaptation of Interslavic for speakers of a specific Slavic language (see the relevant section); enabling the transcription of any Slavic text into a unified Interslavic orthographic form. An additional practical consideration motivates the etymological alphabet: the majority of Slavic speakers have an instinct to read words as they are written. In the standard orthography, where fewer distinctions are encoded, it can be difficult for a reader to determine the recommended pronunciation of an unfamiliar word. The etymological diacritics guide pronunciation more precisely, allowing both writers and speakers to produce — and listeners to perceive — the intended distinctions. The etymological alphabet is a superset of the standard alphabet. The standard forms are always legitimate simplifications of the etymological forms. Users who choose to write etymologically are free to use any subset of the additional letters, with two restrictions: A character, once adopted in a text or conversation, MUST be used consistently throughout. Certain letters come in complementary pairs; using one member of a pair while omitting the other is discouraged. The established pairs are: ę/ų, ė/ȯ, t́/d́ (or ť/ď), and ś/ź.

Because the standard orthography neutralises several Proto-Slavic distinctions, it inevitably produces homographs — pairs of words that are spelled identically but differ in meaning, etymology, and (in natural Slavic languages) pronunciation. The etymological alphabet resolves these collisions by restoring the lost distinctions. Standard form Etymological form A Gloss A Etymological form B Gloss B čest čęsť part, portion (cf. Ru. часть, Pl. część, Cz. část) česť honour (cf. Ru. честь, Pl. cześć, Cz. čest) moč moč power, might moć to be able grad grad hail gråd city, town dobru dobru good (dat. sg.) dobrų good (acc. sg. fem.) In the standard orthography, these pairs can usually be resolved from context. In the etymological orthography, however, they are distinguished both in writing and — because the additional letters carry recommended pronunciations — in speech as well. Usage Guidance Text written with many etymological diacritics may appear intimidating to learners, who might incorrectly assume that the diacritics are obligatory or that etymological spelling is somehow superior. When using this orthography, state explicitly why you are doing so and that these marks are optional additions.
The etymological Latin alphabet adds 13 letters to the standard 27-letter inventory. These letters are divided into additional vowels and additional consonants.

Letter IPA Proto-Slavic / OCS source Standard equivalent Gloss (example) å ɒ ~ ɑ Proto-Slavic *or, *ol (liquid + back vowel sequences) a kråva → krava (cow) ė ə (fleeting) Strong yer *ь [ĭ] e (or silent) pės → pes (dog) ę ɛ̃ ~ ʲæ Proto-Slavic front nasal *ę; OCS Ѧ (jus maloj) e pęť → pet (five) ȯ ə (fleeting) Strong yer *ъ [ŭ] o (or silent) sȯn → son (dream) ų ʊ Proto-Slavic back nasal *ǫ; OCS Ѫ (jus bolšoj) u rųka → ruka (hand) Notes on å: The vowel å occurs exclusively in the sequences rå and lå followed by a consonant. It derives from Proto-Slavic sequences *or and *ol between consonants (e.g., PS *korva → kråva, PS *golva → glåva, PS *borda → bråda, PS *bolna → blåna). It corresponds to positions where Polish has ro/ró and ło/łó, Czech and Slovak have ra/rá and la/lá, South Slavic languages uniformly have ra and la, and East Slavic has (o)ro and (o)lo (the polnoglasije reflex). It MUST NOT appear word-initially, after a vowel, or in positions other than after a liquid. The standard Interslavic orthography follows the South Slavic pattern by writing a in this position. The pronunciation of å in the etymological register is ɒ or ɑ — a vowel intermediate between a and ɔ. To produce this sound, position the mouth as for a and attempt to say ɔ, or vice versa. Etymological Standard Czech Polish Russian Gloss kråva krava kráva krowa корова cow glåva glava hlava głowa голова head bråda brada brada broda борода beard mråz mraz mráz mróz мороз frost tråva trava tráva trawa трава grass glås glas hlas głos голос voice blåto blato bláto błoto болото mud, swamp zlåto zlato zlato złoto золото gold The prefix råz- (from PS *orz-) is a notable case: it surfaces as roz- in Polish and Ukrainian, as raz- in Russian (a South Slavic borrowing via Old Church Slavonic), and as raz-/roz- in Czech and Slovak. In Belarusian, the underlying form is roz- (visible in stressed syllables: розум, розніца), but akanje (the neutralisation of unstressed o to a) masks it in unstressed position, making it appear identical to the Russian raz- form. In standard Interslavic the prefix is written raz-; in the etymological register it is written råz-. Notes on ė and ȯ (fleeting vowels / strong yers): The vowels ė and ȯ represent strong yers: the front yer *ь and the back yer *ъ respectively. In standard Interslavic they surface as e and o; the etymological notation flags them as historically yer-derived vowels that participate in the fleeting vowel alternation (they disappear in certain inflectional contexts). Etymological Standard Gloss Alternation example pės pes dog nom. pes → gen. psa sȯn son dream nom. son → gen. sna lėv lev lion nom. lev → gen. lva dȯšť došť rain nom. došť → gen. dšťа It is also acceptable to write è and ò instead of ė and ȯ. Notes on ę (front nasal): Historically, ę was the Proto-Slavic front nasal vowel ɛ̃. In modern Interslavic it is NOT pronounced as a nasal but as ʲæ — intermediate between a and e with palatalization of the preceding consonant. Acceptable realizations also include ʲa (analogous to Russian я), ʲɛ (analogous to Czech ě), or simply e. In simplified Interslavic, ę is written and read as e; the etymological distinction is meaningful: e.g., česť "honour" vs. čęsť "part/portion." The following table illustrates the reflexes of etymological ę across Slavic languages: Etymological OCS Russian Ukrainian Polish Czech Slovak S/Cr/Sln Gloss ręd рѧдъ ряд ряд rząd řád rád red row, order desęť десѧть десять десять dziesięć deset desať deset ten język ѩзыкъ язык язик język jazyk jazyk jezik language pamęť памѧть память пам'ять pamięć paměť pamäť pamet memory Notes on ų (back nasal): Historically, ų was the Proto-Slavic back nasal vowel ɔ̃. In modern Interslavic it is NOT pronounced as a nasal but as ʊ — a sound intermediate between u and o. It is also acceptable to pronounce it simply as u, making no spoken distinction between ų and u. The grapheme ų was chosen over ǫ because in the majority of Slavic languages this vowel shifted to u. Etymological OCS Russian Bulgarian Macedonian Polish Slovene Gloss pųť пѫть путь път пат pąć pot path dųb дѫбъ дуб дъб даб dąb dob oak sųd сѫдъ суд съд суд sąd sod court rųka рѫка рука ръка рака ręka roka hand

Letter IPA Proto-Slavic / OCS source Standard equivalent Example ć t͡ɕ Proto-Slavic *tj; OCS Щ (šč) č ćuđi → čudži (strange) đ d͡ʑ Proto-Slavic *dj; OCS ЖД (žd) dž krađa → kradža (theft) ĺ (ľ) lʲ Soft l before a weak yer *ь lj (only before j) nasiľje → nasilje (violence) ń nʲ Soft n before a weak yer *ь nj (only before j) hođeńje → hođenje (walking) ŕ rʲ Soft r before a weak yer *ь r pŕvy → prvy (first) t́ (ť) tʲ Soft t before a weak yer *ь t pamęt́ → pamęt (memory) d́ (ď) dʲ Soft d before a weak yer *ь d gvozd́ → gvozd (nail) ś sʲ Soft s before a weak yer *ь s gųś → gųs (goose) ź zʲ Soft z before a weak yer *ь z vitęź → vitęz (hero) Notes on ć and đ: These two letters are the only etymological consonants that require a non-trivial substitution in standard orthography. All other etymological consonants simplify to their base letter (losing only the diacritic); ć and đ map to č and dž respectively. Using ć where standard orthography requires č (and vice versa) constitutes an error, not merely a stylistic choice. Note that đ is the only case in MS Plus where a single letter replaces a standard digraph (dž). The Latin letters ć and đ are taken directly from the Serbo-Croatian Latin alphabet (used in Croatian, Bosnian, Montenegrin, and Serbian Latin orthography), where they carry the same phonetic values [t͡ɕ] and [d͡ʑ]. They are among the few MS Plus letters borrowed from a naturally occurring alphabet rather than designed specifically for the system. In Cyrillic contexts (dictionaries, learning materials), the corresponding letters are the Serbian Ћ ћ and Ђ ђ. These were introduced in the early 19th century by reformers such as Vuk Karadžić and Dositej Obradović as modernizations of the Old Cyrillic letter đerv (Ꙉ), which historically served for both [t͡ɕ] and [d͡ʑ] — illustrating the close phonological relationship between the two sounds. Both ć and đ are widely available in standard Unicode fonts. During the early development of the unified Interslavic project (shortly after the merger of the Slovianski and Novoslovensky projects), alternative diacritics were considered for these letters. One proposal was to use circumflex accents, yielding ĉ and dẑ instead of the current ć and đ. This approach would have been more systematic, giving the diacritic a unique function not shared with other letter types (since the acute accent on ć serves a different purpose than the acute on soft consonants like ś and ź). Ultimately, ć and đ were retained because they are naturally occurring letters whose established phonetic values in Serbo-Croatian match the intended MS Plus pronunciation. Notes on ĺ (ľ) and ń: These letters occur only before a consonant (including before j). Phonetically they are identical to lj and nj; the difference is that ĺ/ń mark etymologically justified softness that is NOT universally reflected in standard Interslavic digraphs. The letters ĺ and ľ are interchangeable variants of the same letter; similarly, the letters t́ and ť, and d́ and ď, are interchangeable pairs. The distinction between ĺ/ń and the standard digraphs lj/nj encodes an important etymological difference. The digraphs lj and nj represent iotation-derived softness (Proto-Slavic *lj, *nj from consonant + *j), which is reflected as a soft consonant in all Slavic languages, North and South alike. By contrast, ĺ and ń represent yer-derived softness (Proto-Slavic *lь, *nь — consonant + front yer), which is soft in Northern Slavic languages but hard in Southern Slavic. For example: polje "field" has iotation-derived lj (Croatian also has polje with a soft L), while veĺmi "very" has yer-derived ĺ (Croatian veoma has a hard L, and Russian весьма has a soft L). In standard orthography, iotation-derived softness is written with the digraphs (lj, nj), while yer-derived softness is dropped entirely (written as hard l, n). The MS Plus register restores the yer-derived softness with the háček letters. Unresolved: Scope of ĺ and ń in the Dictionary The Interslavic dictionary and existing documentation are not yet fully consistent in applying the distinction between iotation-derived softness (lj, nj) and yer-derived softness (ĺ, ń). Some dictionary entries use the iotation digraphs lj or nj where the softness is historically yer-derived — for example, bolj instead of the expected bol/​boĺ (cf. Croatian bol with hard L), or koljce instead of kolce/​koĺce (from *kolьce, cf. kolo). The athematic masculine nouns (kamenj, korenj, prstenj, etc.) similarly use the iotation digraph nj in their nominative form, but their final softness is yer-derived (*kamy, oblique stem *kamen-), suggesting that the standard form should end in hard n and the MS Plus form in ń (i.e., kamen / kameń). A systematic review of the lexicon is needed to ensure that iotation-derived and yer-derived softness are consistently distinguished in the etymological register. Notes on t́/ť and d́/ď: These letters do not appear in any living Slavic alphabet. In Unicode they can only be formed with a combining diacritic (acute accent or haček), which means display may vary across fonts. When using a haček form (ť, ď), note that in many fonts the haček is rendered as an apostrophe. The following comparison table shows etymological vs. standard forms: Etymological Standard Gloss zvańje zvanje calling, vocation stabiľnosť stabilnost stability goŕky gorky bitter grųď grud chest (body) kosť kost bone piśmo pismo letter, writing gręź grez dirt, filth listȯk listok leaflet gråh grah pea noć noč night krađa kradža theft ćuđi čudži strange, foreign

The etymological consonants ť and ď are defined as reflexes of Proto-Slavic yer-softened *tь and *dь. However, the Interslavic dictionary also contains a number of loanwords and proper names in which the standard orthography writes tj or dj — sequences that in practice imply a soft /tʲ/ or /dʲ/ even though standard Interslavic has no such phonemes (see a). In the MS Plus register, ť and ď provide a natural way to represent genuine soft /tʲ/ and /dʲ/ in these words. For example, kostjum could be written kosťum, tjuŕma as ťuŕma, and tjulenj as ťulenj — making the intended pronunciation explicit and resolving the ambiguity that exists in standard orthography. This would extend the use of ť and ď beyond their etymological Proto-Slavic function to cover borrowed soft /tʲ/ and /dʲ/ from non-Slavic sources. The extension is natural: the phonetic value is the same ([tʲ] and [dʲ]), and the letters already exist in the MS Plus inventory. However, this usage has not yet been formally adopted. Unresolved: Extending ť/ď to Loanwords in MS Plus The MS Plus letters ť and ď currently have a strictly etymological scope (Proto-Slavic yer-derived softness). Several dictionary words contain tj and dj sequences that represent a borrowed soft /tʲ/ or /dʲ/ (e.g., kostjum, tjuŕma, tjulenj). Allowing ť and ď in these positions within the MS Plus register would resolve the pronunciation ambiguity without altering the standard orthography. Note that this question is partly moot for words where the tj/dj sequence should be eliminated entirely in favour of a more natural Slavic form — e.g., tulenj instead of tjulenj, gospodža instead of gospodja (see a). The ť/ď extension would apply only to words where no better alternative exists and a genuine soft consonant is the intended pronunciation. Note also that the strong soft yer ė — which is part of the MS Plus inventory and functions correctly in the declinator (e.g., pės → psy) — is not yet searchable in the dictionary tools. Since ė is an official component of the etymological alphabet, this represents a tooling gap that should be addressed.
documents.s: Soft T/D in Loanwords and the tj/dj Problem

documents.t: In the Latin etymological alphabet, all additional information is conveyed exclusively by diacritical marks layered onto the standard letters. A reader who does not recognize a diacritic can always fall back to the standard reading. An analogous approach for Cyrillic — using modified characters such as ӑ, ԙ, ө, ұ, ҷ, ӝ, etc. — would produce text that appears extremely artificial and would be of little help, since the modifications would not be self-explanatory. Nevertheless, among enthusiasts of Interslavic etymology who wish to write etymological diacritics in Cyrillic, the most commonly used convention for å is the Chuvash letter Ӑ ӑ (Cyrillic а with breve). This letter is available in most fonts that support extended Cyrillic. An alternative — a Cyrillic а with a combining ring-above diacritic — is sometimes seen but renders poorly in most fonts, because combining diacritics are not well supported for Cyrillic in the majority of typefaces. For handwriting, either variant is acceptable. An extended Cyrillic alphabet using historical OCS characters (ѣ, ѩ, ѫ) or regional characters (ћ, ђ) would be difficult to read for anyone not familiar with Old Church Slavonic orthography, thus defeating the purpose of Interslavic. For these reasons, the Interslavic etymological alphabet has no standard Cyrillic equivalent. However, in scholarly publications concerned specifically with the history of Slavic languages, the following OCS-derived Cyrillic letters are used conventionally for the etymological vowels: Latin (etym.) Cyrillic (conventional) OCS name Standard Cyrillic equiv. ę ѧ jus maloj (small yus) е ų ѫ jus bolšoj (big yus) у ję- (word-initial) ѩ jotated small yus е jų- (word-initial) ѭ jotated big yus ю These Cyrillic historical forms MAY be used in scholarly contexts but are NOT part of the normative Interslavic Cyrillic orthography. Their use in ordinary Interslavic texts is explicitly discouraged.

The following table provides the complete systematic mapping from etymological Latin to standard Latin. In every case, removing the etymological diacritic yields the standard form, with the single exception of ć → č and đ → dž. Etymological Standard Notes å a only in rå, lå + consonant ė e fleeting; may be zero in certain inflected forms ę e ȯ o fleeting; may be zero in certain inflected forms ų u ć č irregular; diacritic changes the base letter đ dž irregular; diacritic changes the base letter ĺ / ľ lj (before j) or l (elsewhere) ń nj (before j) or n (elsewhere) ŕ r t́ / ť t d́ / ď d ś s ź z

When transliterating an etymological Latin text into Cyrillic, the etymological diacritics are dropped and the standard Cyrillic equivalents are used. No special Cyrillic etymological characters are inserted. Latin (etym.) Cyrillic (standard) å а ė е ę е ȯ о ų у ć ч đ дж ĺ / ľ љ (before j) or л (elsewhere) ń њ (before j) or н (elsewhere) ŕ р t́ / ť т d́ / ď д ś с ź з

When a root ends in a soft consonant in the etymological register, that softness is a lexical property of the root and should in principle persist through all inflected and derived forms. However, the orthography does not always make this visible, creating potential ambiguity. Softness before i: When a soft-stem noun is inflected into a form ending in -i (e.g., genitive singular or nominative plural of feminine i-stems), the softness of the final consonant becomes unmarked in both standard and etymological orthography: Nominative sg. (MS Plus) Genitive sg. / Nom. pl. Underlying pronunciation kosť kosti kɔstʲi — the tʲ persists but is not marked sȯľ soli sɔlʲi — the lʲ persists but is not marked pamęť pamęti pamʲætʲi — the tʲ persists but is not marked In these forms, a reader unfamiliar with the paradigm might not realize that the consonant before -i is soft, since the soft diacritic is conventionally omitted before i. Unresolved: Softness Marking Before I Three approaches have been proposed to address the ambiguity of soft consonants before i: Preserve the soft mark even before i: Write kost́i, soĺi, etc. This is maximally explicit but typographically unusual and not attested in any living Slavic orthography. Use notation only in dictionaries and learning materials: Mark the softness with a special convention (e.g., i vs. í, or an underline) in reference materials, while leaving running text unmarked. This keeps running text clean but requires readers to consult reference materials. Accept the ambiguity: Most Slavic speakers will intuit the correct softness from context and from knowledge of the declension class. The i-stem feminine paradigm itself signals softness. This is the least interventionist approach but offers no help to learners. No consensus has been reached. The current practice is effectively option 3 — softness before i is unmarked. Softness through derivation: When a derivational suffix is attached to a root whose final consonant is soft, the softness should persist. In the etymological register this is marked explicitly (e.g., svińsky from svinь-), but in standard orthography the softness is lost (written svinsky). See the relevant sectiona for the full treatment of softness at root–suffix boundaries in adjective formation.
documents.s: Softness Marking Through Inflection and Derivation

documents.t: The MS Plus diacritic system, while functional, exhibits an internal tension in the mapping between diacritics and phonological functions. Ideally, each diacritic would encode exactly one type of phonological information. In practice, however, the acute accent serves two distinct roles: On consonants like ś, ź, ŕ, ń, ĺ, t́, d́, the acute marks yer-derived palatalization (softening caused by the historical front yer *ь). On ć, however, the acute marks iotation (the reflex of Proto-Slavic *tj), which is a fundamentally different phonological process — iotation produces postalveolar or palatal affricates, while yer-softening produces palatalized versions of the base consonant. Similarly, the háček (caron) has two distinct functions in broader Slavic orthographic tradition: It marks postalveolar sibilants (š, ž, č) — its primary function in Interslavic. In Czech and Slovak, it also marks palatalized stops (ť, ď, ň) — a softening function that overlaps with what Interslavic uses the acute for. This means that the choice between ť/t́ and ď/d́ is not purely aesthetic: it reflects a deeper question about whether the diacritic system should prioritize consistency of function (one diacritic = one phonological process) or naturalness of appearance (using letter forms already established in living Slavic alphabets). The current MS Plus system permits both háček and acute variants for soft T and D (i.e., ť/t́ and ď/d́ are interchangeable), effectively deferring the decision. Unresolved: Diacritic Function Consistency The MS Plus diacritic system mixes phonological functions across its diacritics: the acute accent marks both yer-derived softening (on ś, ź, ŕ, etc.) and iotation (on ć), while the háček marks both sibilant place of articulation (on š, ž, č) and — in the alternate forms ť, ď, ľ — palatalization. A fully consistent system would assign each diacritic a unique function, but this would require either abandoning the naturally occurring Serbo-Croatian ć/đ (which carry strong recognition value for South Slavic speakers) or introducing unfamiliar letter forms. The current compromise — permitting both háček and acute variants — avoids forcing the choice but leaves the system formally inconsistent.

A persistent community tension concerns the positioning of MS Plus within the Interslavic ecosystem. The etymological alphabet was rebranded from its earlier designation as "MS Plus" — a name that implied an enhanced variant of everyday Interslavic — to "etymological alphabet," accompanied by explicit guidance that it "SHALL NOT be used as the default orthography in everyday communication." This repositioning has been contested on several grounds: Practical suitability for everyday use. MS Plus contains fewer distinct letters than the standard Slovak alphabet (which has 46 letters including diacritical variants), and every additional letter carries a concrete phonological or morphological function. The argument that it is too complex for daily use is therefore not self-evident. Emotional and cultural connection. The MS Plus inventory draws letters from naturally occurring Slavic Latin alphabets — ć and đ from Serbo-Croatian, ě from Czech, ń and ś and ź from Polish, ľ and ť and ď from Slovak. This gives Latin-script Slavic speakers the experience of encountering familiar letters from their own writing systems, creating an immediate sense of recognition. Any shift toward artificial diacritic combinations (e.g., d́ instead of ď, or ė instead of è) dilutes this property. Incomplete etymological coverage. MS Plus encodes only a fraction of the etymological information available in the Proto-Slavic source forms. It does not distinguish native from foreign g/h, does not mark the epenthetic l, does not encode the neuter -e/-o variation, and lacks many other distinctions that a true etymological or scientific notation would require. Calling it an "etymological alphabet" overstates its descriptive power while simultaneously discouraging the everyday use for which it is arguably well suited. The counter-argument is that encouraging everyday MS Plus use risks creating a barrier to entry: texts dense with diacritics may intimidate learners, who might incorrectly assume that the marks are obligatory. The current positioning as a scholarly tool avoids this risk. Unresolved: MS Plus Positioning The community has not reached consensus on whether MS Plus should be actively encouraged for everyday use (as a "rich variant" of Interslavic that enhances both pronunciation guidance and cross-Slavic recognizability) or restricted to scholarly and etymological contexts (to avoid intimidating learners). The renaming from "MS Plus" to "etymological alphabet" reflects the latter position, but arguments for the former remain substantive — particularly the observation that MS Plus is no more complex than standard Slovak orthography and that its use of naturally occurring national-language letters creates valuable cultural resonance.
documents.s: The Role and Identity of MS Plus

documents.t: The etymological alphabet does NOT include: vowel length or tone markers; special letters for non-Slavic loanwords (e.g., OCS ѳ and ѵ used only for Greek words, or ü for words of Germanic or French origin); any marker that would be obligatory rather than optional. The etymological alphabet is designed so that any text written in it is also valid standard Interslavic text when the diacritics are stripped. This property ensures backward compatibility with tools and readers that handle only the standard orthography.

In Proto-Slavic and Old Church Slavonic, the vowel yat (ѣ) appeared after all consonants, including those that were already phonetically palatalized (the "functionally soft" consonants: j, č, š, ž, and c). In the development of Interslavic, the letter ě was dropped after j, č, š, and ž on the grounds that these consonants are already soft and the additional softening marker is redundant — so OCS jěsti became jesti, and tęžěje became tęžeje. However, this simplification was not applied consistently. The consonant c is also functionally soft (it is an affricate that patterns with the other postalveolar consonants in its palatalization behaviour), yet the word cěly retains ě after c. This creates an internal inconsistency in the orthography. The inconsistency extends beyond cěly. Etymological ě after š and č was also simplified in forms like oglušeńje (etymologically oglušěńje) and mękčeńje (etymologically mękčěńje).

The treatment of yat after functionally soft consonants involves a fundamental trade-off between two design principles of Interslavic: Orthographic simplicity — Removing ě after consonants that are already soft eliminates a redundant softening marker. Since č, š, ž, j, and c are inherently palatalized, writing e rather than ě after them produces no loss of phonetic information for standard Interslavic pronunciation. Cross-Slavic flavourization potential — Preserving etymological ě in these positions significantly improves the ability to adapt Interslavic text to individual Slavic languages: Polish: ě → a/ą after these consonants (e.g., cěly → cały, jěsti → jeść via jadł) Czech/Slovak: ě → í in Czech (e.g., jěsti → jíst, cěly → cíl "target"); ě → ie in Slovak Bulgarian: the yat alternation (я/е) is better preserved (e.g., jěm → Bg. ям) Russian/Belarusian: etymological ě supports "yokanie" flavourization (ě → ё/е pattern) In other words, the etymological ě carries no additional phonetic information for standard Interslavic but encodes valuable cross-Slavic correspondence data that is used in flavourization.

Regardless of which orthographic solution is adopted, the pronunciation rule is clear: after the functionally soft consonants j, č, š, ž, and c, the letter ě — if written — is pronounced simply as e. The doubled softening (soft consonant + palatalizing vowel) is unnecessary and SHOULD NOT be produced in speech. Unresolved: Treatment of ě after functionally soft consonants The current orthography is inconsistent: ě has been removed after j, č, š, and ž (e.g., jesti, tęžeje) but retained after c (e.g., cěly). Three solutions have been proposed: Restore etymological ě everywhere — write jěsti, jěhati, dråžěje, cěly, oglušěńje, mękčěńje, etc., with a pronunciation note that after already-soft consonants ě is realized as plain e. This maximises flavourization potential. Keep etymological ě everywhere but mark it as phonetically neutral — same spelling as (1), but the specification explicitly states that after j, č, š, ž, and c, the letter ě does not indicate additional softening. Remove ě after c as well — write cely instead of cěly, completing the simplification pattern. This maximises orthographic consistency at the cost of flavourization data. The committee must weigh orthographic regularity against the practical benefits of etymological transparency for cross-Slavic adaptation.

Interslavic has two fully equivalent standard alphabets — one Latin-based and one Cyrillic-based — reflecting the historical division of Slavic writing traditions. Neither alphabet is based on any single national orthography; both were designed as compromises that are intuitively readable across the Slavic world and that permit straightforward, unambiguous transliteration between the two scripts. Published texts are encouraged to provide both Latin and Cyrillic versions so that readers on either side of the Latin–Cyrillic boundary can engage with the text without difficulty.

The standard Interslavic Latin alphabet consists of 27 letters. It uses 23 letters of the basic Latin alphabet — all letters except q, w, and x — plus four letters carrying a caron diacritic: č, š, ž, and ě. Letter IPA Notes A a ɑ ~ a Open back unrounded; as in dar B b b Voiced bilabial stop C c t͡s Voiceless alveolar affricate; as in car Č č t͡ʃ ~ tʂ Voiceless postalveolar affricate; harder than Russian ч D d d Voiced alveolar stop E e ɛ ~ e Mid front unrounded; preceding consonant is not softened Ě ě jɛ ~ ʲɛ Yat; preceding consonant is softened in most Slavic languages F f f Voiceless labiodental fricative G g ɡ ~ ɣ Voiced velar stop; the voiced velar fricative ɣ (as in Belarusian) is an acceptable compromise toward H-Slavic languages, but h is not acceptable H h x Voiceless velar fricative I i i ~ ɪ Close front unrounded J j j Palatal approximant K k k Voiceless velar stop L l l ~ ɫ Lateral approximant M m m Bilabial nasal N n n Alveolar nasal O o ɔ Open-mid back rounded; the close o realization is not recommended P p p Voiceless bilabial stop R r r Alveolar trill; may be syllabic: srdce, trg S s s Voiceless alveolar fricative Š š ʃ ~ ʂ Voiceless postalveolar fricative T t t Voiceless alveolar stop U u u Close back rounded V v v ~ ʋ Voiced labiodental fricative or approximant; w is not recommended Y y ɨ ~ i Close central unrounded; as in Russian ды́м Z z z Voiced alveolar fricative Ž ž ʒ ~ ʐ Voiced postalveolar fricative The letters q, w, and x do not belong to the standard alphabet. They appear only in unadapted loanwords from non-Slavic languages. All vowels are pronounced fully in all positions; vowel reduction does not apply — Interslavic has high vowel quality, meaning vowels in unstressed positions are pronounced the same as in stressed positions. Word-final devoicing is optional: most Slavic languages apply it (except Ukrainian and Shtokavian), and speakers may follow their native habits. Consonants are hard except before ě (where softening is obligatory), and the permanently soft sounds written with digraphs lj and nj. Before i, softening is permitted but not required.

The standard Latin orthography recognizes three digraphs that function as single phonemes: Digraph IPA Notes Dž dž d͡ʒ Voiced postalveolar affricate; the voiced counterpart of č Lj lj ʎ ~ lʲ Palatal lateral approximant; always soft: ljubov, polje Nj nj ɲ ~ nʲ Palatal nasal; always soft: njuhati, konj The sequence šč (ʃt͡ʃ) also occurs in Interslavic and corresponds to Russian щ, as in čudovišče, zaščita. It is a cluster of two distinct phonemes, not a digraph. The sequence rj also occurs frequently in Interslavic (e.g., morje, burja, zarje) and behaves phonetically like a soft counterpart of r, parallel to how lj and nj function as soft counterparts of l and n. However, unlike lj and nj, the Commission has not formally recognized rj as a digraph or phoneme pair. In practice, nearly all occurrences of rj in the Interslavic lexicon trace back to Proto-Slavic soft *r (via iotation: *rj), which makes rj a de facto digraph in everything but name. Note on digraphs and yer-derived softness The digraphs lj and nj in standard orthography represent iotation-derived softness — that is, softness arising from Proto-Slavic iotation (*l + *j, *n + *j), which is reflected in all Slavic languages. A distinct phenomenon, yer-derived softness (from Proto-Slavic *lь, *nь — consonant adjacent to a front yer), produces the same phonetic result in Northern Slavic but a hard consonant in Southern Slavic. In the etymological register (MS Plus), yer-derived softness is written with háček letters ĺ and ń, while the standard orthography writes the consonant as hard. The digraphs lj and nj should therefore be used only for iotation-derived softness. See the relevant section for details. Capitalization of digraphs. When a digraph begins a capitalized word or sentence, only the first letter is capitalized: Dž, Lj, Nj. In all-caps text all letters are capitalized: DŽ, LJ, NJ.

The four letters with diacritics — č, š, ž, and ě — are integral members of the standard alphabet and are mandatory in normative writing. They are not stylistic variants; each represents a phoneme distinct from its unaccented base letter. Č, Š, Ž carry a caron (háček). Omitting the caron and writing c, s, z in their place produces a different phoneme and therefore a different word. These substitutions are not acceptable in standard orthography. Ě ě (the yat letter) represents a front vowel that causes softening of the preceding consonant in most Slavic languages (all except Slovene, Serbian, and Macedonian). Its Cyrillic equivalent is є, borrowed from Ukrainian. When a speaker does not know the correct distribution of ě, writing plain e is an accepted simplification, analogous to the optional use of ё in Russian. The distinction is nonetheless recommended in careful writing. Y y represents the close central (or back) unrounded vowel ɨ, familiar to Russian, Belarusian, and Polish speakers. In South Slavic languages and in spoken Czech and Slovak this vowel has merged with i. Writers targeting a predominantly South Slavic audience, or those uncertain about the correct distribution, may substitute i for y throughout.

A number of words in the Interslavic dictionary contain the letter sequences tj and dj: e.g., bratja, kostjum, tjulenj, tjutjun, tjuŕma, inoplanetjan (with tj); adjutant, gospodja, Madjar (with dj). These sequences create a systematic ambiguity in the standard orthography. Standard Interslavic does not have soft /tʲ/ or soft /dʲ/ as core phonemes — those sounds exist only in the etymological register (MS Plus), where they are written ť and ď and derive from Proto-Slavic yer-softened consonants. The sequences tj and dj in the standard alphabet are therefore intended to represent two separate hard sounds — [t] followed by [j], and [d] followed by [j] — not a softened consonant. However, to any reader familiar with Slavic orthographic conventions, tj visually suggests a soft [tʲ] and dj suggests a soft [dʲ], since this is precisely how softness is marked in many Slavic languages (cf. Russian тья, Croatian đ from historical dj). This ambiguity is compounded by inconsistent behaviour of the Latin-to-Cyrillic transliterator: dj is rendered with an apostrophe (e.g., Мад'яр, господ'я), suggesting that the two sounds are separate, while tj is rendered without one (e.g., братя, тютюн), suggesting a soft consonant. The two treatments are contradictory. Unresolved: Status of tj and dj Sequences Standard Interslavic lacks soft /tʲ/ and /dʲ/ as phonemes, yet the orthography contains tj and dj sequences that imply them — creating ambiguity about pronunciation and transliteration. The following solutions have been proposed: Remove tj/dj where a natural alternative exists. Several of these words have well-attested Slavic forms without a soft /t/ or /d/: tjulenj → tulenj (cf. Czech tuleň), tjutjun → tutun or tytun, gospodja → gospodža (cf. Russian госпожа, Serbian госпођа, Macedonian госпоѓа), Madjar → Madžar (cf. Russian мадьяр but also the widely attested affricate form). Retain tj/dj only at prefix or morpheme boundaries, where the sequence genuinely represents two separate hard sounds — e.g., ad-jutant (Latin prefix ad- + root jutant), kon-jugacija. In these cases the pronunciation should be explicitly marked as [tj] / [dj] (two sounds, hard [t]/​[d] + [j]). For MS Plus, allow ť and ď explicitly in borrowed words where a soft /tʲ/ or /dʲ/ is appropriate (e.g., ťuŕma, kosťum), extending their use beyond the Proto-Slavic yer-derived context. Until a resolution is adopted, writers should be aware that tj and dj in standard orthography are intended as hard [t] + [j] and hard [d] + [j], not as soft consonants.
documents.s: The Sequences tj and dj in Standard Orthography

documents.t: When a writer cannot produce diacritic characters on a given keyboard or input system, the following substitutions are permitted in informal digital contexts (messaging, social media, plain-text environments). Fallback spellings are not accepted in normative documents, dictionaries, or formal publications. Standard Fallback options Notes č cz, cx Do not use ch; West Slavs read ch as x š sz, sx ž zs, zx Do not use zh; Polish ż → zs is natural for Polish speakers ě ie, je, or e Plain e is the simplest and most widely understood option y i Acceptable simplification; see the relevant section above Consistency is required within a single text or conversation. A writer who adopts sz for š must maintain that choice throughout and must not simultaneously use š or sx. Mixing different fallback conventions creates ambiguity. The so-called Volapük encoding (using q, w, x as substitutes for Slavic characters) is explicitly discouraged. Non-letter characters such as ^, *, or ' attached to base letters are similarly discouraged. The English-based digraphs ch, sh, zh are problematic because ch is read as x by West Slavic speakers and sh/zh are unfamiliar in any Slavic tradition.

The Interslavic Cyrillic alphabet is modeled primarily on Serbian Vojvodina (Vuk's) Cyrillic, extended with letters from Ukrainian and other sources to cover the full phonemic inventory. It contains 29 letters and one digraph.

The following table lists all standard Cyrillic letters with their Latin equivalents and IPA values. Cyrillic Latin IPA Notes А а A a ɑ ~ a Б б B b b Ц ц C c t͡s Ч ч Č č t͡ʃ ~ tʂ Pronunciation closer to Ukrainian/Serbian ч than to Russian Д д D d d Е е E e ɛ ~ e Preceding consonant is not softened Є є Ě ě jɛ ~ ʲɛ Yat; borrowed from Ukrainian; preceding consonant is softened Ф ф F f f Г г G g ɡ ~ ɣ ɣ acceptable as compromise; h not acceptable Х х H h x И и I i i ~ ɪ Ј ј J j j From Serbian/Macedonian Cyrillic; see the relevant section К к K k k Л л L l l ~ ɫ Љ љ Lj lj ʎ ~ lʲ From Serbian/Macedonian Cyrillic; always soft М м M m m Н н N n n Њ њ Nj nj ɲ ~ nʲ From Serbian/Macedonian Cyrillic; always soft О о O o ɔ П п P p p Р р R r r May be syllabic С с S s s Ш ш Š š ʃ ~ ʂ Т т T t t У у U u u В в V v v ~ ʋ Ы ы Y y ɨ ~ i See the relevant section З з Z z z Ж ж Ž ž ʒ ~ ʐ

Five letters in the Cyrillic alphabet require specific comment because they are not universal across all Cyrillic-script Slavic languages. Є є (= Latin ě). The Cyrillic rendering of the yat phoneme. The Commission chose this letter from Ukrainian, where it has a pronunciation ([jɛ]) that ideally reflects the Interslavic pronunciation of yat. The historically more correct character ѣ (the traditional yat) was deliberately replaced for the sake of maximum intelligibility: ѣ is not used in any living Slavic language and is unfamiliar to most readers. Writing plain е instead of є is an accepted simplification, exactly parallel to writing e instead of ě in the Latin script. Ы ы (= Latin y). This letter represents the close central unrounded vowel ɨ. It is familiar to East Slavic and Polish readers but absent from South Slavic languages. Writers who are uncertain about its distribution, or who address a predominantly South Slavic audience, may substitute и throughout. Ј ј (= Latin j). Interslavic adopts this letter from Serbian and Macedonian Cyrillic rather than using Russian/Ukrainian й, because й has distributional restrictions (it cannot freely precede consonants or appear in all positions that j can). For writers who cannot produce ј, the following context-sensitive substitutions are recommended: ја → я, ју → ю (iotated vowels; most natural solution, though less familiar to some Serbian and Macedonian readers) јо: write as јо; Russian ё is to be avoided as it carries different connotations је after a vowel: write е; after a consonant: write ье; word-initially: write је ји after a consonant: write ьи; elsewhere: write и Љ љ (= Latin lj). From Serbian and Macedonian Cyrillic. Writers without this letter on their keyboard may write ль instead. Њ њ (= Latin nj). From Serbian and Macedonian Cyrillic. Writers without this letter on their keyboard may write нь instead.

The standard Cyrillic orthography uses one digraph and one common cluster: Cyrillic Latin IPA Notes Дж дж Dž dž d͡ʒ Standard Cyrillic digraph for the voiced affricate Шч шч Šč šč ʃt͡ʃ Cluster corresponding to Russian щ; not a true digraph The letter щ is not part of the standard Interslavic Cyrillic alphabet, though it may appear in texts aimed at a Bulgarian readership as an informal variant of шч.

Both alphabets are considered fully equal in status. The choice of script is the writer's prerogative and reflects audience and context rather than any linguistic hierarchy. The following practical guidelines apply to both scripts: Follow the standard Latin or Cyrillic orthography as closely as possible to ensure maximum intelligibility, especially for readers new to Interslavic. Do not mix Latin and Cyrillic letters within a single word or sentence. Keyboard layouts for both scripts are available and are the recommended input method for standard orthography. When a diacritic character cannot be produced, use the established fallback spellings ( for Latin; for Cyrillic) rather than ad hoc substitutions. Maintain consistency: if a fallback spelling is chosen, apply it uniformly throughout the entire text. Do not use archaic Cyrillic characters (ѣ, ѩ, ѫ, ѱ, etc.) in the standard orthography. Their use in running text seriously impedes intelligibility for modern readers.

This section specifies the normative orthographic conventions for Standard Interslavic beyond the letter-by-letter correspondences already established in the relevant section The rules below apply equally to both the Latin and Cyrillic scripts unless a script is explicitly named.

The standard orthography of Interslavic strives for a bijective (one-to-one) correspondence between phonemes and graphemes. Each phoneme SHOULD be represented by exactly one grapheme or digraph, and each grapheme or digraph SHOULD represent exactly one phoneme. The following documented exceptions to strict bijectivity are normative: Situation Description ě / є optionality The grapheme ě (Latin) / є (Cyrillic) MAY be simplified to e / е in informal registers without violating standard orthography (see the relevant section). y / ы optionality The grapheme y (Latin) / ы (Cyrillic) MAY be substituted by i / и by speakers whose native language does not distinguish the two (see the relevant section). Digraphs as single graphemes dž, lj, nj (Latin) and дж, љ, њ (Cyrillic) each represent a single phoneme and MUST be treated as single orthographic units for all purposes including capitalization and syllabification. Etymological diacritics Letters of the etymological alphabet carry diacritics that are not phonemically contrastive in standard Interslavic and SHOULD be omitted in non-etymological registers, with the exceptions of ć→č and đ→dž. Scripts MUST NOT be mixed within a single continuous text. A text written in the Latin script MUST use only Latin orthographic conventions; a text written in the Cyrillic script MUST use only Cyrillic orthographic conventions.

The first letter of every sentence SHALL be written in uppercase. This rule applies after every full stop, question mark, and exclamation mark that ends a sentence. Dobro jutro. Kak se imaš? When a sentence begins with a digraph (Dž, Lj, Nj in Latin; Дж in Cyrillic), only the first letter of the digraph is capitalized. Latin: Džungla je opasna. (not DŽungla) Cyrillic: Џунгла је опасна. (the digraph Дж acts as a single unit; uppercase form is Џ in Cyrillic)

The following categories SHALL be capitalized in both scripts: Personal names: Ivan, Marija Geographical names (countries, cities, rivers, mountains): Polska, Moskva, Dunaj Names of languages: Medžuslovjansky, Rusky, Česky Names of organizations and institutions when used as official titles Names of historical periods and events when used as proper names: Renesansa, Reformacija

Adjectives derived from proper nouns SHALL be written in lowercase in both scripts. This follows the majority Slavic convention and distinguishes Interslavic from English practice. Derived adjective Gloss Corresponding proper noun slovjansky / словјанскы Slavic Slovjanstvo rusky / рускы Russian Rusija parisky / парискы Parisian Pariz medžuslovjansky / меджусловјанскы Interslavic Medžuslovjansky (as a language name) Exception: When an adjective is part of an official proper name (e.g., the name of a publication, organization, or geographical entity used attributively as a title), it SHALL follow the capitalization of that title.

Month names, day-of-the-week names, and season names SHALL be written in lowercase in ordinary prose in both scripts: januar, februar, mart ponedělok, vtornik, srěda vesna, lěto, jesen, zima These words are capitalized only when they appear as the first word of a sentence or as part of a formal proper title.
documents.s: Months, Days of the Week, and Seasons

documents.t: Interslavic uses the following punctuation marks with the specified normative functions: Mark Name Primary function . točka Ends a declarative sentence or abbreviation , zapjataja Separates clauses, list items, and parenthetical elements ; točka s zapjatoju Separates coordinate clauses with internal commas; separates items in complex lists : dvotočje Introduces a list, quotation, or explanatory clause ? pytajny znak Ends a direct question ! vykličny znak Ends an exclamation or emphatic imperative — tire / dlouhe tire Marks a parenthetical aside, a sudden break, or a predicate without copula in certain registers … mnogotočje Indicates an omission or a trailing pause The space before and after punctuation marks follows continental European convention: no space before a mark, one space after a mark that ends a unit. The colon and semicolon follow the same pattern. The em-dash (—) is set with a thin space or no space on either side consistently within a document; either style is acceptable provided it is applied consistently.
documents.s: Standard Punctuation Marks and Their Usage

documents.t: Three styles of quotation marks are acceptable in Interslavic. The choice of style MAY reflect regional or typographic convention, but MUST be applied consistently within a single document: Style Opening Closing Notes Guillemets « » Common in Russian, Serbian, Bulgarian typography Low-high „ " Common in Czech, Slovak, German typography Curly (English) " " Acceptable in digital contexts Nested quotations use the alternative style from the outermost quotation: if the outer quotation uses «…», the inner quotation uses „…" or "…". Direct quotations of speech MAY alternatively be introduced with an em-dash at the start of a new paragraph, following South Slavic typographic practice: — Dobro jutro, — reče Ivan.

The following rules govern comma placement. They reflect the majority Slavic norm, which differs from English practice in several respects: Subordinate clauses: Every subordinate clause SHALL be set off from the main clause by a comma, regardless of its position (initial, medial, or final). Kogda prispejem, otpočnejem. On verit, čto ona pravi. Coordinating conjunctions: Coordinating conjunctions (i, a, no, ale, ili, ili...ili) that join independent clauses SHALL be preceded by a comma. When they join only words or phrases (without a new finite verb), the comma is omitted. Ona pišet, i on čita. (two clauses → comma) Ona pišet i čita. (same subject, two predicates → no comma) Parenthetical elements: Words, phrases, and clauses used parenthetically SHALL be set off by commas on both sides: Moj brat, Ivan, živi v Praze. Vocatives: Direct address (vocative) SHALL always be set off by commas: Prijatelju, kamo ideš?

The following abbreviations are normative for grammatical annotation in dictionary entries, grammatical tables, and linguistic commentary. They are the same regardless of script. Abbreviation Expansion Gloss nom. nominativ nominative gen. genitiv genitive dat. dativ dative akuz. akuzativ accusative instr. instrumental instrumental lok. lokativ locative vok. vokativ vocative sg. singular singular pl. plural plural m. mužsky rod masculine gender f. žensky rod feminine gender n. středny rod neuter gender anim. životny animate inanim. neživotny inanimate pf. dokonavny perfective impf. nedokonavny imperfective tr. prehodny transitive intr. neprehodny intransitive adj. prilagatelny adjective adv. prislov adverb prep. predlog preposition conj. sojuz conjunction part. čestica particle interj. meždometje interjection inf. infinitiv infinitive comp. komparativ comparative sup. superlativ superlative Abbreviations end with a full stop. When an abbreviation falls at the end of a sentence, only one full stop is used.
