The following abbreviations are accepted in Interslavic running text: Abbreviation Full form Gloss t.j. to jest that is (i.e.) t.zv. tak zvany so-called npr. na primer for example (e.g.) itd. i tako dalje and so on (etc.) itd.itd. i tako dalje i tako dalje and so on and so forth vs. versus versus str. stranica page gl. glava chapter par. paragraf paragraph / section br. broj number (no.) Abbreviations used in running text retain their full stop. When an abbreviation consisting of two abbreviated words is written with an internal full stop (e.g., t.j.), no space is inserted between the two parts. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should confirm whether script-specific Cyrillic abbreviation forms are required (e.g., т.ј. for т.е. in Cyrillic running text), or whether the Latin abbreviations are used across both scripts in technical contexts.

Compounds formed from two or more native Slavic roots SHALL be written as single words without a hyphen. This is the default form for productive compounding in Interslavic: Latin Cyrillic Gloss Components rukopis рукопис manuscript ruka + pisati železnica железница railway železo + -nica milosrdny милосрдны merciful milosrdje starosvětsky старосвѣтскы old-world stary + světy bogobojazlivy богобојазливы god-fearing bog + bojati The solid compound form applies regardless of the grammatical categories of the constituent elements (noun + noun, adjective + noun, verb root + noun, etc.).

A hyphen SHALL be used in the following environments: Compounds containing a proper-noun component: When one element of a compound is or contains a proper noun (a name of a person, place, or language), the compound is written with a hyphen to mark the boundary: češsko-rusky slovnik / чєшско-рускы словник (Czech-Russian dictionary) slovjano-germanske otnošenja / словјано-германске одношења (Slavic-Germanic relations) Coordinate (copulative) compounds: When two equal-status adjectives or nouns are joined to express a combined or dual quality, a hyphen is used: bělо-črveny / бѣло-чрвены (white-and-red) sěvero-vostočny / сѣверо-восточны (north-eastern) Compounds with stand-alone prefixal elements in certain environments: Prefixes that retain independent lexical meaning (such as ne-, prě-, su-) are written solid without a hyphen. However, when a prefix attaches to a proper noun, a hyphen MUST be used: ne-Ivan (non-Ivan, a hypothetical reference) — hyphen required before a proper noun ne znaju → neznam — no hyphen in standard derivation Repeated or reduplicated forms used for emphasis or distributive meaning: malo-malo, den za den-den — these stylistic forms may use a hyphen. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should specify additional environments (if any) in which direction-compound adjectives like sěvero-vostočny should be treated as solid vs. hyphenated, and whether the rule extends uniformly to all compass-point compounds and to toponym-derived directional adjectives.

When two roots are joined in a solid compound, a linking vowel (interfiks) is inserted between them according to the following rule: -o- after a root ending in a hard consonant (the default linking vowel): ruk-o-pis → rukopis / рукопис železo- → železnica / железница -e- after a root ending in a soft consonant (š, ž, č, j, lj, nj) or a root that historically ends in a soft consonant: oruž-e-nosec → oruženosec / оружeносeц (arms-bearer) duš-e-spasitelny / душeспаситeлны (soul-saving) The linking vowel is absent when the first element already ends in a vowel or when the compound is formed with a bound prefix . When the linking vowel is preceded by a fleeting vowel in the root (i.e., a vowel that disappears before suffixes — see the relevant section), the fleeting vowel is dropped and the linking vowel is inserted normally.

When a derivational or inflectional suffix is attached to a root ending in a soft consonant, the following spelling conventions apply: Iotation-derived softness (lj, nj, rj — from Proto-Slavic consonant + *j) is always written with the digraph, even before a suffix: morje → morjsky (not morsky) konj → konjsky (not konsky) Yer-derived softness (from Proto-Slavic consonant + *ь) is written in the etymological register with the appropriate diacritic (ń, ĺ, ŕ, ś, ź, t́, d́) but is dropped in the standard register: MS Plus: svińsky, standard: svinsky MS Plus: ruśsky, standard: russky Before i: Soft consonants before the vowel i are not explicitly marked in either register. The softness is implicit from the declension class or derivational context. See the relevant section for a discussion of this ambiguity and proposed solutions. Unresolved: Scope of Pre-Suffix Softening The standard orthography does not distinguish between roots that end in an inherently soft consonant (where softness should persist through derivation) and roots that end in a hard consonant (where no softening occurs). In the standard register, both morjsky (soft root morj-) and russky (potentially soft root ruś- in MS Plus) look like they follow different rules — the first preserves softness visibly (as a digraph), while the second does not. This asymmetry arises because iotation-derived softness is always visible in standard orthography (via digraphs lj, nj), while yer-derived softness is invisible in standard orthography (no diacritic). The etymological register resolves this by making both types visible, but most users write in the standard register. A clearer statement of the rules governing which consonants soften before which suffixes — and whether this softening extends to s and z — is needed. See the relevant sectiona.
documents.s: Soft Consonant Spelling at Morpheme Boundaries

documents.t: The following conventions govern when numerals are written as words versus digits: Numbers from one to ten SHALL be written as words in running prose: jeden, dva, tri, …, deset. Numbers from eleven upward MAY be written as digits in running prose, especially when precision is important or the number appears in a table or technical context. Numbers beginning a sentence SHALL always be written as words regardless of magnitude. Approximate or round numbers (hundreds, thousands) are typically written as words in literary or formal prose: sto, tyseč, million. When two numerals appear adjacent in a sentence, at least one SHALL be expressed as a word to avoid ambiguity. Large numerals expressed in digits MAY use a thin space or period as a thousands separator (see the relevant section).

Ordinal numerals in running text SHOULD be written as words: prvy, drugy, treti. In references to numbered items (sections, articles, volumes, pages), ordinal numerals MAY be expressed as a digit followed by a period: 1., 2., 3.. This convention follows continental European typographic practice and MUST NOT be confused with a terminal sentence-full-stop. v 3. paragrafu (in the 3rd paragraph) na str. 45 (on page 45) When an ordinal numeral expressed as digit-plus-period falls at the end of a sentence, only one period is written.

Interslavic follows continental European numeric convention: Function Symbol Example Thousands separator period (.) or thin space `` or 1 000 000 Decimal separator comma (,) 3,14 The thin-space convention for thousands grouping (1 000 000) is preferred in typeset documents as it avoids ambiguity with the sentence-final period. The period-as-thousands-separator (1.000) is acceptable in informal or technical writing. Note: The decimal comma contrasts with English convention (which uses a decimal point). Authors translating or adapting content from English sources MUST convert decimal points to decimal commas. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should confirm the preferred thousands separator (thin space vs. period) for normative Interslavic documents and whether a specific Unicode character (U+202F NARROW NO-BREAK SPACE) is required for digital publication.

When a word must be broken across a line, the break SHALL occur at a syllable boundary. The following principles determine syllable boundaries in Interslavic: Every syllable contains exactly one vowel nucleus (or one syllabic r). Intervocalic single consonants belong to the following syllable (onset principle): ma-ti, bra-ta. Digraphs are never split. The digraphs dž, lj, nj (Latin) and дж, љ, њ (Cyrillic) are treated as single consonants for syllabification purposes and SHALL NOT be divided across a line break. A break SHOULD NOT leave a single letter isolated at the beginning or end of a line.

When two or more consonants stand between two vowels, the break point is determined by the onset-maximization principle: as many consonants as are permissible as a word-initial cluster in Interslavic SHALL be assigned to the following syllable. Examples: Word Syllabification Notes sestra ses-tra str- is a valid onset prosba pros-ba sb- is not a standard onset učitelka u-či-tel-ka single consonants to following syllable medžuslovjansky med-žu-slov-jan-sky sl-, j- are valid onsets vostočny vos-toč-ny toč- onset Prefixes and suffixes retain their morphological identity at line breaks: a break MAY occur at a prefix-root or root-suffix boundary even if it does not align perfectly with the phonological syllable boundary. This is a secondary preference when the primary syllabic boundary falls within a morpheme that a reader might find difficult to parse: ob-učati is preferred over obu-čati at a line break, since ob- is a recognizable prefix. TODO: Committee Input Needed The committee should review and confirm the list of permissible word-initial consonant clusters in Standard Interslavic, as this list directly determines onset-maximization decisions at line breaks. Cross-reference with (Phonotactics) when that section is finalized.
documents.s: Rules for Consonant Clusters at Word Breaks

documents.t: When a lexical root contains etymological yat (ě), the yat SHOULD be written consistently across all derivatives and prefixed forms of that root. Inconsistent alternation between ě and e within a single morpheme family undermines the regularity of the orthography and obscures the etymological relationships that the Interslavic spelling system is designed to preserve. This principle applies to: unprefixed and prefixed forms of the same verb (e.g., plěniti and zaplěniti, not *pleniti alongside zaplěniti); verb stems and their derived nouns, adjectives, and verbal nouns; aspectual pairs formed by prefixation or suffixation.

As a general rule, verb infinitive endings in Interslavic are -ěti or -ęti, not *-eti. The ending -eti is legitimate only after functionally soft consonants (j, č, š, ž, c), where ě is simplified to e by the standard orthographic rule (see the relevant section). In all other environments, a verb ending that appears as -eti should be checked against its Proto-Slavic and Old Church Slavonic etymology to determine whether it should be -ěti. Unresolved: Inconsistent ě in verb stems in the dictionary Several verb families in the current dictionary exhibit inconsistent treatment of etymological ě: plěn- family: The unprefixed forms plen, pleniti lack ě, while the prefixed forms zaplěniti, zaplěnjati have it. The Old Church Slavonic form плѣнъ confirms that all forms should use plěn-. trěti "to rub": Currently listed as treti / utreti, but the etymological vowel is ě (cf. Russian тереть, Czech rozetřít). The correct form should be trěti. mrěti "to die": Currently listed as zamreti / umreti, but the etymological vowel is ě (cf. Russian замереть, Czech zemřít). The correct form should be zamrěti / umrěti. dreti vs. drati: The form dreti appears in some sources, but the majority of Slavic languages reflect *dьrati → drati (analogous to *bьrati → brati), with present tense dere. The preferred form is likely drati (dere) rather than either dreti or drěti. These inconsistencies should be resolved by systematic comparison with Proto-Slavic and OCS etymologies, applying the principle that etymological ě is preserved in all positions except after functionally soft consonants.
documents.s: The ě/e Distinction in Verb Endings

documents.t: Transliteration is the systematic, rule-governed conversion of text from the Latin script to the Cyrillic script, or vice versa, without reference to pronunciation rules beyond what the orthography itself encodes. Because the two standard Interslavic orthographies were designed as mutual mirrors, transliteration between them is nearly always one-to-one at the grapheme level; the few cases requiring contextual rules are enumerated in the relevant section and

The table below constitutes the normative mapping for converting Standard Latin orthography to Standard Cyrillic orthography. Every Latin grapheme listed in column 1 MUST be rendered by the corresponding Cyrillic grapheme in column 2. The IPA column is provided for reference only; it does not affect the transliteration rule. Latin Cyrillic IPA (canonical) Notes A a А а a B b Б б b C c Ц ц t͡s Č č Ч ч t͡ʃ D d Д д d Dž dž Дж дж d͡ʒ digraph; see the relevant section E e Е е e Ě ě Ѣ ѣ ě F f Ф ф f G g Г г ɡ H h Х х x I i И и i J j Ј ј j see the relevant section for post-vowel positions K k К к k L l Л л l Lj lj Љ љ ʎ digraph; see the relevant section M m М м m N n Н н n Nj nj Њ њ ɲ digraph; see the relevant section O o О о o P p П п p R r Р р r S s С с s Š š Ш ш ʃ T t Т т t U u У у u V v В в v Y y Ы ы y Z z З з z Ž ž Ж ж ʒ Total: 27 simple letters plus 3 digraphs (dž, lj, nj) on the Latin side; 29 simple letters plus 1 digraph (дж) on the Cyrillic side.

The table below constitutes the normative mapping for converting Standard Cyrillic orthography to Standard Latin orthography. Cyrillic Latin IPA (canonical) Notes А а A a a Б б B b b В в V v v Г г G g ɡ Д д D d d Дж дж Dž dž d͡ʒ digraph; see the relevant section Е е E e e Ж ж Ž ž ʒ З з Z z z И и I i i Ј ј J j j see the relevant section for post-vowel positions К к K k k Л л L l l Љ љ Lj lj ʎ М м M m m Н н N n n Њ њ Nj nj ɲ О о O o o П п P p p Р р R r r С с S s s Т т T t t У у U u u Ф ф F f f Х х H h x Ц ц C c t͡s Ч ч Č č t͡ʃ Ш ш Š š ʃ Ы ы Y y y Ѣ ѣ Ě ě ě Note: The Cyrillic letters Є є (used in some informal or regional variants as an alternative for Ѣ ѣ) transliterate to Ě ě. The letter Й й, used by some writers instead of Ј ј, transliterates to J j; however, Ј ј is the standard Cyrillic form.
Three Latin digraphs function as single phoneme units and each maps to a single Cyrillic unit, or vice versa. These MUST be treated atomically — never split — during transliteration.
documents.s: Digraph and Cluster Handling in Transliteration

documents.t: The digraph dž (Latin) represents the single affricate d͡ʒ and MUST be rendered as the digraph дж (Cyrillic) in its entirety. Latin Cyrillic Gloss džez джез jazz džungla джунгла jungle Capitalization rule: when dž begins a sentence or proper noun, only the first letter is capitalized: Džez, not DŽez. In all-caps contexts the full digraph is capitalized: DŽEZ / ДЖЕЗ.

The digraph lj (Latin) represents the palatal lateral ʎ and MUST be rendered as the single Cyrillic letter љ. Conversely, љ (Cyrillic) MUST be rendered as lj (Latin). Latin Cyrillic Gloss ljubov љубов love polje поље field Capitalization rule: at word-initial position, only L is capitalized: Ljubljana / Љубљана. In all-caps: LJUBOV / ЉУБОВ.

The digraph nj (Latin) represents the palatal nasal ɲ and MUST be rendered as the single Cyrillic letter њ. Conversely, њ (Cyrillic) MUST be rendered as nj (Latin). Latin Cyrillic Gloss njuhati њухати to smell konj конь* horse * Note: конь is an acceptable Cyrillic alternative for коњ in contexts where њ is unavailable on a keyboard; however, the normative form is коњ. See the relevant section

A digraph sequence that spans a morpheme boundary MUST NOT be treated as a single digraph. The following method resolves such ambiguities: Latin script: A sequence d+ž, l+j, or n+j that crosses a morpheme boundary — for example, the prefix nad- followed by a root beginning with ž — is not a digraph. In standard Latin orthography this ambiguity is rare and normally resolved by context; however, in etymological or careful-register writing a hyphen MAY be inserted at the morpheme boundary to make the split explicit: nad-žiti (to outlive), distinguishing it from any potential nadžiti. Cyrillic script: Because лj and нj are already single letters Љ / Њ in Cyrillic, and the digraph дж is written as a two-letter sequence, ambiguous clusters involving д+ж across a morpheme boundary MUST be marked with a hyphen when the reading is otherwise unclear: под-жечи (to set fire to, cf. podjěči).
documents.s: Distinguishing digraphs from letter sequences at morpheme boundaries

documents.t: In informal Cyrillic writing, the letter ј is sometimes replaced by iotated vowel letters following a vowel: Normative Cyrillic Informal alternative Latin equivalent ај ай aj еј ей ej ој ой oj уј уй uj ыј ый yj These informal alternatives do not affect Latin transliteration: all Cyrillic forms above transliterate uniformly to Latin j following the corresponding vowel. Conversely, when converting Latin to Cyrillic, the normative form with ј MUST be used; the Russian-style forms with й are permitted only as keyboard-fallback alternatives (see of the core orthography spec).
documents.s: The letter j (Latin) / ј (Cyrillic) after vowels

documents.t: Some writers use iotated vowel letters (ја, је, ји, ју) or pre-iotated letters (я, є/е, и, ю) instead of the sequence ј + vowel. For transliteration purposes these sequences MUST be resolved as follows: Cyrillic form Latin transliteration ја or я ja је or є (when = je) je ји ji ју or ю ju јa after a consonant (as soft marker) ja The normative Cyrillic form for all of the above is the sequence ј + plain vowel letter. Transliteration software SHOULD normalize informal Cyrillic iotated forms to the normative ј-sequence form before outputting Latin.

The etymological (scientific) alphabet contains additional letters beyond the standard set. These letters are defined and described in the relevant section; they appear only in scholarly or etymological-register texts. The transliteration correspondences for etymological letters are given below for completeness.

Latin (etym.) Cyrillic (etym.) IPA Notes Å å Ӑ ӑ ɒ ~ o syllabic liquid context: rå, lå Ę ę Ѧ ѧ ʲæ ~ ɛ̃ historically front nasal Ų ų Ѫ ѫ ʊ ~ u historically back nasal Ė ė Ъ ъ* ə strong front yer Ȯ ȯ Ъ ъ* ə strong back yer; see note Ć ć Ћ ћ t͡ɕ Proto-Slavic *tj; standard: č Đ đ Ђ ђ d͡ʑ Proto-Slavic *dj; standard: dž Ŕ ŕ / Ř ř Рь рь rʲ soft syllabic r Ĺ ĺ / Ľ ľ Ль ль lʲ soft l before consonant only Ń ń Нь нь nʲ soft n before consonant only Ś ś Сь сь sʲ Ź ź Зь зь zʲ Ď ď / D́ d́ Дь дь dʲ Ť ť / T́ t́ Ть ть tʲ * In the etymological Cyrillic register, ė and ȯ share the Cyrillic letter Ъ; the distinction is encoded only in the Latin form. In practice, the Cyrillic etymological register does not maintain a full one-to-one correspondence for the strong yer letters, and authors working in the etymological register are advised to use the Latin script.
Cyrillic (etym.) Latin (etym.) Notes Ӑ ӑ Å å Ѧ ѧ Ę ę Ѫ ѫ Ų ų Ъ ъ (as strong yer) Ė ė or Ȯ ȯ context-dependent; see note above Ћ ћ Ć ć Ђ ђ Đ đ

For quick reference, the following table collects all cases where simple letter-by-letter substitution is insufficient. Situation Rule Latin dž / lj / nj Treat as single unit; map to Cyrillic дж / љ / њ Cyrillic дж Treat as single unit; map to Latin dž Cyrillic љ / њ Map to Latin lj / nj Digraph spanning morpheme boundary Insert hyphen; do not merge Cyrillic ј after vowel (informal й) Normalize to ј; transliterate as Latin j Cyrillic iotated forms я, ю, є, ји Resolve to j + plain vowel before transliterating Cyrillic Є є (= Ѣ ѣ variant) Transliterate as Latin Ě ě Cyrillic Й й (= Ј ј informal) Transliterate as Latin J j Etymological letters Apply table; standard forms follow –
Sound system: phonemic inventory, phonotactics, stress, and phonological processes.

Inflection in Interslavic is kept as regular as possible. Nevertheless, alternations such as palatalization and iotation of consonants are pervasive across all Slavic languages. They play a crucial role in both inflection and word formation, and cannot be avoided even in the most simplified register of Interslavic — to do otherwise would produce forms that are heavily artificial and unnatural to every Slavic ear. This section specifies all morphophonological alternations that are normative in Standard Interslavic. Rules stated with SHALL or MUST are obligatory; rules stated with SHOULD are the strong default; rules stated with MAY represent acceptable variation.

Palatalization is the conditioned change of the velar consonants /k g h/ — and, in a subset of environments, the dental affricate /c/ — to the postalveolar consonants /č ž š/. Input (Latin) Output (Latin) IPA change k č /k/ → [t͡ʃ] g ž /ɡ/ → [ʒ] h š /x/ → [ʃ] c č /t͡s/ → [t͡ʃ] This alternation SHALL apply in each of the following environments: a. Vocative singular of masculine nouns (before -e): Nominative Vocative Gloss Bog Bože God Unverified Content The example vlk → vlče is not present in the source material and the base form vlk (without a vowel) is unusual for ISV. | vlk | vlče | wolf | | hlåpec | hlåpče | boy | Unverified Content The example duh → duše is not present in the source material. The source only lists Bog → Bože and hlåpėc → hlåpče as vocative examples. | duh | duše | spirit | b. Present-tense stem of consonant-stem verbs (before -e, -eš, etc.): Infinitive 2sg. Pres. Gloss pekti pečeš to bake mogti možeš can Unverified Content The example strěgti → strěžeš is not present in the source material. The source only lists pekti → pečeš and mogti → možeš as present-tense palatalization examples. | strěgti | strěžeš | to guard | c. Derivation: before the suffix -i- in derived verbs: Base noun/adj. Derived verb Gloss muka mučiti to torment sluga služiti to serve Unverified Content The example suhi → sušiti is not present in the source material. The source only lists muka → mučiti and sluga → služiti as derived-verb palatalization examples. | suhi | sušiti | to dry | d. Derivation: before specific derivational suffixes: The velars SHALL palatalize before the suffixes -an(in), -ba, -ec, -ica, -ina, -išče, -je, -ji, -nik, -ny, -ok/-ka/-ko, -sky, -stvo, and similar palatalizing suffixes. Base Derived form Gloss (base → derived) rųka rųčny hand → manual muha muška fly → small fly Unverified Content The examples Čeh → češsky and drug → družba are not present in the source material. The source only uses rųka → rųčny and muha → muška as suffix-palatalization examples. | Čeh | češsky | Czech (person) → Czech (adj.) | | drug | družba | friend → friendship |
documents.s: First (Velar) Palatalization: k g h c → č ž š č

documents.t: First palatalization SHALL NOT apply in the regular declension of nouns, adjectives, or pronouns, except in the vocative singular described in the relevant sectiona. Examples demonstrating absence of palatalization in declension: Form Grammatical context Gloss Čeh nom. sg. Czech (person) Čehi nom. pl. Czechs dȯlgy nom. sg. m. long dȯlgi nom. pl. animate long dȯlge nom. pl. inanimate long Unverified Content The source material does not discuss second (progressive) palatalization. This entire subsection, including the claim that its results are preserved in lexicalized forms, has no basis in the primary source.
documents.s: Scope Restriction: Palatalization is Not a General Declension Rule

documents.t: The historical second (progressive) palatalization — whereby velars following a front vowel shifted to /c z s/ — is not a productive synchronic rule in Interslavic. However, its results are preserved in a number of lexicalized forms and SHOULD be recognized by learners. TODO: Committee Input Needed A curated list of lexicalized second-palatalization forms in the Standard Interslavic lexicon needs to be compiled and attached to this section (e.g., knez from *kŭnędzĭ, otec from *otŭkŭ, etc.).

Iotation is the conditioned change of a hard consonant when it is followed by the palatal glide /j/. The result differs from simple softening: the /j/ is absorbed into the consonant and does not appear in the surface form. Iotation is distinct from palatalization and from simple palatal softening .

Unverified Content The detailed iotation table below includes IPA values, specific ISV example words (e.g., topjų, krmjų, krvjų, družų, koljų, klonjų, govorjų, košų, važų, svěćų, vođų, piščų, pušćų, jěžđų), and the zg → žž/žđ row that are not present in the source material. The source provides the consonant-change rules but not these specific example forms. The complete mapping of consonant + /j/ sequences to iotated outputs is specified in the table below. Input consonant (Latin) + /j/ → Output (Latin) IPA Interslavic example Gloss p pj /pj/ topjų I sink (tr.) b bj /bj/ ljubjų I love m mj /mj/ krmjų I feed f fj /fj/ — — v vj /vj/ krvjų I bleed k č /t͡ʃ/ kričų I shout g ž /ʒ/ družų I accompany h š /ʃ/ slušati to listen l lj /ʎ/ koljų I slaughter n nj /ɲ/ klonjų I bow r rj /rj/ govorjų I speak s š /ʃ/ košų I mow z ž /ʒ/ važų I weigh t ć (written č) /t͡ɕ/ ~ /t͡ʃ/ svěćų / svěčų I light d đ (written dž) /d͡ʑ/ ~ /d͡ʒ/ vođų / vodžų I lead sk šč /ʃt͡ʃ/ piščų I squeak st šć (written šč) /ʃt͡ɕ/ pušćų / puščų I release zg žž / žđ /ʒd͡ʑ/ — — zd žđ (written ždž) /ʒd͡ʑ/ jěžđų I ride Note on labials: In East Slavic and South Slavic languages, labial consonants + /j/ historically produced an epenthetic /l/ (e.g., Russian куплю, земля). In Interslavic this epenthesis SHALL NOT apply; the labial is simply written with j following it: kupjų, zemja, lovja, zatopjen. This approach is consistent with West Slavic usage and maximizes regularity. Note on t/d iotation: The etymological outputs ć and đ (IPA [t͡ɕ] and [d͡ʑ]) are the results found in Serbo-Croatian, which Interslavic adopts as its reference for this class. In standard orthography these are written as č and dž (Cyrillic ч and дж). The distinction between ć/đ and č/dž is therefore of etymological significance only and does not affect standard spelling. Note on st/zd iotation and the clusters šć and žđ: The iotation of st → šć and zd → žđ constitutes a distinct alternation pattern whose reflexes across Slavic languages differ substantially from those of simple t/​d iotation. For example: čistiti → čišćeńje/​čišćeny ("cleaning/cleaned"), jezditi → ježđeńje/​ježđeny ("riding/ridden"). There are two approaches to pronouncing these clusters in MS Plus. In the first approach (influenced by East Slavic, Polish), the preceding hard sibilant assimilates the softness of the following affricate: šć is pronounced [ɕt͡ɕ] (with š becoming [ɕ]) and žđ is pronounced [ʑd͡ʑ] (with ž becoming [ʑ]). This approach is reflected in IPA transcriptions in the Dynamic Dictionary. In the second approach (as in Serbo-Croatian, Czech, Slovak), each consonant retains its independent pronunciation: šć is [ʂt͡ɕ] and žđ is [ʐd͡ʑ], with the sibilant remaining hard. Both approaches are acceptable; the second is simpler in that each letter has a fixed pronunciation regardless of context. Unverified Content The note below about Proto-Slavic *kt/*gt clusters and the examples moć, noć, peć is not discussed in the source material. Note on kt/gt clusters: Proto-Slavic *kt and *gt before front vowels also merged with the iotation output of /t/; the reflex in Interslavic is ć (written č) / đ (written dž) as in Serbo-Croatian: moć "power," noć "night," peć "oven."

Iotation SHALL apply in the following morphological environments: a. First person singular present tense of -iti class verbs: The stem-final consonant undergoes iotation before the first-person ending -jų. Infinitive 1sg. Pres. Gloss prositi prošų to ask voditi vodžų to lead nositi nošų to carry ljubiti ljubjų to love govoriti govorjų to speak b. Past passive participle of -iti class verbs (before -jeny/-jeno/-jena): Infinitive PPP Gloss tratiti tračeny wasted, spent Unverified Content The examples světiti → svěčeny and prositi → prošeny are not in the source material. The source only provides tratiti → tračeny as an example of iotation in the past passive participle. | světiti | svěčeny | lit, illuminated | | prositi | prošeny | asked, requested | | posvętiti | posvęćeny | consecrated | | roditi | rođeny | born | The same iotation applies to verbal nouns (ending in -ćeńje/-đeńje in the etymological register, -čeńje/-dženje in standard): posvętiti → posvęćeńje "consecration," roditi → rođeńje "birth." The iotation of st and zd clusters follows the same pattern: čistiti → čišćeńje/​čišćeny "cleaning/cleaned," jezditi → ježđeńje/​ježđeny "riding/ridden." c. Imperfective derivation from perfective verbs in -iti (before -jati/-jovati): Perfective Imperfective Gloss nagraditi nagradžati to reward (ipfv.) Unverified Content The example osvětiti → osvěčati is not in the source material. The source only provides nagraditi → nagradžati as an imperfective derivation example. | osvětiti | osvěčati | to illuminate (ipfv.) | | ograditi | ograđati | to fence in (ipfv.) | | nasytiti | nasyćati | to saturate (ipfv.) | The alternation of stem-final -titi/-diti to -ćati/-đati (standard -čati/-džati) is one of the most productive iotation patterns in Interslavic verb morphology. d. With the palatalizing suffix -je (verbal nouns and place names): Base Derived form Gloss dělati dělanje doing, action morje primorje seashore

Iotation SHALL NOT apply across a prefix-stem boundary when the stem begins with j-. The prefix and stem retain their respective phonemes unchanged. Prefix + stem Result Gloss s- + jesti sjesti to sit down Unverified Content The example v- + jati → vjati is not in the source material, which only provides s+jesti → sjesti for this rule. Also, the line below stating vjati → *žati is internally inconsistent since vjati is already listed as the correct result above. | v- + jati | vjati | to take (pfv.) | These SHALL NOT be spelled šesti, vjati → *žati, etc.

When a consonant that is already soft is followed by /j/, the consonant and glide remain unchanged; no further iotation occurs. The softener blocks the change. In the etymological register, sequences such as ljj, njj are written as ľj, ńj to avoid gemination of j; similarly ŕj, t́j, d́j, śj, źj, šj, žj, čj. In standard orthography these are written simply as lj, nj, rj, tj, dj, sj, zj, šj, žj, čj. Where a stem ends in -j, the following suffix-initial j- is absorbed: e.g., dvoj-iti → dvoj-jų → dvojų. This pattern applies in: a. Verbal nouns formed with the palatalizing suffix -'je: Participle stem Verbal noun Gloss dělany dělanje doing pisany pisanje writing b. Place-name suffix -'je after already-soft consonants: Base Derived Gloss morje primorje seashore c. Possessive suffix -'ji (adjectival): Noun Possessive adj. Gloss Bog Božji God's, divine kot kotji cat's d. Instrumental singular of kost-type nouns (before -'jų): Noun Instr. sg. Gloss kost kostju bone (with) noć (= noč) nočju night (with) Practical consequence: The suffixes -je, -ji, and the instrumental ending -jų trigger iotation only of velars (k g h) and c; they do NOT trigger iotation of dentals (t d s z) or other consonants in standard Interslavic.
documents.s: Interaction of Soft Consonants with /j/: Palatalization Without Iotation

documents.t: Old Slavic did not permit /o/ after a soft consonant. Whenever such a sequence arose through inflection or suffixation, the /o/ was replaced by /e/. This rule is maintained as a productive alternation in Interslavic.
documents.s: The o/e Alternation After Soft Consonants

documents.t: After a soft consonant, the following ending and suffix vowels SHALL be changed as shown: After hard consonant After soft consonant -o -e -ov -ev -om -em -ogo -ego -oj -ej The same alternation applies within the suffixes -ost, -ovati, and -ovy when their stem ends in a soft consonant.

Hard-stem form Soft-stem form Gloss / grammatical note okno morje nom. sg. neuter gradov krajev gen. pl. masculine kogo čego gen. sg. pronoun tomu → togo čemu → čego gen. sg. demonstrative Unverified Content The doc uses svěžost but the source material has svěžest́ (with the soft sign and -est- suffix, not -ost). The tomu → togo / čemu → čego row is also not in the source. | svěžost | nočevati | noun/verb with suffix |

The phoneme /y/ MUST NOT follow a soft consonant (see the relevant section). As a consequence, inflectional endings containing /y/ alternate with endings containing /i/ or /e/ depending on whether the stem ends in a hard or soft consonant.

In adjectival and pronominal endings, /y/ becomes /i/ after soft consonants. Hard-stem form Soft-stem form Grammatical context dobry svěži nom. sg. m. adj. jegovyh mojih gen. pl. pronominal Unverified Content The example novomu / trěćemu is not in the source material. The source only provides svěži/dobry and mojih/jegovyh as examples for this alternation. | novomu | trěćemu | dat. sg. m. adj. |
documents.s: Adjectives and Pronouns: y → i After Soft Consonants

documents.t: In noun endings, the soft counterpart of /y/ is /e/, not /i/. Hard-stem form Soft-stem form Grammatical context domy kraje nom. pl. m. ženy zemje nom. pl. f. Unverified Content The example slovy / morja / morje is not in the source material, which only provides masculine and feminine examples for this alternation. The dual form morja / morje and the label "gen./nom. pl. n." are unverified. | slovy | morja / morje | gen./nom. pl. n. |
documents.s: Nouns: y → e After Soft Consonants

documents.t: The phoneme /ě/ MUST follow a hard consonant or /c/ in standard Interslavic (see the relevant section). Consequently, in the dative and locative singular of feminine nouns with a soft stem, the ending -ě is replaced by -i. This is the inverse of the y → e rule : whereas the y/e alternation reflects the impossibility of /y/ after soft consonants, this alternation reflects the impossibility of /ě/ after soft consonants. Hard-stem form Soft-stem form Grammatical context ženě zemji dat./loc. sg. f. Unverified Content The examples sestě / nočji and ženbě / zemji are not in the source material. The source only provides ženě / zemji as an example for this alternation. The form sestě is also questionable as sestra is an a-stem noun. | sestě | nočji (= noči) | dat./loc. sg. f. | | ženbě | zemji | dat./loc. sg. f. |
documents.s: ě → i in Dative/Locative of Soft-Stem Feminine Nouns

documents.t: A characteristic feature of Slavic morphophonology is the existence of "fleeting" (movable) vowels — vowels that appear in some forms of a word but disappear in others. These reflect the Common Slavic reduced vowels (jers) ъ and ь, which were lost in "weak" syllabic positions and vocalized to /o/ and /e/ in "strong" positions. In most cases, a fleeting vowel appears when its absence would produce an impermissible final consonant cluster, and disappears when a vowel-initial ending follows.

Fleeting vowels appear in the following morphological environments: a. Nominative (and inanimate accusative) singular of certain masculine nouns: These include nouns ending in the suffixes -ėc and -ȯk, plus a number of other lexical items. Nom. sg. Gen. sg. Gloss otėc otca father pěsȯk pěska sand pės psa dog sȯn sna dream krȯv krvi blood Unverified Content The example dėn / dne is not in the source material, which only lists otėc/otca, pěsȯk/pěska, pės/psa, sȯn/sna, and krȯv/krvi. | dėn | dne | day | b. Genitive plural of feminine and neuter nouns that would otherwise end in a consonant cluster: Nom. sg. Gen. pl. Gloss okno okėn window miska misȯk bowl Unverified Content The example sestra / sestėr is not in the source material, which only lists okno/okėn and miska/misȯk for genitive plural fleeting vowels. | sestra | sestėr | sister | c. Nominative singular masculine of certain pronouns: Form Related form Gloss vėś vśa (f.) all, entire d. Before certain derivational suffixes: Base Derived form Gloss pismo pisėmny written, scriptural e. The prepositions s and v before consonant clusters or difficult pronouns: Preposition Context Gloss s sȯ mnojų with me (instr. sg.) v vȯ vsih in all (loc. pl.) s- (prefix) sȯdržati to contain v- (prefix) vȯjdti to enter

In standard orthography, fleeting vowels are not distinguished from regular vowels in spelling; the word is written in its full (strong) form with o or e. In the etymological register, fleeting vowels MAY be marked with the dot diacritic: ȯ for fleeting /o/ and ė for fleeting /e/. Dictionaries SHOULD mark fleeting vowels to signal the alternation to learners and NLP tools. Unverified Content The SHOULD requirement for dictionaries to mark fleeting vowels, the cross-Slavic reflex table below, and the detailed historical explanation of *ъ and *ь reflexes across Russian, Czech, Serbo-Croatian, and Bulgarian are not present in the source material. The source only states that fleeting vowels are marked with ȯ and ė in the etymological alphabet and are reflexes of the Common Slavic jers. The historical basis: ȯ descends from Proto-Slavic *ъ (hard jer) and ė from *ь (soft jer). The different vowel qualities reflect the different reflexes of the two jers across the Slavic languages: Etymological mark Proto-Slavic source Russian reflex Czech reflex Serbo-Croatian reflex ȯ *ъ о (stressed) / ∅ (unstressed) e / ∅ a / ∅ ė *ь е (stressed) / ∅ (unstressed) e / ∅ e / ∅ The cross-Slavic divergence of the hard jer *ъ (producing o in Russian, e in Czech/Slovak, a in Serbo-Croatian, ъ [ɤ/ɐ] in Bulgarian) is the main phonological reason the ȯ diacritic was introduced: it allows consistent etymological spelling where the standard letters would differ across national conventions.
documents.s: Fleeting Vowel Marking in Citation Forms

documents.t: Within obstruent clusters, voicing assimilates regressively: each obstruent in the cluster takes on the voicing of the rightmost obstruent. This rule applies within words and SHOULD be reflected in pronunciation; it is not indicated in standard orthography. Rule Environment Example Pronunciation Gloss Voiceless + voiced → voiced + voiced cluster-internal prosba [ˈprɔzba] request Voiced + voiceless → voiceless + voiceless cluster-internal vod+ka [ˈvɔtka] vodka (loanword)

The consonants /v/ and /j/ SHALL NOT trigger devoicing of a preceding obstruent. They are phonologically transparent with respect to voicing assimilation: an obstruent before /v/ or /j/ retains its underlying voicing specification.

Final devoicing (the neutralization of voiced/voiceless obstruent contrasts in word-final position) is optional in Standard Interslavic. All major Slavic languages apply final devoicing except Ukrainian and Shtokavian (Serbian/Croatian/Bosnian), where word-final obstruents retain their voicing. Speakers MAY apply final devoicing or not, according to their native habits — neither approach significantly affects intelligibility. The following devoicing correspondences apply in word-final position: Voiced Devoiced Example Devoiced pronunciation b p hlěb [xlʲɛp] d t sad [sat] dž č vodž [vɔʈ͡ʂ] g k geolog [gɛɔɫɔk] v f arhiv [arxif] z s prikaz [prikas] ž š bagaž [bagaʂ] Unverified Content This entire section on vowel reduction () has no basis in the primary source material, which does not discuss vowel reduction or unstressed vowel quality. All normative claims (SHALL, MAY) in this section are unsourced.

Unstressed vowel reduction — the process by which unstressed vowels merge into a central or mid vowel (as in Russian аканье / akan'je, where unstressed /o/ and /a/ merge to [ɐ] or [ə]) — is NOT prescribed in Standard Interslavic. All vowels SHALL maintain their full phonemic quality regardless of stress position. This ensures maximum intelligibility across the Slavic language area, since vowel quality carries lexical and morphological information that would otherwise be lost.

Speakers from languages with strong vowel reduction (Russian, Ukrainian, to a lesser extent Bulgarian) MAY apply their native reduction patterns in casual or spontaneous speech. This is an acceptable allophonic variation and does not render the speech non-standard, provided the phonemic distinctions remain recoverable from context. In formal, written, or normative contexts, full vowel quality SHALL be maintained.

Section Alternation Trigger environment Example Obligatory? k g h c → č ž š č Before -e (vocative, verb present); before -i-; before palatalizing suffixes Bog → Bože; pekti → pečeš SHALL Consonant + j → iotated output 1sg. pres. of -iti verbs; PPP; imperfective derivation; suffix -je prositi → prošų; tratiti → tračeny SHALL Unverified Content The summary example kosji does not appear in section itself, which uses kotji (from kot). This is an internal inconsistency. The form kosji (from kos, blackbird) is unverified. | | Soft cons. + j → unchanged | Already-soft consonant before suffix -je/-ji/-jų | kosji (not \*košji) | SHALL | | | -o/-ov/-om/-ogo/-oj → -e/-ev/-em/-ego/-ej | After soft consonant | gradov → krajev; okno → morje | SHALL | | | y → i (adj./pron.) or y → e (noun) | After soft consonant | dobry → svěži; domy → kraje | SHALL | | | ě → i | Dat./loc. sg. f. after soft consonant | ženě → zemji | SHALL | | | Fleeting o/e: appears in strong position, absent in weak | Nom. sg. m.; gen. pl. f./n.; before certain suffixes | otėc ~ otca; okno ~ okėn | lexically determined | | | Voicing assimilation (regressive) | Obstruent clusters | prosba → [ˈprɔzba] | pronunciation only | | | Vowel reduction | Unstressed position | — | NOT normative |

Interslavic is neither a national language, nor does it attempt to emulate one. Instead, it is based on twelve national languages, each with its own phonology and orthography tailored to fit its particular characteristics. In general, the further South one goes in the Slavic world, the smaller the phoneme inventory becomes. Both spoken and written Interslavic SHALL aim to occupy the middle ground among these languages as much as possible. A high number of phonemes makes intuitive writing and pronunciation harder for speakers accustomed to fewer phonemes. A low number, conversely, makes it harder to map a character or sound to an equivalent in one's own language, which has a negative effect on intelligibility. For example, the ideal pronunciation of the word for "five" would be something like [pjætʲ]. If written as pęt́, a Russian speaker can easily recognize their own пять, a Serb their own pet, and a Pole their own pięć; but a Serb has no way of knowing when their own e and t correspond to ę and t́ without consulting a dictionary. On the other hand, the simplified form pet could be misread as Russian петь "to sing" or Polish pet "cigarette butt." To resolve this dilemma, Interslavic has a basic set of phonemes present in all or the vast majority of Slavic languages, more or less with the same phonetic values. In addition, Interslavic has a set of optional (etymological) phonemes that connect directly to Old Church Slavonic and reflect particular phonetic distinctions between individual languages. These optional phonemes MAY be written by means of the Interslavic etymological alphabet (formerly known as Naučny Medžuslovjansky).

Basic Interslavic has seven vowel phonemes. Five of them — /a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, /u/ — have a relatively uniform pronunciation across speakers and are mandatory in all registers. The remaining two — /ě/ and /y/ — have a pronunciation that may differ between speakers. The following table lists the core vowels with their canonical IPA values and permitted allophonic realizations: Grapheme (Latin) Grapheme (Cyrillic) IPA (canonical) Allophonic range Example Gloss a а /a/ [a] ~ [ä] mama mother e е /e/ [e], [ɛ] meso meat i и /i/ [i] mi we o о /o/ [ɔ] dom house u у /u/ [u] ruka hand ě є /ě/ [jɛ], [ʲe], [e] rěka river y ы /y/ [ɨ], [i], [ɪ] syn son Notes on /ě/: This phoneme represents the historical yat. Its core realization is [jɛ], matching the pronunciation of Ukrainian є. It softens the preceding consonant, with the degree of softening left to the speaker. East Slavic speakers typically pronounce it [jɛ] or [ʲe]; South Slavic speakers (especially Serbian/Croatian) as [e]. The phoneme /ě/ MUST always follow a hard consonant or /c/ (see the relevant section). Notes on /y/: The ideal pronunciation is [ɨ], as in Russian ы, Polish y, and Ukrainian и. The realization [ɪ] is acceptable but too close to [i] to reliably maintain the contrast. The German-style [y] (rounded front vowel) is not found in any Slavic language and should not be used. The phoneme y never softens the preceding consonant, which is significant for morphology (e.g., the hard/soft declension distinction: dobry → dobrogo vs. svěži → svěžego). In South Slavic and Ukrainian, /i/ and /y/ have merged into one vowel; substituting all occurrences of y with i is an acceptable simplification in written Interslavic, though this obscures certain morphological patterns. The phoneme /y/ MUST NOT follow a soft consonant, appear word-initially, or appear after a vowel (see the relevant section).

In addition to the seven core vowels, Interslavic has five optional vowels that belong to the etymological register. Their diacritical marks are not written in the standard (basic) orthography; they SHALL only appear in etymological-register texts. Unverified Content The canonical IPA /ɛ̃/ for ę in the table below uses a nasal vowel symbol, but the source explicitly states that ę is "NOT pronounced as nasal vowels like in Polish." This contradicts both the source and the note on /ę/ later in this document (which also says "NOT a nasal vowel"). Grapheme (Latin) Grapheme (Cyrillic) IPA (canonical) Relationship to standard Example Gloss å а̊ /ɒ/ ~ /ɑ/ standard equivalent is a; pronounced as o in Northern flavourised forms kråva cow ė ė /ə/ fleeting vowel; corresponds to e when pronounced pes (nom.) / pės (etym.) dog ę ѧ /ɛ̃/ ~ /ʲæ/ pronounced as ja in flavourised forms; as e in simplified forms pęť five ȯ ȯ /ə/ fleeting vowel; corresponds to e when pronounced son (nom.) / sȯn (etym.) dream ų ѫ /ʊ/ pronounced as u; in flavourised forms indistinguishable from u rųka hand Notes on /ę/: Historically, /ę/ represents the Proto-Slavic front nasal vowel *ę. In modern Interslavic it is NOT a nasal vowel but is pronounced approximately [ʲæ] — intermediate between [a] and [e], with palatalization of the preceding consonant. It is also acceptable to pronounce it [ʲa] (analogous to Russian я), [ʲɛ] (analogous to Czech ě), or simply [e]. In simplified Interslavic, ę is written and pronounced as e; however, in the etymological register, ę and e are distinct: e.g., česť "honour" vs. čęsť "part/portion." Notes on /ų/: Historically, /ų/ represents the Proto-Slavic back nasal vowel *ǫ. In modern Interslavic it is NOT a nasal vowel but is pronounced [ʊ] — a sound intermediate between [u] and [o]. It is also acceptable to pronounce it simply as [u], making no distinction between ų and u in speech. The Cyrillic etymological form uses the Old Church Slavonic letter Ѫѫ (jus bolšoj). Notes on /å/: This vowel MUST be preceded by a liquid consonant (r or l) and followed by a consonant. It never occurs word-initially or after a vowel. It derives from Proto-Slavic *or and *ol sequences. In standard Interslavic it is written and pronounced as a (following the South Slavic reflex). In Northern flavourised Interslavic it may be written and pronounced as o (following the Polish and East Slavic reflexes). The ideal etymological pronunciation is [ɒ] or [ɑ] — intermediate between [a] and [ɔ]. Unverified Content The vowel feature matrix below is not present in the source material. The distinctive-feature classifications (High, Back, Round, Nasal) are an editorial elaboration not found in the original phonology description.

The following matrix covers all twelve vowels (core + optional) with their distinctive features: Phoneme High Back Round Nasal (historical) Notes /a/ − − − − open central /e/ − − − − mid front /i/ + − − − high front /o/ − + + − mid back /u/ + + + − high back /ě/ ± − − − variable; front /y/ + + − − high back unrounded /å/ − + ± − optional; open back; between [a] and [ɔ] /ė/ − − − − optional; schwa-like; fleeting /ę/ − − − + (hist.) optional; front, historically nasal /ȯ/ − + + − optional; mid-back; fleeting /ų/ + + + + (hist.) optional; high back, historically nasal Unverified Content This systematic allophonic variation table is not present in the source material. The source provides general guidance that "pronunciation of most sounds is variable, depending on the nationality of the speaker" and notes some variation on the "open/closed and front/back" scale, but does not enumerate per-vowel allophonic ranges as presented here.

Interslavic permits the following allophonic variation in natural speech: /a/: realized uniformly as [a] across all positions. /e/: MAY be realized as [e] or [ɛ]; the more open realization [ɛ] is characteristic of South Slavic speakers. The pronunciation of e is always hard — the preceding consonant is never softened. This contrasts with some northern Slavic languages (notably Russian and Polish) where the native reflex of Proto-Slavic *e softens the preceding consonant in inherited vocabulary while remaining hard in loanwords. Interslavic makes no such distinction: e is uniformly hard in all words regardless of origin. /i/: realized uniformly as [i], similar to the vowel in English "knee" or "beach"; in unstressed syllables MAY reduce toward [ɪ]. Czech speakers should note that the Interslavic i is always the close front [i], not the more centralized [ɪ] that Czech uses for short i and y. Regarding softening of preceding consonants before i, standard Interslavic imposes no prescriptive rules — each speaker MAY soften or not according to their native habits. /o/: the standard realization is [ɔ] (open-mid back rounded vowel), regardless of position in the word. Pronouncing it as a close [o] is not recommended, as it sounds unnatural in a Slavic context. /u/: realized uniformly as [u]. /ě/: the core realization is [jɛ], which matches the Ukrainian letter є that represents this phoneme in Cyrillic. The phoneme softens the preceding consonant, but the degree of softening is left to the speaker's preference. For example, sědy (grey-haired) may be pronounced [sjɛdɨ], [sʲɛdɨ], or [ɕɛdɨ]; tělo (body) may be [tjɛlɔ], [tʲɛlɔ], or [t͡sʲɛlɔ]; dělo (affair) may be [djɛlɔ], [dʲɛlɔ], or [dzʲɛlɔ]. However, the extreme Polish-style double palatalization (e.g., [t͡ɕ] for t before ě) is not recommended, as it may be confused with entirely different phonemes and impair intelligibility. /y/: the ideal realization is [ɨ] (as in Russian ы, Polish y, or Ukrainian и). The realization [ɪ] is acceptable but somewhat too close to [i], while [ʏ] (sometimes attributed to certain Russian speakers) is too deep. The German [y] (as in über) is not acceptable — no Slavic language uses this sound. The phoneme y never softens the preceding consonant, which has morphological significance: e.g., dobry → dobrogo (hard declension), but svěži → svěžego (soft declension) — when the orthography is simplified by writing only i, this declension-class distinction is lost. Speakers whose native language does not distinguish /i/ from /y/ MAY merge both to [i]. Interslavic has syllabic /r/ and /ŕ/ (the latter belonging to the optional etymological set). A consonant is syllabic when it is preceded by a consonant and not followed by a vowel. In this position, /r/ and /ŕ/ function as syllabic nuclei and are pronounced with a preceding schwa: [ər] and [ʲər] respectively. Latin Cyrillic IPA Gloss trg трг [tərg] market cukr цукр [ʦukər] sugar Unverified Content The examples pŕvy, cŕkòv, and čŕv below are not in the source material. The source provides only trg, mŕtvy, and cukr as syllabic-r examples. Latin Cyrillic IPA Gloss pŕvy пŕвы [pʲərvɪ] first cŕkòv цŕков [ʦʲərkov] church čŕv чŕв [ʧʲərv] worm Unverified Content The discussion of Proto-Slavic syllabic l/ľ, the òl representation, the examples vòlk/pòlk/žòlty, and the claim about East Slavic reflexes of syllabic r are not present in the source material. The source only mentions syllabic r and ŕ with examples trg, cukr, and mŕtvy. Proto-Slavic had four syllabic consonants: hard /r/, soft /ŕ/, hard /l/, and soft /ľ/. In modern Interslavic, syllabic /l/ is no longer used; the sequence that would have been syllabic /l/ is instead represented as òl (Latin) / ол (Cyrillic) with the optional fleeting-vowel notation ò: Latin Cyrillic Gloss vòlk волк wolf pòlk полк regiment žòlty жолты yellow Syllabic /r/ is attested in Czech, Slovak, Serbo-Croatian, Slovene, and Macedonian, justifying its retention in Interslavic. The hard/soft distinction for syllabic /r/ is supported by East Slavic reflexes: hard syllabic /r/ corresponds to Russian ор, while soft syllabic /r/ corresponds to Russian ер or ёр.

In addition to the 23 core consonants, Interslavic has seven optional consonant phonemes available in the etymological register. These phonemes are normally written as their core equivalents in standard orthography; the etymological letters are used only when the distinction is phonologically significant at the etymological level. Latin (etym.) Cyrillic (etym.) IPA Standard equiv. Example (etym.) Example (std.) Gloss ŕ рь /rʲ/ r pŕvy prvy first ľ љ /lʲ/ lj ovaľny ovaljny oval ń нь /nʲ/ nj hođeńje hođenje going ť ть /tʲ/ t pamęť pamęt memory ď дь /dʲ/ d gvozď gvozd nail ś сь /sʲ/ s gųś gųs goose ź зь /zʲ/ z vitęź vitęz hero Unverified Content The detailed distribution rules below for etymological consonants (when to use ľ vs. lj, ń vs. nj, ŕ vs. rj, etc.) go beyond what the source material specifies. The source states only that ľ and ń are used for writing lj and nj before j to avoid gemination, and that the acute-accent letters replace Cyrillic soft sign. The specific rules about stem-final contexts and morpheme boundaries are not in the source. Notes on usage of etymological consonants: The core digraphs lj and nj are used where the softness is present in both standard and etymological Interslavic (i.e., recognized across most Slavic languages). The letters ľ, ń are used only where the softness appears exclusively in the etymological register. Specifically: nj (standard) is used in most positions: e.g., konj (horse), banja (bath); ń (etymological) is used primarily before j: e.g., hođeńje (going), iměńje (estate). rj (standard) appears before vowels: e.g., morje (sea), burja (storm); ŕ (etymological) appears before consonants and word-finally, and also before vowels in inflected forms where the stem-final soft /r/ is preserved: e.g., lěkaŕ (doctor, nom.) → lěkaŕa (gen.). ľ (etymological) appears before j and at morpheme boundaries when a hard stem-final /l/ is softened by a suffix: e.g., siľny (strong) from sila (force); nasiľje (violence). The etymological consonants ť, ď, ś, ź are written in all positions where the corresponding soft sounds occur (word-finally, before consonants, before j, and before vowels): e.g., gosť (guest), ryś (lynx), gvozď (nail), ťma (darkness). The acute-accent letters (ť, ď, ś, ź, ŕ) in the etymological Latin orthography replace the Cyrillic soft sign ь, which was originally an ultrashort vowel [ĭ] before becoming a softening marker.

Like all Slavic languages, Interslavic MUST distinguish between hard (plain) and soft (palatalised) consonants. This distinction is binary and phonologically significant. Two Sources of Consonant Softness​ The soft consonants lj, nj, and (in the etymological register) ŕ can arise from two historically distinct processes, which have different reflexes across modern Slavic languages: Iotation-derived softness (Proto-Slavic *lj, *nj, *rj — consonant + *j). This softness is reflected in all modern Slavic languages, both Northern and Southern. Examples: kralj "king" (< *korljь), polje "field" (< *polje), konj "horse" (< *konjь), morje "sea" (< *morje). In standard orthography these are written with the digraphs lj, nj, rj. Yer-derived softness (Proto-Slavic *lь, *nь, *rь — consonant + front yer). This softness is reflected in Northern Slavic languages (Russian, Polish, Czech, Slovak, etc.) but not in Southern Slavic (Serbian, Croatian, Slovene, etc.), where the consonant remains hard after the yer was lost. Examples: velmi/​veĺmi "very" (< *velьmi; cf. Croatian veoma with hard L), den/​dėń "day" (< *dьnь; cf. Croatian dan with hard N), kamen/​kameń "stone" (< *kamy, gen. *kamene; cf. Croatian kamen with hard N). In the etymological register (MS Plus), these two types are distinguished orthographically: iotation-derived softness uses the digraphs lj, nj, while yer-derived softness uses the háček letters ĺ (or ľ), ń, ŕ. In standard orthography, yer-derived softness is simply dropped (the consonant is written hard), since Southern Slavic speakers pronounce it hard. Unresolved: Iotation vs. Yer-Derived Softness The current Interslavic orthography and dictionary are not yet fully consistent in distinguishing iotation-derived softness (present in all Slavic languages) from yer-derived softness (present only in Northern Slavic). Some entries conflate the two: for instance, bolj appears with the iotation digraph lj, but the word derives from *bolь (yer-softened), and Croatian/Serbian have hard L (bol). The standard form should arguably be bol (hard), with boĺ in MS Plus. Similarly, velmi should be veĺmi in MS Plus rather than veljmi. The athematic masculine nouns (kamenj, korenj, etc.) present the same issue — the final softness is yer-derived, not iotated, so the standard form could end in a hard consonant (kamen) with the MS Plus form using ń (kameń). The status of rj is additionally ambiguous: unlike lj and nj, the sequence rj is not officially recognized as a phoneme pair by the Commission, yet it functions as one in practice (nearly all occurrences correspond to Proto-Slavic soft *r). Whether rj should be permitted word-finally (e.g., rybarj vs. rybaŕ) remains undecided. The theoretical framework for this distinction can be stated precisely in terms of Proto-Slavic etymology: Iotated consonants (Proto-Slavic *lj, *nj, *rj — consonant fused with the glide *j) were inherently soft from the earliest reconstructable stage. The softness is independent of any adjacent yer. Examples: *korljь → kralj, *konjь → konj, *morje → morje. Crucially, even in the South Slavic languages — which lost yer-derived softness entirely — iotated softness is preserved: Croatian kralj, konj, more all retain the palatalized consonant. Yer-softened consonants (Proto-Slavic *lь, *nь, *rь, *tь, *dь, *sь, *zь — consonant adjacent to the front yer) acquired their softness from the yer. When the yer was lost in the "fall of the yers" (c. 10th–12th century), the softness persisted in Northern Slavic languages but was lost in most Southern Slavic languages. Examples: *silьnъ → Russian сильный (soft L) but Croatian silan (hard L); *kostь → Russian кость (soft T) but Croatian kost (hard T); *dьnь → Russian день (soft N) but Croatian dan (hard N, with vowel change). This asymmetry explains a key design property of Interslavic: the standard orthography writes iotated softness explicitly (with digraphs lj, nj, rj) because it is universal across Slavic, but drops yer-derived softness entirely because it would be alien to South Slavic readers. The MS Plus register restores yer-derived softness for the benefit of Northern Slavic readers. Known dictionary errors arising from conflation of the two types include dėnj (the word for "day"), where the Proto-Slavic form *dьnь indicates yer-softening, not iotation — the correct standard form should be den (hard) with MS Plus dėń, not dėnj. Similarly, myslj ("thought," from *myslь) uses the iotation digraph despite yer-derived softness — cf. Croatian misao with no trace of softness, confirming that the correct standard form should be mysl with MS Plus mysĺ. Resolving this systematically would require auditing the dictionary to classify each instance of lj, nj, rj by its Proto-Slavic origin and adjusting the standard and MS Plus forms accordingly. Hard consonants: Labials: p /p/, b /b/, f /f/, v /v/, m /m/ Hard dentals/alveolars: t /t/, d /d/, s /s/, z /z/, n /n/, r /r/, l /l/ Velars: k /k/, g /g/, h /x/ Soft consonants: Postalveolars: š /ʃ/, ž /ʒ/, č /t͡ʃ/, dž /d͡ʒ/ Soft dentals/alveolars (core): lj /lʲ/, nj /nʲ/ Soft dentals/alveolars (etymological): ŕ /rʲ/, ť /tʲ/, ď /dʲ/, ś /sʲ/, ź /zʲ/ Palatal approximant: j /j/ Special status of c /t͡s/: The affricate c [t͡s] is pronounced hard phonetically, but it behaves like a soft consonant morphologically — i.e., it conditions the same morphophonemic alternations that soft consonants trigger. Its voiced counterpart [d͡z] does not occur in Interslavic. Softening: Softening is the process of adding the feature [+palatal] to a consonant, resulting in a more palatal pronunciation (marked in IPA as [ʲ]). In Interslavic, only lj and nj are mandatory soft consonants; the etymological set adds ť, ď, ś, ź, ŕ. East Slavic speakers are likely to pronounce soft consonants as softened (palatalised) dental or alveolar consonants [Cʲ]; West Slavic speakers more often as fully palatal consonants [C̟]. Both pronunciations are equally correct, although the East Slavic realization is probably easier to understand for South Slavic speakers. Soft consonants before vowels: Before i, ě, ę, ė, and ŕ, a hard consonant MAY be softened or palatalised. For example, buditi (to wake) may be pronounced [buditi], [budʲitʲi], or [buɟici]. The affricate pair ć /t͡ɕ/ and đ /d͡ʑ/ deserve special mention: these are the iotated counterparts of t and d respectively, and are of etymological origin (Proto-Slavic *tj and *dj). In standard orthography they are written and pronounced as č and dž; the distinction between ć/č and đ/dž is etymological only. The MS Plus pronunciation of ć and đ was chosen as the midpoint of Slavic reflexes: [t͡ɕ] and [d͡ʑ], identical to the Serbo-Croatian (Shtokavian) pronunciation. These sounds are close to English "ch" and "j" respectively, but softer — produced with maximally relaxed, lowered jaw position. Unlike the other iotated consonants (*lj, *nj, *rj, whose reflexes are relatively uniform across Slavic), the reflexes of Proto-Slavic *tj and *dj have diverged dramatically: Russian щ/ч and жд/ж, Ukrainian/Belarusian ч and дж, Czech c and z, Polish c and dz, Slovak c/ť and dz/ď, Serbo-Croatian ć and đ, Slovene č/t and j/d, Macedonian ќ/ш/т and ѓ/д, Bulgarian щ/т and жд/д. The standard Interslavic forms č and dž match the Ukrainian and Belarusian reflexes, while ć and đ in MS Plus match the Serbo-Croatian reflexes. ć and đ form a voiceless–voiced pair and exhibit a high degree of symmetry in their morphophonological behaviour. The tj and dj Sequences and the Absence of Soft T/D​ It is important to distinguish the etymological soft consonants ť /tʲ/ and ď /dʲ/ — which belong exclusively to the MS Plus register — from the letter sequences tj and dj that appear in certain dictionary words (e.g., bratja, kostjum, tjulenj, adjutant, gospodja, Madjar). Standard Interslavic has no soft /tʲ/ or /dʲ/ phoneme. The core consonant inventory includes only hard /t/ and /d/; their iotated counterparts are the postalveolar affricates č /t͡ʃ/ and dž /d͡ʒ/ (or ć /t͡ɕ/ and đ /d͡ʑ/ in MS Plus), not soft stops. The sequences tj and dj in the standard orthography are therefore intended as clusters of two separate hard sounds — [t] + [j] and [d] + [j] — not as soft consonants. However, this conflicts with the visual expectation of any Slavic reader, for whom tj and dj naturally suggest softened [tʲ] and [dʲ]. The result is a gap in the phonemic system: the orthography implies sounds that the phonology does not recognise. Unresolved: Phonemic Status of Soft T/D in the Consonant Inventory Standard Interslavic lacks soft /tʲ/ and /dʲ/ as phonemes, yet the dictionary contains words with tj and dj sequences that imply them — creating ambiguity about pronunciation and transliteration. Several of these words have natural alternatives that avoid the problem entirely: tjulenj → tulenj (cf. Czech tuleň), tjutjun → tutun or tytun, gospodja → gospodža (cf. Russian госпожа, Serbian госпођа), Madjar → Madžar. Words like adjutant and konjugacija contain morpheme boundaries (ad-jutant, kon-jugacija) where the pronunciation is genuinely [d.j] and [n.j] — two separate sounds — and should be retained as-is. For the MS Plus register, the existing letters ť and ď could be extended to cover borrowed soft /tʲ/ and /dʲ/ in words where no better alternative exists. See the relevant sectiona (Standard Latin Orthography) and (Etymological Alphabet) for a full discussion of this issue.
