The following notes describe the permitted allophonic variation for consonants in standard Interslavic: c /t͡s/: Uniform across Slavic languages. Non-Slavic speakers should note this is always [t͡s], never [k] or [s]. Morphologically, c behaves as a soft consonant — words ending in c follow the soft declension pattern (e.g., Amerikancev, not *Amerikancov). č /t͡ʃ/: MAY be realized anywhere in the range from hard [ʈ͡ʂ] (as in Polish, Slovak, Belarusian, and most South Slavic languages) to softer [t͡ʃ] (as in Czech). Both are acceptable. However, the very soft [t͡ɕ] realization is not recommended in standard Interslavic, as it is reserved for the distinct phoneme ć in the MS Plus register. dž /d͡ʒ/: As the voiced counterpart of č, permitted realizations range from hard [d͡ʐ] to softer [d͡ʒ]. The very soft [d͡ʑ] is not recommended, as it corresponds to đ in MS Plus. In loanwords (e.g., džaz, džungla, džentlemen), speakers should use the harder realization regardless of register. g /g/: Interslavic is a G-Slavic language, meaning the letter g corresponds to h in the H-Slavic languages (Czech, Slovak, Upper Sorbian, Ukrainian). The standard pronunciation is [g]. As a compromise toward H-Slavic speakers, the Belarusian-style voiced velar fricative [ɣ] is also acceptable. However, [h] is NOT acceptable — speakers should not substitute a voiceless glottal or pharyngeal fricative. Unresolved: The G/H Simplification Interslavic collapses a complex four-way distinction in the Slavic consonant system into a simple two-phoneme system (g /g/ and h /x/). In reality, the Slavic languages exhibit up to four distinct categories: Common Slavic g — the inherited velar stop that remains [g] in "G-Slavic" languages (Russian, Polish, Serbian, Bulgarian, Macedonian) but shifted to [ɦ] or [h] in "H-Slavic" languages (Czech, Slovak, Ukrainian, Belarusian, Upper Sorbian). This is the source of the famous doublet Bogdan (G-Slavic) / Bohdan (H-Slavic). Common Slavic h — the inherited voiceless fricative corresponding to Cyrillic Х. It surfaces as [x] in East and South Slavic but as the digraph ch [x] in West Slavic. In standard Interslavic, this merges with h /x/. Foreign g — the [g] in loanwords from Latin, Greek, English, etc. This is realized as [g] even in H-Slavic languages: Czech logika, Slovak gravitácia, Ukrainian ґравітація (with the special letter Ґ). Interslavic does not distinguish this from native g. Foreign h — the [h] in loanwords from Greek, Germanic, and other languages. Its reflexes vary: Latin-script languages write h, East Slavic typically uses Г (with Russian [g] pronunciation), and Cyrillic South Slavic uses Х. Polish further distinguishes h from ch in spelling. Interslavic does not distinguish this from native h. The two-phoneme simplification is adequate for everyday communication and is consistent with the Serbian model (which also has just [g] and [x]). However, it means that flavorization to H-Slavic languages cannot be fully automated: an algorithm cannot determine whether a given g should become Czech h (native) or remain g (foreign) without access to etymological information not encoded in the standard or MS Plus orthography. l /l/ and lj /lʲ/: These form one of only two mandatory hard–soft consonant pairs in standard Interslavic. Slavic languages use four main realizations of l: "lazy l" [w] (modern Polish ł, also found in Slovenian and Bulgarian), dark [ɫ], neutral [l], and palatal [ʎ]. The [w] realization is acceptable for hard l but not recommended, as it impairs intelligibility. Each speaker should choose a consistent pair of hard and soft variants from the following options: (a) [ɫ] (hard) / [l] (soft) — the historical Polish pattern; (b) [l] (hard) / [ʎ] (soft) — the Shtokavian pattern; (c) [ɫ] (hard) / [ʎ] (soft) — the East Slavic pattern. Whatever pair is chosen, consistency is important: a speaker's hard and soft l must always be distinguishable. Before i, the neutral [l] is natural for most Slavic speakers (e.g., list = [list], not [ɫist]). n /n/ and nj /nʲ/: The second mandatory hard–soft pair. Pronounced [n] and [ɲ] respectively by all Slavic speakers. Before ě, softening of n is always obligatory (e.g., někto is always [ɲɛktɔ]). Before i, softening is optional (e.g., nikto may be [niktɔ] or [ɲiktɔ]). When mandatory softening occurs, the orthography marks it explicitly with j (Latin) or њ (Cyrillic) — e.g., njih is always pronounced [ɲix]. r /r/: Always a trilled [r], as in all Slavic languages. The sequence rj, although not recognized by the Commission as a separate phoneme, in practice functions as the equivalent of historical soft /ŕ/ — nearly all occurrences of rj in the lexicon correspond to Proto-Slavic soft /r/ rather than two separate phonemes. This sequence may be pronounced either as two sounds [rja] or as a single softened [rʲa] (e.g., burja = [burja] or [burʲa]). In the standard Cyrillic orthography, this is written рј (not рь, since the standalone soft sign is not part of the standard Cyrillic letter inventory). š /ʃ/: MAY be realized from hard [ʂ] (as in most Slavic languages) to softer [ʃ] (as in Czech). Both are acceptable. v /v/: The standard realization is [v]. The labiodental approximant [ʋ] is also acceptable. The semivowel [w] is not recommended. Importantly, v does NOT lose its voicing in non-final positions — unlike in Polish, where v devoices to [f] after voiceless consonants (e.g., krvav- is [krvav-], not *[krfav-]; tvoj is [tvɔj], not *[tfɔj]). ž /ʒ/: MAY be realized from hard [ʐ] (the majority Slavic pronunciation) to softer [ʒ] (as in Czech). Both are acceptable.

The table below summarizes the relationship between softened, palatalised, and iotated consonant forms — demonstrating the three distinct processes that produce palatal consonants in Interslavic. Optional/etymological phonemes are indicated with (etym.): Base Softened Iotated (standard) Notes l lj lj softening = iotation for /l/ n nj nj softening = iotation for /n/ r ŕ (etym.) rj softening ≠ iotation in standard t ť (etym.) č / ć (etym.) iotation yields postalveolar d ď (etym.) dž / đ (etym.) iotation yields postalveolar s ś (etym.) š iotation yields postalveolar z ź (etym.) ž iotation yields postalveolar k — č palatalisation only g — ž palatalisation only h — š palatalisation only p — pj labials do not palatalise b — bj labials do not palatalise m — mj labials do not palatalise v — vj labials do not palatalise Unverified Content The notes "palatalisation only" for k, g, h in the table above are misleading. The source describes k+j→č, g+j→ž, h+j→š as iotation (not merely palatalisation). The source treats palatalisation and iotation as producing the same result for velars, but labels both processes explicitly. For the full morphophonemic alternation rules (palatalisation, iotation, the o→e rule, y→i/e rule, fleeting vowels), see the relevant section (Morphophonemic Alternations).

Interslavic orthography is based on etymology rather than pronunciation, so consonant clusters arise that may appear unpronounceable to speakers of non-Slavic languages — for example, vozvršenje. The language does not impose phonotactic constraints on consonant clusters beyond what is inherited from the attested Slavic lexicon. Constraints do exist, however, in the co-occurrence of certain vowels and consonants.

Unverified Content The source material does not discuss syllable structure, syllable templates, or maximal syllable forms. The source states only that "every syllable contains one of the following vowels" and lists them. The formal syllable-template analysis in this subsection appears to be editorial elaboration.

The maximal syllable template in Interslavic is: (C)(C)(C) V (C)(C)(C) where V (the syllabic nucleus) is any of the following: a vowel: a, å, e, ę, ė, ě, i, o, ȯ, u, ų, y syllabic r or ŕ (preceded by a consonant and not followed by a vowel) Every syllable SHALL contain exactly one syllabic nucleus. Syllabic r and ŕ are pronounced with a preceding schwa: /tərg/ (trg), /ʦukər/ (cukr), /mjərtvɪ/ (mŕtvy). Unverified Content The source material does not discuss pronunciation advice for difficult clusters, epenthesis, or cluster reduction. This guidance is editorial. Because Interslavic orthography is etymological, the written form may produce onset and coda clusters that exceed the phonotactic norms of any individual Slavic language. Such clusters SHOULD be pronounced as they are in the speaker's native Slavic language, with epenthesis or cluster reduction permitted as natural adaptations.

Unverified Content The source material does not discuss onset constraints, a "positive-attestation criterion," or epenthetic vowel insertion. The source states only that orthography is etymological and clusters may appear unpronounceable. This subsection is editorial elaboration. Interslavic permits any consonant onset cluster that is attested in at least one standard Slavic language. There are no additional restrictions on onset complexity beyond this positive-attestation criterion. Speakers whose native language does not permit a given cluster MAY insert an epenthetic vowel to aid pronunciation without this being considered an error in spoken Interslavic.

Unverified Content The source material does not discuss coda constraints, the "positive-attestation criterion" for codas, or obstruent voicing assimilation rules. This subsection is editorial elaboration. Coda clusters are likewise governed by the positive-attestation criterion: any coda sequence attested in at least one Slavic language is permitted. Obstruent voicing assimilation applies across syllable and morpheme boundaries (see , Morphophonemic Alternations): a voiced obstruent in coda position assimilates to the voicing of a following obstruent, and word-finally obstruents MAY devoice in accordance with the speaker's native-language phonology.

Unverified Content The source material does not mention a sonority hierarchy, cluster well-formedness criteria, or sonority-based onset/coda preferences. This entire subsection is editorial elaboration drawing on general phonological theory. The preferred cluster structure follows the sonority hierarchy: Class Examples Sonority (ascending) Stops p b t d k g lowest Affricates c č dž ↑ Fricatives f v s z š ž h ↑ Nasals m n nj ↑ Liquids l lj r ↑ Glides j ↑ Vowels a e i o u … highest In the preferred (canonical) onset, sonority SHOULD rise toward the nucleus; in the preferred coda, sonority SHOULD fall away from the nucleus. Departures from the sonority hierarchy are well-attested in the Slavic lexicon (e.g., sv-, zv-, čr-) and are fully permitted in Interslavic as inherited clusters.

Unverified Content The source material does not provide an inventory of permitted onset clusters. This table and the "illustrative, not exhaustive" framing are editorial additions. The example words appear plausible but have not been verified against the source. The following onset clusters are attested in the Interslavic lexicon. This list is illustrative, not exhaustive; clusters not listed here are permitted when they occur in an attested source form. Cluster Example Gloss br- brat brother cr- crn black čr- čŕv worm dr- drug friend gl- glava head gn- gnězdo nest gr- grad city kl- klasa class kn- kniga book kr- krest cross kv- květ flower pl- plěsati to dance pr- pravda truth sk- skovoroda frying pan sl- slovo word sm- smrt death sn- sněg snow sp- spati to sleep sr- srce heart st- stol table str- strana country sv- světlo light tr- trava grass vr- vrěme time zdr- zdravje health zl- zlato gold zn- znak sign zv- zvon bell TODO: Committee Input Needed The positive inventory of onset clusters should be reviewed and expanded by the committee with reference to the full Interslavic lexicon. Clusters arising in loanwords and neologisms should be explicitly addressed.
documents.s: Positive Inventory of Permitted Onset Clusters

documents.t: Unverified Content The source material does not provide an inventory of permitted coda clusters. This table is an editorial addition. While some examples (e.g., smrt, trg) appear in the source in other contexts, the systematic coda inventory is unsourced. Additionally, the gloss "hazel" for lesk appears incorrect; in Slavic languages, "lesk" typically means "lustre/shine." The following coda clusters are attested in the Interslavic lexicon. Clusters arising only in loanwords are noted accordingly. Cluster Example Gloss Notes -st most bridge native -sk lesk hazel native -zd gvozd nail native -zn bězn abyss native -nt agent agent mostly loanwords -nk bank bank mostly loanwords -rt smrt death syllabic r -rg trg market syllabic r -lk pòlk regiment native -lv Lvov Lviv proper noun -št pušt wilderness native TODO: Committee Input Needed The coda cluster inventory should be reviewed and completed by the committee. The treatment of loanword-only clusters (particularly from Latin/Greek international vocabulary) needs explicit policy.
documents.s: Positive Inventory of Permitted Coda Clusters

documents.t: The following rules govern which vowels may follow which consonants. Unverified Content The source does not reference a "Proto-Slavic law of syllabic harmony" or the principle that "hard and soft sounds within a syllable must be congruent." The source simply states that there are limitations on combining certain vowels with certain consonants. These restrictions reflect the Proto-Slavic law of syllabic harmony — the principle that hard and soft sounds within a syllable must be congruent — and its modern reflexes in the Slavic languages.

The phoneme /y/ (Latin y, Cyrillic ы) is subject to strict distributional constraints: /y/ MUST NOT follow a soft consonant. The soft consonants that prohibit /y/ are: š /ʃ/, ž /ʒ/, č /t͡ʃ/, dž /d͡ʒ/, c /t͡s/, lj /lʲ/, nj /nʲ/, j /j/ — and in the etymological register: ŕ, ť, ď, ś, ź. /y/ MUST NOT appear word-initially. /y/ MUST NOT appear immediately after a vowel. In morphological contexts where a soft-consonant stem would ordinarily take an ending containing /y/, the /y/ SHALL be replaced: In adjective and pronoun declension: /y/ → /i/ (e.g., dobr-y but svěž-i). In noun declension: /y/ → /e/ (e.g., dom-y but kraj-e; žen-y but zemj-e). Because several Slavic languages do not distinguish /i/ from /y/, substituting all occurrences of y with i is an acceptable simplification in written Interslavic. Context Example Gloss after hard consonant (correct) dobry good (adj., m.sg.) after soft consonant (correct) svěži fresh (adj., m.sg.) after soft consonant (incorrect) svěžy —

The phonemes /ě/ (Latin ě, Cyrillic ѣ) and /ŕ/ (Latin ŕ, Cyrillic рь) MUST follow a hard consonant or the affricate c /t͡s/ in their base (lexical) forms. In morphological contexts where a soft-consonant stem would ordinarily take /ě/, the /ě/ SHALL be replaced by /i/: Dative and locative singular of feminine nouns: žen-ě (woman) but zemj-i (earth). Unverified Content The source describes these cases differently: it says ě and ŕ are "redundant" after j and č, and that they "can be pronounced in such way that they soften it." The source does not call them "exceptions" or say they are "lexicalized." The SHALL requirement to treat them as "listed forms rather than rule violations" is an editorial interpretation. Apparent exceptions such as jěsti (to eat) and čŕny (black) arise because j and č are soft consonants. These exceptions are lexicalized and SHALL be treated as listed forms rather than rule violations. Form Example Note After hard consonant rěka river — regular After c (affricate) cělovat́ to kiss — regular After soft consonant (dative) zemji earth — /ě/ → /i/ Apparent exception jěsti to eat — lexicalized

Unverified Content The source says o and ȯ "rarely follow a soft consonant," which is weaker than the SHOULD NOT normative requirement stated here. The vowels /o/ (Latin o, Cyrillic о) and /ȯ/ (Latin ȯ, Cyrillic ȯ) SHOULD NOT follow a soft consonant in native Slavic vocabulary. This restriction reflects the Proto-Slavic o→e rule (see , Morphophonemic Alternations), by which /o/ was historically replaced by /e/ after a soft consonant in grammatical endings and suffixes. Exceptions are permitted in: International loanwords: majonez (mayonnaise), čokolada (chocolate). Proper nouns and internationalisms not subject to Slavic morphophonology. In native Slavic morphology, endings containing /o/ become /e/ after soft consonants: Ending After hard consonant After soft consonant -ov (gen.pl.) gradov krajev -om (instr.sg.) gradom krajem -ogo (gen.sg. adj.) kogo čego -oj (dat.sg. adj.) dobr-oj svěž-ej
documents.s: Distribution of /o/ and /ȯ/ after Soft Consonants

documents.t: The optional vowel /å/ (Latin å, Cyrillic а̊) is subject to two positional constraints: /å/ MUST be preceded by a liquid consonant — either r /r/ or l /l/. /å/ MUST be followed by a consonant; it does not appear in word-final position or before a vowel. Additionally, /å/ MUST NOT appear word-initially or after a vowel (see the relevant section). Unverified Content The etymological explanation referring to Proto-Slavic *ā development is not present in the source material. The source confirms the distributional constraint but does not provide this historical derivation. This distribution is inherited from Proto-Slavic, where *ā following a liquid consonant in a closed syllable developed differently from *ā in other environments. In flavourised Interslavic, /å/ is written and pronounced as o. Example IPA Gloss tråva /trɑva/ grass dlåg /dlɑg/ debt glåva /glɑva/ head (etym.)

The following vowels and syllabic consonants MUST NOT appear word-initially or immediately after another vowel: å, ę, ė, ě, ȯ, ų, y, syllabic r, syllabic ŕ Unverified Content The source confirms the word-initial restriction but does not mention "standard and etymological registers" applying uniformly, nor does it provide the Proto-Slavic historical explanation given here. This restriction applies uniformly in both the standard and etymological registers. These phonemes are historically conditioned: they developed from Proto-Slavic phonemes in specific syllabic environments and have no attested word-initial or post-vocalic distribution across the Slavic languages. Vowel Permitted position Prohibited position å after liquid + before consonant word-initially; after vowel ę after consonant word-initially; after vowel ė after consonant (fleeting) word-initially; after vowel ě after hard consonant or c word-initially; after vowel ȯ after consonant (fleeting) word-initially; after vowel ų after consonant word-initially; after vowel y after hard consonant word-initially; after vowel; after soft consonant syllabic r / ŕ after consonant, before consonant or word-finally word-initially; after vowel

Unverified Content The source confirms that geminates arise only from prefixing, suffixing, or rare loanwords, but does not prescribe how geminates should be pronounced. The SHALL requirement to pronounce them as "single (lengthened or unreleased) consonants" and the characterization as "surface phenomenon" vs "underlying phonological representations" are editorial additions. Geminate (double) consonants DO NOT occur in native Interslavic vocabulary as underlying phonological representations. Geminates arise only at morpheme boundaries as a surface phenomenon and SHALL be pronounced as single (lengthened or unreleased) consonants in natural speech; pronounced as two distinct consonants at morpheme boundaries is also acceptable depending on the speaker's native phonological habits. Contexts in which geminates arise: Prefixation, when a prefix ending in a consonant is attached to a stem beginning with the same consonant: od- + dati → oddati (to give away) v- + voz → vvoz (import) Suffixation, when a stem-final consonant is followed by a suffix beginning with the same consonant: kon + -ny → konny (equestrian) rus + -sky → russky (Russian, adj.) Loanwords, where the geminate is part of the source-language form: motto In all cases, the standard orthography retains the spelling that reflects the morphological structure (od-dati, kon-ny), even when pronunciation reduces the geminate.

Stress in Interslavic is free: no single fixed-position rule is imposed for all words. Every speaker may follow the accentual habits of their own native Slavic language. Because Interslavic is designed to be produced comfortably by speakers of all Slavic languages — each of which has its own accentual system — the standard imposes only a small set of guidelines that maximise intelligibility and give the language a pleasant melodic regularity.

Stress placement in Standard Interslavic is formally free: no single fixed-position rule (initial, penultimate, or final) is imposed for all words. Every speaker may follow the accentual habits that feel natural to them. The freedom of stress is a deliberate design choice. Forcing all speakers to adopt a single fixed-stress system would make Interslavic feel unnatural for the majority, who come from languages with free or mobile stress. Conversely, attempting to prescribe a full lexical stress dictionary would impose an unrealistic burden on learners and speakers.

In practice, stress SHOULD fall on the second or third syllable from the end of the word. The following table summarises the preferred positions by word length: Word length Preferred stress positions Monosyllable The only syllable Disyllable First (= penultimate) syllable Trisyllable or longer Second or third syllable from the end Within these limits the speaker has freedom: it is equally correct to say sílaba (third from end) or silába (second from end). The choice is a matter of personal preference and may vary from word to word. Importantly, in words of three or more syllables stress should not fall on the very first syllable (unless it coincides with the second or third position from the end), and it should not fall on the very last syllable (see also on affixes).

Stress should not fall on prefixes. In a disyllabic word that consists of a prefix plus a root, this rule takes priority over the general preference to avoid final-syllable stress. For example: Word Stress Gloss naglås naglǻs (not náglås) stress, accent prikaz prikáz command nadpis nadpís inscription vȯzhod vȯzhód east, sunrise něčto něčtó something In words of three or more syllables the conflict does not arise, because stress can always fall on a non-initial, non-final syllable. Note that pre- and su- in the borrowed words prefiks and sufiks are also prefixes (though not of Slavic origin), so stress on the last syllable (prefíks, sufíks) is equally permissible.

Analogously, stress should not fall on derivational suffixes and productive endings. Instead it falls on the syllable immediately before the suffix. This is particularly relevant for words with the suffix -cija: Word Stress Gloss investicija invesTÍcija investment investicijejų invesTÍcijejų investment (instr.sg.) This pronunciation is natural for East Slavic speakers. It may look unfamiliar to Polish speakers, who habitually place stress on the penultimate syllable; however, the Polish pronunciation of the cognate word (inwesTYcja) actually has the same stress position relative to the suffix, and so the Interslavic pattern invesTÍcija is in practice equally accessible. (Note: in Old Polish and modern Silesian, the stress in such words falls on -cy- — inwestyCYja — a pattern that Lechitic speakers should be aware of when speaking Interslavic.) The same principle applies to all other derivational suffixes and prefixes.
documents.s: Suffixes and derivational endings are typically unstressed

documents.t: The infinitive ending -ti is a grammatical suffix, and the general principle of not stressing suffixes applies. At the same time, the second-or-third-from-end rule gives the speaker a choice. Both patterns are correct: Ukrainian/Slovenian style: načiNÁti Other styles: naČÍnati A Serbo-Croatian (Štokavian) or Czech/Slovak speaker might say NÁčinati, but this places stress on the first syllable of a four-syllable word, which the guidelines discourage. The forms načínati and naČÍnati are therefore more widely recommended.

Stress SHOULD remain on the same syllable of the stem across the entire inflectional paradigm of a given word. Adding case endings to nouns and adjectives, or personal endings to verbs, does not move the stress. This is especially important for Polish speakers, whose native language places stress on the penultimate syllable of every word form, causing the stress to shift as endings are added. In Interslavic, this shift should be avoided. Examples — adjective dóbry: Form Word Note Nom.sg.m. dÓbry stress on stem Gen.sg.m. dÓbrogo stress stays (not dobRÓgo) Dat.sg.m. dÓbromu stress stays (not dobrÓmu) Instr.pl. dÓbrymi stress stays (not dobrÝmi) Further examples: Base form Inflected forms Note intenSÍVny intenSÍVnomu stress stays TRÁKtor TRÁKtory, TRÁKtorami stress stays Ágent Ágenti, Ágentami stress stays dělaJŲ́ći dělaJŲ́ćego, dělaJŲ́ćemu stress stays This pattern closely resembles Russian stress behaviour and will be natural and easy to understand for East Slavic speakers, though it is accessible to all. Note that this can result in up to four unstressed syllables (as in TRÁKtorami or Ágentami), which is normal and natural.
documents.s: Stress does not shift with inflectional endings

documents.t: While inflectional endings do not move the stress , derivational processes (forming a new word from an existing root) are not bound by the same constraint. A derived word is a new lexical item and may carry stress on a different syllable than its base, subject to the general rules above . Examples: Base word Derived word Note góvor (speech) govóriti (to speak) stress shifts to a different syllable bezpokójny (restless) bezpokojéńje (anxiety) stress moves before the suffix -eńje privýkly (accustomed) privykáti ~ privýkati (to get used to) both positions are correct izmágati (to exhaust) izmagány (exhausted) stress shifts
documents.s: Stress may shift between derived forms

documents.t: Interslavic permits both short and long conjugation forms for verbs. These are independent paradigms, and each may have its own stress position. Once a stress position is chosen within a paradigm, however, it remains fixed across the inflectional forms of that paradigm (per ). For example, from the infinitive privýkati: Form Short conjugation Long conjugation 1sg. já privýkam ja privykájų 1pl. mý privýkamo my privykájemo Past m.sg. ón privýkal — Past f.sg. óna privýkala — Note that it is not necessary to say já privýkajų merely because the infinitive is privýkati — such rigid consistency across derivational boundaries is neither required nor more natural. The general rules apply independently to each word form.

Unverified Content This entire section on intonation and prosody — including declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamative contours, and focus-marking prosody — has no basis in the source material. The source mentions only "use prosody as well as you can" in a general remark, with no specific intonation rules or contour descriptions.

Standard Interslavic declarative sentences use a falling intonation contour: pitch rises to a local peak near the beginning of the intonational phrase and falls progressively toward the end, reaching its lowest point on the final stressed syllable or after it. This pattern is typologically common across Slavic languages and SHALL be treated as the canonical contour for statements. Example: Já govòrjų po-medžuslovjansky. — pitch falls across the predicate toward the sentence-final word.

Yes/no questions formed by intonation alone (without a question particle) use a rising intonation contour, typically with the rise occurring on or just before the final content word of the question. Example: Ty govòriš po-medžuslovjansky? — pitch rises on the final stressed syllable. This pattern mirrors the usage common in South Slavic languages and is accessible to speakers of other branches.

Wh-questions (formed with interrogative words such as kto, čto, kde, kogda, kako, čemu) use a falling contour similar to declaratives. The interrogative word itself typically carries a pitch peak, after which pitch falls across the rest of the clause. Example: Kde ty živěš? — pitch peak on kde, then falls.

Questions formed with the postposed particle li or the fronted particle či do not require a rising intonation contour. Declarative intonation (falling) SHALL be used with li- and či-questions. The particle itself signals the interrogative function; a rising contour is therefore redundant and MAY be perceived as foreign or stylistically marked. Examples: Govòriš li po-medžuslovjansky? — falling contour. Či ty govòriš po-medžuslovjansky? — falling contour.
documents.s: Intonation with question particles li and či

documents.t: Imperative sentences use an emphatic-fall contour: pitch rises sharply on the stressed syllable of the main verb and falls steeply thereafter. The overall contour is shorter and more abrupt than in declaratives. Exclamative sentences (expressing surprise, admiration, or strong assertion) use a similar emphatic-fall pattern, often with expanded pitch range. Examples: Govòri! — sharp fall after the stressed syllable. Kakój krasívy gorod! — expanded-range falling contour.

New-information focus (rhematic focus, narrow focus) and contrastive focus are both signalled in Interslavic by a local pitch peak on the focused constituent. This peak is higher than the surrounding prosodic context and is followed by a fall before the end of the intonational phrase. New-information focus typically falls on the rightmost new-information constituent in neutral word order (see the relevant section). Contrastive focus may fall on any constituent that is being set in opposition to an alternative, regardless of its position in the clause. In written Interslavic, focus is not orthographically marked. Speakers SHALL use prosodic prominence to signal focus in spoken production; readers and listeners SHALL interpret prosodically prominent constituents as focused. Examples: Neutral: Já kúpil knígu. — focus on knígu (new information), pitch peak on stressed syllable. Contrastive: JÁ kupil knigu (not someone else) — pitch peak on já.

Interslavic is based on the common heritage of all Slavic nations, and differences are bridged by building compromises. All Slavic languages are treated equally, although it cannot be avoided that a Slavic interlanguage is closer to "middle-of-the-road" languages like Slovak or Rusyn than to peripheral languages like Sorbian or Macedonian. Interslavic is explicitly not meant to be "more Slavic than the Slavs" by purifying it from foreign influences, even though a language based on the commonalities of the Slavic languages will automatically eliminate many local (non-Slavic) influences anyway. There is no reason to exclude international vocabulary, provided that it can be recognised by all, or at least most, Slavic speakers. Interslavic has two primary purposes: Maximum intelligibility for all Slavs, accomplished by systematically providing the largest common denominator of the Slavic languages, both in terms of grammar, orthography, syntax, and vocabulary. Ease of learning and use, primarily for Slavs, but also for non-Slavs — without sacrificing the Slavic character of the language. When a common solution cannot be found across Slavic languages, a compromise is built. If even a compromise is impossible, a choice must be made or both solutions may be offered.

Interslavic lexical, phonological, and grammatical choices SHALL maximize passive comprehension by speakers of Slavic languages without prior Interslavic study. Word roots, grammatical endings, and phonological forms SHALL be selected according to the weighted-majority voting mechanism described in the relevant section When a root or form exists in all Slavic languages in the same meaning, it SHALL be adopted without modification. When only one or two languages diverge from the majority, the majority form SHALL nonetheless apply. Vocabulary that is broadly recognisable to Slavic speakers SHALL be preferred over vocabulary familiar only to speakers of a single branch. International vocabulary MAY be adopted if used in most Slavic languages and recognisable by the majority of Slavic speakers.

Interslavic MUST preserve grammatical categories that are universal to or widespread among the Slavic languages. Unverified Content The source material lists gender, cases, and verbal aspect but does not mention "animacy distinction" as a core structural feature. The MUST NOT rule about features absent from all Slavic languages is not explicitly stated in the source. Grammatical gender, case system, verbal aspect, and animacy distinction SHALL be retained as core structural features. Features entirely absent from all natural Slavic languages MUST NOT be introduced. Words SHALL NOT be borrowed directly from any individual Slavic language. Instead, the derivation SHOULD proceed from the hypothetical Proto-Slavic or Old Church Slavonic base form, then subjected to systematic derivation rules (see the relevant section). Irregularity is to be avoided unless preserving it is essential for naturalistic recognisability. Artificiality is to be avoided as well: the language SHOULD feel organic to a Slavic speaker.

Interslavic SHOULD keep grammar as simple and regular as possible without sacrificing its Slavic character. Irregularities SHALL be reduced to the minimum set unavoidable for naturalism. Every grammatical ending SHALL be instantly recognisable, even if the inflected form looks different in a reader's own language. For example, the genitive singular masculine ending -a is shared by all Slavic languages and is therefore the normative Interslavic solution for that form. Verbal conjugation classes SHALL be reduced to a manageable system derived from two stems: the infinitive stem and the present-tense stem. The present-tense stem SHALL in almost all cases be the regular result of the infinitive stem; only a small residual set needs to be learned separately. Unverified Content The source discusses simplifying paradigms to avoid "endless tables" but does not prescribe a normative requirement for compact table presentation. Nominal and verbal paradigms SHALL be presented in compact tables, avoiding redundancy.

No single Slavic language SHALL function as the default or primary model for Interslavic. All three Slavic subfamilies — East, West, and South — SHALL be treated on an equal basis. The voting system defined in the relevant section operationalises this neutrality through balanced subgroup weighting. When two candidate forms receive equal votes, population weighting may serve as a tiebreaker. Languages with fewer than approximately one million speakers (e.g., Sorbian, Kashubian, Rusyn) do not participate in formal voting but SHALL still be considered in the evaluation of proposed forms.

Interslavic accommodates users with varying levels of proficiency and differing communication needs through two defined usage registers. Basic Interslavic (also referred to as Slovianto / Словянто) is a simplified subset intended primarily for non-Slavs or beginners: It uses a minimalistic grammar characterised by the absence of elements not strictly required for simple communication. It is structured in progressive levels: Level 1 omits grammatical gender and noun cases; Level 2 introduces grammatical gender and basic verbal conjugation; Level 3 introduces the case system. Unverified Content The source says memorising the forms should be "a matter of minutes rather than of hours," not that a learner can express themselves on a basic level within minutes of study. A learner at Level 1 can express themselves on a basic level within minutes of study. Standard Interslavic is the full register described by this specification: It includes grammatical gender, seven cases, verbal aspect, and full verbal conjugation. It is primarily intended for Slavs and for non-Slavs who need to communicate at a serious level. Unverified Content The source says the advanced level is suitable for "contacts and publications" and "insight into the nature of Slavic." The claims about "literary use" and "scholarly engagement with Slavic linguistics" are not in the source. It is suitable for written publications, literary use, and scholarly engagement with Slavic linguistics. Unverified Content The source says "Ideally, level 1 should be a subset of level 2" — a descriptive aspiration, not a normative SHALL requirement. The register names "Basic Interslavic" and "Standard Interslavic" are not used in the source, which refers to "Simple level" and "Advanced level." The two registers SHALL be defined such that Basic Interslavic is a strict subset of Standard Interslavic. A learner MAY advance from one register to the other without invalidating previously acquired knowledge.

Word roots and grammatical patterns in Interslavic SHOULD be traceable to reconstructed Proto-Slavic. Interslavic forms are not borrowed directly from any modern Slavic language. Instead, derivation proceeds from the reconstructed Proto-Slavic or attested Old Church Slavonic base form, which is then subjected to systematic phonological simplification. This approach guarantees internal consistency: whenever a root or morpheme occurs in a compound, it SHALL have the same form regardless of which particular language the compound is drawn from. The phonological development of modern Slavic languages from Proto-Slavic is highly regular. Once a speaker recognises how sounds in their own language correspond to Interslavic, they will be able to both recognise and predict unfamiliar words. For example, the Common Slavic sequences tj/dj become č/ž in East Slavic, c/dz in Polish, c/z in Czecho-Slovak, č/j in Slovene, ć/đ in Serbo-Croatian, ḱ/ǵ in Macedonian, and št/žd in Bulgarian. The Interslavic compromise is č/dž (e.g., svěča / свѣча 'candle' and medžu / меджу 'between'), which has the additional advantage that dž is the voiced equivalent of č, creating phonological symmetry.

Unverified Content The source material mentions OCS only briefly as a reference for deriving word forms. The detailed normative claims in this subsection — including the etymological register with specific characters (ě, å, ę, ų, ȯ), the claim that OCS SHALL NOT function as the default model, the guidance on when to consult OCS forms, and the characterisation of Interslavic as a "modern continuation of OCS" — are not present in the source and could not be verified against it. Old Church Slavonic (OCS) provides a well-attested historical reference point that is very close to Proto-Slavic. It serves as an important source for Interslavic but SHALL NOT function as the default model for contemporary usage. OCS forms SHOULD be consulted when the reconstruction of a Proto-Slavic base form is uncertain or when a modern Slavic consensus is absent. The etymological register of Interslavic orthography (see the relevant section) draws on OCS-era distinctions such as ě / ѣ, and the optional vowels å, ę, ų, and ȯ. OCS phonology, grammar, and syntax are archaic and in many respects incompatible with the needs of modern communication; they SHALL NOT be imposed on standard Interslavic usage. Interslavic may be described as a modern continuation of OCS: it begins where OCS ends, incorporating the subsequent shared development of the Slavic languages. One could say that making Interslavic is essentially the undoing of the divergences that caused the Slavic languages to drift apart.

Interslavic draws on the following languages in its voting and derivation process: Russian Ukrainian Belarusian Polish Czech Slovak Unverified Content The source material lists Sorbian among languages with fewer than one million speakers that "do not participate in the voting process." It is not listed as a full source language in the source's six subgroups. Upper and Lower Sorbian Slovene Serbo-Croatian (Serbian, Croatian, Bosnian) Bulgarian Macedonian Unverified Content The source material does not list Old Church Slavonic as a 12th source language or assign it a tiebreaker/etymological reference role in the voting process. The source lists only the six subgroups of living languages. Old Church Slavonic (as a tiebreaker and etymological reference) For voting purposes, these languages are grouped into six subgroups, each carrying one vote: Subgroup Languages East Slavic A Russian East Slavic B Ukrainian and Belarusian West Slavic A Polish West Slavic B Czech and Slovak South Slavic A Slovene and Serbo-Croatian (Serbian/Croatian/Bosnian) South Slavic B Bulgarian and Macedonian When two languages within one subgroup disagree on a form, each receives ½ vote. When two candidate forms are tied on subgroup votes, population size SHALL serve as the tiebreaker. Languages with fewer than approximately one million speakers do not participate in formal voting but SHALL remain eligible for consideration.
documents.s: Relationship to the Twelve Source Languages

documents.t: Root selection, form selection, and meaning assignment each proceed through the following decision criteria in priority order: If a word root exists in all Slavic languages in the same meaning, it SHALL be adopted. If only one or two languages diverge, the majority form SHALL be adopted. If two or more groups can be distinguished, each based on a different root: a. For each candidate root, cognates in remaining languages are identified along with their meanings. b. "Semantic families" are constructed by tracing those meanings to related roots. c. The form that occurs in the most languages and carries the most "average" meaning SHALL be selected. If no clear majority solution emerges, the following options are considered in order: a. The form occurring in the greatest number of languages. b. The form most intuitively understandable, even if used in only one language. c. Creation of synonyms (both forms are permitted). d. Greater weight to larger and better-known languages over smaller ones. e. An international equivalent. f. Engineered construction where no natural option satisfies the criteria. These rules describe a general tendency; when a different solution is evidently superior, common sense SHALL prevail over mechanical application of the voting procedure. The voting mechanism is a normative tool, not a mechanical algorithm that overrides linguistic judgment.

Unverified Content The entire section (Relationship to Other Languages and Projects), including subsections , , and , has no basis in the primary source material. The source does not discuss Esperanto, Slovio, simplified Slavic schemes, or Russian as a pan-Slavic lingua franca.
documents.s: Relationship to Other Languages and Projects

documents.t: Interslavic is a zonal constructed language — an auxiliary language for communication with or among speakers of a relatively homogeneous family of languages (the Slavic languages). It SHALL NOT be evaluated against the criteria of a universal auxiliary language. Esperanto was designed for global use and maximises cultural neutrality and grammatical simplicity for learners of any background. As a result, knowledge of Esperanto is useful only in communication with other Esperantists. Interslavic, by contrast, is designed so that speakers of Slavic languages can understand it without prior study — without even necessarily knowing that they are dealing with a constructed language. Although Interslavic grammar is more complex than Esperanto's grammar, it is extremely easy for Slavic speakers, because the essential skill is recognising how forms in one's own language correspond to Interslavic forms. The benefits of Interslavic and the benefits of Esperanto are of an entirely different order and serve different communicative purposes.
documents.s: Distinction from Esperanto and Other Universal Auxlangs

documents.t: Several projects have attempted to construct a Slavic auxiliary language by drastically simplifying one existing Slavic language or by building on an Esperanto-like schematic framework (e.g., Slovio). Interslavic is structurally distinct from all such approaches. Interslavic preserves full case morphology (seven cases), grammatical gender, and verbal aspect, because these categories are present in the natural Slavic languages and their presence maximises passive intelligibility for Slavic speakers. Simplifying a natural Slavic language (e.g., stripping Russian of its soft consonants and reducing declensions) does not produce Interslavic — it produces "bad Russian." In mixed groups, native speakers of the base language are always at an advantage, violating the neutrality criterion. Interslavic vocabulary relates to all Slavic languages in a predictable, rule-governed way. A speaker who understands the correspondence rules can predict unfamiliar words. Schemes based on one language do not have this property for speakers of other branches.
documents.s: Distinction from Simplified or Reduced Slavic Schemes

documents.t: Russian is the native or well-known second language of approximately half the Slavic-speaking world. Despite this reach, it SHALL NOT be treated as a substitute for Interslavic. Russian has undergone substantial independent development from Common Slavic. Its syntax differs significantly from other Slavic languages; the verb "to have" is practically absent, and the verb "to be" is heavily reduced in both form and use. Without prior study, a West or South Slav is unlikely to understand Russian. Russian has historically been imposed as a lingua franca on Central and Eastern European countries, and many speakers — both Slavic and non-Slavic — associate it with political coercion. Using Russian as a pan-Slavic medium raises a legitimate political-neutrality objection. Russian would not serve equally well across all intra-Slavic communication pairs (e.g., a Polish–Croatian exchange). Interslavic draws on Russian as one of six equally-weighted subgroups, ensuring that Russian input is properly represented without being dominant. TODO: Committee Input Needed Section may require expansion to address the relationship of Interslavic to Neoslavonic (Novoslověnsky) and other recent naturalistic projects that post-date the original Steen source materials. The committee should clarify whether a normative comparison is warranted, or whether a cross-reference to the historical survey suffices.
documents.s: Distinction from Russian as a Pan-Slavic Lingua Franca

documents.t: This section specifies the semantic and pragmatic rules of Interslavic: how grammatical and lexical choices map onto meaning, how context shapes interpretation, and how speakers deploy language to perform social actions. The rules defined here apply to all standard written and spoken Interslavic regardless of flavorizational register.

This chapter is organized into the following subsections: Section Topic Tense, Aspect, and Mood (TAM): interaction matrix, modality, evidentiality Lexical Semantics: semantic fields, polysemy, synonymy, false cognates Pragmatics: register, politeness, deixis, information structure, discourse markers, speech acts
documents.s: Scope of the Semantics and Pragmatics Chapter

documents.t: Interslavic exhibits the following semantic properties that shape interpretation across all domains of the grammar: Grammatical aspect as a primary semantic category. Every finite verb form in Interslavic carries either perfective (pf.) or imperfective (impf.) aspect. Aspect is not merely stylistic — it determines whether an event is construed as a bounded, completed whole or as an ongoing/habitual/repeated process. This interacts systematically with tense to produce the full range of temporal meanings (see the relevant section). No grammatical articles; definiteness is pragmatic. Because Interslavic has no articles, the specific vs. generic and definite vs. indefinite distinction is conveyed through context, demonstratives (toj / та / то), and information structure. Context and word order are therefore semantically significant (see the relevant section). Modality expressed lexically and through the conditional. Interslavic does not have a dedicated morphological mood for epistemic or deontic meanings beyond the indicative, conditional, and imperative. Modal meanings are instead expressed through modal predicates (možno, trěba, dolžen, moći, uměti) and through the conditional mood (see the relevant section). Evidentiality is not grammaticalized. Interslavic does not encode the source of information in verb morphology. Evidential meanings ("reportedly", "apparently", "I heard that") are expressed by lexical means only (see the relevant section).

All rules in this chapter that use the modal verbs MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY, and SHOULD NOT carry the following normative force: MUST / SHALL — an absolute semantic or pragmatic requirement. SHOULD — the strongly recommended default; deviation is permitted but may reduce communicative transparency. MAY — an explicitly permitted option. SHOULD NOT — a usage that is technically grammatical but actively discouraged as opaque or misleading to a cross-Slavic audience.

No Source Material Available This file has no direct source material for verification. All content in this section requires expert review to confirm accuracy and consistency with established Interslavic standards. This section specifies the principles governing the assignment of meaning to Interslavic words: how the lexicon is organized into semantic fields, how polysemous words are treated, how synonyms and near-synonyms relate to one another, and how false cognates (false friends) are identified and managed.

The Interslavic standard lexicon is organized around semantic fields — clusters of related meanings that together cover a major domain of human experience or knowledge. Coverage of each field is verified against the twelve source languages of the weighted voting system (see the relevant section).

The following semantic fields are recognized as foundational. Each field MUST be representable with core vocabulary that is widely intelligible across all major Slavic language groups. Unverified Content In the table below, several words in the "Time and calendar" row appear regionally biased: segodnja is Russian-oriented (cf. Czech dnes, Polish dzisiaj, BCS danas); jutro meaning "tomorrow" reflects Polish usage — in most other Slavic languages jutro means "morning," not "tomorrow." These may not represent pan-Slavic consensus forms. Unverified Content The verb "to see" appears as viditi in "Basic actions" but as viděti in "Cognition and perception." The source verb grammar file (grammar-verbs.md) uses viděti. The viditi form may be an error. Field Examples of core vocabulary The human body glava "head", ruka "hand/arm", noga "leg/foot", oko "eye", uho "ear" Kinship and family otec "father", mati "mother", brat "brother", sestra "sister", syn "son", dočer "daughter" Time and calendar čas "hour/time", denj "day", tydenj "week", měsęc "month", leto "year/summer", segodnja "today", včera "yesterday", jutro "tomorrow" Space and direction město "place", sěver "north", jug "south", zapad "west", vostok "east" Nature and environment voda "water", zemlja "earth/land", nebo "sky/heaven", ogonj "fire", drěvo "tree/wood" Basic actions idti "to go", govoriti "to speak", viditi "to see", dati "to give", věděti "to know" Cognition and perception mysliti "to think", znati "to know", čuti "to feel/hear", viděti "to see", razuměti "to understand" Social and institutional narod "people/nation", zemlja "country/land", grad "city", zakon "law", vlada "government" Numbers and quantities jedin "one", dva "two", tri "three", mnogo "many", malo "few/little" Properties and qualities dobry "good", zly "bad/evil", veliky "great/large", maly "small", novy "new", stary "old"

When assigning vocabulary to a semantic field, the committee SHOULD verify that the selected form achieves the broadest possible intelligibility by: Consulting word forms in all twelve source languages. Confirming that the form is recognizable to speakers of at least the majority of those languages. Noting where regional gaps exist (e.g., a form that is opaque to East Slavic speakers) and, where possible, providing an alternative or gloss. TODO: Committee Input Needed Define the complete inventory of semantic fields to be covered in the standard lexicon. Specify minimum coverage depth (number of core items) for each field. Determine whether technology, medicine, and law form separate specialized sublexicons or are handled under the general international vocabulary rules in the relevant section
Polysemy — a single word form carrying multiple related but distinct meanings — is a pervasive feature of the Interslavic lexicon, as it is in all natural Slavic languages.

Polysemous words are listed in the dictionary with all attested meanings enumerated and numbered. No a priori disambiguation rule exists: context determines the intended meaning. Example: leto "summer" (season) — primary meaning in Russian, Ukrainian, Bulgarian "year" — primary meaning in South and West Slavic (Serbian/Croatian ljeto, Czech léto) Unverified Content The example v eto leto uses eto, which appears to be a direct borrowing from Russian (это). The standard ISV demonstrative pronoun is typically to or toje. This example may not reflect correct ISV usage. Both meanings are standard in Interslavic. Writers SHOULD be aware that the word may be ambiguous in certain contexts (e.g., v eto leto — "this summer" or "this year") and MAY add clarifying context when precision is required. Example: mir "peace" (absence of conflict) "world" (the universe, mankind) Unverified Content The form vselennaja closely mirrors Russian вселенная and may not be a pan-Slavic ISV form. Verify whether this is the accepted ISV word for "universe." Both meanings are attested across Slavic. Context and collocation typically disambiguate: mir i vojna "peace and war"; mir i vselennaja "world and universe."

When a polysemous word creates genuine ambiguity in a specific context, the writer MAY use the following strategies: Collocation: combine the word with a modifier or collocate that activates the intended reading. Paraphrase: use a less ambiguous synonym or phrase (see the relevant section). Explicit gloss: in formal or technical texts, follow the word with a parenthetical equivalent. Writers SHOULD NOT rely on typographical devices (e.g., quotation marks, italics) alone to disambiguate polysemous words.

The Interslavic dictionary MAY list multiple acceptable synonyms for a single concept. This reflects the reality that different Slavic source languages contribute competing roots, and both (or several) forms may enjoy broad recognition.

Each synonym entry in the dictionary SHOULD be marked for: Marker Meaning (neutral) No regional or register restriction; recommended default. (formal) Preferred in written, official, or literary contexts. (colloquial) Characteristic of informal speech; SHOULD be avoided in formal texts. (N.Sl.) North Slavic orientation (Russian, Polish, Czech/Slovak). (S.Sl.) South Slavic orientation (Serbian/Croatian, Bulgarian, Slovenian). (W.Sl.) West Slavic orientation (Polish, Czech, Slovak, Sorbian). Example: "to speak" Unverified Content The forms rěčati and bajati as synonyms for "to speak" could not be verified against source material. The form rěčati is not attested in the verb grammar source; verify it is a recognized ISV form and not an ad hoc construction. The register labels (formal/literary, archaic/folkloric) are also unverified. Form Register/Region govoriti neutral, pan-Slavic rěčati formal/literary bajati archaic/folkloric Writers SHOULD default to the form marked (neutral) unless a specific stylistic effect is desired.

Some near-synonyms differ not in register but in semantic coverage — they have overlapping but non-identical referential ranges. In such cases the dictionary entry SHOULD specify the difference. Example: znati vs. věděti Both mean "to know," but: znati expresses acquaintance with persons, places, and facts — knowing in the sense of being familiar with. It is the neutral everyday default. věděti is the athematic verb (see the relevant section) conveying direct or authoritative knowledge; it appears in more formal and philosophical registers. TODO: Committee Input Needed Document all major synonym pairs where the register or regional marking is not yet settled by committee decision. In particular: toljko / toliko, ili / abo, čto / što / kaj as interrogatives, and similar items where competing forms serve partially overlapping functions.

False cognates (false friends) are words whose Interslavic form closely resembles a word in another Slavic language, but the two words differ significantly in meaning. These present a particular risk to communication because a reader relying on native-language intuitions may misread the Interslavic text.

Type Description Complete false friends The Interslavic form and the superficially similar native form have entirely unrelated meanings. Partial false friends The two forms share some but not all meanings; one language's usage is narrower or shifted. Deceptive cognates The forms are etymologically related but have diverged in meaning through independent semantic change.

The following pairs represent the most common sources of misreading across major Slavic language groups. This list is illustrative, not exhaustive. Unverified Content The urod entry below contradicts other standard files (e.g., 08-lexicon/01-selection.md), which use the form uroda with meaning "harvest; produce" and list Polish uroda as meaning "beauty" (not "freak"). In Polish, uroda means "beauty," while Russian/Czech урод/úrod means "freak." The description here appears to have the false-friend relationship inverted. Interslavic form Interslavic meaning Superficially similar form That form's meaning Language(s) affected urod "harvest, yield" (primary); "freak" (secondary) urod "freak, monster" only Polish (primary meaning shifted) čas "time; hour" čas / час "time" (Cz.) / "hour" (Ru.) Czech, Russian (each captures only one sense) leto "summer; year" лето "summer" only Russian (год = year; ISV leto covers both) nebo "sky; heaven" nebe "sky; palate (of mouth)" Czech (anatomical meaning absent from ISV) stol "table" stol "chair" Slovenian (stol = chair) vrěd "harm, damage" vreda "value, worth" Serbian/Croatian (vrednost = value)

When writing Interslavic for an audience that includes speakers of a language where a false friend is known to occur, the writer SHOULD: Prefer a synonym or paraphrase that does not trigger the false-friend association for that audience. Add a brief parenthetical gloss on first use in a long or formal text. Consult the false-friends appendix of the dictionary (see X). TODO: Committee Input Needed Compile a comprehensive false-friends reference organized by source-language interference. Prioritize pairs that produce meaning reversals or strongly misleading readings over pairs that merely differ in scope or connotation.

Unverified Content This file has no direct source material for fact-checking. The pragmatic rules, ISV example forms, and phrasebook entries below have not been verified against an authoritative reference and require expert review. This section specifies the pragmatic rules of Interslavic: how speakers and writers choose among grammatically equivalent forms to achieve communicative goals, how social relationships are encoded in address forms, how information is structured within and across sentences, and how language is used to perform social actions.

Interslavic maintains the T/V distinction inherited from all Slavic languages: the second-person singular pronoun ty (T-form) and the second-person plural pronoun vy (V-form) are used differently depending on social context. Form Primary use ty Informal address to one person; family members, close friends, children, peers in informal settings vy Formal address to one person; also the standard second-person plural regardless of formality When vy is used as a polite singular address (the V-form), verbs and predicates MUST agree in the second-person plural. The L-participle in compound tenses, however, agrees with the actual (singular) referent in gender. Formal address to one man: Vy govorite dobro, gospodine. — "You speak well, sir." (verb: 2pl; referent: sg. masc.) Formal address to one woman: Vy ste skazala, čto... — "You said that..." (L-participle: sg. fem.) The choice between ty and vy when addressing an individual is a pragmatic decision, not a grammatical one. The following general conventions apply: Situation Expected form Addressing a child ty Addressing a close friend or family member ty First contact with an adult stranger vy (default safe choice) Professional or official context vy Informal online or community context ty (increasingly common across Slavic languages) Explicit mutual agreement to use informal address ty Writers of Interslavic texts SHOULD use vy as the default when the audience is unknown or mixed, and SHOULD use ty only when the communicative context clearly establishes informality.

Formal address uses titles and honorifics in the vocative case when directly addressing a person. Standard titles include: Title Meaning Vocative gospodin Mr., Sir gospodine gospoda Mrs., Ms., Madam gospodo doktor Doctor doktore profesor Professor profesore Unverified Content The form Gospoline does not match the vocative gospodine given in the table above. This may be a typo for Gospodine. Titles are used with the surname or alone: Gospoline Kovačev, ... In written formal correspondence, the greeting formula uses the vocative: Mnogouvažajemy gospodine Ivanov! — "Dear Mr. Ivanov," TODO: Committee Input Needed Unverified Content Mixed scripts detected: kандидат and доктор are written in Cyrillic within a Latin-script context. These should likely be transliterated to Latin ISV (kandidat, doktor) or consistently presented in Cyrillic. Establish a standardized inventory of formal titles and their vocative forms. Determine the recommended convention for gender-neutral formal address. Specify whether academic titles (magistr, kандидат, доктор) follow Latin-script or Cyrillic-script-dominant conventions. :::

Certain synonym pairs in Interslavic are register-differentiated: one form is appropriate for formal writing, the other for informal speech. The dictionary SHOULD mark register for all such pairs. Writers SHOULD use formal vocabulary in: official documents, academic texts, news reporting, public announcements correspondence with unknown or senior addressees any context where the Interslavic text represents a community or institution Writers MAY use colloquial vocabulary in: informal personal correspondence creative fiction representing spoken dialogue community forums where an informal tone is the established norm TODO: Committee Input Needed Compile the register-differentiated synonym list. Flag items where the committee has not yet reached consensus on which variant is the formal default.
documents.s: Register Vocabulary: Formal vs. Colloquial Synonyms

documents.t: Deixis is the encoding of spatial, temporal, and personal reference relative to the speech situation.

Personal pronouns are the primary person-deictic system of Interslavic. The full paradigm is specified in the relevant section The basic deictic values are: Pronoun Person deictic value ja Speaker ty Addressee (singular informal, or singular formal V-form replaced by vy) on / ona / ono Third person (absent referent), masculine / feminine / neuter my Speaker + others vy Addressees (plural); or polite singular (V-form) oni / one Third persons (plural)

Interslavic has two demonstrative series expressing spatial distance from the speaker: Series Deictic value Notes toj / ta / to Distal (that, those) — entity not in the immediate vicinity of the speaker The neutral default demonstrative in modern Interslavic sej / sa / se Proximal (this, these) — entity in the immediate vicinity of the speaker More archaic in character; use is stylistically optional Both demonstratives decline for gender, number, and case in the same pattern as adjectives (see the relevant section). The proximal series sej / sa / se is optional: a writer MAY always use toj to mean "this" when pointing to nearby objects, relying on context to supply the proximal reading. The proximal series SHOULD be used when a proximal–distal contrast needs to be made explicit within a single text segment. Toj čelovek je moj prijatelj. — "That man is my friend." (distal) Sej čelovek je moj prijatelj. — "This man is my friend." (proximal, explicit contrast)
documents.s: Spatial Deixis: toj / ta / to vs. sej / sa / se

documents.t: Temporal deictic expressions locate events relative to the moment of speech: Expression Meaning teper now, at this moment segodnja today včera yesterday jutro tomorrow togda then, at that time (past or future) nedavno recently skoro soon uže already ješče still; yet These expressions are deictic anchors: their reference shifts with the time of the utterance. In reported speech or narrative, the deictic center may shift to a past reference point (narrative present), in which case temporal adverbs are reinterpreted relative to that past anchor.

Interslavic discourse organizes clauses into topic (what the sentence is about; typically given, already-known information) and comment (what is said about the topic; typically new information). The default position for the topic is sentence-initial. The default position for the comment (especially the most informative, rhematically prominent element) is post-verbal or sentence-final. [Moja sestra [topic] kupila knigu [comment].] — "My sister bought a book." (neutral: sister is topic, book is new info) [Knigu [topic] kupila moja sestra [comment].] — "The book was bought by my sister." (book is topicalized; sister is the new/contrasted info) This principle interacts with pro-drop : when the topic-subject is recoverable from context, it is typically omitted.

Focused constituents — those bearing the most informationally prominent content — are marked primarily by prosodic prominence (stress in speech). In writing, focus may be conveyed by: Fronting the focused constituent to a pre-verbal or sentence-initial position. Using a cleft-like structure (To jest on, ktory... — "It is he who..."). Italics or underlining in informal writing (a typographic convention, not a grammatical rule). Contrastive focus signals that the focused constituent is being distinguished from an explicit or implicit alternative: Ja kupil KNIGU, ne žurnal. — "I bought a BOOK, not a magazine." (contrastive focus on knigu) See the relevant section for cross-reference to questions and information structure in the syntax chapter.

In coherent discourse, successive sentences maintain cohesion through theme–rheme chaining: the rheme (new information) of one sentence typically becomes the theme (given information) of the next. Ona kupila knigu. — "She bought a book." Kniga jest o istoriji. — "The book is about history." Istorija — eto ejo ljubimaja tema. — "History is her favorite topic." Each sentence takes the rheme of the preceding sentence as its theme, creating a chain of shared reference that signals discourse coherence. Writers SHOULD maintain this pattern in formal expository prose.

Discourse markers are words and phrases that signal logical, temporal, or rhetorical relationships between clauses, sentences, or larger discourse units. They do not contribute propositional content but guide the reader's interpretation of how segments connect. The following table lists the primary discourse markers of Interslavic with their functions: Marker Function Example a Contrast or light additive ("and/but") On rabotaje, a ona odpočiva. "He works, but she rests." i Simple additive conjunction ("and") On čita i piše. "He reads and writes." no / ale Adversative ("but, however") Ja hotel, no ne mog. "I wanted to, but couldn't." jednak Concessive contrast ("however, nevertheless") Jednak, situacija jest složna. "However, the situation is complex." zato Causal result ("therefore, that is why") On ne prišel, zato my čekali. "He didn't come, that is why we waited." zatom Logical conclusion ("therefore, thus") Zatom možemo rekti, čto... "Therefore we can say that..." ibo / zane Causal explanation ("because, for") Ona ustala, ibo mnogo rabotala. "She was tired, because she worked a lot." prěže Temporal precedence ("before") Prěže togo, my dělali inačě. "Before that, we did it differently." potom / potem Temporal sequence ("then, afterwards") Potom on odšel. "Then he left." naprimer Exemplification ("for example") Naprimer, v ruskym... "For example, in Russian..." tj. (to jest) Reformulation ("that is, i.e.") Slavjansky jezyk, tj. prědslovjansky... "A Slavic language, i.e., Proto-Slavic..." takože Additive ("also, too, likewise") Takože trěba pamętati... "It is also necessary to remember..." mimo togo Additive ("moreover, in addition") Mimo togo, zakon rěči, čto... "Moreover, the law states that..."

This subsection provides the comprehensive discourse connector inventory from Vojtech Merunka's handbook on Interslavic (Chapter 21, "Связь между словами" / "Word Connections"). The inventory is organized by semantic category. Grammatical labels: adv. = adverb, prep. G. = preposition governing the genitive, prep. A. = preposition governing the accusative, prep. I. = preposition governing the instrumental, prep. D. = preposition governing the dative, N. = nominative complement. Usage note: a connector placed as the first word of a sentence carries stronger force than the same connector placed further within the sentence. Addition​ ISV English gloss i and ili or ni ... ni ... neither ... nor ... navyše moreover, furthermore, in addition ješče (adv.) furthermore, in addition, also takože also, likewise kromě (adv.) besides, except iznovo (adv.) again, anew izprva, v prvyh firstly, in the first place v vtoryh secondly v tretjih thirdly nakonec (adv.) finally, lastly dalje (adv.) further, next poslědno (adv.) lastly, in conclusion Time​ ISV English gloss kogda (adv.) when potom (adv.), poslje (prep. G.) after, afterwards; after (something) v tečenji ... Beyond logical connectors, discourse cohesion is also maintained through content transitions — devices that carry the same topic or reference forward from one sentence or paragraph to the next. Merunka identifies four main techniques: 1. Pronouns. A noun subject is followed by a personal pronoun to maintain reference to the same entity, and by a possessive pronoun to shift to something associated with that entity. See the relevant section for the full pronoun paradigm. Personal pronouns: ja, ty, on, ona, ono, my, vy, oni Possessive pronouns: moj, tvoj, naš, vaš; the genitive-possessive forms of on/ona/ono/oni are jego/jej/jego/jih Example: Fido spi. On jest dobry pes. Jego dom jest pod jablonjeju. — "Fido is sleeping. He is a good dog. His house is under the apple tree." 2. Keyword repetition. Repeating the words that designate the key concepts of the discourse maintains topical continuity. Example: Unverified Content Inconsistent spelling: the word appears as both pamětnik and památnik (with ě vs á) within the same example. One form should be used consistently. Praga imaje mnogo historičnyh pamětnikov. Tute památniky sut stare domy, mosty, theatra i hramy. Najstarši pražsky pamětnik jest iz romanskoj ery. — "Prague has many historical monuments. These monuments are old houses, bridges, theatres and churches. The oldest Prague monument is from the Romanesque era." 3. Synonyms. Using synonyms for the same referent confirms the partner's understanding and avoids repetition. Example: Vaše auto jest lěpo. Ale myslim, že taky voz jest mnogo skupy. — "Your car looks nice. But I think such a vehicle is very expensive." 4. Demonstrative pronouns. Demonstrative pronouns (toj, ta, to, ti, te and their extended forms tutoj, tamtoj) refer back to what has already been mentioned. See the relevant section and for the full declension. Example: Moj brat imaje veliky dom. Kako stary jest toj dom? — "My brother has a big house. How old is that house?"
Speech acts are communicative actions performed through language. The following subsections specify the standard Interslavic forms associated with major speech act types.

Assertions (declarative speech acts) are performed using declarative sentence form: subject–verb–object order with falling intonation (or period in writing). Interslavsky jest slavjansky jezyk. — "Interslavic is a Slavic language." No special marking is required for an assertion. The indicative mood is the default for assertions; the conditional is used for hypothetical assertions (see the relevant section).

Yes/no questions and content questions are specified in the relevant section Briefly: Yes/no questions are formed with rising intonation, with the particle či sentence-initially, or with li after the verb. Content questions are formed with an interrogative word (čto, kto, kde, kogda, kako, počemu, etc.) in sentence-initial position. See the relevant section for full specification.

Direct commands use the imperative mood (see the relevant section): Daj mně knigu! — "Give me the book!" (2sg imperative) Pojdemo! — "Let's go!" (1pl imperative) Polite requests SHOULD use one of the following softening strategies: Conditional: Mogli by vy mně pomoći? — "Could you help me?" Interrogative + conditional: Prosim, možete li mně rěčti, kdě jest...? — "Could you please tell me where...?" Mitigating adverb: Prosim, dajte mně... — "Please give me..." The bare imperative without a politeness marker is appropriate in informal contexts (with ty addressees) but SHOULD NOT be used in formal address.

Offers and invitations use hortative or interrogative structures: Hortative (1pl imperative): Pojdemo v restoran! — "Let's go to a restaurant!" Interrogative offer: Hoćete li čaj ili kafu? — "Would you like tea or coffee?" Declarative offer: Ja mogu pomoći. — "I can help." (implicit offer) Modal interrogative: Mogu li vam što predložiti? — "May I offer you something?"

Standard greetings, farewells, thanks, and apologies function as formulaic speech acts — their communicative function is largely independent of their compositional meaning. The following forms are conventional in Interslavic; speakers SHOULD be aware that specific greetings vary by source-language culture. Standard ISV Latin orthography typically uses nj (i.e., do svidanja). "until the meeting") | | Farewell (informal short) | zbogom | "Farewell" (S.Sl. orientation) | | Thanks | dękuju / blagodarju | "Thank you" (dękuju W./S.Sl.; blagodarju E.Sl. oriented) | | Response to thanks | prosim / nič za to | "You're welcome" | | Apology | izvinite / prostite | "Excuse me / Forgive me" | | Acknowledgment of apology | nič / ně beda | "It's nothing / No problem" | Unverified Content The form zovų sę uses nasal vowel characters (ų, ę) which are Polish-style and not part of standard ISV Latin orthography. This section specifies the semantic interpretation of tense and aspect combinations, the interaction of aspect with negation and phase verbs, the system of modal expressions, and the treatment of evidentiality in Interslavic.

Unverified Content The source material describes an "anterior future" nowhere. The source mentions present, past (compound), future, plus optional aorist/imperfect (merged) and pluperfect, but no anterior future tense. Interslavic has two tense systems. The standard system (used in the vast majority of contexts) uses three tenses: present, past, and future. The extended system adds an aorist/imperfect distinction, a pluperfect, and an anterior future; these are optional and literary in character (see for morphological forms). Within either system, every finite verb bears one of two aspects: Imperfective (impf.) — the action is presented as ongoing, repeated, habitual, or in progress at the reference time. Perfective (pf.) — the action is presented as a bounded, completed whole; the focus is on its result or endpoint. The interaction of tense and aspect produces the interpretations listed in the matrix below.

Tense Imperfective aspect Perfective aspect Present Ongoing action; habitual or repeated action; generic statement. Ja pišu pismo. "I am writing a letter." / "I write letters (habitually)." Not used for present reference. A present-tense form of a pf. verb is future in meaning (see Future row below). Past (compound) Action was ongoing or repeated at past reference time; past habit. Ja jesm pisal pismo. "I was writing a letter." / "I used to write letters." Action was completed at past reference time; focus on result. Ja jesm napisal pismo. "I (have) wrote a letter (and finished it)." Future Ongoing, habitual, or repeated action in the future. Ja budu pisati pismo. "I will be writing a letter." Single completed event anticipated in the future. Ja budu napisati pismo. "I will write (and finish) a letter." Unverified Content The source says the perfective present for future "is rather to be avoided, as it can easily be misunderstood by South Slavs," but does not specify that they would "misread it as a present tense." The claim that the analytic future is "the recommended standard form" is editorial interpretation not stated in the source. Note on perfective present forms: Writers SHOULD NOT use the present-tense form of a perfective verb with present meaning. In Interslavic, using the perfective present for future reference (Ja napišu pismo) is permitted as a variant but SHOULD be avoided in formal writing, because South Slavic speakers may misread it as a present tense. The analytic future with budu is the recommended standard form (see the relevant section).

The extended system provides finer temporal distinctions, primarily useful in literary or narrative prose. Unverified Content The source explicitly states that "In modern Interslavic, we simplify this system somewhat by merging the imperfect with the aorist." A separate imperfect tense (row 3 below) distinct from the aorist is not supported by the primary source. The anterior future (row 5 below) is not mentioned anywhere in the source material and cannot be verified. However, Roberto Lombino's proposal (2022) provides detailed morphological rules for distinguishing the aorist from the imperfect; see the morphology chapter for formation details. Form Name Interpretation Example Present of byti + L-participle Perfect Past action with present relevance; action just completed; result visible now. Ja jesm pisal. Aorist endings on infinitive stem Aorist Action fully completed in the past, no present connection. Typically used with perfective verbs. The 2sg./3sg. have zero endings. Ja sdělah. On sděla. Imperfect endings on present stem Imperfect Extended, ongoing, or repeated past action (background narration). Typically used with imperfective verbs. Formed from the 3sg. present with -jah/-ěh endings. Ja dělajah. Ja dělajaše. Past of byti + L-participle Pluperfect Action anterior to a past reference point. Ja běh pisal. Future of byti + L-participle Anterior future Action completed before a future reference point. Ja budu pisal. When the aorist and imperfect co-occur in a narrative, the aorist expresses the foregrounded event (the main narrative action) and the imperfect expresses the background condition against which that event occurs. This mirrors the Past Simple / Past Continuous distinction in English. The general correlation between aspect and tense is: perfective verbs take the aorist, imperfective verbs take the imperfect.
