However, cross-combinations are permitted: an imperfect of a perfective verb expresses repeated completed actions (Vsegda, kogda pogledijah... "Every time I looked..."), while an aorist of an imperfective verb emphasizes that an ongoing activity came to an end (Hodih v tu školu do prošlogo leta "I went to that school until last year [and then stopped]"). Writers who use the extended system SHOULD NOT do so in communication with audiences unfamiliar with it, as the simple past endings are not widely intelligible to speakers of North Slavic languages. Unresolved: Status of the aorist/imperfect distinction The primary grammar (Jan van Steenbergen) merges the aorist and imperfect into a single "simple past" tense. Roberto Lombino's proposal (2022) argues that this merger is unnatural for speakers of Bulgarian, Macedonian, and Serbian/Croatian, and provides three strategies for forming a morphologically distinct imperfect. The key semantic question is whether the extended tense system should formally recognize two separate past synthetic tenses (aorist and imperfect) or treat the "simple past" as a single undifferentiated category. If the distinction is adopted, additional decisions are needed: Which imperfect formation strategy (from 3sg. present with -jah, with -ěh, or from passive participle stem) should be recommended as primary? How should the compound past (L-participle) interact semantically with the aorist and imperfect? The compound past with auxiliary omission (on pisal) can be ambiguous with the simple past in function. Should the imperfect of perfective verbs and the aorist of imperfective verbs be documented as standard or marked as advanced usage?

Unverified Content This entire section on aspect-negation interaction, including the genitive of negation rule, has no basis in the source material, which does not discuss negation semantics. Aspect interacts with negation in predictable ways: Negated perfective expresses failure to complete an expected action: On ne sdělal zadaňje. — "He did not complete the assignment." Negated imperfective expresses absence of an ongoing or habitual action: On ne dělal zadaňje. — "He was not doing / did not (habitually) do the assignment." Under negation, imperfective is often preferred when the statement expresses a general fact, denial of habit, or absence of activity. Perfective is used when the negation specifically denies the completion of a bounded act. The genitive of negation (see the relevant section) applies to direct objects of negated verbs; this is a syntactic reflex of the semantic scope of negation over the object's referential status.

Unverified Content This entire section on phase verbs and the MUST requirement for imperfective complements has no basis in the source material, which does not discuss phase verbs or their complement requirements. Phase verbs — verbs denoting the beginning, continuation, or cessation of an activity — MUST take an imperfective complement. This applies to: Unverified Content The verb prodolžati (row 3) appears Russian-influenced and is not attested in the source material. The verb rabotati in its example is likewise not found in the source (which uses dělati for "to do/work"). Phase verb Meaning Example načeti to begin On načel čitati knigu. "He began reading the book." začeti to start Ona začela govoriti. "She started speaking." prodolžati to continue My prodolžajem rabotati. "We continue working." prestati to stop, cease On prestal paliti. "He stopped smoking." končati to finish Ona končila pisati. "She finished writing." The imperfective complement is required because phase verbs quantify over the duration or progress of an activity, which is semantically incompatible with viewing the event as a completed bounded whole. TODO: Committee Input Needed Confirm the complete list of phase verbs that require imperfective complements. Clarify whether zakyp­ljati / počynati and related inchoatives follow the same pattern. Specify behavior when the complement is a stative verb.

Unverified Content This entire modality section (, including all subsections on epistemic, deontic, and dynamic modality) is not covered by the source material, which does not discuss modal predicates, epistemic adverbs, or deontic expressions. Only the conditional mood forms have partial support in the source. Interslavic expresses modal meanings — possibility, necessity, obligation, permission, ability — primarily through modal predicates (lexical items functioning as impersonal predicates or agreeing adjectives/verbs) and through the conditional mood. There is no morphological category of "modal auxiliary" as a separate grammatical class.

Epistemic modality concerns the speaker's degree of confidence in the truth of a proposition. Expression Meaning Syntactic behavior Example možno it is possible; perhaps Impersonal predicate + infinitive, or sentence adverb Možno, čto on zabyl. "It's possible he forgot." věrojatno probably, likely Sentence adverb Ona věrojatno pridět. "She will probably come." naverně certainly, surely Sentence adverb On naverně zna. "He certainly knows." možebiti maybe, perhaps Sentence adverb Možebiti ona jeste doma. "Maybe she is at home." podobno apparently, it seems Sentence adverb Podobno, on ne prišel. "Apparently he didn't come." Epistemic expressions are sentence adverbs that scope over the entire proposition. They do not agree with any noun phrase and do not alter the tense of the main clause.

Deontic modality concerns obligation, necessity, and permission. Expression Meaning Syntactic behavior Example trěba it is necessary; one must Impersonal predicate + infinitive (dative experiencer optional) Trěba rabotati. "One must work." Mně trěba idti. "I need to go." dolžen, dolžna, dolžno ought to, is obliged to Agreeing short-form adjective + infinitive On jest dolžen platiti. "He ought to pay." možno it is permitted; one may Impersonal predicate + infinitive Možno hoditi suda. "One may go here." nělzě it is not permitted; one must not Impersonal predicate + infinitive Nělzě kuriti. "Smoking is not permitted." iměti to have to; to be supposed to Full verb + infinitive On iměje skazati pravdu. "He is supposed to tell the truth." Unverified Content The use of iměti as a modal verb meaning "to have to; to be supposed to" (last row above) is not attested in the source material. The source does not discuss iměti as a modal construction. Trěba is strictly impersonal and does not agree with any subject. Dolžen is an agreeing predicate adjective and MUST agree with the grammatical subject in gender and number.

Dynamic modality concerns ability — whether an agent is capable of performing an action. Unverified Content The source uses the infinitive form mogti (not moći), explicitly noting that mogti is preferred over the "expected but less transparent" moči. The SHOULD NOT claim about substituting moći and uměti is also not in the source. Additionally, the form umě in the uměti row appears incorrect: per the source conjugation rules, uměti follows first conjugation with -j- stem (uměj-), giving 3sg uměje, not umě. Expression Meaning Distinction moći to be able to (circumstantially); to be allowed to Expresses circumstantial or physical ability, or contextual permission. On može to sdělati. "He can do that (he has the means / he is allowed)." uměti to know how to; to have the skill Expresses learned, acquired ability or skill. Ona umě plavati. "She knows how to swim." The distinction between moći and uměti is obligatory in formal Interslavic. Writers SHOULD NOT substitute one for the other.

The conditional mood (L-participle + conditional forms of byti: byh, by, by, byhmo, byste, by) expresses hypothetical, counterfactual, and polite meanings in addition to its core conditional function (see the relevant section). Use Form Example Hypothetical / counterfactual Past conditional Ja byl byh to sdělal, ibo... "I would have done it, if..." Polite request Conditional Mogli by vy mně pomoći? "Could you help me?" Wish or desire Conditional Ja byh hotel/a pobyti tamo. "I would like to be there." Purpose clause complement Conditional after že by / da by Ona prišla, že by my mogli govoriti. "She came so that we could talk." The conditional is the preferred polite register for requests. Using the imperative alone in a formal context is pragmatically marked as abrupt (see the relevant section).
documents.s: The Conditional as a Modality Marker

documents.t: Unverified Content This entire section on evidentiality has no basis in the source material, which does not discuss evidentiality, renarrative mood, or evidential adverbs. The specific lexical items listed (e.g., slyhom, po skazaniju, kažetsja, po slovam, dle) are not attested in the source. Interslavic does not grammaticalize evidentiality. The morphological system of tense and aspect does not encode the source of information (direct witness, hearsay, inference). Evidential meanings that some Slavic languages convey through specific tense-aspect combinations (e.g., the Bulgarian renarrative mood) are not part of the Interslavic standard. Evidential meanings are expressed in Interslavic exclusively by lexical means: Evidential type Expression Example Hearsay / reported slyhom, po skazaniju, kako govorjat Slyhom, on prišel včera. "I heard he came yesterday." Inference / appearance podobno, vidno, kažetsja Vidno, čto ona ne spala. "It is evident she didn't sleep." Quotation po slovam, dle + genitive Po slovam gazety... "According to the newspaper..." Assumption věrojatno, naverně Věrojatno, on zna. "He probably knows." TODO: Committee Input Needed Determine whether a standardized set of evidential adverbs and parentheticals should be formalized in the lexicon. Specify whether podobno and vidno may also function as manner adverbs (potential ambiguity).

This section specifies the syntactic rules of Interslavic: the principles that govern how words combine into phrases, clauses, and sentences. The rules defined here apply to all standard written and spoken Interslavic regardless of flavorizational register.

This chapter is organized into the following subsections: Section Topic Basic Clause Structure: word order, agreement, pro-drop, existentials Noun Phrase Structure: modifier order, numerals, apposition, possession Verb Phrase Structure: copular sentences, case government Coordination: conjunctions, agreement, ellipsis, gapping Subordination: complement clauses, conditionals, purpose, temporal Relative Clauses: ktory, free relatives, resumptive pronouns Negation Questions and Information Structure Special Constructions

Interslavic exhibits the following typological properties, which together determine the overall shape of its syntax: Morphologically rich inflection. Grammatical relations (subject, object, indirect object, etc.) are expressed by case endings on nouns, pronouns, adjectives, and numerals. Word order is therefore available to express discourse-level functions (topic, focus, contrast) rather than grammatical functions. Neutral word order: SVO. Subject–Verb–Object is the pragmatically unmarked default. Other orderings are grammatical but discourse-marked (see the relevant section). Pro-drop. Overt subject pronouns are optional when recoverable from verb morphology (see the relevant section). Prepositional phrases and case government. Prepositions govern specific cases. The case selected by a preposition depends on the preposition's lexical identity and, for spatial prepositions, on whether motion or static location is expressed (see the relevant section). No articles. Interslavic has no grammatical articles. Definiteness MAY be indicated pragmatically by the demonstrative toj / та / то (тoj / та / то), but this is not obligatory (see the relevant section).

The syntactic rules in this chapter presuppose the morphological forms specified in the Morphology chapter. In particular: Case assignment rules reference the seven-case system described in the relevant section Verbal agreement rules reference the person/number paradigms in the relevant section Coordination agreement references the gender system in the relevant section

All rules in this chapter that use the modal verbs MUST, SHALL, SHOULD, MAY, and SHOULD NOT carry the following normative force: MUST / SHALL — an absolute grammatical requirement; violation produces an ungrammatical sentence. SHOULD — the strongly recommended default; deviation is grammatical but stylistically marked. MAY — an explicitly permitted option; neither preferred nor dispreferred. SHOULD NOT — a usage that is grammatical but actively discouraged as stylistically poor or cross-dialectally opaque.

This section specifies the structural principles governing simple clauses in Interslavic: canonical constituent order, pragmatic deviations from that order, subject-verb agreement, pro-drop, and the syntax of existential sentences.

Interslavic employs Subject – Verb – Object (SVO) as its neutral, pragmatically unmarked word order. This is the preferred order for declarative sentences where no constituent bears special emphasis or discourse prominence. Otec kupil knigu. — "Father bought a book." Other orderings (SOV, VSO, OVS, etc.) are syntactically grammatical but pragmatically marked: they signal topic–focus structuring, contrast, or stylistic register. Writers and speakers SHOULD use SVO as the default unless a specific pragmatic effect is intended. Modifiers (adjectives, demonstratives, genitival attributes) normally precede the noun they modify. This is not an absolute requirement, but pre-nominal placement is the most neutral and unambiguous order.

Because Interslavic uses inflectional case endings to signal grammatical roles, constituent order is available to convey information structure rather than grammatical function. Unverified Content The following Topic/Focus/clause-final position table and the pragmatically marked order examples are not present in the source material (grammar-syntax.md). The source states only that word order is free and that placing the object before the subject does not help clarity. The detailed information-structure analysis is an unsourced elaboration. Position Pragmatic value Pre-verbal (sentence-initial) Topic — given, already-known information Post-verbal Focus — new, emphasized, or contrastive information Clause-final Heaviest stress in neutral intonation; most prominent focus position Placing the object before the subject does not in itself clarify meaning, because case endings already identify who does what to whom. However, sentences where a personal pronoun or the pronoun kto functions as subject or object are always unambiguous even with inverted order, since these forms have dedicated accusative forms. Examples of pragmatically marked order: Topic-initial (object fronted for contrast): Knigu otec kupil, ne žurnalu. "The book father bought, not the magazine." Focus on subject (subject postposed): Kupil knigu otec. "It was father who bought the book." Unverified Content The following section on subject-verb agreement , including the MUST statement about person/number agreement and the L-participle agreement table, has no basis in the source material (grammar-syntax.md). The source does not discuss verb agreement rules.

Finite verbs in Interslavic MUST agree with their subject in person and number. Ja čitam. — "I read." (1sg) On čita. — "He reads." (3sg) My čitamo. — "We read." (1pl) In compound tenses formed with the L-participle (past/conditional), the L-participle MUST agree with the subject in gender and number. The auxiliary byti carries person–number agreement. Subject L-participle form Example Masculine singular -l on kupil Feminine singular -la ona kupila Neuter singular -lo ono kupilo Plural (all genders) -li oni kupili When the subject is a coordinated NP with heads of mixed gender, the plural L-participle defaults to the masculine plural form -li. Unverified Content The following section on pro-drop , including the claim that Interslavic is a pro-drop language and the SHOULD/NOT-required statements about pronoun use, has no basis in the source material (grammar-syntax.md). The source does not discuss subject pronoun omission.

Interslavic is a pro-drop language: an overt subject pronoun is OPTIONAL when the grammatical person and number of the subject are recoverable from the verb's inflectional ending. Čitam knigu. — "I am reading a book." (subject ja omitted) Kupili smo hljěb. — "We bought bread." (subject my omitted) Overt subject pronouns SHOULD be used: for emphasis: Ja to znam, ne ty. — "I know that, not you." for contrast: On pišet, ona čita. — "He writes, she reads." when disambiguation is needed and the inflectional form is ambiguous (e.g., some archaic or colloquial 1sg/3sg syncretic forms). Subject pronouns are NOT required in any position; their absence is the stylistically neutral choice in running prose when the referent is clear. Unverified Content The entire section on existential and presentational sentences , including affirmative existentials with jest/sųt, the genitive of negation with nět, and the rules for copula jest omission, has no basis in the source material (grammar-syntax.md). The source does not discuss existential constructions, genitive of negation, or copula omission.

Existential sentences ("there is / there are") are formed with the copula jest (3sg) or sųt (3pl) followed by a nominative subject NP, typically accompanied by a locative or circumstantial phrase. V městě jest bolnica. — "There is a hospital in the city." Na stolu sųt knigi. — "There are books on the table." The existential subject is typically indefinite or newly introduced into the discourse (presentational use).

Under sentential negation, the subject (or direct object) of an existential/presentational clause SHOULD shift from nominative (accusative) to genitive. This is the genitive of negation. Affirmative: Jest voda. — "There is water." (nominative) Negative: Nět vody. — "There is no water." (genitive) Affirmative: Sųt studenti. — "There are students." Negative: Nět studentov. — "There are no students." The negative existential particle nět ("there is no") is regularly used in this construction and does not require a separate form of byti.
documents.s: Negative Existential Sentences: Genitive of Negation

documents.t: The copula jest is frequently omitted in 3rd person present tense copular and existential sentences, particularly in informal or neutral register. This omission is grammatical and common: On (jest) doktor. — "He is a doctor." Ona (jest) zdrava. — "She is healthy." The omission of jest is not permissible in other persons (1sg jesm, 2sg jesi, etc.) in copular sentences.
documents.s: Omission of jest in Third-Person Copular Sentences

documents.t: The passive voice is grammatically available in Interslavic but SHOULD be used with caution, as its naturalness varies across the Slavic readership.

The analytic passive is formed with byti + past passive participle (PPP, suffix -n-/-t-): Pica je dělana. — "The pizza is being made." (present passive) Pica była dělana. — "The pizza was made." (past passive) Unverified Content The specific mention of Bulgarian and Macedonian as languages that do not use PPP in present passive constructions is not in the source material. The source states more generally that "for others the past passive participle cannot be used for a present tense construction at all" without naming specific languages. While grammatically correct, this construction can sound unnatural to Slavic speakers unaccustomed to frequent use of byti in the present tense, or to those whose native languages (e.g., Bulgarian, Macedonian) do not use PPP in present passive constructions in the same way.

When the agent is unknown or indefinite, two constructions are preferred over the analytic passive: Third-person plural with null subject ("impersonal active"): Dělajut picu. — "They make pizza / One makes pizza / Pizza is being made." Unverified Content The reflexive particle is written as sę here, but the source material (grammar-syntax.md) uses se in the same example: "Pica dělaje se". Verify whether sę or se is the correct standard form. Reflexive-passive with sę: Pica dělaje sę. — Literally "Pizza makes itself"; translated as "One makes pizza / Pizza is being made." When the agent is known, a normal active sentence SHOULD be used in preference to the passive.

Purpose clauses express the goal or intention of the action in the main clause. They are introduced by the conjunctions že or da followed by the conditional (by + L-participle). Turisti posětet Niderlandiju, že by fotografovali tulipany. "Tourists visit the Netherlands to take photos of the tulips." Turisti posětili Niderlandiju, že by fotografovali tulipany. "Tourists visited the Netherlands to take photos of the tulips." Ona prinosi jabloka, da by děti byli zdrave. "She brings apples so that the kids will be healthy." Ona prinosila jabloka, da by děti byli zdrave. "She brought apples so that the kids would be healthy." The tense of the main clause (present or past) does not affect the form of the purpose clause: the conditional by + L-participle is used in both cases. Unverified Content The entire section on the da + present tense construction , including its use as an infinitive alternative, the full conjugation paradigm table, reflexive particle placement, and the purposive/volitional conjunction usage, has no basis in the source material (grammar-syntax.md). The source only discusses da in the context of purpose clauses with the conditional (da + by + L-participle), not as an alternative to the infinitive.

The construction da + present tense is attested in all South Slavic languages and is available in Interslavic as an optional alternative to the infinitive after modal and other auxiliary verbs. This construction is particularly appropriate when communicating with speakers of Bulgarian and Macedonian, which lack an infinitive entirely. Writers targeting a pan-Slavic audience MAY prefer this construction to increase intelligibility for South Slavic readers. The verb following da stands in the present tense and agrees with its own subject, regardless of the tense of the governing verb. Person/Number da construction Infinitive construction 1sg ja moram da idem ja moram idti 2sg ty moraš da ideš ty moraš idti 3sg on mora da ide on mora idti 1pl my moramo da idemo my moramo idti 2pl vy morate da idete vy morate idti 3pl oni morajųt da idųt oni morajųt idti The reflexive particle sę in these constructions is placed after da: Ja moram da sę vråtim. — "I must return." This construction may replace the infinitive in virtually any syntactic environment: To mora da sę zna. — "This must be known." Htěl jesm da kažem. — "I wanted to say." My bųdemo da čitamo. — "We will read." Ne mogl by da prijědeš? — "Couldn't you come?"
documents.s: Da as an Alternative to the Infinitive

documents.t: In South Slavic usage, da also functions as a conjunction meaning "so that / in order that / that (with volitional force)". In this role it introduces a finite subordinate clause in the present tense or conditional, without a following infinitive: Prišel sem da ti kažem. — "I came so that I might tell you." / "I came to tell you." Želim da mi otkryješ istinų. — "I want you to reveal the truth to me." Naši prědki sųt sę boroli da bysmo žyvěli ščęstlivo. — "Our ancestors fought so that we might live happily." This usage overlaps functionally with the purpose clause construction described in the relevant section (using že/da + conditional). Both patterns are grammatically acceptable; the da + present tense variant is more characteristic of South Slavic style, while že/da + conditional is more pan-Slavic in distribution. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Information-structural marking and intonation:** The outline calls for specification of how clause-final stress interacts with other focus-marking strategies (particles, word order) and whether any formal focus particles beyond li are available. The current Steen source addresses question formation and general word order but does not provide a systematic account of topic–focus structure beyond the basics stated here. A committee decision on normative rules for focus constructions (e.g., contrastive že, focus particles) is needed. ** — Copula omission across registers:** The conditions under which jest omission is permitted vs. obligatory (formal vs. informal register; written vs. spoken) require further specification. The source material confirms that omission is common in 3rd person but does not prescribe conditions. ** — Scope of da + present tense:** Whether this construction is normatively restricted to specific verb classes (modal, phasal, volitional) or is freely available with any governing verb needs committee determination. The Ranmaru source suggests broad availability; formal codification of any restrictions is outstanding.

This section specifies the syntactic rules governing coordination in Interslavic: the behavior of coordinating and correlative conjunctions, agreement in coordinated noun phrases, and the conditions under which material may be elided or gapped in the second conjunct.

Unverified Content The designated primary source (grammar-conjunctions.md) contains only preposition material, not conjunctions. The entire conjunction inventory table below has no basis in the provided source material. While common Slavic conjunctions like i, a, ale, ili are plausible, items such as jednako ("however"), pa ("so/and so"), ubo ("therefore"), and poněže ("whereas") cannot be verified. Interslavic has the following coordinating conjunctions: Conjunction Meaning / Use i "and" — additive coordination a "and / but" — additive or mild contrastive ale "but" — adversative; corrective contrast ili "or" — disjunctive (inclusive and exclusive) abo "or" — disjunctive (chiefly informal) no "but" — adversative; strong contrast jednako "however" — concessive adversative pa "so / and so" — consecutive ubo "therefore" — illative než / neželi "than" — comparative poněže "whereas" — adversative/concessive

Unverified Content The MUST and SHOULD constraints on coordination of like syntactic categories are not supported by the provided source material, which covers prepositions rather than coordination syntax. These rules appear to be inferred from general linguistic principles rather than sourced from Interslavic-specific documentation. Coordinated elements MUST belong to the same syntactic category (both NPs, both VPs, both APs, both clauses, etc.) or at minimum to compatible grammatical functions within the clause. Coordination of unlike constituents (e.g., an NP conjoined with a PP) is grammatically ill-formed and SHOULD be avoided. Grammatical (NP + NP): otec i mati — "father and mother" Grammatical (VP + VP): čita i piše — "reads and writes" Grammatical (clause + clause): On pišet, a ona čita. — "He writes, but she reads."

Unverified Content The placement rules and the MAY permission for comma-separated lists are not addressed in the provided source material. These conventions follow general Slavic patterns but are not verified against Interslavic-specific documentation. Coordinating conjunctions stand between the elements they conjoin. They are invariable (they do not inflect for any grammatical category). When three or more elements are coordinated, the conjunction typically appears only before the final conjunct; earlier conjuncts MAY be separated by commas. knigi, žurnaly i gazety — "books, magazines and newspapers" On govori, piše i čita. — "He speaks, writes and reads."

Unverified Content This entire subsection on correlative conjunctions (the paired inventory, the MUST constraint, and all examples) has no basis in the provided source material. The correlative pairs kako … tako i …, ne jedino … ale takože …, čim … tym …, and koliko … toliko … cannot be verified. Correlative conjunctions work in pairs, with the first element appearing before the first conjunct and the second element before the second conjunct. The following correlative pairs are established in Interslavic: Pair Meaning ili … ili … "either … or …" ni … ni … "neither … nor …" kako … tako i … "both … and …" ne jedino … ale takože … "not only … but also …" čim … tym … "the … the …" (comparative) koliko … toliko … "the … the …" (proportional) Correlative conjunction pairs MUST conjoin constituents of the same syntactic category, following the same constraint as simple coordinating conjunctions . Ni otec ni mati ne zna. — "Neither father nor mother knows." Kako otec, tako i syn. — "Both father and son." Ne jedino čita, ale takože piše. — "Not only reads, but also writes."

Unverified Content The MUST rule requiring plural verb agreement with coordinated subjects is not addressed in the provided source material. This follows general Slavic convention but is unsourced for Interslavic specifically. When two or more NPs are coordinated as the subject of a clause, the finite verb MUST agree in the plural: Otec i mati sųt doma. — "Father and mother are at home." Kniga i žurnal ležat na stolu. — "The book and the magazine lie on the table." TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Verb agreement with "nearest conjunct" (proximity agreement):** Some Slavic languages permit singular agreement with a coordinated subject when the verb precedes the coordinated NP (proximity rule). Whether Interslavic should normatively permit or prohibit this construction requires committee determination.

Unverified Content The entire gender resolution subsection — the MUST rule for masculine plural default -li, the feminine plural -ly rule, and the claim that neuter plural L-participles share the masculine plural form — has no basis in the provided source material. These rules cannot be verified. When coordinated subjects of mixed grammatical gender govern a compound tense containing an L-participle, the L-participle MUST take the masculine plural form -li as the default resolution. Otec i mati prišli. — "Father and mother came." (masc. plural default) On i ona kupili sut knigu. — "He and she have bought a book." When all coordinated subjects are grammatically feminine, the feminine plural form -ly is used: Mati i dočka prišly. — "Mother and daughter came." When all coordinated subjects are grammatically neuter, the neuter plural form -li is used (neuter plurals share the form with masculine plural for L-participles).

Unverified Content This entire subsection on ellipsis in coordination (VP ellipsis, NP ellipsis, the MUST constraint on case marking retention, and all examples) has no basis in the provided source material. The rules and examples appear to be constructed from general linguistic principles. Ellipsis is the omission of material in the second (or subsequent) conjunct when that material is recoverable from the first conjunct or from the discourse context. Interslavic permits ellipsis under recoverability, subject to the constraint that case marking on residual material MUST be maintained.

The verb phrase of the second conjunct may be omitted when it is identical to the first-conjunct VP: On čita knigu, ona — žurnal. "He reads a book, she [reads] a magazine." The dash (—) optionally marks the ellipsis site in written Interslavic. The residual NP žurnal retains the accusative case it would bear as the direct object of the elided verb.

A noun within an NP may be elided when the referent is clear from context, leaving the modifier(s) to stand alone. The modifier retains the case appropriate to the syntactic position of the whole NP: Kupil jesm bělų knigu i červenų [knigu]. "I bought a white book and a red [book]." The elided noun's case is preserved on the remaining adjective (červenų = accusative feminine singular).

Unverified Content This entire subsection on gapping (including the MUST constraint on case retention, the SHOULD recommendation against non-parallel gapping, and all examples) has no basis in the provided source material. Gapping is a form of ellipsis specific to coordination in which the verb of the second conjunct is omitted when it is identical to the verb of the first conjunct, while non-verbal constituents in the second conjunct are retained. Otec kupil knigu, a syn — žurnal. "Father bought a book, and son [bought] a magazine." Gapping differs from VP ellipsis in that subject and object of the second conjunct both surface, while only the verb is absent. Unverified Content Internal consistency issue: the text refers to syna as nominative, but the example above uses syn (not syna). The form syna would typically be genitive or accusative animate, not nominative. The correct nominative form syn is used in the example, but the explanatory text below contradicts it. Case marking in gapped clauses: All remaining constituents in the second conjunct MUST retain the grammatical case they would bear if the verb were present. In the example above, syna would be nominative (subject) and žurnal accusative (direct object), which they are. Gapping is most natural when the two conjuncts are parallel in structure (same argument structure, same verb). Gapping across non-parallel structures is possible but SHOULD be avoided as it may reduce clarity. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Backward gapping / "stripping":** The source material does not address stripping (gapping where only one remnant remains in the second conjunct, often with a contrastive particle). Whether stripping is normatively sanctioned in Interslavic requires committee specification. Example: On pišet, a ona [piše] nět. ("He writes, but she does not.")

Unverified Content The MUST and MUST NOT rules about case agreement in coordinated NPs are not addressed in the provided source material. The source confirms that s governs the instrumental (supporting that specific example), but the general coordination-and-case rule itself is unsourced for Interslavic. When NPs are coordinated as a single argument of a verb or preposition, all NPs in the conjunction MUST bear the same case — the case appropriate to their shared syntactic function. Govorim s otcom i materju. "I speak with father and mother." (both instrumental after s) Videl jesm brata i sestru. "I saw brother and sister." (both accusative as direct objects; animate brata, inanimate-pattern sestru) Mismatched case within a coordinated NP argument is ungrammatical and MUST NOT occur.

Unverified Content This entire file lacks direct source material. The source file (grammar-syntax.md by Jan van Steenbergen) does not cover negation syntax. All rules, examples, and paradigms in this section are AI-generated extrapolations from general Slavic patterns and cannot be verified against the original Interslavic documentation. This section specifies the syntax of negation in Interslavic: sentential negation with the particle ne, constituent negation, the obligatory double-negation concord system with negative pronouns and adverbs, the genitive of negation under sentential negation, and the special behavior of negated existential byti.

Sentential (clausal) negation is expressed by the particle ne, which is placed immediately before the finite verb. This negates the proposition expressed by the entire clause. Otec ne kupil knigu. "Father did not buy a book." Ona ne čita. "She does not read." My ne poidemo tamo. "We will not go there." The particle ne is a proclitic: it is pronounced without a break before the verb and orthographically written as a separate word. In compound verb forms (auxiliary + participle), ne precedes the auxiliary: On ne byl kupil knigu. "He had not bought the book." Ona ne jest zdrava. "She is not healthy."

The particle ne may also precede a non-verbal constituent to negate that specific constituent rather than the entire clause. This is constituent negation (narrow-scope negation) as opposed to sentential (wide-scope) negation. Ne otec kupil knigu, a mati. "Not father bought a book, but (rather) mother." Ona prinosi ne jabloka, a hrušky. "She brings not apples, but pears." Unverified Content The form posětetь contains a stray Cyrillic soft sign character (ь) mixed into Latin-script ISV text. This is likely a character encoding error; the correct form would be posětet. Turisti posětetь ne Niderlandiju, a Belgiju. "The tourists will visit not the Netherlands, but Belgium." In constituent negation, the finite verb is not negated; only the targeted constituent is. Constituent negation commonly co-occurs with a corrective clause introduced by the adversative conjunction a ("but rather").

Interslavic employs an obligatory negative concord system. When a negative pronoun, adjective, or adverb is present in a clause, the finite verb MUST also be negated by ne. The co-occurrence of two or more negative elements does not produce a positive (double-negative) interpretation; both negatives are required to express a single negation. Negative pronouns and adjectives: Form Gloss nikto nobody, no one ničto / ništo nothing nikaky no kind of, not any ničij nobody's nijedin not a single, none Unverified Content Several forms in the negative adverbs table below conflict with the morphology chapter (, pronouns correlative table): nigde should be nikdě (the form nigde mirrors Russian нигде); nikako should be nikak; nikoľko should be nikoliko (the form nikoľko mirrors Russian нисколько); and namo as a standalone negative adverb is not attested in the morphology tables (only nikamo is listed). Negative adverbs: Form Gloss nigde nowhere namo / nikamo to nowhere nikogda never nikako in no way nikoľko not at all (in quantity) Examples of obligatory negative concord: Nikto ne přišel. "Nobody came." (NOT "Somebody came"; ne is obligatory) Ona ničto ne kupila. "She bought nothing." Unverified Content The form byliь contains a stray Cyrillic soft sign character (ь). The correct form would be byli. The same issue affects viděliь in the relevant section below. My nigde ne byliь. "We were nowhere." On nikogda ne lže. "He never lies." Nikako ne moram to dělati. "I must do that in no way / I by no means have to do that." The negative pronoun or adverb may appear in any syntactic position (subject, object, adverbial), but the verbal ne MUST always accompany it. Omission of verbal ne in the presence of a negative concord item is ungrammatical.

Under sentential negation (when ne precedes the finite verb and negates the entire clause), a direct object that would otherwise be in the accusative case SHOULD shift to the genitive. This is the genitive of negation. Accusative under affirmation → Genitive under negation: Affirmative (accusative) Negative (genitive) Ona čita knigu. "She reads a book." Ona ne čita knigi. "She does not read a book." On kupil hlěb. "He bought bread." On ne kupil hlěba. "He did not buy bread." My viděliь otca. "We saw father." My ne viděliь otca. "We did not see father." The genitive of negation applies most consistently to direct (accusative) objects of transitive verbs. Its application is stronger with inanimate objects than with animate ones, and stronger in more formal or literary registers. The shift to genitive is also triggered in existential and presentational clauses under negation — see the relevant section TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Obligatoriness of the genitive of negation:** The degree to which the genitive shift is obligatory vs. optional (especially with animate direct objects, with specific NPs, or in colloquial registers) requires a formal normative decision. Some Slavic languages treat this shift as categorical; others treat it as optional or conditioned by definiteness and animacy. The committee should specify the Interslavic norm.

The verb byti in its existential function ("to exist, there is/are") behaves specially under negation: The subject of a negated existential byti takes the genitive case (genitive of negation extended to existential subjects): Affirmative: Jest voda. — "There is water." (nominative) Negative: Ne jest vody. — "There is no water." (genitive) Affirmative: Bog jest. — "God exists." Negative: Boga ne jest. — "God does not exist." (genitive) The short negative existential form nět is available as a lexicalized alternative to ne jest in existential negation: Nět vody. — "There is no water." (equivalent to Ne jest vody.) Nět studentov. — "There are no students." The form nět is invariable (it does not inflect for number) and is restricted to the present tense existential context. For past and future negated existentials, the full form ne bylo (past) and ne budet (future) are used: Ne bylo vody. — "There was no water." Ne budet vody. — "There will be no water." The genitive marking on the subject in negated existential sentences is parallel to the genitive of negation on direct objects and is part of the same general principle: negation extends its scope to the NP most directly governed by the negated predicate, shifting it from nominative/accusative to genitive. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Status of nět:** Whether nět is treated as a grammaticalized particle (like Russian нет) or as a contracted verbal form should be formally specified. Its morphosyntactic status affects whether it can carry tense morphology, combine with aspect, or appear in embedded clauses. A committee decision is needed. ** — Negative concord with stacked negatives:** When multiple negative items appear in the same clause (e.g., "nobody never did anything to anyone"), the rules governing accumulation of negative concord items in a single clause — and whether this is stylistically neutral or marked — should be specified.

Unverified Content The primary source material for syntax (Jan van Steenbergen's grammar-syntax) contains only a brief note that "modifiers usually precede the noun" and that this is "the most neutral and the clearest way." The vast majority of this document — including canonical modifier ordering, numeral case government, apposition rules, possessive adjective distribution, agreeing/non-agreeing modifier theory, sentence-to-NP transformations, and capitalization rules — has no basis in the provided source material and appears to be editorial elaboration. This section specifies the internal structure of noun phrases (NPs) in Interslavic: the canonical ordering of pre-nominal modifiers, the use of demonstratives as quasi-definiteness markers, the case government of numeral phrases, the syntax of appositives, the distribution of genitive possession versus possessive adjectives, and the distinction between agreeing and non-agreeing modifiers, including the rules for transforming full sentences into NP constituents.

Attributive adjectives in Interslavic NORMALLY precede the noun they modify. Pre-nominal placement is the neutral, pragmatically unmarked position. dobry čověk — "a good person" stara kniga — "an old book" Unverified Content The form vysoko derevo appears incorrect: vysoko is the adverb form (see the relevant section), while the neuter nominative adjective should be vysoke derevo. This contradicts the agreement rule stated in this section. vysoko derevo — "a tall tree" Postpositive (post-nominal) adjectives are syntactically grammatical but stylistically marked: they occur in poetic, archaic, or emphatic registers, or in fixed nominal phrases and titles. car slavny — "the glorious tsar" (elevated or poetic register) Writers SHOULD use pre-nominal placement in neutral prose. Post-nominal placement MAY be used when a deliberate stylistic or emphatic effect is intended.

Unverified Content The canonical modifier ordering (Demonstrative > Possessive > Ordinal > Adjective > Noun) and the MUST requirement for full gender/number/case agreement are not addressed in the source material. This ordering appears to be editorial inference from general Slavic typology. When a noun is modified by more than one pre-nominal element, the elements SHOULD appear in the following canonical order: Demonstrative > Possessive > Ordinal numeral > Adjective(s) > Noun Slot Category Example 1 Demonstrative toj, ta, to 2 Possessive moj, jeji, našego 3 Ordinal numeral pervy, vtory 4 Adjective(s) dobry, veliky 5 Head noun čověk, kniga Example with multiple slots filled: toj moj pervy stary drug "that old first friend of mine" All pre-nominal modifiers MUST agree with the head noun in gender, number, and case.
documents.s: Canonical Modifier Order within the NP

documents.t: Interslavic has no grammatical definite or indefinite article. The demonstrative pronoun toj / ta / to MAY be used to signal definiteness — specifically, anaphoric reference to an entity already established in the discourse, or reference to a uniquely identifiable entity. Anaphoric use: Videl jesm muža. Toj muž był vysok. "I saw a man. The man was tall." Identifying use: Toj gorod jest stolica strani. "That/The city is the capital of the country." This use of toj / ta / to is pragmatically optional and is not grammatically obligatory. Its use is RECOMMENDED where disambiguation between definite and indefinite reference is communicatively important, but its absence does not produce an ungrammatical sentence. The demonstrative retains its full morphological agreement (gender, number, case) with the head noun in all uses (see for paradigms).

Unverified Content The entire numeral phrases section , including the distinction between numerals 1–4 (agreement) and 5+ (genitive plural government), and the neuter singular verbal agreement rule, has no basis in the provided source material.

Cardinal numerals 1–4 and all compound numerals ending in 1–4 govern agreement: the numeral and the noun agree in case. The noun stands in the nominative (or the case appropriate to its syntactic function), and the numeral precedes the noun. Numeral Example Gloss 1 jedin čověk "one person" 2 dva muža "two men" (nom/acc) 3 tri ženy "three women" 4 četyri dny "four days" For numerals 2–4, the noun takes the genitive singular when the numeral phrase is in nominative or accusative position.

Cardinal numerals 5 and above govern genitive plural of the noun when the numeral phrase occupies nominative or accusative position. The entire NP is treated as a neuter singular for the purposes of verbal agreement in such position. pět čověkov — "five people" (genitive plural) deset knig — "ten books" (genitive plural) Pět studentov prišlo. — "Five students came." (neuter singular verb) In other syntactic cases (genitive, dative, instrumental, locative), the numeral agrees with the noun in the appropriate case, and the noun stands in the plural of the relevant case. s pětimi čověkami — "with five people" (instrumental plural)

TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Collective numerals (dvoje, troje, etc.) and approximate constructions (okolo pěti, s deset):** The source material does not provide a comprehensive syntactic account of collective numeral phrases or approximate numeral constructions. Committee specification of their case government, agreement behaviour, and distribution relative to cardinal numerals is needed.

Unverified Content The apposition rules, including the MUST requirement for case agreement and the SHOULD preference for declining fixed proper names, are not addressed in the source material. An appositive is a noun phrase that immediately follows and further identifies or renames its head noun. Appositives in Interslavic MUST agree in case with the noun they modify. They also typically agree in number; agreement in gender is not required when the appositive belongs to a different inherent-gender class than the head. gorod Varšava — "the city of Warsaw" (both nominative) v gorode Varšavě — "in the city of Warsaw" (both locative) s profesorom Kovalom — "with Professor Koval" (both instrumental) Personal titles and professional designations preceding a proper name follow the same rule: both title and name decline in case. Nominative: doktor Novak Genitive: doktora Novaka Dative: doktoru Novaku Appositives that are fixed proper names (names of streets, institutions, brands) which are morphologically uninflectable in a given language variant MAY be left undeclined; however, declining them is preferred and SHOULD be done whenever the morphological paradigm permits.

Unverified Content The distinction between possessive adjective and genitive NP strategies, including the SHOULD preference for possessive adjectives with simple possessors, is not addressed in the source material. Interslavic offers two strategies for expressing possession within an NP. Their distribution is governed by the morphological status of the possessor.

When the possessor is a single uninflected noun (a personal name or a simple common noun), a possessive adjective derived from that noun SHOULD be used. Possessive adjectives are formed with the suffixes -ov/-ev (masculine/neuter possessors) and -in (feminine possessors), as specified in the relevant section The possessive adjective precedes the possessed noun and agrees with it in gender, number, and case. bratov dom — "brother's house" (masc. possessor → -ov) materin golos — "mother's voice" (fem. possessor → -in) Ivanovo pismo — "Ivan's letter" (proper name → -ov/-o-)

When the possessor is a pronoun, a modified NP, a proper name already bearing a modifier, or any structure that cannot felicitously form a possessive adjective, the possessor is expressed as a genitive NP following the possessed noun. kniga togo čověka — "that man's book" (modified NP) Unverified Content The form mogo does not match the standard ISV possessive pronoun paradigm. The genitive masculine form is mojego (see pronoun paradigms). This may be an error. dom mogo starogo brata — "my old brother's house" stolica Francii — "the capital of France" (geographical proper name) The genitive possessor follows the possessed head noun. It does not agree with the head but stands in the genitive case regardless of the case of the head noun phrase as a whole.
documents.s: Genitive NP (for complex or inflected possessors)

documents.t: Both strategies are available for simple personal names; the possessive adjective is preferred in neutral prose for personal names. Preferred: Mariin syn — "Maria's son" Also grammatical: syn Marii — "the son of Maria" TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Normative preference between possessive adjective and genitive NP for personal names:** The above preference reflects the general Slavic tendency. Committee confirmation that this represents the normative standard for Interslavic (rather than a mere stylistic note) is needed.

Interslavic distinguishes two structural types of nominal modifier: agreeing modifiers (soglasoványje oprědělenja) and non-agreeing modifiers (nesoglasoványje oprědělenja). Both types can co-occur within the same NP.

An agreeing modifier corresponds in gender, number, and case to the noun (or pronoun, or numeral) it modifies, and inflects together with it as the NP changes case. Agreeing modifiers are expressed by adjectives and adjectival pronouns. They are normally placed before the head noun (see the relevant section). The modifier inflects in lockstep with the head: Case Example Gloss Nominative dobry čověk "a good person" Genitive dobrogo čověka "of a good person" Dative dobromu čověku "to a good person"

A non-agreeing modifier does not change its form when the head noun changes gender, number, or case. It retains its own fixed grammatical form regardless of the case of the NP it belongs to. Non-agreeing modifiers are typically expressed by: a noun (or pronoun) in the genitive case, often with a preposition — positioned after the head noun; less commonly, by another prepositional phrase. The canonical example is a noun phrase with the preposition iz ("from"): Case of NP Example Gloss Nominative čověk (N.) iz Ameriky (G.) "a person from America" Genitive čověka (G.) iz Ameriky (G.) "of a person from America" Dative čověku (D.) iz Ameriky (G.) "to a person from America" The modifier iz Ameriky remains invariantly in the genitive throughout all cases of the head NP. The head noun čověk inflects normally; the non-agreeing modifier does not.

A single head noun may carry both an agreeing and a non-agreeing modifier simultaneously. In such constructions, the agreeing modifier inflects with the head while the non-agreeing modifier remains fixed: dobromu čověku iz Ameriky "to a good person from America" — dobromu (D.) agrees with čověku (D.); iz Ameriky remains in the genitive throughout.

Unverified Content The entire sentence-to-NP transformation section , including the three transformation strategies (byti-predicate, active participle, and gerund), is not addressed in the source material. In complex noun phrases, a full sentence can be condensed into a single NP constituent. Interslavic inherits from its Slavic base three productive transformation strategies. All three rest on the agreeing/non-agreeing distinction described in the relevant section

When a sentence whose predicate is byti ("to be") is compressed into a modifier, the components undergo the following mapping: The subject (nominative) → non-agreeing modifier: preposition od + genitive (ablative/separative construction). Other non-nominative constituents → non-agreeing modifiers in their original case or with a preposition. Passive sentences follow the same rule. The sole difference is that the agent (expressed by the instrumental in the passive clause) maps to a non-agreeing modifier with od + genitive. Example (passive): Slovo jest pisano Bogom (I.) → Slovo od Boga (G.) "The word is written by God" → "the word of God" Example (active byti sentences): Three source sentences: Muž jest dobry. — "The man is good." Toj muž jest iz Ameriky. — "That man is from America." Vidimo tu togo muža. — "We can see that man here." Sentences 1 and 2 compress into modifiers of sentence 3: Vidimo tu dobrogo muža iz Ameriky. "We can see the good man from America here." The nominative subject muž of sentence 1 supplied the agreeing adjective dobry → dobrogo; the predicate iz Ameriky of sentence 2 supplied the non-agreeing modifier iz Ameriky.
documents.s: Transformation of Subject Sentences with byti

documents.t: When a sentence with a non-byti verb predicate is compressed into a modifier of another sentence, the verbal predicate is transformed into an active present participle (agreeing modifier), while the remaining clause constituents become non-agreeing modifiers. Example: Source sentences: Žena piše pismo. — "The woman is writing a letter." Ta žena sedi doma. — "That woman is sitting at home." Sentence 1 is compressed into a modifier of sentence 2: Žena pišuča pismo sedi doma. "The woman writing a letter is sitting at home." The verb piše → active participle pišuča (agreeing with žena); the object pismo remains as a non-agreeing accusative constituent within the expanded NP.

When an entire sentence must be nominalized into a single NP (for use as subject, object, or in another nominal position), the verbal predicate is transformed into a gerund (verbal noun), and all remaining constituents become non-agreeing modifiers. The procedure is: The subject (nominative) → non-agreeing modifier: od + genitive. The direct object (accusative) → non-agreeing modifier: genitive (without preposition). Adverbs → agreeing adjectives modifying the gerund. Example: Source sentence: Moj brat dobro rysuje naš dom. "My brother draws our house well." Source constituent Case → Transformed form moj brat (subject, N.) Nominative → od mojego brata (G.) dobro (adverb) — → dobro (adj. modifying gerund) rysuje (verb) — → rysovanje (gerund, N.) naš dom (object, A.) Accusative → našego doma (G.) Result: Dobro rysovanje našego doma od mojego brata (lit. "Good drawing of our house by my brother")

A single head noun may carry more than one modifier — agreeing or non-agreeing — simultaneously. This is not a separate syntactic construction but a direct consequence of the relevant section and : each modifier independently satisfies its own agreement or non-agreement requirements while all depend on the same head. Example: Kto jest toj filosof učitelj naš? "Who is that philosopher and our teacher?" Here the single head has multiple nominal predicates; the same principle allows multiple pre- and post-nominal modifiers to stack on one noun.

Unverified Content The capitalization rules, including the MUST requirement for unique entities, are not addressed in the source material. Since Interslavic is a constructed language, it has no single rigid orthographic tradition for capitalization. Writers MAY follow the conventions of their own native language. However, one simple and universally applicable rule is RECOMMENDED: The name of any unique or one-of-a-kind entity MUST be written with a capital letter. Examples: Example Gloss Slovačska republika "Slovak Republic" Europska unija "European Union" Naum Ohridsky "Naum of Ohrid" (Ohrid being a unique proper name) This rule ensures that references to geographically, institutionally, or personally unique entities are consistently distinguished in writing.

Unverified Content This file has no dedicated source document for question syntax. The core examples in the relevant section and can be partially verified against Jan van Steenbergen's syntax page (grammar-syntax.md), but the detailed rules and additional sections ( li-placement, multiple wh-questions, embedded questions) are elaborations without direct source backing. This section specifies the syntactic mechanisms for forming questions in Interslavic: yes/no questions (polar questions) using intonation, the či particle, or the li clitic; and constituent (wh-) questions involving fronting of interrogative pronouns and adverbs. It also covers multiple wh-questions and embedded (indirect) questions.
Interslavic provides three grammatically equivalent strategies for forming polar (yes/no) questions. All three are acceptable; the choice is stylistic or register-based.

The simplest strategy is to use a declarative sentence with rising intonation. No structural change is required; the question is signaled solely by pitch contour. Otec kupil knigu? "Has father bought a book?" This strategy is fully grammatical in both written and spoken registers. In written text it is marked by the question mark.

The particle či is placed at the very beginning of the clause, preceding the subject. The rest of the clause retains declarative word order. Či otec kupil knigu? "Has father bought a book?" The particle či is borrowed from the South and West Slavic interrogative tradition. It is neutral with respect to which element of the proposition is being questioned: the entire proposition is put in question.

The particle li is placed immediately after the constituent that is the focus of the question. Most commonly this is the finite verb, but it may follow any focused constituent. The focused constituent is typically moved to clause-initial position to precede li. Kupil li otec knigu? "Has father bought a book?" (focus: the buying) Otec li kupil knigu? "Was it father who bought a book?" (focus: the subject) Knigu li kupil otec? "Was it a book that father bought?" (focus: the object) The li construction is the most explicit means of indicating which constituent is being questioned and is therefore the most information-structurally precise of the three strategies.

The particle li is a post-positive clitic: it must attach to the word or phrase immediately to its left and cannot appear clause-initially or clause-finally on its own. Placement rules: Li is placed directly after the constituent that bears the question focus. The focused constituent is typically moved to the front of the clause, so that li appears early in the sentence. The canonical pattern is: [Focused constituent + li] + [rest of clause]. Focus constituent Example Translation Verb Kupil li otec knigu? "Did father buy a book?" Subject Otec li kupil knigu? "Was it father who bought a book?" Object Knigu li kupil otec? "Was it a book that father bought?" When the verb is the default focus (i.e., the entire proposition is questioned without special constituent focus), the verb-li construction functions equivalently to the či-initial and intonation-only strategies.

Content questions (wh-questions) are formed by moving an interrogative pronoun or adverb to the clause-initial position. The rest of the clause retains normal word order. Interslavic interrogative words include: Interrogative Gloss kto who čto / što what koj / koji / ktory which kaky what kind of koliko how many / how much kde where kamo whither (to where) kųda whence (from where) kogda when kako how začto / počto why Unverified Content The gloss of kųda as "whence (from where)" may be incorrect. In most Slavic languages, cognates of kuda mean "whither (to where)", not "whence (from where)". "Whence" is typically expressed with a prefixed form such as odkųda. This gloss could not be verified against source material. Unverified Content The forms začto and počto for "why" could not be verified in the source material. The source correlatives table distinguishes "why (purpose)" from "why (reason)" but does not confirm these specific lexical forms. počto in particular resembles Russian почто rather than a pan-Slavic compromise form. The interrogative pronoun or adverb MUST move to clause-initial position. The case marking on the interrogative reflects its grammatical role in the clause, not its surface position. This means that the moved interrogative bears the same case it would bear in its base position within the clause. Koju knigu kupil otec? "Which book did father buy?" (koju = accusative, direct object) Kde otec kupil tu knigu? "Where did father buy that book?" Kto kupil knigu? "Who bought the book?" (kto = nominative, subject) Komu dal otec knigu? "To whom did father give the book?" (komu = dative) Because case endings mark grammatical roles independently of word order, wh-movement does not create ambiguity: Koja žena ljubi togo muža? "Which woman loves that man?" (woman = nominative subject, man = accusative object) Koju ženu ljubi toj muž? "Which woman does that man love?" (man = nominative subject, woman = accusative object) Where disambiguation is needed beyond what case marking provides, a passive construction makes the roles explicit: Koja žena jest ljubjena od togo muža? "Which woman is loved by that man?"

When a clause contains more than one questioned constituent (multiple wh-question), the following rule applies: One interrogative pronoun or adverb is moved to clause-initial position. The remaining interrogative(s) stay in their base (in-situ) position within the clause. Kto komu dal knigu? "Who gave a book to whom?" Fronting of all interrogatives simultaneously (pied-piping of multiple wh-words) is also attested and is grammatically permissible, but this is a marked construction characteristic of more formal or rhetorical registers: Kto čto jěl? "Who ate what?" (both interrogatives fronted — permissible but marked) TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Multiple wh-fronting:** The exact distribution of in-situ vs. fronted second interrogatives in multiple wh-questions requires further specification. In particular, whether superiorty effects (constraining which interrogative must front) apply in Interslavic, and whether any ordering preference between fronted interrogatives exists, should be addressed by the committee.

Embedded (indirect) questions use the same interrogative pronouns and adverbs as direct questions. The embedded clause does not undergo inversion of any kind; it retains the word order of a declarative clause with the interrogative element in front. Ne znam, kto kupil knigu. "I don't know who bought the book." Ona spytala, kde jest bolnica. "She asked where the hospital is." Skažite mi, koliko to stoit. "Tell me how much that costs." The embedded question is introduced by the interrogative word alone; no additional complementizer (že, čto) is inserted. The verb of the embedded clause carries a tense appropriate to its own temporal reference, without obligatory sequence-of-tense adjustment (see the relevant section). Embedded yes/no questions are introduced by li (retained from the direct question) or by či: Ne znam, kupil li otec knigu. "I don't know whether father bought a book." Ona spytala, či on pridot. "She asked whether he would come." TODO: Committee Input Needed – — Register distribution of the three polar question strategies: The relative frequency and register appropriateness of intonation-only vs. či-initial vs. li-clitic strategies has not been formally specified. A normative recommendation on preferred usage in formal written Interslavic would be useful. ** — Indirect question with li:** Whether the li-strategy for embedded yes/no questions requires movement of the focused constituent (as in direct questions) or allows it to remain in situ needs committee clarification.

Unverified Content This file has no direct source material in the original Interslavic grammar by Jan van Steenbergen. The source material covers relative pronoun forms (ktory, koj, iže) and their inflection (in the pronouns section), but does not address relative clause syntax, gap vs. resumptive pronoun strategies, long-distance extraction, or free relative clause structure. Most of the syntactic analysis in this file is likely AI-generated and should be reviewed by the committee. This section specifies the structure, pronoun selection, inflection, and extraction strategies for relative clauses in Interslavic, including headed relative clauses, the gap vs. resumptive pronoun alternation, animacy effects, and free (headless) relative clauses.

The primary relative pronoun in Interslavic is ktory ("who, which, that"). It introduces headed relative clauses — clauses that modify an explicit antecedent noun phrase. Ktory agrees with the antecedent in gender and number, and takes the case required by its grammatical function within the relative clause (subject, direct object, indirect object, etc.). It is inflected exactly like an ordinary hard-stem adjective. Unverified Content The verb form dah in the table below and in section is a South Slavic aorist form. Standard ISV past tense of dati would be dal. Function in relative clause Example Subject žena, ktora čita — "a woman who reads" Direct object děte, ktore ja viděl — "the child that I saw" Indirect object (dative) muž, ktoremu dah knigu — "the man to whom I gave the book" Genitive (possession) žena, ktoroj muž je lekar — "the woman whose husband is a doctor" Locative (place) město, v ktorom on žive — "the city in which he lives" Full example with internal structure: Ja viděl muža, ktory prišel včera. "I saw the man who came yesterday." (antecedent muž is masculine singular; ktory = nominative masculine singular, functioning as subject of the relative clause) Unverified Content The form kturų below appears to be a typo for ktorų. The parenthetical explanation correctly says ktorų but the example sentence has kturų. Kniga, kturų on čital, jest interesna. "The book that he was reading is interesting." (antecedent kniga is feminine singular; ktorų = accusative feminine singular, functioning as direct object) The relative clause immediately follows the antecedent NP. Separation of the relative clause from its antecedent by intervening material is possible but stylistically marked.

The pronoun koj ("which") is the South Slavic variant of the relative pronoun. It is inflected like the possessive pronoun moj (soft-stem adjective paradigm) and is used in precisely the same syntactic construction as ktory. Koj and ktory are fully interchangeable as relative pronouns; their meanings are identical. The choice between them is a matter of stylistic register or dialect orientation: ktory — pan-Slavic, appropriate in all contexts and registers koj — South Slavic flavor; appropriate when targeting a South Slavic readership or for stylistic variation Examples with koj: žena, koja čita — "a woman who reads" děte, koje ja viděl — "the child that I saw" muž, kojemu dah knigu — "the man to whom I gave the book" In all cases where koj appears, ktory may be substituted, and vice versa.

A third, more archaic relative pronoun iže is also available, primarily in elevated or literary style. Its declension is irregular: Nominative (all genders, both numbers): iže Other cases: inflected like the corresponding forms of the personal pronoun on / ona / ono with the suffix -že appended: Masculine genitive singular: jegože Masculine dative singular: jemuže Feminine genitive singular: jejže etc. Iže is not recommended for ordinary prose; it is retained here for recognition purposes and for writers adopting a Church Slavonic-influenced register.

Interslavic relative clauses normally employ the gap strategy: the position from which the relative pronoun has been extracted is left empty (a syntactic gap). No resumptive pronoun is inserted in the extraction site. The relative pronoun itself, appearing at the head of the relative clause, bears the case that corresponds to its grammatical role in the embedded clause. This is what makes the case inflection of ktory/koj syntactically crucial. Correct (gap strategy): Muž, ktorogo [∅] ja ljubim, jest doktor. "The man whom I love is a doctor." (ktorogo = genitive/accusative of animate masculine; gap in object position) Incorrect (resumptive pronoun — not used in standard Interslavic): Muž, ktory ja jeho ljubim, jest doktor. (non-standard) The absence of resumptive pronouns in canonical relative clauses is a structural requirement of standard Interslavic, not merely a preference.

In cases of deeply embedded extraction — where the gap would be separated from the relative pronoun by one or more clause boundaries — a resumptive pronoun MAY appear in the extraction site. This is a licensed exception to the gap strategy specified in the relevant section Long-distance extraction arises when the antecedent is relativized out of an embedded complement clause: Muž, ktory ona skazala, čto ona jemu věrit, jest tamo. "The man whom she said she trusts is there." (resumptive jemu in the dative position within the embedded clause) The use of a resumptive pronoun in such contexts reduces processing difficulty and is grammatically acceptable. Writers SHOULD use a resumptive pronoun when the extraction site is more than one clause boundary away from the relative pronoun. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Resumptive pronoun conditions:** The exact conditions licensing (or requiring) a resumptive pronoun in long-distance extraction — number of clause boundaries, type of embedding predicate, island constraints — are not specified in the source material. Committee determination of normative rules for long-distance relativization is needed.

The accusative form of the relative pronoun ktory follows the animacy distinction that applies to all masculine adjective-like forms in Interslavic: Inanimate masculine singular accusative = same as nominative: ktory Animate masculine singular accusative = same as genitive: ktorogo Animacy Antecedent Relative pronoun (accusative) Example Inanimate stol "table" (m.) ktory stol, ktory ja viděl — "the table that I saw" Animate muž "man" (m.) ktorogo muž, ktorogo ja viděl — "the man whom I saw" Feminine žena "woman" (f.) ktorų žena, ktorų ja viděl — "the woman whom I saw" Neuter děte "child" (n.) ktore děte, ktore ja viděl — "the child that I saw" The same animacy rule applies to koj in its accusative forms.
documents.s: Animacy Effects in Relative Pronoun Selection

documents.t: A free relative clause (also called a headless relative clause) is a relative clause that lacks an explicit antecedent noun. The relative pronoun itself incorporates both the role of "head" and "relativizer". Interslavic forms free relative clauses with the pronouns kto ("whoever"), čto ("whatever"), and ktory / koj ("whichever") used without an overt antecedent NP: Kto žela, može priti. "Whoever wishes may come." Čto on skazal, jest pravda. "What he said is true." / "Whatever he said is true." Ktory pridet pervym, dostanet nagradu. "Whichever (one) comes first will receive a prize." In free relative clauses with kto and čto, the pronoun takes the case required by its function in the matrix clause (since there is no antecedent NP to whose case it must agree). When kto or čto also has a syntactic role within the embedded clause, the case of the embedded function takes precedence, and the matrix function is supplied by the clause as a whole. Ja dam knigu tomu, komu ona nužna jest. "I will give the book to whoever needs it." (demonstrative tomu serves as matrix indirect object; komu = dative in embedded clause) TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Iže in formal/liturgical texts:** The precise scope of permissible iže usage — whether it is purely archaic/liturgical or may appear in any elevated register — requires a committee ruling. The source describes it as a "third option" without prescribing restrictions. ** — Case matching in free relatives:** When the role of the free relative clause in the matrix clause differs from the role of the pronoun within the embedded clause (the "case mismatch" problem), the norm for which case form the free relative pronoun takes has not been established in the source material. Committee codification of the standard resolution is needed.

This section specifies the syntax of special clause types in Interslavic that have distinctive structural properties: imperatives (direct commands, hortatives, and optatives); impersonal constructions (meteorological predicates, dative-experiencer constructions, modal impersonals); reflexive constructions and their three functions; and complement control (subject-control and object-control verbs).

Unverified Content The entire (Imperatives) section — including imperative formation rules, paradigm tables, consonant alternation rules, irregular imperatives, hortative, and optative constructions — has no basis in the primary source material (grammar-syntax.md), which does not address imperative morphology or syntax. The imperative mood is used to express direct commands, requests, invitations, and prohibitions. Interslavic imperatives are morphologically formed for three person–number combinations and syntactically may be supplemented by a third-person optative construction using nehaj.

The 2nd-person imperative is the canonical form of the direct command, addressed to the person(s) the speaker is speaking to directly. Formation: The imperative stem is derived from the 3rd person singular present tense form (in the traditional/non-contracted conjugation). If the stem ends in the glide -j-, the imperative endings attach directly; otherwise, the linking vowel -i- is inserted. 3sg present Imperative stem 2sg imperative Gloss dělajeт dělaj- dělaj do! umějeт uměj- uměj know how! věrujeт věruj- věruj believe! neseт nes- nesi carry! postaviт postavi- postavi put! pijeт pij- pij drink! myjeт myj- myj wash! Consonant alternations before the linking vowel -i-: When the linking vowel -i- is added to a stem ending in a velar consonant, First Palatalization applies: k → č, g → ž, h → š. (These alternations apply because the imperative stem is taken from the 3sg, which has already undergone the vowel -e- alternation, but the palatal glide trigger in the 2sg ending conditions palatalization of the preceding consonant.) pomožeт → pomoži — "help!" izrěčeт → izrěči — "pronounce! utter!" Verbs in -avati: Verbs with the suffix -va- in the infinitive form their imperative from the infinitive stem with the linking glide -j- rather than from the present stem: davati → davaj — "give! (habitual)" sòzdavati → sòzdavaj — "create! (habitual)" Irregular imperatives: Verb Imperative 2sg Gloss byti bųď be! jěsti jěď eat! věděti věď know! dati daj give! Full paradigm of the imperative (three forms): The Interslavic imperative has three forms: 2nd person singular, 1st person plural (hortative), and 2nd person plural (plural command or polite singular command). The plural and 1st-plural forms are built by adding -mo and -te respectively to the 2sg stem: dati byti davati věriti věrovati 2sg daj bųď davaj věri věruj 1pl dajmo bųďmo davajmo věrimo věrujmo 2pl dajte bųďte davajte věrite věrujte Negation of the imperative: Negative commands (prohibitions) are formed by placing ne before the imperative form: Ne dělaj tego! — "Don't do that!" Ne bųďte glupi! — "Don't be foolish!" Pragmatic note: The direct 2nd-person imperative is used for direct, unhedged commands and requests. For requests that require politeness, speakers may prefer the conditional (by + L-participle) or phrases with modal verbs such as možete li "could you".

The 1st-person plural imperative (hortative) expresses an inclusive invitation or proposal — "Let's (all) do X" — addressing both the speaker and the interlocutor(s). The form is constructed by adding -mo to the 2sg imperative stem: Idemo! — "Let's go!" Dělajmo! — "Let's do (it)!" Bųďmo veseli! — "Let's be merry!" Pijmo za zdorovje! — "Let's drink to health!" The hortative is an inclusive form: it necessarily includes the speaker. For an exclusive directive to a group not including the speaker, the 2nd-person plural imperative (-te form) is used instead. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Hortative vs. indicative 1pl distinction:** The outline notes that the hortative 1pl is distinct from the indicative 1pl present by stress pattern. The exact stress rule distinguishing dělajmo (imperative) from any indicative 1pl form has not been fully specified in the source material. A committee decision on whether stress is the primary or only distinguishing feature, and what the normative stress placement is, is needed.

For 3rd-person commands, wishes, and optative expressions ("let him/her/it/them do X"), Interslavic uses the construction nehaj followed by the verb in the 3rd-person indicative (present or past as appropriate). Nehaj on pride! — "Let him come!" Nehaj ona čita. — "Let her read." Nehaj bųde světlo! — "Let there be light!" Nehaj oni sami rěšajut. — "Let them decide for themselves." The particle nehaj is invariable. It may also be used with 1st and 2nd person verbs for stronger optative or permissive nuance, though the imperative forms are more usual in those persons.

Unverified Content The entire (Impersonal Constructions) section — including weather verbs, dative-experiencer constructions, and modal impersonals — has no basis in the primary source material (grammar-syntax.md), which does not discuss impersonal constructions. Impersonal constructions are clauses that lack a nominative subject. In Interslavic, impersonal constructions appear in several structural types.

Meteorological events and natural phenomena are expressed by 3rd-person singular verbs with no overt subject: Sněži. — "It snows / It is snowing." Doždi. — "It rains / It is raining." Grmi. — "It thunders." Světaje sę. — "It is dawning." No dummy subject pronoun is inserted. The verb appears in the default 3sg form.

Many psychological states and physical sensations are expressed impersonally with a dative experiencer (the person who undergoes the state, in the dative case) and no nominative subject: Mně jest holodno. — "I am cold." (lit. "To me it is cold.") Unverified Content The form Tebu is suspect. The expected ISV dative of ty is tebě (or tebi). The form Tebu does not follow standard ISV pronoun declension patterns. Tebu jest žarko. — "You are hot." (lit. "To you it is hot.") Jemu jest skučno. — "He is bored." Nam jest veselo. — "We are merry." Mně sę hočet spati. — "I feel like sleeping." (lit. "To me it wants to sleep.") The predicate in these constructions is an invariable adverbial/predicative form (often ending in -o) or an impersonal verb.
