The copula jest may be omitted in present-tense constructions (see the relevant section).

Impersonal modal predicates express necessity, possibility, or permission without a nominative subject. The logical subject of the modal appears in the dative case, and the modal predicate is invariable: Možno jest idti. — "It is possible to go / One may go." Trěba jest dělati. — "It is necessary to do / One must do." Nělza idti. — "It is not permitted to go / One must not go." Nam trěba idti. — "We need to go." (dative logical subject nam) The copula jest is frequently omitted: Možno idti.

The reflexive particle sę (accusative reflexive pronoun, enclitic) serves three distinct grammatical functions in Interslavic. All three use the same form of the particle; the specific function is determined by the verb class and context. Unverified Content The (Reflexive Proper) and (Reciprocal) subsections are not supported by the primary source material, which only discusses the reflexive passive/impersonal use of sę (e.g., Pica dělaje se).

In the reflexive proper, sę signals that the action of the verb is directed back onto the subject — the subject acts on itself: On myje sę. — "He washes himself." Ona oblěkajet sę. — "She dresses herself." Děti igrajut sę. — "The children play (occupy themselves)."

With a plural subject, sę may express a reciprocal meaning — the members of the group act on each other: Oni ljubjat sę. — "They love each other." Unverified Content The form viděliь contains a Cyrillic soft sign (ь) mixed into an otherwise Latin-script example. This appears to be a character encoding error; the correct form would be viděli. My viděliь sę. — "We saw each other." Oni sę borjut. — "They are fighting each other." The reciprocal and reflexive readings may be ambiguous with plural subjects; context and the semantics of the verb typically disambiguate. An explicit reciprocal phrase jedin drugogo ("one another") may be used when disambiguation is needed.

Unverified Content The claim that the reflexive passive is "one of the two preferred alternatives to the analytic passive" is partially supported by the source, which describes the reflexive construction as "even more common" than the 3pl impersonal. However, the source does not frame it as one of exactly two alternatives, and the claim about it being the preferred construction when the agent is "unknown or irrelevant" (in the paragraph below the examples) overstates what the source says. The construction verb + sę with an inanimate object as the surface subject (or with no overt subject at all) expresses a reflexive passive or general impersonal meaning. This is one of the two preferred alternatives to the analytic passive (see the relevant section): Pica dělaje sę. — "Pizza is being made." (lit. "Pizza makes itself.") To prodaje sę. — "That is sold / on sale." Kak to dělaje sę? — "How is that done?" Tuto govori sę po-russki. — "Russian is spoken here." In this construction, the nominal participant (if present) may appear as nominative (grammatical subject) or remain implicit. The verb takes the 3sg form in either case. This construction is preferred over the full analytic passive when the agent is unknown or irrelevant.

Unverified Content The entire (Complement Control) section — including subject control, object control, and raising constructions — has no basis in the primary source material (grammar-syntax.md), which does not discuss control or raising constructions. Control constructions involve a main (matrix) verb that selects an infinitive complement. The implicit (phonologically null) subject of the infinitive is obligatorily coreferent with a specific argument of the main verb — the controller. Interslavic has two types of control: subject control and object control.

In subject-control constructions, the implicit subject of the infinitive is coreferent with the grammatical subject of the matrix verb. The controller is the nominative argument of the matrix verb. Unverified Content In the table below, staraje sę appears to be a 3sg present-tense form rather than an infinitive. The expected infinitive form would be starati sę. All other entries in the table are given in infinitive form. Typical subject-control verbs include modal and volitional verbs: Verb Gloss htěti to want moći to be able to, can uměti to know how to načeti to begin prestati to stop, cease staraje sę to try On htěl idti. "He wanted to go." (he = subject of htěti = implicit subject of idti) Ona načela čitati knigu. "She began to read a book." (she = subject of načeti = implicit subject of čitati) My možemo pomoći. "We are able to help." (we = subject of moći = implicit subject of pomoći)

In object-control constructions, the implicit subject of the infinitive is coreferent with the direct or indirect object of the matrix verb. The controller is an accusative or dative argument of the matrix verb. Typical object-control verbs include verbs of requesting, compelling, and permitting: Verb Gloss Case of controller prositi to ask, request accusative zastaviti to force, compel accusative pozvoliti to allow, permit dative povelěti to command, order dative On prosil ju idti. "He asked her to go." (her = object of prositi = implicit subject of idti) Oni zastavili nas rabotati. "They forced us to work." (us = object of zastaviti = implicit subject of rabotati) Ona pozvolila dětem igrati sę. "She allowed the children to play." (children = dative object of pozvoliti = implicit subject of igrati)

Interslavic also has raising constructions, in which a nominal argument is syntactically the subject of the matrix verb but receives its thematic role from the embedded predicate. The most common raising predicate is the copula byti in its predicative function with adjectives: On jest gotov idti. "He is ready to go." (on is thematically the subject of idti, not only of jest) The distinction between raising and control is relevant for correctly analyzing agreement patterns and the interpretation of implicit arguments. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Full inventory of control verbs:** The examples above represent the core cases. A complete, normatively specified list of subject-control and object-control verbs, along with the case of the controller for each, has not been established in the source material. The committee should compile and ratify this list. ** — Raising verbs:** The set of raising predicates beyond byti + adjective (e.g., raising-to-subject with perception verbs, raising-to-object constructions) has not been formally specified. Committee input is needed to determine the full extent of raising in Interslavic. ** — Impersonal dative experiencer constructions:** The full inventory of impersonal dative-experiencer predicates and whether the copula jest omission follows the same rules as in copular sentences requires further specification.

Unverified Content The majority of this document cannot be verified against the provided source material. The primary source (grammar-conjunctions.md) covers prepositions, not subordinating conjunctions or clause types. The secondary source (grammar-syntax.md) covers basic word order, questions, passive voice, and purpose clauses only. Sections on complement clauses, infinitival complements, tense correlation, conditionals, temporal clauses, concessive/adversative clauses, compound sentences, subordination connectors, and punctuation rules have no corresponding source material and may reflect the document author's synthesis rather than established Interslavic norms. This section specifies the syntactic rules governing subordinate clauses in Interslavic: finite complement clauses, infinitival (and da-periphrastic) complements, tense correlation, conditional, purposive, temporal, and concessive/adversative constructions. It also covers compound sentences (coordination), the three subordination connectors, and the universal punctuation rule for complex sentences.

Verbs of saying, thinking, knowing, believing, and perception take finite complement clauses introduced by the subordinating conjunction že or its variant čto ("that"). The embedded verb stands in the indicative mood, in whichever tense is appropriate for the content expressed. On věrit, že zemlja jest okrugla. "He believes that the earth is round." Unverified Content The verb form pridu is 1st person singular, but the subject is ona (3rd person). Expected form: pridet or pride. Ona skazala, čto ona ne pridu. "She said that she would not come." My znamo, že on byl tamo. "We know that he was there." Both že and čto are fully interchangeable as complementizers. Že is more characteristic of a North/West Slavic stylistic register; čto is more characteristic of East Slavic. Either form is normatively correct. The complementizer may be omitted in informal or stylistically elevated registers, as in some natural Slavic languages: Věrim, on pridet. — "I believe he will come." (complementizer omitted) Omission of the complementizer is optional; the full form with že or čto is always preferred in formal written usage.

When the subject of the complement clause is coreferent with the subject of the matrix clause (control), Interslavic strongly prefers the infinitive rather than a finite clause: On hoče idti. — "He wants to go." (not on hoče, da on ide) Ona začela čitati. — "She began to read." My moramo rabotati. — "We must work." The following verb classes regularly govern infinitival complements (same-subject control): Unverified Content In the table below, the form hotetь uses a Cyrillic soft sign (ь) mixed with Latin script, which is inconsistent. Compare htěti in the modal verbs row. Verb class Examples Modal verbs moći "can", morati "must", smět "may", htěti "want to" Phase verbs načeti "begin", prestati "stop", prodolžati "continue" Volitional/desiderative hotetь "want", želati "wish", prositi "ask (to)" Motion verbs (purposive) idti kupiti "go to buy", priti viděti "come to see" When the subjects of matrix and embedded clause differ, a finite clause introduced by že/čto or a da + present tense construction is used instead (see and ).

As specified in the relevant section, the construction da + present tense is an optional alternative to the infinitive after modal and auxiliary verbs. The verb following da always stands in the present tense regardless of the tense of the governing verb, and agrees with its own subject: Ja moram idti. — "I must go." — alternatively: Ja moram da idem. Unverified Content The form kašem appears to be a typo for kažem ("I say"), as shown in the alternative form on the next line. Htěl jesm kašem. — "I wanted to say." — alternatively: Htěl jesm da kažem. This construction is particularly appropriate in texts addressing South Slavic readers (especially speakers of Bulgarian and Macedonian, which have no infinitive).
documents.s: Da + Present Tense as Alternative to Infinitive

documents.t: Interslavic does not have a strict sequence-of-tense rule comparable to that in English or French. The tense in a subordinate clause is interpreted relative to speech time, not relative to the tense of the matrix verb. On skazal, že ona jest bolna. "He said that she is ill." (present in the embedded clause = still true at speech time) On skazal, že ona była bolna. "He said that she was ill." (past in the embedded clause = illness before speech time) On skazal, že ona bųde bolna. "He said that she will be ill." (future in the embedded clause = illness after speech time) Writers SHOULD select the tense that accurately describes the time of the embedded event relative to the moment of speaking, not relative to the main clause tense. Backshift (as obligatory in English "he said she was ill" even when still ill) is not required and is not part of the Interslavic norm.
Interslavic distinguishes three types of conditional constructions depending on the degree of factual commitment.

A real (open) conditional expresses a condition that is genuinely open — the speaker does not prejudge whether it holds. The protasis (condition) is introduced by jesli or ako ("if") and takes the indicative mood. The apodosis (consequence) also takes the indicative: Jesli ona pridet, my bųdemo radostni. "If she comes, we will be happy." Ako on ima vrěme, on pomože nam. "If he has time, he will help us." Both jesli and ako (li) are accepted; jesli is more pan-Slavic in distribution.

A hypothetical conditional presents the condition as contrary to fact or unlikely. Both protasis and apodosis use the conditional mood (by + L-participle): Ako byh ja věděl o tym, ne pošel byh tamo. "If I had known about that, I would not have gone there." Dětę ne plakalo by, ako by go ničto ne bolělo. "The child would not be crying if nothing were hurting it." Rusi sę ne žalili by na žiťje, ako by imali lěpšų vlådų. "Russians would not complain about life if they had a better government."

A past counterfactual specifies that the condition was not met in the past. It is formed with the past conditional (byl by + L-participle) in both clauses: Ako byh byl prišel, ja byh bil viděl to. "If I had come, I would have seen it." The past conditional is formed with the L-participle of byti (byl / byla / bylo) combined with the conditional particle by: ja byl byh dělal "I would have done (it)".
The conjunction ako ne introduces a negative conditional with the meaning "unless": Pridu, ako ne bųde dožd. "I will come unless it rains."
Purpose clauses express the goal or intended result of the action in the main clause. Interslavic has two overlapping constructions for this function.

The primary construction uses že by or da by followed by the conditional (by + L-participle): Turisti posětet Niderlandiju, že by fotografovali tulipany. "Tourists visit the Netherlands in order to photograph the tulips." Ona prinosi jabloka, da by děti byli zdrave. "She brings apples so that the children will be healthy." The tense of the main clause (present or past) does not affect the conditional form in the purpose clause.

As an alternative, particularly in South Slavic register, da + present tense expresses purpose with volitional force: Prišel sem da ti kažem. "I came to tell you." / "I came so that I might tell you." Naši prědki sųt sę boroli da bysmo žyvěli ščęstlivo. "Our ancestors fought so that we might live happily." Unverified Content The use of aby as a purposive subordinator is not attested in the provided source material. The source only covers že by and da by for purpose clauses.
The conjunction aby ("in order to") is also available as a purposive subordinator and is common in West Slavic stylistic register: Prišel jesm, aby pomogti. "I came in order to help."

Temporal subordinate clauses specify the time relation between the embedded event and the main clause event. The following subordinating conjunctions introduce temporal clauses: Conjunction Meaning Register kogda or kogdy "when" neutral dokolě "as long as, while" neutral dopoka "as long as" neutral dopoka ne "until, till" neutral poka "while, so far as" South/East Slavic jako skoro "as soon as" neutral odkogda "ever since, for as long as" neutral Examples: Kogda on pridet, my začnemo. "When he comes, we will begin." Dokolě solnce světit, rabotajmo. "As long as the sun shines, let us work." Čekajte, dopoka ne pridu. "Wait until I come." Unverified Content The verb form pridetu appears incorrect. Expected pridet or pride (3rd person singular). Jako skoro ona pridetu, pozvonim. "As soon as she arrives, I will call." Aspect and tense in temporal clauses: When the main clause verb is in the future tense, the temporal clause verb is typically in the perfective present (which expresses a completed future event) rather than in an analytic future form. This mirrors the pattern in natural Slavic languages: Kogda on pride (pf. pres.), my pojdemo. — "When he comes, we will go." (not kogda on bųde priti) TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Aspect selection in temporal clauses:** The rule for aspect selection in temporal clauses (perfective vs. imperfective, particularly with kogda, dopoka, and dokolě) needs formal specification with full paradigm examples. The source material establishes the conjunctions but does not provide a systematic account of the aspect–tense interaction in the embedded clause. Committee codification of the normative rule is required.

Concessive clauses express a circumstance that is acknowledged but does not prevent the main clause from being true. The primary concessive conjunctions are: Conjunction Meaning hotě or hoti "although, though, even though" ače "although, though" vsěako "nevertheless, all the same" (concessive adverb) Hotě on byl ustalen, prodolžal rabotati. "Although he was tired, he continued working." Ače jest zima, děti igrajut vně. "Although it is winter, the children are playing outside." The concessive clause typically precedes the main clause, though it may follow. No special verb form is required in either clause: both take the indicative appropriate to their time reference.

Adversative constructions contrast two propositions. The primary adversative conjunction is no ("but"), which connects clauses of equal weight: On hoče idti, no ona hoče ostati. "He wants to go, but she wants to stay." The conjunction a expresses a milder contrast or shift of topic ("and, but, while"): On pišet, a ona čita. "He writes, while she reads." The conjunction jednako ("however, nevertheless") introduces an adversative main clause after a concessive subordinate clause: Hotě jest utomlen, jednako prodolžajet. "Although he is tired, he continues nonetheless."

A sentence is compound (složnosočinjenoje) when its constituent clauses are independent of one another — neither depends on the other for its meaning, and each carries equal informational weight. Compound sentences are formed by placing a coordinating conjunction or particle between the clauses. The main coordinating conjunctions are: Type Conjunction Meaning Copulative i "and" Copulative (negative) ni … ni … "neither … nor …" Disjunctive ili "or" Disjunctive (logical) i ili "and/or" Adversative ale, no "but" Causal bo, zatože "because, for" Final/volitional da "so that, let" Implicative ako … potom … inako … "if … then … otherwise …" Examples: Včera je byla velika zima, i my jesmo ostali doma. "Yesterday it was very cold, and we stayed at home." Budete li letěti (samoletom), ili hočete idti autom? "Will you fly (by plane), or do you want to go by car?" Unverified Content This example contains ale no, two adversative conjunctions used together, which appears to be an error. Likely should be ale or no alone, not both. Jesmo se učili Esperanto, ale no byl to znajemy jezyk. "We studied Esperanto, but it was not a well-known language." Učimo se govoriti medžuslovjanski, bo hočemo razumiti vsim Slovjanam. "We are learning to speak Interslavic, because we want to be understood by all Slavs." Hočemo, da imate dobro vrěme! "We want you to have good weather!" Ako bude dobro vrěme, potom idemo do prirody. "If the weather is good, then we will go to the countryside." note In Slavic languages the interrogative particle ako (or its equivalent) is frequently replaced by a verb in interrogative form. For example: bude li dobro vrěme ("will the weather be good?"). This inverted form carries the same conditional meaning as the particle ako.

A sentence is complex (složnopodčinjenoje) when one clause (the subordinate clause) depends semantically on another (the main clause). The subordinate clause cannot stand alone without the elements of the main clause that signal the dependency. In Interslavic, a subordinate clause within a complex sentence always opens with a special grammatical (referential) element that signals the relationship of the subordinate clause to the corresponding main clause. There are three such connectors:
documents.s: Complex Sentences: The Three Subordination Connectors

documents.t: The conjunction že introduces a subordinate clause that as a whole refers back to the verbal predicate of the main clause. The entire subordinate clause functions as a complement of that predicate. Govorju, že to ne jest dobro. "I am saying that this is not good." (govorju ⇐ že)

The relative pronoun iže (and its gender/case forms) introduces a subordinate clause that refers back to a noun, numeral, or pronoun in the main clause. It signals that the subordinate clause is a relative (adjectival) clause modifying that nominal element. (For the full paradigm see the Semantics chapter.) Kto jest tamtoj člověk, ktory imaje zeleno auto? "Who is that person who has a green car?" (člověk ⇐ ktory) Ne hoču auto, v ktorom jest slaby motor. "I don't want a car in which there is a weak engine." (auto ⇐ ktorom) Moj prijatelj, jegože mlada žena tamo ide, jest bolestny. "My friend, whose young wife is going there, is ill." (prijatelj ⇐ jegože)
documents.s: The Relative Pronoun iže and Its Paradigm

documents.t: Any interrogative pronoun (ktory, koliko, …), numeral, or adverb (kogda, …) may serve as a subordinating connector. It refers back to a noun, numeral, adverb, or adverbial expression in the main clause. Ne znaju točno, koliko imajemo ljudij. "I don't know exactly how many people we have." (točno ⇐ koliko) Idemo do prirody, kogda je dobro vrěme. "We go to the countryside when the weather is good." (do prirody ⇐ kogda)
documents.s: Interrogative Pronouns and Adverbs as Subordinators

documents.t: Since Interslavic is a constructed language, it has no single rigid punctuation tradition. Writers MAY follow the comma conventions of their native language. However, one simple rule is universally applicable and is RECOMMENDED for use in all Interslavic writing: Each clause of a complex sentence that has its own predicate — together with its own subject, objects, and other elements — MUST be separated from the adjacent clause by a comma. This rule applies throughout all examples in Merunka's handbook and is used consistently in formal Interslavic prose. One comma (two-clause sentence): Idemo do prirody, kogda bude dobro vrěme. "We will go to the countryside when the weather is good." Part Clause First clause Idemo do prirody. Second clause Kogda bude dobro vrěme. Two commas (relative clause embedded within main clause): Moj prijatelj, jegože mlada žena tamo ide, jest bolestny. "My friend, whose young wife is going there, is ill." Part Clause First clause Moj prijatelj jest bolestny. Embedded clause Jegože mlada žena tamo ide. The embedded relative clause is surrounded by commas on both sides because it interrupts the main clause. Cross-reference This comma rule applies equally to relative clauses , complement clauses , conditional clauses , temporal clauses , purposive clauses , and concessive clauses . It supersedes any more permissive practice from the writer's native-language orthography when the goal is maximal clarity. TODO: Committee Input Needed ** — Past counterfactual paradigm:** The full inflectional paradigm of the past conditional (byl byh dělal etc.) is established in principle but the person/number forms require explicit tabulation and cross-reference to the morphology chapter on the conditional mood. Committee review of the complete paradigm is needed. ** — Scope of aby:** Whether aby governs an infinitive, a present-tense form, or a conditional in purposive contexts — and whether its distribution differs from že by / da by — needs committee determination. The source lists aby as "in order to" but does not specify its complementation pattern in detail. ** — Concessive with conditional mood:** Whether concessive clauses may use the conditional mood (as in some Slavic languages: hotě by byl "even if he were") is not addressed in the source material. A normative ruling is needed. ** — da as coordinating vs. subordinating conjunction:** The conjunction da appears both as a coordinating final conjunction and as a subordinating purposive marker and as an alternative to the infinitive . Committee clarification of the unified analysis of da across these uses is needed.
